
> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** All Mermaid node identifiers above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `P1`, `C4`, `N21`) — none collide with reserved Mermaid keywords such as `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style`. Labels are kept uppercase alphanumeric and free of markdown bold markers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Universal Design. List its seven principles.**

**Model Answer:**
Universal Design, coined by Ronald L. Mace in 1997, is the design of products and environments such that they can be used by all people, to the greatest extent possible, without requiring adaptation or specialized design.

The seven principles are:
1. Equitable Use
2. Flexibility in Use
3. Simple and Intuitive Use
4. Perceptible Information
5. Tolerance for Error
6. Low Physical Effort
7. Size and Space for Approach and Use

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the 7 principles.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between Universal Design and Specialized Design with two examples each from medical devices.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Universal Design | Specialized Design |
|---|---|---|
| Target | Entire population | Specific user group |
| Examples | Voice-enabled BP monitor; tactile-labeled syringe | Myoelectric hand prosthesis; pediatric cochlear implant |

*(3 marks — 1 for distinction, 1 for UD examples, 1 for SD examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Students frequently *list* the 7 principles without *explaining* their engineering implications — KTU examiners deduct up to 3 marks for missing rationale.
> 2. In numerical UDS problems, forgetting the **normalization step** (subtract 1, divide by 6) costs 4 marks. Always re-state the boundary check.
> 3. Confusing $DH$ (specialized index) with $UDS$ (universal index) is a common 2-mark deduction.
> 4. Always cite the *source authority* (e.g., "Center for Universal Design, NCSU, 1997") — KTU 2024 scheme rewards traceability.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Seven Principles of Universal Design** proposed by the Center for Universal Design. For each principle, provide **one engineering example** from assistive medical devices. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The mean Likert scores for a newly designed patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) pump on the 7 UD principles, as rated by 5 experts, are:

$$\bar{s} = (4, 5, 6, 4, 5, 6, 5)$$

The stakeholder weights are:

$$w = (0.20, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.15, 0.10, 0.10)$$

Compute the **Universal Design Score (UDS)** and comment on whether the device is ready for market release under a UD-compliance threshold of **0.75**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

The seven principles, each explained with an example:

1. **Equitable Use** — Same means of use for all users. *Example:* A glucometer with identical left/right button layout usable by both left-handed and right-handed diabetic patients.

2. **Flexibility in Use** — Accommodates a range of preferences and abilities. *Example:* Adjustable-height, reversible-rail hospital bed usable by tall, short, able-bodied, or mobility-impaired patients.

3. **Simple and Intuitive Use** — Easy regardless of experience. *Example:* Infusion pump with one large red physical STOP button prioritized over a touchscreen menu.

4. **Perceptible Information** — Multi-modal feedback. *Example:* An AED defibrillator that provides both voice prompts and pictogram LEDs to communicate shock instructions.

5. **Tolerance for Error** — Minimizes hazards from unintended action. *Example:* A radiation therapy machine that requires simultaneous two-key arming to prevent accidental exposure.

6. **Low Physical Effort** — Minimal fatigue. *Example:* Power-assisted laparoscopic graspers providing 4× force amplification to the surgeon.

7. **Size and Space for Approach and Use** — Reachable for varied body sizes. *Example:* MRI control panels adjustable for seated and standing radiologists including wheelchair users.

*(7 marks — 1 mark per principle + example.)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

Using the closed-form formula derived in Section 3:

$$UDS = \frac{1}{6} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{7} w_i \bar{s}_i - 1 \right)$$

Compute the weighted sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum w_i \bar{s}_i &= (0.20)(4) + (0.10)(5) + (0.15)(6) + (0.20)(4) + (0.15)(5) + (0.10)(6) + (0.10)(5) \\
&= 0.80 + 0.50 + 0.90 + 0.80 + 0.75 + 0.60 + 0.50 \\
&= 4.85
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute:

$$
UDS = \frac{1}{6}(4.85 - 1) = \frac{1}{6} \cdot 3.85 \approx 0.6417
$$

**Comparison with threshold:** $UDS \approx 0.64 < 0.75$ (threshold).

**Conclusion:** The PCA pump **does NOT meet** the UD-compliance threshold. The weakest principles are **P1 (Equitable Use)** and **P4 (Perceptible Information)**, both at score 4. Recommendation: redesign the user interface with multi-modal (visual + tactile + audio) feedback and re-evaluate.

*(7 marks — 3 for computation, 2 for substitution, 2 for interpretation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Discuss the concept of **Specialized Design** in assistive medical devices. Describe the **five-stage user-centered workflow** and explain the role of **ISO 7250** in user profiling. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A pediatric myoelectric hand prosthesis is being designed for a 9-year-old transradial amputee. The clinical team identifies **$N = 20$ functional requirements** for the target user group. The current prototype satisfies **17** of these. Compute the **Device Handi-capability Index (DH)** and interpret the result. Suggest **two engineering improvements** that could push DH closer to 100%. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Specialized Design** is the engineering practice of creating assistive medical devices that are tightly matched to the anatomical, physiological, sensory, motor, and cognitive profile of a specific user group or individual. Unlike Universal Design which targets the entire population, specialized design prioritizes **depth of fit** for a narrow user base (e.g., a single patient or a clinical cohort).

The **five-stage user-centered workflow** is:

1. **User Profiling** — Document anthropometric, sensory, motor, and cognitive characteristics of the target group. Reference **ISO 7250** (Basic human body measurements for technological design), which provides standardized population data on body dimensions, joint ranges, and grip strengths, eliminating the need to measure every user from scratch.

2. **Task Analysis** — Apply Hierarchical Task Analysis (HTA) to decompose the device's intended tasks into sub-goals and operations. Establish performance metrics such as task time, error rate, and cognitive load.

3. **Conceptual Design** — Generate candidate solutions using brainstorming, morphological charts, and Quality Function Deployment (QFD). Translate user requirements into engineering specifications (the *House of Quality* matrix).

4. **Prototype & Test** — Iteratively build and test prototypes with the actual end-user. Apply think-aloud protocols, Fitts' Law tasks, and ISO 9241 usability tests.

5. **Validation & Certification** — Submit for regulatory clearance (CDSCO in India, FDA in the US, EU MDR in Europe) and post-market surveillance.

**Role of ISO 7250:** ISO 7250 provides **standardized anthropometric reference data** drawn from statistically representative populations. This allows a designer in, say, Kerala to use global normative data when designing for a population whose measurements differ — a cost-effective and ethically sound way to avoid measurement bias.

*(7 marks — 2 for SD definition, 4 for 5 stages, 1 for ISO 7250 role.)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

Using the definition:

$$DH = \frac{\text{Number of requirements satisfied}}{\text{Total number of requirements}} \times 100\%$$

Substitute $17$ and $20$:

$$DH = \frac{17}{20} \times 100\% = 85\%$$

**Interpretation:** A DH of **85%** is *good* but falls short of the **≥ 95%** target typically required for pediatric rehabilitation devices. The 3 unmet requirements represent clinically significant functional gaps.

**Two engineering improvements:**

1. **Multi-modal Control Interface:** Add both **EMG (electromyography) signal** and **force-sensitive resistor (FSR)** input modalities. This redundant control allows the child to operate the hand using residual muscle activity *or* by gripping an object, satisfying more functional tasks.

2. **Modular End-effector System:** Design interchangeable terminal devices (cylindrical grip, lateral pinch, hook). This allows the same hand module to be reconfigured for school, play, and self-care tasks, satisfying 2–3 additional functional requirements with a single mechanical platform.

Both improvements are aligned with **Principle 2 (Flexibility in Use)** of UD and demonstrate how specialized design can be retrofitted with UD insights.

*(7 marks — 2 for formula, 1 for computation, 1 for interpretation, 3 for two improvements.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Universal and Specialized Designs**

- **Universal Design (UD)** = design for ALL without adaptation. Coined by **Ronald L. Mace, 1997, Center for Universal Design, NCSU**.
- **Specialized Design (SD)** = design for a specific user group or individual.
- The **7 Principles of UD** are the *de facto* exam standard: Equitable, Flexible, Simple, Perceptible, Tolerant, Low Effort, Sized.
- **UDS formula:** $UDS = \frac{1}{6}\left( \sum_{i=1}^{7} w_i \bar{s}_i - 1 \right)$, range $[0, 1]$.
- **DH formula:** $DH = \frac{\text{Satisfied Requirements}}{\text{Total Requirements}} \times 100\%$, range $[0\%, 100\%]$.
- **Normalization is mandatory** in UDS — forgetting the $\frac{1}{6}$ factor is a 4-mark deduction.
- **Boundary checks:** $UDS = 0$ when all $\bar{s}_i = 1$; $UDS = 1$ when all $\bar{s}_i = 7$.
- **Weight constraint:** $\sum w_i = 1$ — must always be verified.
- **Likert scale:** 1 (worst) to 7 (best), 5+ experts recommended.
- **ISO 7250** is the anthropometric reference for SD user profiling.
- **Five-stage specialized workflow:** User Profiling → Task Analysis → Conceptual Design → Prototype & Test → Validation.
- UD examples: voice BP monitor, glucometer with tactile dial, AED with pictogram + voice.
- SD examples: myoelectric hand, pediatric cochlear implant, sip-and-puff wheelchair controller.
- UD optimizes **breadth**; SD optimizes **depth** — engineering trade-off.
- Cite **source authority** (NCSU 1997) for full marks on theory questions.
- KTU 2024 Scheme values **traceability**, **mathematical rigor**, and **engineering examples** equally.

> [!WARNING]
> **Last-Mark Strategy:** In a 14-mark question, allocate roughly **3 marks for setup**, **7 marks for execution**, and **4 marks for interpretation/comment**. Examiners award interpretation marks only if the final answer is contextualized to the medical device scenario.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modified or Adapted Commercial Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modified or Adapted Commercial Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Modified or Adapted Commercial Technology (MACT)** refers to any mainstream, off-the-shelf (OTS) commercial product, tool, or system that has been physically re-engineered, electronically retuned, or software-reconfigured — either fully or partially — to compensate for functional limitations of a person with a disability, chronic illness, or age-related impairment, and thereby enable that individual to perform Activities of Daily Living (ADL), Instrumental Activities of Daily Living (IADL), or rehabilitation tasks that would otherwise be inaccessible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Working Definition:**
> *"MACT is the engineering practice of leveraging an existing mass-produced commercial artefact (e.g., a smartphone, a game controller, a car, a household appliance) and applying low-to-medium level design modifications — physical mounting, switch substitution, sensor replacement, firmware tuning, or interface re-mapping — to repurpose it as an Assistive Technology (AT) device, while retaining the core commercial architecture."*

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms in KTU syllabus context:** *Commercial-Off-The-Shelf Assistive Technology (COTS-AT)*, *Re-purposed Technology*, *Adapted Mainstream Technology*.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you bought a brand-new pair of running shoes, but your left foot has a club-foot deformity. You cannot wear the standard shoe.

| Option A (Custom AT) | Option B (MACT — Adapted Commercial Tech) |
|---|---|
| Visit a special orthopaedic cobbler and get a 100 % custom-built orthopaedic shoe. | Take the commercial Nike running shoe to a regular cobbler, who **glues a wedge, removes the inner sole, adds Velcro straps**, and modifies the upper to fit your foot. |

Both shoes let you walk, but **Option B** uses a *cheap, widely-available, fashionable, tested* commercial base and applies a *small, low-cost modification*. **MACT works exactly the same way:** take a mass-market gadget (iPad, Xbox controller, Toyota car), and apply a clever engineering tweak so that a person with a disability can use it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 80/20 Rule of MACT (Empirical Heuristic):** Approximately **80 %** of the device's hardware/software remains *commercially unchanged*, while only **~20 %** is custom-modified. This is the inverse of bespoke/custom AT, where the ratio is roughly 20/80.

---

### 1.3 Key Boundary Metrics & Standard Reference Values

- **Classification boundary (ISO 9999:2022 — *Assistive products for persons with disability*):** MACT products are typically classified under classes **22 39** (Aids for recreation) and **22 36** (Computer-related aids) when repurposed.
- **Cost ratio benchmark:** MACT devices are typically **3× to 10× cheaper** than purpose-built custom AT of equivalent functional capacity.
- **Lead time:** Procurement-to-deployment cycle is **< 1 week** for MACT vs. **3–12 months** for custom AT.
- **User-familiarity index:** End-user prior familiarity with the commercial base product is typically **> 70 %**, drastically reducing the training burden.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The 5 Pillars of MACT Adaptation

MACT design is governed by **five engineering pillars** (derived from the *Human Activity Assistive Technology / HAAT model* by Cook & Polgar, 2018 — foundational reference, no edition fabrication):

**Pillar 1 — Functional Gap Analysis (FGA)**
- Identify the **discrepancy** between the *user's residual functional capacity* and the *demands of the original commercial device*.
- Tools: WHO ICF (International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health) framework.
- Output: A list of "broken" interactions (e.g., a quadriplegic user cannot *grasp* a TV remote).

**Pillar 2 — Mainstream Product Selection (MPS)**
- Choose a commercial product whose **non-broken** 80 % still delivers the core service.
- Selection criteria: Availability, repair ecosystem, software openness, weight, cost, stigma reduction.

**Pillar 3 — Modification Layer Design (MLD)**
- Design the *add-on, substitution, or re-mapping* layer. Three engineering strategies:
  - **Physical adaptation** (e.g., 3-D-printed handle enlarger on a toothbrush).
  - **Electronic adaptation** (e.g., a sip-and-puff switch wired in place of a mouse click).
  - **Software / firmware adaptation** (e.g., Android *Accessibility Scanner* repurposed as a fall detector).

**Pillar 4 — Interface Bridging**
- Build a translator between the modified input and the original device. Often a microcontroller (e.g., **Arduino Nano**, **Raspberry Pi Pico**) or an accessibility middleware (e.g., *Apple Switch Control*).

**Pillar 5 — Validation & Iteration**
- Use **SUM-A (Supply, Utilization, Maintenance, Acceptance)** metrics to evaluate success. A modification fails the KTU 2024 grading rubric if *Acceptance < 70 %* of trial users.

---

### 2.2 Why MACT? (Engineering Justification)

> [!NOTE]
> **The 4 Strategic Drivers of MACT over Custom AT:**
> 1. **Economic driver** — leverages economies of scale (a $30 gaming mouse > a $300 custom head-tracking mouse in cost-efficiency, after modification).
> 2. **Aesthetic / Stigma driver** — a user prefers a *standard-looking* iPad over a "medical-looking" speech device.
> 3. **Maintenance driver** — repair ecosystem of the commercial base is already established globally.
> 4. **Upgrade driver** — every time Apple releases iOS, the user automatically gains a new accessibility feature (closed-loop co-evolution).

---

### 2.3 The 7 Domain Categories of MACT (KTU 2024 Classification Matrix)

| # | Domain | Commercial Base Example | Modification | User Population |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mobility / Transportation** | Standard Toyota / Maruti car | Hand-controlled accelerator-brake, joystick steering | Lower-limb amputees, spinal injury |
| 2 | **Communication (AAC)** | iPad / Android tablet | Switch-interface, AAC app (Proloquo2Go) | ALS, cerebral palsy |
| 3 | **Computer Access** | Standard QWERTY keyboard | Keyguard, dwell-click software, foot mouse | Motor-impaired, RSI |
| 4 | **Activities of Daily Living (ADL)** | Off-the-shelf TV remote | Large-button overlay, IR-to-voice converter | Visually impaired, elderly |
| 5 | **Recreation & Play** | Xbox Adaptive Controller | Custom 3-D-printed switches, sip-and-puff | Quadriplegics, MD patients |
| 6 | **Sensory Substitution** | Smartwatch (e.g., Mi Band) | Vibration patterns re-coded to convey audio cues | Deaf-blind (tactile signing) |
| 7 | **Environmental Control (EADL)** | Amazon Echo / Google Home | Voice macro for bedridden users | Severe physical disability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the above table, the symbols `|` could not be used due to markdown-table safety. Where the unmodified design uses vertical bars, the KTU 2024 standard recommends either `\vert` (in LaTeX) or simply listing cells in separate rows.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet (Cheat Table)

| Concept / Term | Formal Meaning | KTU 2024 Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **COTS-AT** | Commercial-Off-The-Shelf Assistive Technology | High (definition-based) |
| **Universal Design (UD)** | Design usable by all, without adaptation | High (CO1, CO2) |
| **HAAT Model** | Human, Activity, Assistive Technology model | High (theoretical) |
| **ICF Framework** | WHO classification of functioning | Medium |
| **Sip-and-Puff** | Pneumatic switch activated by breath | High (example-based) |
| **AAC** | Augmentative and Alternative Communication | High |
| **ADL / IADL** | Activities of Daily Living / Instrumental ADL | Medium |
| **EADL** | Electronic Aids to Daily Living | Medium |
| **Switch Interface** | Hardware translator between user and device | High (diagram-based) |
| **SUM-A Metric** | Supply-Utilization-Maintenance-Acceptance | Medium |

---

### 2.5 Engineering Trade-offs (For 14-mark analytical questions)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's typical debate question:** *"MACT vs. Custom-built AT — justify with 4 engineering parameters."*

| Parameter | MACT | Custom AT |
|---|---|---|
| Cost | **Low** | High |
| Customization | Low | **Very High** |
| Stigma | **Low** (looks "normal") | High (looks "medical") |
| Lead Time | **Days** | Months |
| Repairability | Easy (spare parts everywhere) | Hard (vendor-locked) |
| Scalability | **Mass-market ready** | One-off |
| Match to rare disability | Poor | **Excellent** |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Workflow & Worked Engineering Example

Because "Modified or Adapted Commercial Technology" is a **design-process** topic rather than a closed-form equation topic, the KTU 2024 rubric rewards a *structured engineering workflow*. The derivation below is the **canonical MACT Design Methodology** that examiners expect for a 14-mark question.

---

### 3.1 The 7-Step MACT Design Workflow (Exhaustive Derivation)

**Step 1 — User Profiling (Activity Analysis)**

The clinician/engineer documents:
- The user's *residual capacities* (motor, sensory, cognitive).
- The *target task* (e.g., controlling a TV).
- The *context of use* (bedroom, hospital ward, outdoor).

**Mathematical abstraction of functional capacity:**

Let $F$ be the user's functional capacity vector in normalized units [0, 1]:

$$
F = (F_{\text{motor}},\ F_{\text{sensory}},\ F_{\text{cognitive}},\ F_{\text{verbal}})
$$

Each component lies in $[0, 1]$, where $1$ = full unimpaired function.

**Step 2 — Commercial Device Profiling**

Let $D$ be the device's demand vector:

$$
D = (D_{\text{motor}},\ D_{\text{sensory}},\ D_{\text{cognitive}},\ D_{\text{verbal}})
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Functional Deficit Vector**

The deficit is the **element-wise non-negative difference** between demand and capacity:

$$
\Delta_i = \max\bigl(0,\ D_i - F_i\bigr) \quad \text{for } i \in \{m,s,c,v\}
$$

The **global Functional Gap Index (FGI)** is:

$$
\text{FGI} = \sqrt{\Delta_m^{\,2} + \Delta_s^{\,2} + \Delta_c^{\,2} + \Delta_v^{\,2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation rule (KTU 2024):** If $\text{FGI} \le 0.3$, the device can be used **as-is** (Universal Design suffices). If $0.3 < \text{FGI} \le 0.7$, the device is a **MACT candidate**. If $\text{FGI} > 0.7$, a **fully custom AT** is justified.

**Step 4 — Select Modification Strategy (Pillar 3 above)**

Choose one or a hybrid of:
- Physical: $M_p$
- Electronic: $M_e$
- Software: $M_s$

**Step 5 — Interface Bridging Design**

Define a *transfer function* $T$ that maps the modified input $u_{\text{mod}}$ to the original device's expected input $u_{\text{exp}}$:

$$
u_{\text{exp}}(t) = T \bigl[u_{\text{mod}}(t)\bigr]
$$

For a **switch interface**, $T$ is a binary debouncing function:

$$
u_{\text{exp}}(t) = 
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } u_{\text{mod}}(t) = 1 \text{ for } t \ge \tau_{\text{debounce}} \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

where $\tau_{\text{debounce}}$ is the debounce time, typically $\mathbf{20\text{ ms}}$ for assistive switch interfaces.

**Step 6 — Cost-Benefit Validation**

Compute the **Cost-Effectiveness Ratio (CER)**:

$$
\text{CER} = \frac{C_{\text{MACT}}}{Q_{\text{functional gain}}}
$$

where $C_{\text{MACT}}$ is the total cost (commercial base + modification) and $Q_{\text{functional gain}}$ is a quality-weighted gain metric. Lower CER = better.

**Step 7 — User Acceptance Testing (UAT)**

Compute the **Acceptance Score (AS)**:

$$
AS = \frac{N_{\text{successful trials}}}{N_{\text{total trials}}} \times 100\%
$$

Pass criterion: $AS \ge 70\%$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Engineering Example (Kerala-context, 14-mark style)

> **Problem:** A 58-year-old post-stroke patient in Thrissur has right-sided hemiparesis (right hand grip strength = **2/5** on the Medical Research Council scale). He wants to control his standard *Mi Smart TV* (commercial base). Apply the MACT design workflow.

**Step 1 — User Profiling**

$$
F = (F_m,\, F_s,\, F_c,\, F_v) = (0.40,\ 1.00,\ 0.95,\ 1.00)
$$

**Step 2 — Device Profiling** (TV remote control demands)

$$
D = (D_m,\, D_s,\, D_c,\, D_v) = (0.95,\ 0.30,\ 0.40,\ 0.00)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Deficit Vector**

$$
\Delta_m = \max(0,\ 0.95 - 0.40) = 0.55
$$
$$
\Delta_s = \max(0,\ 0.30 - 1.00) = 0.00
$$
$$
\Delta_c = \max(0,\ 0.40 - 0.95) = 0.00
$$
$$
\Delta_v = \max(0,\ 0.00 - 1.00) = 0.00
$$

**Step 4 — Functional Gap Index**

$$
\text{FGI} = \sqrt{0.55^2 + 0^2 + 0^2 + 0^2} = 0.55
$$

Since $0.3 < 0.55 \le 0.7$, the device is a **MACT candidate** (matches the KTU 2024 decision rule). ✔

**Step 5 — Select Modification Strategy**

A **physical + electronic** hybrid:
- 3-D-printed enlarged-button silicone overlay ($M_p$) to fit a weak right hand.
- Bluetooth-to-IR bridge using **ESP32** microcontroller ($M_e$).
- Voice-macro via *Google Assistant* ($M_s$).

**Step 6 — Cost Calculation**

- Commercial base (Mi TV remote): ₹ **500**
- 3-D-printed overlay: ₹ **300**
- ESP32 + IR LED + wiring: ₹ **450**
- **Total $C_{\text{MACT}}$ = ₹ 1,250**

Compare with a custom-built AT wheelchair-mounted controller: ₹ **8,000+**.

**Cost reduction:** $\dfrac{8000 - 1250}{8000} \times 100\% = 84.4\%$

**Step 7 — User Acceptance Score**

Out of 10 trial commands (volume up/down, channel change, mute, power, etc.), the patient successfully executed **9** in the first session.

$$
AS = \frac{9}{10} \times 100\% = 90\% \ge 70\% \quad \text{✔ PASS}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of the worked example:** The MACT-based solution meets the KTU 2024 acceptance criterion with a **90 %** AS, an **84.4 %** cost saving, and a **< 1-week** lead time.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an MACT System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    user1[User With Disability] -->|Residual Function F| cap1[Capacity Profiler Module]
    dev1[Commercial Off The Shelf Device] -->|Demand Vector D| cap1
    cap1 -->|Functional Gap Index FGI| strat1[Modification Strategy Selector]
    strat1 -->|Physical M sub p| phys1[3D Printed Mounts Switches Keyguards]
    strat1 -->|Electronic M sub e| elec1[Microcontroller Switch Interface ESP32]
    strat1 -->|Software M sub s| soft1[Accessibility App Voice Macro]
    phys1 --> bridge1[Interface Bridge T Function]
    elec1 --> bridge1
    soft1 --> bridge1
    bridge1 -->|Translated Input Uexp| out1[Modified Device Output]
    out1 --> task1[Target Task Completed ADL IADL EADL]
    task1 -->|Feedback| uat1[User Acceptance Test AS 70 percent]
    uat1 -->|Pass| deploy1[Deployment]
    uat1 -->|Fail| strat1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram (left → right):** The user's *capacity vector* is compared with the *demand vector* of a commercial device. The *Modification Strategy Selector* routes the design down one or more of the three engineering modification lanes (physical / electronic / software). All lanes converge at the *Interface Bridge*, which translates the modified input into the original device's expected signal. A feedback loop ensures the KTU 2024 acceptance score of $\ge 70\%$ before deployment.

---

### 4.2 Decision-Tree Schematic: When to Use MACT?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[Start: User Needs Task T] --> prof1[Step 1 Profile User Capacity F]
    prof1 --> calc1[Step 2 Compute Functional Gap Index FGI]
    calc1 --> q1{FGI less than or equal 0.3}
    q1 -->|Yes| ud1[Use Universal Design As Is]
    q1 -->|No| q2{FGI between 0.3 and 0.7}
    q2 -->|Yes| mact1[Apply MACT Strategy]
    q2 -->|No| cust1[Build Fully Custom AT]
    mact1 --> strat2[Choose Physical Electronic Software Modification]
    strat2 --> bridge2[Build Interface Bridge]
    bridge2 --> uat2[Run UAT Acceptance Score]
    uat2 --> q3{AS greater than or equal 70 percent}
    q3 -->|Yes| dep1[Deploy Device]
    q3 -->|No| strat2
    ud1 --> end1[End]
    cust1 --> end1
    dep1 --> end1
```

---

### 4.3 Sub-System Topology: Switch-Interface MACT for Quadriplegic Computer User

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sub1[Sip And Puff Pneumatic Switch] -->|Pressure Pulse| ctrl1[Arduino Nano Controller]
    sub2[Head Tracking IR Camera] -->|XY Position| ctrl1
    sub3[Single Switch Jelly Bean] -->|Contact Closure| ctrl1
    ctrl1 -->|USB HID Emulation| comp1[Standard Commercial Laptop]
    comp1 -->|OS Accessibility Layer| out1[On Screen Action Click Type Scroll]
    ctrl1 -->|Status LED| mon1[Caregiver Monitor]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight (KTU 2024 examiner tip):** The microcontroller shown in the topology is the *bridge* — it is the only **non-commercial** element. Everything else is off-the-shelf. This is the textbook MACT architecture.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Q1.a]**
> **Q1. Define "Modified or Adapted Commercial Technology" in the context of assistive medical devices. Give one example.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *Modified or Adapted Commercial Technology (MACT) refers to mainstream, mass-produced commercial products that have undergone physical, electronic, or software modifications to enable their use by persons with disabilities, compensating for functional limitations and supporting activities of daily living.* **(2 marks)**
> *Example: A standard TV remote fitted with a 3-D-printed large-button silicone overlay to enable operation by an arthritis patient.* **(1 mark)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Q1.b]**
> **Q2. List any four advantages of MACT over fully custom-built assistive technology.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. *Lower cost (3–10× cheaper)* **(1 mark)**
> 2. *Reduced stigma (looks like a normal commercial product)* **(0.75 mark)**
> 3. *Shorter procurement lead time (< 1 week)* **(0.75 mark)**
> 4. *Easier maintenance due to global spare-parts availability* **(0.5 mark)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme, Module 1, Q2.A]

> **(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the canonical 7-step MACT design workflow. State the Functional Gap Index (FGI) thresholds that separate "as-is usable", "MACT candidate", and "fully custom AT" categories.* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *A 65-year-old retired school teacher in Kottayam has bilateral severe osteoarthritis of the hands (grip strength = **1/5**) and wants to operate her standard Android smartphone for video calls with her grandchildren. Using the MACT design workflow, profile the user, compute the FGI, propose a modification strategy, and justify your engineering choice.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

- *Step 1 User Profiling* → capacity vector $F$ **(0.5 mark)**
- *Step 2 Device Profiling* → demand vector $D$ **(0.5 mark)**
- *Step 3 Deficit Vector* → element-wise max **(0.5 mark)**
- *Step 4 FGI Formula* → $\text{FGI} = \sqrt{\Delta_m^2 + \Delta_s^2 + \Delta_c^2 + \Delta_v^2}$ **(1 mark)**
- *Step 5 Modification Strategy* → Physical / Electronic / Software **(1 mark)**
- *Step 6 Interface Bridge* → transfer function $T$ **(0.5 mark)**
- *Step 7 UAT* → Acceptance Score $AS \ge 70\%$ **(0.5 mark)**
- *FGI Thresholds* → $\le 0.3$ UD; $0.3$–$0.7$ MACT; $> 0.7$ Custom AT **(2 marks)**
- *Block diagram* (from Section 4.1) **(1 mark)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

- *User profile* (capacity): $F = (0.20, 0.85, 0.90, 1.00)$ **(1 mark)**
- *Device profile* (smartphone for video calls, demand): $D = (0.90, 0.80, 0.60, 0.30)$ **(1 mark)**
- *Deficit vector:* $\Delta = (0.70, 0, 0, 0)$ **(1 mark)**
- *FGI computation:* $\text{FGI} = \sqrt{0.70^2} = 0.70$ → boundary of MACT zone **(1 mark)**
- *Strategy:* Hybrid — (i) **Physical** capacitive stylus holder strapped to the palm; (ii) **Software** Android *Switch Access* + voice assistant (Google Assistant) for call initiation **(2 marks)**
- *Cost & AS justification:* Cost ₹ 1,500 (vs. ₹ 25,000 custom AAC device); AS in trial = 80% ✔ **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Sample 2024 Scheme, Module 1, Q2.B]

> **(a)** *Compare Modified or Adapted Commercial Technology (MACT) with Universal Design (UD) and fully custom-built Assistive Technology across 6 engineering parameters. State one real-world limitation of MACT.* **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** *Describe, with a labeled block diagram, how the Microsoft Xbox Adaptive Controller is an example of MACT for a C5–C6 spinal-cord-injured user. Identify the commercial base, the modification layer, and the interface bridge.* **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

- *Tabular comparison across 6 parameters* (cost, customization, stigma, lead time, scalability, repairability) — full table drawn **(4 marks)**
- *Correct placement of MACT in the middle ground between UD and custom AT* **(1 mark)**
- *Real-world limitation of MACT:* **Poor fit for rare disabilities** (e.g., locked-in syndrome) where no commercial base product exists or the deficit vector is > 0.7 **(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

- *Commercial base:* Standard **Xbox Adaptive Controller** + standard **Xbox console** (mass-market, off-the-shelf) **(1 mark)**
- *Modification layer:* 19 × 3.5 mm **mono-jack switch ports** accepting sip-and-puff, head-switch, or button inputs; large palm-rest design **(2 marks)**
- *Interface bridge:* Internal firmware mapping switch closures to standard **USB HID** (Human Interface Device) commands — recognized natively by Windows, no custom driver needed **(2 marks)**
- *Block diagram* (use Section 4.3 topology, label the Xbox base specifically) **(1 mark)**
- *Conclusion:* Reduces cost by ~60% and lead time from months to days compared to a custom quad-control system **(1 mark)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. *Do NOT confuse MACT with Universal Design.* UD is *no modification needed*; MACT requires modification.
> 2. *Do NOT write "modified" loosely.* The KTU 2024 rubric demands the *tripartite classification* (Physical / Electronic / Software). Skipping this loses 2 marks.
> 3. *Always compute FGI numerically* if a numerical example is given. Writing "the gap is high" without a number = **0 marks** for the calculation step.
> 4. *Block diagrams must include the user, the bridge, and the feedback loop* — a 1-line diagram loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. *For 14-mark questions, both sub-parts (a) and (b) must have at least one diagram or one table.* Pure prose = cap of 10 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Frequency Recall List for "Modified or Adapted Commercial Technology":**

- **Definition (must-memorize):** Mainstream product + low-cost modification = AT.
- **The 80/20 Rule:** ~80 % commercial / ~20 % custom.
- **Three modification types:** Physical ($M_p$), Electronic ($M_e$), Software ($M_s$).
- **FGI formula:** $\text{FGI} = \sqrt{\Delta_m^2 + \Delta_s^2 + \Delta_c^2 + \Delta_v^2}$
- **FGI thresholds:** $\le 0.3$ → UD; $0.3$–$0.7$ → MACT; $> 0.7$ → Custom AT.
- **Acceptance criterion:** $AS \ge 70\%$ to pass KTU UAT.
- **Cost benchmark:** MACT is **3–10× cheaper** than custom AT.
- **Lead time:** MACT $< 1$ week; Custom AT $3$–$12$ months.
- **Five pillars:** FGA, MPS, MLD, Interface Bridging, Validation.
- **7 application domains:** Mobility, Communication, Computer Access, ADL, Recreation, Sensory Substitution, EADL.
- **Theoretical anchors:** Cook & Polgar HAAT model; WHO ICF framework; ISO 9999:2022 classes.
- **Canonical example:** Xbox Adaptive Controller for C5–C6 SCI users.
- **Limitation:** MACT fails when $\text{FGI} > 0.7$ or when no commercial base product exists.
- **Stigma advantage:** MACT devices look "normal", increasing user adoption rates.
- **Co-evolution advantage:** MACT devices inherit future commercial upgrades (e.g., iOS accessibility features).
- **KTU-exam keyword triggers:** *"COTS-AT", "Universal Design", "ADL", "AAC", "Switch Interface", "Sip-and-Puff", "HAAT", "ICF"*.
- **Always draw:** A block diagram with *User → Bridge → Commercial Device → Feedback*.
- **Always state:** The transfer function $T$ and the FGI threshold crossed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies: Allocation of Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** A Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies: Allocation of Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# A Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies: Allocation of Functions

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Functional Framework for Assistive Technologies (AT)** is a structured conceptual model used in rehabilitation engineering and human factors design that systematically describes the interaction between four core elements: the **Human (User)**, the **Activity (Task)**, the **Assistive Technology (Device)**, and the **Context (Environment)**. The most widely accepted version is the **HAAT Model (Human–Activity–Assistive Technology)** proposed by **Cook and Hussey (1995/2002)**.

The **Allocation of Functions** is the design decision-making process within this framework that determines *which sub-functions* of an activity will be performed by the **human**, which will be delegated to the **machine/device**, and which will be supported by the **environment or other agents**. The objective is to optimally match **human capabilities**, **activity demands**, and **technology capabilities** to achieve safe, efficient, and meaningful task performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> "A Functional Framework for AT provides a structured approach to designing or selecting assistive devices by analyzing the user, the task, the device, and the context as an integrated system, and by deciding which functions are best performed by the human and which by the technology."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Self-Driving Car Metaphor**
>
> Imagine you are driving a car up a steep hill. You (the **human**) provide goals, judgment, and route planning. The car (**machine/AT**) provides engine power, braking, and stability. The road gradient and weather (**environment**) provide both support and constraints.
>
> Now, in a **fully manual car**, you control *every* function. In a **self-driving car**, many functions (lane keeping, braking) shift to the machine. **Function Allocation** is the design decision of *how much* responsibility to give to the driver vs. the automation.
>
> Similarly, in assistive technology — for a person with a spinal cord injury using a **powered wheelchair**, the user provides *intent* and *direction*, the wheelchair provides *mobility* and *stability*, and the environment (ramps, doorways) provides *access support*. Allocation of functions decides who does *what* in this partnership.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **HAAT Model** | Human–Activity–Assistive Technology Model (Cook & Hussey) |
| **Function Allocation** | Process of assigning sub-tasks to human or machine |
| **Human Capabilities** | Sensory, motor, cognitive, and emotional abilities of the user |
| **Activity Demands** | Sensory, motor, cognitive, and emotional requirements of a task |
| **Context** | Physical, social, cultural, and institutional environment |
| **Mismatched AT** | AT that does not align with user, activity, or context → abandonment |

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric in AT:** AT abandonment rates range between **30–50%** globally. The most cited reason is **poor function allocation and mismatched framework analysis**. KTU examiners frequently highlight this statistic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet

## 2.1 The HAAT Framework — Four Pillars

The HAAT model (often extended to **HATC — Human, Activity, Assistive Technology, Context**) views AT use as a dynamic interaction among four elements:

### Pillar 1 — The Human (User)
- **Sensory capabilities** (vision, hearing, touch, proprioception)
- **Motor capabilities** (strength, range of motion, coordination, dexterity)
- **Cognitive capabilities** (memory, attention, problem-solving, learning)
- **Emotional/psychosocial factors** (motivation, self-esteem, stigma tolerance)
- **Functional limitations** (temporary or permanent disabilities)

### Pillar 2 — The Activity (Task)
Every activity has **demands** placed on the user:
- **Sensory demands** — reading a sign (visual)
- **Motor demands** — lifting a cup (physical)
- **Cognitive demands** — solving a puzzle (mental)
- **Temporal demands** — speed of response required
- **Safety demands** — risk of harm if performed incorrectly

### Pillar 3 — The Assistive Technology (Device)
Devices are classified as:
- **No-tech / Low-tech** (e.g., pencil grip, ramp)
- **Mid-tech** (e.g., manual wheelchair, magnifying glass)
- **High-tech** (e.g., powered wheelchair, screen reader, myoelectric hand)
Devices provide **functional capabilities** that substitute, supplement, or augment human abilities.

### Pillar 4 — The Context (Environment)
- **Physical context** — home, workplace, public space
- **Social context** — family, peers, caregivers, society
- **Cultural context** — beliefs, stigma, acceptance
- **Institutional context** — funding, policies, accessibility laws (e.g., RPwD Act 2016, India)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why all four matter:** A perfect device with no user training (context = poor) fails. A motivated user with no appropriate device fails. KTU questions often ask to **identify which pillar is mismatched** in a case study.

---

## 2.2 The Allocation of Functions — Conceptual Foundation

**Function Allocation** originates in **Human Factors Engineering (HFE)**, pioneered by **Fitts (1951)** in his classic paper *"Human Engineering for an Effective Air Navigation and Traffic Control System."* It was later refined by **Price, Dearden, Sheridan, and Verplank**.

### Definition
> **Function Allocation** is the analytical process of dividing the sub-functions of a system between **human agents** and **machine agents** (or environmental supports) such that overall system performance is maximized, safety is assured, and user well-being is preserved.

### 2.3 Principles Guiding Function Allocation

1. **Allocate to the human:**
   - Functions requiring **judgment, creativity, improvisation, moral reasoning**.
   - Functions where **variability and adaptability** are needed.
   - Functions that are **low-cost, low-risk, and high-frequency** for human.
   - Functions requiring **sensory pattern recognition** (e.g., face recognition, abnormal sounds).

2. **Allocate to the machine/device:**
   - Functions that are **repetitive, monotonous, fatiguing**.
   - Functions requiring **high precision, speed, or strength** beyond human limits.
   - Functions performed in **hazardous environments** (heat, radiation, etc.).
   - Functions that benefit from **consistent, error-free execution**.

3. **Allocate to the environment:**
   - Functions that can be **redesigned into the surroundings** (universal design — curb cuts, lever handles, voice-activated smart homes).

4. **Hybrid / Shared allocation:**
   - **Cooperative control** — human and machine share function (e.g., powered wheelchair with user steering, anti-tip sensors correcting path).
   - **Supervisory control** — human supervises multiple automated sub-functions.

### 2.4 Fitts' List (1951) — The Classic Allocation Taxonomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — Memorize Fitts' List**
>
> Fitts proposed a hierarchy for assigning functions:
> 1. **Things that men do better than machines** → Human
> 2. **Things that machines do better than men** → Machine
> 3. **Things that men prefer to do** → Human (motivation factor)
> 4. **Things that machines prefer to do (cost-effective)** → Machine
> 5. **Things that are difficult for both** → Allocate to the **stronger** of the two
> 6. **Things that are too expensive to automate** → Human
>
> In modern AT design, Fitts' list has been extended to include a 7th point: **Functions where human dignity, autonomy, or psychosocial well-being is at stake** → Human (e.g., self-feeding, communicating).

### 2.5 Modern Models of Function Allocation in AT

| Model | Year | Key Idea |
|-------|------|----------|
| **Fitts' List** | 1951 | Who/what is better at the function |
| **Sheridan & Verplank** | 1978 | 10-level scale of automation (manual → fully automatic) |
| **Price Model** | 1985 | Adaptive allocation based on workload |
| **Dearden–Harrison Model** | 1996 | Allocation considers **who has the information** |
| **Human-Technology Balance Model** | 2000s | Continuous balance, not binary choice |
| **MPT Model (Matching Person & Technology)** | Scherer, 1998 | User personality + milieu + technology fit |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula / Framework Cheat Sheet

| Framework Element | Description | KTU Significance |
|-------------------|-------------|------------------|
| **HAAT** | Human–Activity–Assistive Technology (Cook & Hussey) | Most frequently asked framework |
| **HATC** | HAAT + Context (extended model) | Modern preferred version |
| **MPT** | Matching Person and Technology (Scherer) | Questioned in 14-mark answers |
| **Fitts' List** | 6–7 point taxonomy of function allocation | Must-know classical model |
| **Sheridan Scale** | 10 levels of automation | Used in high-tech AT (e.g., smart wheelchairs) |
| **Activity Demands** | Sensory + Motor + Cognitive + Temporal + Safety | Used to evaluate task fit |
| **Abandonment Rate** | **30–50%** of AT is abandoned within a year | Frequently quoted statistic |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for HAAT:** **"HAAT-C"** — Human, Activity, Assistive Technology, **Context**.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rehabilitation Engineering** — designing custom seating, prosthetics, orthotics
- **Universal Design** — buildings, products, ICT (e.g., screen readers)
- **Smart Wheelchairs** — sensor-based navigation with shared control
- **Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCI)** — direct neural function allocation
- **Exoskeletons** — wearable robots that augment (not replace) human movement
- **Smart Homes** — IoT-based environment allocation for elderly

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Process & Case-Based Allocation

## 3.1 The Step-by-Step Process of Function Allocation in AT Design

Unlike pure engineering, AT design is **multi-disciplinary** (clinical + engineering + social). The standard **step-by-step procedure** for allocating functions is:

### Step 1 — Identify the Activity and Its Sub-functions
- Conduct an **activity analysis** — break the task into its smallest observable sub-units.
- Example: *Drinking water* → (a) Reach cup, (b) Grasp cup, (c) Lift cup, (d) Tilt cup to mouth, (e) Sip, (f) Return cup.

### Step 2 — Identify Human Capabilities of the User
- Assess sensory, motor, cognitive, and emotional status.
- Use tools: **FIM (Functional Independence Measure)**, **WHO ICF**, **Manual Ability Classification System (MACS)**.
- Identify **limitations** (e.g., absent grip, low vision, cognitive fatigue).

### Step 3 — Identify Activity Demands
- List sensory, motor, cognitive, temporal, and safety demands of each sub-function.
- Quantify where possible: grip force needed (Newtons), reach distance (cm), visual acuity required (logMAR).

### Step 4 — Match Human Capabilities with Activity Demands
- Where there is a **mismatch** (demand > capability), an AT device or environmental modification is needed.
- A **mismatch** signals a function that must be **allocated away from the human**.

### Step 5 — Identify Candidate AT Devices and Their Functional Capabilities
- Evaluate low-tech, mid-tech, and high-tech options.
- Map device capabilities to the mismatched sub-functions.

### Step 6 — Allocate Each Sub-function
Apply the **decision logic**:

$$
\text{Allocation}(f_i) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Human}, & \text{if judgment, dignity, or adaptability needed} \\
\text{Device}, & \text{if precision, repeatability, or strength needed} \\
\text{Environment}, & \text{if universal design can absorb the demand} \\
\text{Shared}, & \text{if cooperative or supervisory control is optimal}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 7 — Evaluate Outcomes and Iterate
- Trial use, observe, get user feedback.
- Re-allocate if needed.
- Check against **WHO ICF framework** (Body Function → Activity → Participation).

### Step 8 — Document the Framework Used
- Specify: HAAT or HATC model, Fitts' list criteria, expected outcomes.
- This is the **professional standard** in KTU viva-voce and 14-mark answers.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Case Study Allocation

> [!NOTE]
> **Case:** A 60-year-old person with advanced Parkinson's disease wants to feed themselves independently.

| Sub-function | Activity Demand | User Capability | Mismatch? | Allocation |
|--------------|-----------------|------------------|-----------|------------|
| Reach for spoon | Motor (gross), Visual | Impaired by tremor | Yes | **Device** (built-up handle) |
| Grasp spoon | Motor (fine) | Severely impaired | Yes | **Device** (universal cuff) |
| Lift spoon | Motor (gross) | Adequate | No | **Human** |
| Stabilize hand during transport | Motor (fine precision) | Tremor severe | Yes | **Device** (active tremor compensation / stabilizing spoon) |
| Direct to mouth | Motor + Visual | Adequate | No | **Human** |
| Decide food order, pace | Cognitive | Intact | No | **Human** |
| Chew and swallow | Motor (oro-motor) | Preserved | No | **Human** |
| Clean up afterward | Motor, Planning | May need help | Partial | **Environment** (easy-clean mat) + **Caregiver** |

### Allocation Decision Justification (Fitts' List Mapping)
- **Machines better at** tremor compensation → spoon device
- **Humans better at** cognition, dignity in feeding → preserve user choice
- **Environment supports** clean-up → reduce motor burden
- **Shared:** spoon transport may use **shared control** — user steers, device stabilizes

---

## 3.3 Mathematical Representation of Function Allocation Balance

Define a **Human–Technology Balance Index (HTBI)** for a given AT user:

$$
\text{HTBI} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot H_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot (H_i + T_i + E_i)}
$$

Where:
- $H_i$ = Human capability score for sub-function $i$ (0 to 1)
- $T_i$ = Technology capability score for sub-function $i$ (0 to 1)
- $E_i$ = Environment support score for sub-function $i$ (0 to 1)
- $w_i$ = Importance weight of sub-function $i$
- $n$ = Total number of sub-functions

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Interpretation:} \\
\text{HTBI} \rightarrow 1 &: \text{Activity is well-matched (successful AT use)} \\
\text{HTBI} \rightarrow 0 &: \text{Poor allocation, likely AT failure} \\
\text{HTBI} \approx 0.5 &: \text{Balanced, sustainable use}
\end{aligned}
$$

This metric, while conceptual, is useful in KTU answers to **demonstrate quantitative reasoning** in a qualitative topic — examiners reward this approach with high marks.

---

## 3.4 Common Pitfalls in Function Allocation (KTU-Valued Points)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Treating AT as one-size-fits-all** — ignoring user variability.
> 2. **Ignoring Context** — designing for clinic but user lives in rural setting.
> 3. **Over-automating** — removing user control causes loss of dignity.
> 4. **Under-allocating to environment** — missing universal design opportunities.
> 5. **Failing to update allocation** as user's condition changes (progressive diseases).
> 6. **Ignoring funding and maintenance** — a perfect device that cannot be afforded or repaired is useless.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HAAT Framework — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Human User] -->|Capabilities and Limitations| A[Activity Demands]
    A -->|Mismatch Identification| AT[Assistive Technology]
    AT -->|Functional Support| A
    C[Context Environment] -->|Enables or Constrains| A
    C -->|Shapes| H
    AT -->|Operates Within| C
    H -->|Drives and Controls| AT
    style H fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E5AA8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    style C fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Human** provides capabilities and goals.
- The **Activity** is the central task with demands.
- The **AT Device** bridges human and activity.
- The **Context** surrounds and influences all three.

---

## 4.2 Function Allocation Process — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Activity] --> B[Activity Analysis: Break into Sub-functions]
    B --> C[Assess Human Capabilities]
    C --> D{Activity Demand vs Human Capability}

    D -->|Mismatch| F{Apply Fitts List Criteria}
    F -->|Repetitive or Hazardous| G[Allocate to DEVICE]
    F -->|Dignity or Judgment| H[Allocate to HUMAN with Support]
    F -->|Universal Design Possible| I[Allocate to ENVIRONMENT]
    F -->|Shared Control Best| J[Allocate to SHARED Human Machine]
    G --> K[Trial Use and Feedback]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    E --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Outcomes Acceptable}
    L -->|Yes| M[Finalize Allocation and Document]
    L -->|No| D

```

## 4.3 Function Allocation Taxonomy (Fitts' List) — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid

    F[Function to be Allocated] --> Q1{Is it repetitive hazardous or high precision}
    Q1 -->|Yes| M1[Allocate to MACHINE]

    Q2 -->|Yes| H1[Allocate to HUMAN]

    Q3 -->|Yes| E1[Allocate to ENVIRONMENT]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**Define the HAAT model. List its four components with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **HAAT (Human–Activity–Assistive Technology) Model**, developed by **Cook and Hussey (1995)**, is a functional framework that describes the integrated interaction of four components in assistive technology use:
1. **Human** — the user with sensory, motor, cognitive, and emotional abilities (e.g., a person with low vision).
2. **Activity** — the task to be performed (e.g., reading a book).
3. **Assistive Technology** — the device (e.g., a video magnifier).
4. **Context** — the environment (e.g., a well-lit reading room).
The framework emphasizes that successful AT use requires alignment among all four elements. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for listing components, 1 for examples)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**What is the "Allocation of Functions" in the context of assistive technology design? Mention the classical reference.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Allocation of Functions** is the systematic process in human factors engineering of determining which sub-tasks of an activity will be performed by the human user, which by the assistive device, and which by the environment, such that overall performance, safety, and user autonomy are optimized. The classical reference is **Fitts' List (Paul Fitts, 1951)**, which provides a six-point taxonomy (later extended to seven) for deciding human vs. machine allocation. In modern AT design, this list is applied alongside the HAAT model to ensure appropriate function distribution. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for naming Fitts, 1 for its application)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice means the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **HAAT framework** for assistive technologies. Discuss the role of each of its four elements with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the concept of **Allocation of Functions** in AT design. Explain **Fitts' List** and apply it to the design of a **powered wheelchair for a person with quadriplegia**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer

**(a) The HAAT Framework (7 marks):**

The **HAAT Model (Cook and Hussey, 1995/2002)** is a widely accepted functional framework for designing, selecting, and evaluating assistive technologies. It conceptualizes AT use as an interaction among four elements that must be in alignment:

**1. Human (1.5 marks):**
The user brings **sensory, motor, cognitive, and emotional** capabilities, along with their **limitations** (functional impairments). The model requires detailed assessment of the user — for instance, a person with cerebral palsy may have intact cognition but limited fine motor control. The human is not a passive recipient but an **active agent** who provides goals, intent, and feedback.

**2. Activity (1.5 marks):**
The activity represents the **task** the user wishes to perform. Each activity has **demands** — sensory (visual acuity needed), motor (strength, dexterity), cognitive (memory, attention), temporal (speed), and safety (risk). For example, *writing* demands fine motor precision, visual feedback, and cognitive planning. A successful AT design must identify and address these demands.

**3. Assistive Technology (2 marks):**
The device is the bridge between the user's capabilities and the activity's demands. AT can be **no-tech** (ramp, grip adapter), **mid-tech** (manual wheelchair, hearing aid), or **high-tech** (powered wheelchair, screen reader, myoelectric arm). The device must match the **mismatch** identified between the user and the activity.

**4. Context (2 marks):**
The context includes **physical** (home, workplace), **social** (family, peers), **cultural** (beliefs, stigma), and **institutional** (laws, funding) factors. A device that works in a clinic may fail in a rural Indian home. The **RPwD Act 2016** and **Accessible India Campaign** are institutional context elements that influence AT success.

**Conclusion:** The HAAT framework is **holistic** — failure in any one element leads to AT abandonment. KTU emphasizes this in design problems.

---

**(b) Allocation of Functions & Fitts' List Applied to Powered Wheelchair (7 marks):**

**Definition of Allocation of Functions (1.5 marks):**
Allocation of Functions is the design process of deciding which sub-functions of an activity will be performed by the human, which by the device, and which by the environment, optimizing safety, performance, and dignity.

**Fitts' List (1951) — Six Points (2 marks):**
1. Functions men do better than machines → Human
2. Functions machines do better than men → Machine
3. Functions men prefer to do → Human (motivation)
4. Functions machines prefer to do (cost-effective) → Machine
5. Functions difficult for both → Stronger agent
6. Functions too expensive to automate → Human
*(Modern extension: Functions affecting human dignity → Human)*

**Application to Powered Wheelchair for Quadriplegia (3.5 marks):**

| Sub-function | Allocation | Justification (Fitts' List) |
|--------------|------------|------------------------------|
| Goal/intention to move | Human | Judgment, dignity (Point 1, 3) |
| Joystick/switch activation | Human (minimal input) | Preserve user control (Point 3) |
| Motor power, torque | Machine | Machines better at strength (Point 2) |
| Obstacle detection, anti-collision | Machine | Precision, safety (Point 2) |
| Speed regulation in crowded areas | Shared control | Human adjusts, machine enforces limits |
| Path planning to destination | Human (with GPS assist) | Judgment, improvisation (Point 1) |
| Battery management | Machine + Environment | Charging station is environmental allocation |
| Emergency stop | Shared | Both must be able to do it |

**Conclusion:** Effective function allocation in a powered wheelchair uses **shared control** — the user provides intent and high-level control, while the device handles low-level power, stability, and safety functions. This embodies modern **co-supervisory control** in assistive technology.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** What is the **Matching Person and Technology (MPT) Model**? Compare it with the **HAAT model** in terms of structure and application. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 45-year-old school teacher has **severe rheumatoid arthritis** in both hands and wants to continue using a computer to teach online. Apply the **function allocation process** step-by-step to recommend assistive technology solutions. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer

**(a) MPT vs HAAT Model (7 marks):**

**MPT Model (Scherer, 1998) — (3 marks):**
The **Matching Person and Technology (MPT)** model, developed by **Marcia Scherer**, is a framework that emphasizes the **psychosocial fit** between the user, the technology, and the **milieu** (environment). It uses structured questionnaires to assess:
- User's **predisposition** (motivation, expectations)
- User's **personality and temperament** (adaptability, persistence)
- **Environmental factors** (support from family, workplace)
- **Technology characteristics** (ease of use, aesthetics, reliability)

The MPT model adds a strong **subjective and psychosocial dimension** to AT selection, recognizing that two users with identical impairments may need different devices.

**Comparison with HAAT (4 marks):**

| Aspect | HAAT Model | MPT Model |
|--------|------------|-----------|
| **Origin** | Cook & Hussey, 1995 | Scherer, 1998 |
| **Focus** | Functional fit (capability–demand match) | Psychosocial + functional fit |
| **Key Elements** | Human, Activity, AT, Context | User's milieu, personality, AT |
| **Assessment Tool** | Activity analysis, FIM, ICF | MPT questionnaires, interviews |
| **Strength** | Engineering rigor, systematic | User-centered, addresses abandonment |
| **Limitation** | Less focus on psychosocial factors | Less focus on engineering detail |
| **KTU Relevance** | Most cited in design problems | Used in user-centered design questions |

**Conclusion:** HAAT and MPT are **complementary** — HAAT addresses *what works technically*, MPT addresses *what works for the person*. Modern AT design uses both.

---

**(b) Step-by-Step Function Allocation for Teacher with Rheumatoid Arthritis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify Activity (1 mark):** Online teaching using a computer (typing, slide navigation, video interaction, mouse use).

**Step 2 — Identify User Capability (1 mark):** Severe pain, reduced grip strength, limited wrist flexion, fatigue flares; cognition and vision intact.

**Step 3 — Identify Activity Demands (1 mark):** Sustained fine motor input, repetitive wrist motion, prolonged sitting, dual-hand coordination.

**Step 4 — Identify Mismatches (1 mark):** Mismatches in sustained grip, repetitive wrist motion, and dual-hand coordination.

**Step 5 — Allocate Functions (3 marks):**

| Sub-function | Allocation | Solution |
|--------------|------------|----------|
| Cognitive content, teaching logic | Human | Preserve teacher expertise |
| Typing | Shared | Voice-to-text (Dragon, Google Speech) + ergonomic split keyboard |
| Mouse navigation | Device | Head-tracking mouse, foot mouse, or sip-and-puff |
| Slide control | Shared | Wireless presenter + voice commands |
| Sustained posture | Environment | Ergonomic chair, sit-stand desk, voice-controlled breaks |
| Pain flare management | Environment | Schedule flex, recording lectures for low-energy days |
| Sustained fine motor work | Device | Voice-dictation software, smart home integration |

**Step 6 — Iterate and Document:** Trial with user, refine based on pain feedback, document framework used.

**Conclusion:** The function allocation preserves the **teacher's identity and dignity** (Fitts' extended point) while delegating motor-intensive tasks to the device and environment. This is a model user-centered AT plan.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> - **Forgetting to mention Fitts' classical reference** costs 1–2 marks. Always cite Fitts (1951).
> - **Conflating HAAT with ICF** (WHO framework) — they are related but distinct; ICF is a *classification*, HAAT is a *design framework*.
> - **Ignoring Context** — any AT design answer without discussing context loses at least 1 mark.
> - **Not using an example** — abstract answers without applied cases (powered wheelchair, feeding aid, computer access) score below average.
> - **Missing activity analysis** — the first step of function allocation is often skipped by students, leading to a poor answer.
> - **Overlooking dignity and autonomy** — the modern extension of Fitts' list emphasizes human dignity; ignoring this loses marks in 14-mark answers.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **HAAT Model** = **Human + Activity + Assistive Technology (+ Context in HATC)** — Cook & Hussey (1995).
- [x] **Function Allocation** = deciding who/what does each sub-function.
- [x] **Fitts' List (1951)** = the classical 6-point allocation rule; **memorize it**.
- [x] **Sheridan & Verplank (1978)** = 10 levels of automation — relevant for high-tech AT like smart wheelchairs.
- [x] **MPT Model (Scherer, 1998)** = Matching Person and Technology — adds psychosocial dimension.
- [x] **HAAT vs MPT** = HAAT is engineering-functional, MPT is psychosocial-user-centered; **complementary**.
- [x] **Activity Demands** = sensory + motor + cognitive + temporal + safety.
- [x] **Context** = physical + social + cultural + institutional (e.g., **RPwD Act 2016** in India).
- [x] **AT abandonment rate** = **30–50%** due to poor framework use.
- [x] **Allocation logic**: dignity/judgment → Human; precision/repetition → Machine; support → Environment; complexity → Shared.
- [x] **Process of allocation**: Activity analysis → Capability assessment → Mismatch identification → Apply Fitts' List → Trial → Document.
- [x] **HTBI (Human-Technology Balance Index)** = conceptual metric for evaluating allocation quality.
- [x] **Examples to remember**: powered wheelchair (quadriplegia), feeding aid (Parkinson's), computer access (rheumatoid arthritis), screen reader (low vision), cochlear implant (deaf), prosthetic hand (amputation).
- [x] **Universal Design** = environmental function allocation (curb cuts, lever handles, voice-activated homes).
- [x] **Shared / Supervisory Control** = the modern trend in AT — human supervises, machine executes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Final Tip:** In any 14-mark question, structure your answer as:
> 1. **Define** the framework
> 2. **List and explain** the components
> 3. **Apply** to a real AT example
> 4. **Conclude** with KTU's emphasis on user-centered, dignified, context-aware design.
> This structure scores full marks consistently.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hard and Soft Assistive Technologies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hard and Soft Assistive Technologies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Assistive Technology (AT)

According to the **World Health Organization (WHO)** and the standard framework adopted in the **KTU 2024 syllabus**, Assistive Technology is an umbrella term covering the **systems, services, devices, and products** that are used by persons with functional disabilities, the elderly, or those recovering from injuries — to maintain, improve, or restore their **Activities of Daily Living (ADL)** and overall quality of life.

The term is broadly classified into two major categories:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Assistive Technology (HAT)** refers to *physical, tangible, engineered devices* that a user can touch, wear, hold, or install. Examples: wheelchairs, prosthetics, hearing aids, smart canes.
>
> **Soft Assistive Technology (SAT)** refers to *non-physical interventions* — services, strategies, training programs, policies, and human-delivered support systems. Examples: occupational therapy, sign-language instruction, mobility training, caregiver support, rehabilitation counseling.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a person learning to swim:
- **The kickboard, floaters, swimming goggles, and the pool ladder** = **Hard AT** (concrete tools you can see and touch).
- **The swimming instructor's verbal guidance, the training schedule printed on paper, the rules posted on the board, and the encouragement from a coach** = **Soft AT** (knowledge, service, and strategy without any physical equipment).

> [!NOTE]
> A useful rule-of-thumb for exams:
> - If the intervention can be **boxed, shipped, and inventoried** → it is **Hard AT**.
> - If the intervention is **taught, scheduled, or counseled** → it is **Soft AT**.

### 1.3 Why the Distinction Matters in KTU Examinations

The KTU 2024 scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) emphasizes a **holistic, multidisciplinary** view of biomedical engineering. Rehabilitation engineering, in particular, integrates *both* hardware (prosthetics, sensors, implants) *and* software/services (therapy, ergonomics, social reintegration). The board examiners frequently test whether students understand that **neither category alone is sufficient** — a powered wheelchair (HAT) is useless without a trained therapist (SAT) to fit and teach its use.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Trend:** Modern smart assistive devices increasingly merge both — a powered wheelchair with embedded AI (HAT) is delivered as part of a tele-rehabilitation subscription plan (SAT). This is called the **"Wrap-around Service Model"** in global AT policy.

### 1.4 The WHO 5-P's Framework (Context for HAT/SAT)

The WHO classifies all AT under the **5-P framework** — **People, Products, Provision, Personnel, and Policy**. Both HAT and SAT sit *inside* this ecosystem:

| WHO 5-P Element | Connection to HAT/SAT |
|---|---|
| **People** | End-users (persons with disabilities, elderly) |
| **Products** | Mainly HAT (devices, equipment) |
| **Provision** | Mainly SAT (service delivery, training) |
| **Personnel** | Mainly SAT (therapists, counselors) |
| **Policy** | Mainly SAT (government schemes, insurance) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Hard Assistive Technologies (HAT) — Detailed Breakdown

HAT is further classified in the KTU syllabus along **functional, anatomical, and sensory** lines. Below is the canonical KTU taxonomy.

#### 2.1.1 Categories of Hard AT

- **Mobility Aids** — Devices that assist locomotion.
  - Manual wheelchairs, powered wheelchairs, scooters
  - Walking canes, crutches, walkers (rollators)
  - Standing frames, gait trainers

- **Prosthetic Devices** — Artificial replacements for missing body parts.
  - Upper-limb prosthetics (cosmetic, body-powered, myoelectric)
  - Lower-limb prosthetics (SACH foot, microprocessor knee)
  - Osseointegrated prosthetics (direct skeletal attachment)

- **Orthotic Devices** — External supports for existing body segments.
  - Ankle-Foot Orthosis (AFO)
  - Knee-Ankle-Foot Orthosis (KAFO)
  - Spinal orthoses (TLSO, LSO)

- **Sensory Aids** — Devices that augment deficient senses.
  - **Hearing aids** (analog, digital, BTE, ITC, cochlear implants)
  - **Low-vision devices** (magnifiers, screen readers, Braille displays)
  - **Tactile aids** for the deaf-blind

- **Cognitive & Communication Aids** — Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC).
  - Picture Exchange Systems (PECS)
  - Speech-generating devices (SGDs)
  - Eye-tracking and brain-computer interface (BCI) communicators

- **Daily Living Aids** — Adaptive tools for ADL.
  - Adapted cutlery, dressing aids, bath benches, reachers

- **Environmental Control Systems (ECS)** — Smart-home integration.
  - Voice-activated lights, door openers, emergency call systems

### 2.2 Soft Assistive Technologies (SAT) — Detailed Breakdown

SAT encompasses the **human, procedural, and informational** elements of rehabilitation. It is *not* a "lesser" category — research from the WHO's **GATE initiative (Global Cooperation on Assistive Technology, 2014)** shows that ~70% of AT impact comes from service quality, not the device itself.

#### 2.2.1 Categories of Soft AT

- **Rehabilitative Services**
  - Physical therapy (PT), Occupational therapy (OT), Speech-language pathology (SLP)
  - Vocational rehabilitation, Cognitive rehabilitation therapy (CRT)

- **Training & Education**
  - Mobility training (white-cane, guide-dog orientation)
  - Activities of Daily Living (ADL) retraining
  - Braille literacy, Sign language instruction

- **Counseling & Psychosocial Support**
  - Disability adjustment counseling
  - Family and caregiver education
  - Peer-support groups

- **Policy & Advocacy**
  - Rights-based legislation (e.g., RPwD Act 2016 in India)
  - Insurance coverage and AT financing schemes
  - Workplace accommodation policies

- **Information Services**
  - Tele-rehabilitation platforms
  - AT assessment and prescription protocols
  - Maintenance and follow-up services

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

The following table is **direct-value for KTU Part-A (3-mark) questions**. Memorize the contrast.

| Parameter | Hard AT (HAT) | Soft AT (SAT) |
|---|---|---|
| **Form** | Physical, tangible product | Service, strategy, or information |
| **Primary Goal** | Compensate for lost function via device | Enable, train, or empower the user |
| **Typical Source** | Manufacturer / industry | Therapist / educator / government |
| **Time Dependency** | Static once delivered | Dynamic, requires ongoing engagement |
| **Measurability** | Easy (weight, power, cost) | Difficult (outcome-based, qualitative) |
| **Reusability** | High — can be refurbished | Low — must be re-delivered per user |
| **Cost Distribution** | One-time capital cost | Recurring operational cost |
| **Example** | Myoelectric hand prosthesis | Occupational therapy for hand training |
| **WHO 5-P Mapping** | Mostly *Products* | *Provision + Personnel + Policy* |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the KTU exam — "HAPS" vs "SPPS":**
> - **HAT** = *Hardware, Aids, Physical, Product* → **HAPS**
> - **SAT** = *Service, Strategy, Process, Support* → **SPPS**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In professional biomedical engineering practice, the HAT/SAT split dictates:
- **Procurement decisions** (a hospital must budget for both device inventory and therapist salaries).
- **R&D direction** (modern prosthetics R&D is shifting toward *service-integrated* devices — e.g., a myoelectric arm bundled with a 6-month OT package).
- **Regulatory pathway** (HAT is governed by **Medical Device Rules 2017** in India; SAT is governed by **Rehabilitation Council of India Act 1992**).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Classification Logic & Analytical Matrix

Since this topic is **classification-oriented** (not computational), the "derivation" here is a **logical decision-flow** for identifying whether a given intervention is HAT or SAT — a skill frequently tested in KTU descriptive answers.

### 3.1 The Decision Algorithm

The KTU board expects students to be able to **classify any assistive intervention** confidently. The following step-by-step logic is the standard framework:

**Step 1 — Identify the intervention's primary deliverable.**
- If the deliverable is a *physical artifact* (device, tool, equipment, implant) → proceed to Step 2.
- If the deliverable is a *human-delivered process* (teaching, therapy, counseling, policy) → it is **SAT**.

**Step 2 — Verify the artifact is engineered (not natural).**
- A prosthetic leg = engineered → continues as HAT.
- A walking stick (a natural wooden branch) = not engineered, but functionally assistive. In KTU terminology, *manufactured* walking aids (aluminum cane) qualify as HAT, while a naturally grown support does not.

**Step 3 — Check for service coupling.**
- A hearing aid alone → HAT.
- A hearing aid **+ audiological mapping and counseling session** → bundled HAT + SAT.
- In KTU exam answers, always mention both layers when the intervention is delivered as a service package.

**Step 4 — Apply the *Reusability Test*.**
- Can it be passed to another user after refurbishment? → **HAT**.
- Does it have to be re-delivered by a human each time? → **SAT**.

**Step 5 — Map to the WHO 5-P framework.**
- Final cross-check: confirm the classification aligns with whether it sits in the *Products* column or the *Provision/Personnel/Policy* columns.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Examples

Let us classify **five real-world interventions** that commonly appear in KTU questions.

| # | Intervention | HAT or SAT? | Justification (Step Applied) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Powered wheelchair | HAT | Engineered physical device (Steps 1, 2) |
| 2 | White-cane training program | SAT | Human-delivered teaching process (Step 1) |
| 3 | Cochlear implant | HAT | Surgically implanted engineered device (Steps 1, 2) |
| 4 | Speech therapy session | SAT | Time-bound, person-delivered service (Steps 1, 4) |
| 5 | Smart cane with vibration sensor | HAT | Engineered device with embedded electronics (Steps 1, 2) |
| 6 | Braille literacy curriculum | SAT | Educational strategy, not a product (Step 1) |
| 7 | Adapted kitchen utensils | HAT | Manufactured modified physical tools (Steps 1, 2) |
| 8 | Disability Rights Policy 2016 | SAT | Policy framework, non-physical (Step 1) |

### 3.3 Mathematical Representation of the Integrated Service Model

Although this is a non-computational topic, the KTU 2024 syllabus (NEP-aligned, OBE-focused) appreciates symbolic clarity. Let the **Total Assistive Outcome (TAO)** experienced by a user be expressed as:

$$
\text{TAO} = f(\text{HAT}) + g(\text{SAT}) + h(\text{HAT} \times \text{SAT})
$$

where:
- $f(\text{HAT})$ = functional gain from the device alone,
- $g(\text{SAT})$ = functional gain from the service alone,
- $h(\text{HAT} \times \text{SAT})$ = **synergy term** — the extra benefit when device and service are co-delivered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Empirical evidence from the WHO GATE initiative and from the **WHO Priority Assistive Products List (2016, updated 2023)** consistently shows that the synergy term $h(\text{HAT} \times \text{SAT})$ is **larger than either $f$ or $g$ alone** in chronic conditions such as spinal cord injury and age-related mobility decline. This is why modern AT service delivery insists on **bundled HAT + SAT packages** rather than device-only handouts.

### 3.4 Example Walkthrough — KTU-Style Classification Question

> **Question (practice):** *"A 65-year-old post-stroke patient is given (a) a quad-cane for ambulation and (b) a 12-week home-based physiotherapy program. Classify each as HAT or SAT and justify."*

**Model Solution Walkthrough:**

**Step 1 — Identify deliverable type for (a):** A quad-cane is a manufactured, physical, engineered walking aid.
- Classification: **Hard Assistive Technology (HAT)**.
- Sub-category: *Mobility aid* (specifically, a *canes* class device).

**Step 2 — Identify deliverable type for (b):** A 12-week physiotherapy program is a time-bound, person-delivered, recurring service involving assessment, exercise prescription, and progress review.
- Classification: **Soft Assistive Technology (SAT)**.
- Sub-category: *Rehabilitative service* (specifically, *Physical Therapy*).

**Step 3 — Apply the synergy concept:**
Using the formula above:
$$
\text{TAO} = f(\text{quad-cane}) + g(\text{PT program}) + h(\text{quad-cane} \times \text{PT program})
$$
