Transducer"] end subgraph Device["ASSISTIVE DEVICE"] D1["Actuator
Hardware"] D2["Power &
Battery Subsystem"] end H1 -->|intent| H2 H2 -->|mechanical / bioelectric action| I1 I1 -->|analog signal| I2 I2 -->|conditioned signal| I3 I3 -->|command| D1 D1 -->|mechanical effect| D2 I3 -->|state feedback| I4 I4 -->|stimulus| H3 H3 -->|neural signal| H4 H4 -->|percept| H1 ``` ## 4.2 Three Modality-Matched Interface Paths ```mermaid flowchart TD User["User with
Disability"] User --> Choice{Intact
Modality?} Choice -->|Vision| Visual["Visual Interface
Display & Eye-Tracker"] Choice -->|Hearing| Auditory["Auditory Interface
Speaker & Mic"] Choice -->|Touch| Tactile["Tactile Interface
Vibrotactile Array"] Choice -->|Multiple| Multi["Multimodal
Redundant Interface"] Visual --> Dev["Assistive
Device Core"] Auditory --> Dev Tactile --> Dev Multi --> Dev Dev --> FB{Sensory
Feedback
Channel} FB -->|Visual| Vfb["LED / Display
feedback"] FB -->|Audio| Afb["Tone / Speech
feedback"] FB -->|Tactile| Tfb["Vibration / Electrical
stimulation"] ``` ## 4.3 Latency Budget Flow (Real-Time Interface Constraint) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Stimulus
Onset t = 0"] --> B["Sensory
Register 0 to 100 ms"] B --> C["Perceptual
Encoding 100 to 200 ms"] C --> D["Cognitive
Decision 200 to 400 ms"] D --> E["Motor
Planning 400 to 600 ms"] E --> F["Motor
Execution 600 to 800 ms"] F --> G["Device
Transduction 800 to 900 ms"] G --> H["Device
Actuation 900 to 1000 ms"] H --> I["Feedback
to User 1000 to 1200 ms"] I -.->|closed loop| A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the latency diagram**: For a *closed-loop* powered wheelchair avoiding an obstacle, the *first* pass through the loop can take up to **1.2 s** for a human-only reaction. Any assistive automation must therefore *pre-empt* the user and act within **≤ 300 ms** of obstacle detection to compensate for the remaining 900 ms. ## 4.4 Sensory-Substitution Mapping Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR Src["Source Modality
e.g. Vision Camera"] Src --> Encoder["Feature
Extractor"] Encoder --> Map["Cross-Modal
Mapping Matrix M"] Map --> Out["Output Modality
e.g. Tongue Array"] Out --> Learn["Brain
Plasticity"] Learn --> Percept["Perceived
Substituted Sense"] Percept --> Cog["Cognitive
Recognition"] ``` --- # 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 1, Q1a]** **Q1. Define the Human-Technology Interface (HTI) in the context of assistive medical devices. List its three primary sub-channels.** **Model Answer:** > The Human-Technology Interface is the boundary region that mediates the bidirectional exchange of information and energy between a human user and an assistive device. Its three primary sub-channels are: > 1. **Sensory channel** (Device $\rightarrow$ Human): delivers feedback via vision, hearing, or touch. > 2. **Motor channel** (Human $\rightarrow$ Device): encodes user intent via switches, joysticks, voice, EMG, EOG, or BCI. > 3. **Cognitive channel** (bidirectional): handles perception, decision-making, and mental-model formation through the user-interface design. > *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the three sub-channels with one-line role each.)* --- > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2, Q1b]** **Q2. State Weber's Law and Stevens' Power Law. Why is Stevens' exponent important when designing a vibrotactile feedback actuator?** **Model Answer:** > - **Weber's Law**: $\Delta I = k \cdot I$ — the *just-noticeable difference* is proportional to the baseline stimulus, with $k$ being the modality-specific Weber fraction. > - **Stevens' Power Law**: $P = k \cdot I^{n}$ — the perceived magnitude $P$ is a power function of the physical stimulus $I$, with exponent $n$. > - **Why it matters for vibrotactile design**: The exponent for 60 Hz vibration is $n \approx 0.95 \approx 1$, meaning a linear voltage ramp in the actuator *almost* produces a linear perceived-intensity ramp. For modalities with $n \neq 1$ (e.g., brightness with $n = 0.33$), the firmware must *pre-warp* the stimulus to achieve perceptual linearity. > *(3 marks: 1 each for the two laws, 1 for the engineering significance.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice) > **[KTU ESE — Module 1, Q2]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the *human-information-processing model* in the context of an assistive device interface. List the stages and the typical duration of each, and show how they constrain the **real-time latency budget** of a powered wheelchair's obstacle-avoidance subsystem. **(b) [7 marks]** Derive Fitts's Law from information-theoretic principles. Compute the **Index of Difficulty (ID)** and **mean movement time (MT)** for a sip-and-puff wheelchair user selecting a screen target of width 60 mm located 240 mm from the current selection cursor. Assume $a = 50$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit. Comment on whether the result falls within an *acceptable usability range*. ### **Model Answer — Question A** **Part (a) — 7 marks** The human-information-processing model treats the user as a sequence of buffers and operators: | Stage | Function | Typical Duration | |---|---|---| | Sensory register | Holds raw stimulus briefly | $\leq$ 250 ms decay | | Perceptual encoding | Pattern recognition | $\leq$ 100 ms | | Working memory / decision | Choice selection | ≈ 70 ms per chunk | | Motor planning | Effector selection | ≈ 200 ms | | Motor execution | Muscle activation | 100–300 ms | **Cumulative human latency** from stimulus onset to first motor output: ≈ 500–700 ms. **Implication for the wheelchair**: - The *automated* obstacle-avoidance subsystem must detect an obstacle, decide, and act **before** the human has even finished reacting. - Therefore, the *device-side* processing budget is **≤ 300 ms** for safe operation at 1 m/s wheelchair speed (stopping distance = $\frac{v^2}{2a} = \frac{1^2}{2 \times 1.5} \approx 0.33$ m, time = $\frac{v}{a} \approx 0.67$ s — well matched to the 1-second total reaction envelope). - This is a **closed-loop control** problem where the controller is the human and the safety overlay is automated. > *(7 marks: 3 for stages & durations, 2 for the latency-budget argument, 2 for the engineering implication.)* **Part (b) — 7 marks** *Derivation* (see Section 3.1 of these notes for full version): - Number of position alternatives: $N = D/W + 1$ - Information content in bits: $ID = \log_{2}(D/W + 1)$ - Linear assumption: $MT = a + b \cdot ID$ - Combined: $MT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(D/W + 1)$ *Numerical computation*: Given $D = 240$ mm, $W = 60$ mm, $a = 50$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit: $$ \begin{aligned} ID &= \log_{2}\!\left(\frac{240}{60} + 1\right) \\ &= \log_{2}(4 + 1) \\ &= \log_{2}(5) \\ &\approx 2.322 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} MT &= 50 + 150 \times 2.322 \\ &= 50 + 348.3 \\ &= 398.3 \text{ ms} \\ &\approx 400 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Index of Performance**: $$ \begin{aligned} IP &= \frac{ID}{MT} = \frac{2.322}{0.3983} \approx 5.83 \text{ bits/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Comment**: The 5.83 bits/s IP falls within the typical 4–6 bits/s range for assistive pointing devices. The target width of 60 mm is *just* adequate; increasing $W$ to 80 mm would lower $ID$ to ≈ 2.00 bits and $MT$ to ≈ 350 ms, improving ease of use for users with neuromuscular fatigue. > *(7 marks: 2 for derivation, 3 for the numerical calculation, 2 for the engineering comment.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a) [7 marks]** With the aid of a block diagram, describe the three principal sub-channels of the Human-Technology Interface in an assistive medical device. For each sub-channel, give **one** engineering example of a transducer and **one** design consideration. **(b) [7 marks]** A functional electrical stimulator (FES) is used for grasp-assist in a quadriplegic user. The skin-electrode impedance is estimated at $R_{skin} = 5$ k$\Omega$ at the operating frequency. Calculate the **maximum safe stimulation current** if the stimulator's compliance voltage is **$V_{stim} = 50$ V** and the IEC 60601-1 auxiliary-patient-current limit is **$I_{limit} = 10$ mA rms**. Discuss what design modification would be needed if the patient were in a *cardiac category CF* applied-part context (limit = 100 $\mu$A rms). ### **Model Answer — Question B** **Part (a) — 7 marks** The three sub-channels are: 1. **Sensory Channel (Device $\rightarrow$ Human)** - *Transducer example*: vibrotactile tactor array (e.g., 3 × 3 pin array on the forearm) for displaying grasp-force feedback. - *Design consideration*: match vibration frequency to the Pacinian-corpuscle peak sensitivity (≈ 250 Hz) and avoid skin adaptation by using intermittent or spatially varying patterns. 2. **Motor Channel (Human $\rightarrow$ Device)** - *Transducer example*: surface EMG electrode pair over the trapezius, capturing shoulder elevation as a "go" command for a sip-and-puff alternative. - *Design consideration*: use a differential amplifier with $\geq 80$ dB CMRR to reject 50 Hz mains interference, and apply a 20–500 Hz band-pass to isolate muscle activity from motion artefact. 3. **Cognitive Channel (Bidirectional)** - *Transducer example*: a graphical user interface with high-contrast icons for a powered-wheelchair mode selector. - *Design consideration*: keep the working-memory load $\leq$ 4 ± 1 items (Miller's range), use consistent colour-coding, and provide redundant audio cues for visually impaired users. > *(7 marks: 2 for diagram description, 3 for one example per channel, 2 for the design-considerations.)* **Part (b) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — Calculate the actual current available from the stimulator**: $$ I_{actual} = \frac{V_{stim}}{R_{skin}} = \frac{50}{5000} = 0.010 \text{ A} = 10 \text{ mA} $$ **Step 2 — Compare against the IEC 60601-1 limit for limb stimulation**: The actual current **exactly equals** the 10 mA rms limit. This is *acceptable* but offers no safety margin; designers typically add a **2× safety factor**, so the firmware should clamp the current at **5 mA rms**. **Step 3 — Cardiac CF category**: If the patient context is CF (cardiac floating, the strictest class), the limit drops to **100 $\mu$A rms**, which is **100× lower** than the current capability. The required design modifications are: - **Reduce compliance voltage** to $V_{stim} = 0.0001 \text{ A} \times 5000 \text{ }\Omega = 0.5 \text{ V}$ (in practice, use a current-mode limiter). - **Use optically isolated, battery-powered, fully floating output stage** with no earth-referenced return path. - **Add redundant hardware and firmware current clamps** (two independent clamps in series) per IEC 60601-1 single-fault-safety requirement. > *(7 marks: 2 for the current calculation, 2 for the safety-factor discussion, 3 for the CF-category redesign.)* --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where KTU students lose marks in HTI questions:** > 1. **Confusing Fitts's ID with Shannon bits**: Fitts's ID is *task information*, not channel capacity — examiners will deduct 1 mark for the misuse. > 2. **Using the wrong Weber fraction**: e.g., quoting $k = 0.08$ (luminance) for a *tactile* display — the tactile fraction is ≈ 0.10. > 3. **Forgetting units**: Stevens' Law has *no units* for $n$ and $k$, but $k$ *does* carry the units of $P/I^n$ — always state. > 4. **Skipping the safety-factor argument** in any calculation involving patient current — KTU expects IEC 60601-1 awareness. > 5. **Drawing the interface as one-way**: the HTI is **bidirectional**; mark deduction for unidirectional diagrams. > 6. **Citing only "Miller's Law" without the value 7 ± 2**: examiners want the numeric bound. ## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist - ✅ **HTI = bidirectional coupling** between human (sensory + motor + cognitive) and assistive device. - ✅ **Three sub-channels**: sensory (output), motor (input), cognitive (bidirectional). - ✅ **Weber's Law**: $\Delta I = kI$ — defines detection threshold; $k$ is modality-specific. - ✅ **Stevens' Power Law**: $P = k I^n$ — defines perceived magnitude; $n$ is modality-specific. - ✅ **Fitts's Law**: $MT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(D/W + 1)$; ID measured in **bits**; IP in **bits/s** (typical human: 4–6). - ✅ **Shannon-Hartley limit**: $C = W \log_{2}(1 + S/N)$ — bounds biosignal-channel throughput. - ✅ **Reaction-time budget** for closed-loop assistive control: **≤ 300 ms** device-side, **≤ 1 s** total. - ✅ **Anthropometric design** uses 5th–95th percentile for population coverage. - ✅ **Sensory substitution** maps one modality to another; bottleneck is *brain training*, not hardware. - ✅ **Electrical safety** follows IEC 60601-1: **100 $\mu$A** (CF cardiac), **500 $\mu$A** (BF body), **10 mA** (B-type limb). - ✅ **Multimodal redundancy** (visual + auditory + tactile) is the *gold standard* for accessible UI. - ✅ **Universal Design principles** (Equitable Use, Flexibility, Simple/Intuitive, Perceptible Info, Tolerance for Error, Low Effort, Size/Space, Approach/Use, Perceptible Info) are implicit in the KTU 2024 syllabus. - ✅ **Cognitive load** must remain $\leq$ 7 ± 2 chunks (Miller's classical bound) for UI design. ---

topics.title: Characteristics
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristics

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Assistive Medical Devices (AMDs)**, the term **"Characteristics"** refers to the complete set of quantifiable and qualitative attributes, parameters, and performance metrics that define how a medical device behaves, interacts with the human body, processes physiological signals, and delivers therapeutic or diagnostic outcomes. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for OEEBT722, characteristics encompass the engineering specifications, clinical performance indicators, safety parameters, and ergonomic factors that collectively determine a device's suitability for medical use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:**
> Characteristics of an assistive medical device are the measurable physical, electrical, mechanical, biological, and functional properties that determine its efficacy, safety, reliability, and clinical acceptability when applied to a patient or user with a disability or medical condition.

The formal classification of device characteristics, as adopted by international regulatory frameworks such as **ISO 13485**, **IEC 60601-1**, and the **US FDA 21 CFR**, groups characteristics into the following categories:

| Characteristic Domain | Description |
|---|---|
| **Technical Characteristics** | Electrical, mechanical, and signal-processing properties |
| **Clinical Characteristics** | Patient outcomes, therapeutic efficacy, diagnostic accuracy |
| **Safety Characteristics** | Risk mitigation, electrical safety, biocompatibility |
| **Operational Characteristics** | Power consumption, battery life, usability, ergonomics |
| **Regulatory Characteristics** | Compliance with medical device standards and classifications |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The Car Dashboard Analogy**
>
> Think of an assistive medical device the way you think about a **car**. A car has a dashboard that tells you everything important: fuel level (analogous to **battery/power characteristic**), engine temperature (analogous to **thermal characteristic**), speedometer accuracy (analogous to **signal fidelity**), brake responsiveness (analogous to **safety/response characteristic**), and seat comfort (analogous to **ergonomic characteristic**).
>
> Similarly, every medical device has its own "dashboard" of characteristics. When a doctor prescribes a **hearing aid** or a **pacemaker**, they need to know these "dashboard readings" to ensure the device will work correctly for that specific patient. Just as you would not buy a car without knowing its mileage and safety ratings, no medical device can be prescribed or used without fully understanding its characteristics.

## 1.3 Fundamental Performance Metrics

The **core measurable characteristics** that appear repeatedly in KTU examination questions are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Five Pillars of Device Characteristics:**
> 1. **Sensitivity** — The ability of the device to correctly detect a true condition (true positive rate)
> 2. **Specificity** — The ability of the device to correctly reject false conditions (true negative rate)
> 3. **Accuracy** — How close the measured value is to the true value
> 4. **Precision** — How reproducible the measurements are under identical conditions
> 5. **Reliability** — The probability that the device performs its intended function over a specified period

These five parameters are **bold** emphasized because they appear in almost every KTU question paper for biomedical and assistive device courses.

## 1.4 Standard Physical and Engineering Constants

Several standard values appear consistently in the study of assistive medical device characteristics:

- **Standard Human Body Temperature:** **37 °C (98.6 °F)** — the reference baseline for thermal characteristics
- **Normal Resting Heart Rate:** **60–100 beats per minute (bpm)** — reference for cardiac device design
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** **101.325 kPa (760 mmHg)** — reference for pressure-sensing devices
- **Human Hearing Range:** **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz)** — fundamental for audiometric device design
- **Normal Blood Pressure:** **120/80 mmHg** — reference for sphygmomanometer design
- **Body Surface Potential Range:** **0.5 mV to 5 mV (ECG)** — reference for cardiac signal characteristics
- **Skin Electrical Resistance:** **1 kΩ to 100 kΩ** depending on moisture and electrode type

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

The **characteristics** of any assistive medical device can be systematically understood by analyzing five interlocking layers:

### Layer 1 — Input Characteristics
These describe how the device receives signals or stimuli from the patient or environment.

- **Input Signal Range:** The minimum and maximum amplitude/frequency the device can accept
- **Input Impedance:** The opposition to current flow at the input terminal (typically high to avoid signal loading)
- **Common Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** The ability to reject noise common to both inputs

### Layer 2 — Transfer Characteristics
These describe how the device processes the input to produce an output.

- **Transfer Function:** The mathematical relationship $H(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)}$ between output and input
- **Gain:** The multiplicative factor relating output to input, $G = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$
- **Bandwidth:** The range of frequencies over which the device operates within specification, defined as $BW = f_H - f_L$
- **Phase Response:** The phase shift introduced as a function of frequency

### Layer 3 — Output Characteristics
These describe what the device delivers to the patient or clinician.

- **Output Power:** Energy delivered per unit time, $P = V \times I$ (Watts)
- **Output Impedance:** Resistance presented to the load (typically low for power devices)
- **Dynamic Range:** Ratio between largest and smallest detectable signals, $\text{DR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{max}}{V_{min}}\right)$ dB
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Quality metric, $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ dB

### Layer 4 — Reliability & Safety Characteristics
These describe how the device behaves over time and under fault conditions.

- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Average operational time between failures
- **Failure Rate ($\lambda$):** Number of failures per unit time, often modeled as a constant in exponential reliability
- **Biocompatibility Index:** Material compatibility with human tissue (ISO 10993)
- **Electrical Leakage Current:** Maximum allowed current that can flow through the patient, **typically ≤ 10 µA** for Type CF (cardiac floating) devices

### Layer 5 — Usability Characteristics
These describe the human-device interaction.

- **Ergonomic Compliance:** Match to human anatomical dimensions (anthropometry)
- **Cognitive Load:** Mental effort required to operate the device
- **Accessibility:** Compatibility with users having varying abilities

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a high-yield reference. Memorize these formulas — they appear directly in KTU Part A and Part B questions.

| # | Characteristic | Formula / Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensitivity | $\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1 or %) | Diagnostic device performance |
| 2 | Specificity | $\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1 or %) | Diagnostic device performance |
| 3 | Accuracy | $\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1 or %) | Overall correctness |
| 4 | Precision | $\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Dimensionless (positive predictive value) | Repeatability of measurement |
| 5 | Gain (Amplifier) | $A_v = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$ | Dimensionless (or dB) | Signal amplification |
| 6 | Gain in dB | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_v \vert$ | Decibels (dB) | Amplifier specification |
| 7 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L$ | Hertz (Hz) | Frequency response |
| 8 | Quality Factor | $Q = \frac{f_0}{BW}$ | Dimensionless | Resonant circuits |
| 9 | SNR | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) | Signal quality |
| 10 | CMRR | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) | Noise rejection |
| 11 | Reliability (exponential model) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Dimensionless | Time-dependent reliability |
| 12 | MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours | Reliability metric |
| 13 | Output Power | $P = V \times I = I^2 R = \frac{V^2}{R}$ | Watts (W) | Power delivery |
| 14 | Power in dBm | $P_{dBm} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P}{1\,\text{mW}}\right)$ | dBm | RF device output |
| 15 | Dynamic Range | $\text{DR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{max}}{V_{min}}\right)$ | dB | Signal handling range |
| 16 | Noise Figure | $NF = 10 \log_{10}(F)$ where $F = \frac{\text{SNR}_{in}}{\text{SNR}_{out}}$ | dB | Receiver sensitivity |
| 17 | Thermal Noise Voltage | $V_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | Volts (V) | Johnson-Nyquist noise |
| 18 | Bioelectric Signal Amplitude | ECG: 0.5–5 mV, EEG: 10–100 µV, EMG: 50 µV–5 mV | Volts | Input design reference |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Notation Rule:** In the table above, absolute value bars have been replaced with `\vert` to prevent breaking the markdown table. Use `\vert x \vert` in your written answers, never `|x|` inside a table cell.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These characteristics are not abstract — they directly govern clinical decisions. For example:

- A **cochlear implant** requires a **dynamic range of at least 30–40 dB** to map the wide range of human hearing into electrical stimulation.
- An **ECG monitor** must have a **CMRR of at least 80 dB** to reject the common-mode 50/60 Hz powerline interference that would otherwise swamp the millivolt-level cardiac signal.
- A **defibrillator** must deliver a precise **truncated exponential biphasic waveform** of 150–200 Joules within milliseconds, requiring exact **output energy characteristics**.
- An **infusion pump** must maintain **flow accuracy within ±5%** and **flow precision within ±2%** — these are direct regulatory requirements per **IEC 60601-2-24**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sensitivity, Specificity, Accuracy, and Precision

Consider a **diagnostic assistive device** (e.g., a glucose monitor or pulse oximeter screening algorithm) tested on a population. The outcomes are tabulated in a **confusion matrix**:

| | **Condition Positive** | **Condition Negative** |
|---|---|---|
| **Test Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Positive (FP) |
| **Test Negative** | False Negative (FN) | True Negative (TN) |

**Step 1: Define Sensitivity**

Sensitivity is the proportion of actual positives correctly identified.

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{\text{Number of True Positives}}{\text{Total Actual Positives}}$$

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**Step 2: Define Specificity**

Specificity is the proportion of actual negatives correctly identified.

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{\text{Number of True Negatives}}{\text{Total Actual Negatives}}$$

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$$

**Step 3: Define Accuracy**

Accuracy is the overall proportion of correct results.

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

**Step 4: Define Precision (Positive Predictive Value)**

Precision is the proportion of positive test results that are truly positive.

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

**Step 5: Worked Numerical Example**

A new **fall-detection wearable** is tested on **500 elderly patients**. Of these, **200 actually experienced falls** (actual positives) and **300 did not** (actual negatives). The device correctly detected **180 falls** (TP) and missed **20** (FN). It falsely raised alarms for **30 patients** who did not fall (FP) and correctly gave no alarm for **270 patients** (TN).

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 90\%$$

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{270}{270 + 30} = \frac{270}{300} = 0.90 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 90\%$$

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{180 + 270}{500} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.90 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 90\%$$

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{180}{180 + 30} = \frac{180}{210} \approx 0.857 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 85.7\%$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Reliability Function from Exponential Failure Model

For many electronic medical devices, the failure rate $\lambda$ is approximately constant over the useful life (the "useful life" region of the bathtub curve).

**Step 1: Probability of failure in time $dt$**

$$P(\text{failure in } dt) = \lambda \, dt$$

**Step 2: Probability of survival in time $dt$**

$$P(\text{survive } dt) = 1 - \lambda \, dt$$

**Step 3: Survival over time interval $t$ (broken into $n$ small steps, $dt = t/n$)**

$$R(t) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda t}{n}\right)^n$$

**Step 4: Apply the exponential limit identity $\lim_{n \to \infty}\left(1 - \frac{x}{n}\right)^n = e^{-x}$**

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 5: Derive MTBF**

Since the expected value of an exponential distribution is the reciprocal of the rate:

$$\text{MTBF} = \mathbb{E}[T] = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

**Step 6: Numerical Worked Example**

A **ventilator** has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 0.0005$ failures per hour. Calculate reliability after 1000 hours and the MTBF.

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.0005} = 2000 \;\text{hours}$$

$$R(1000) = e^{-0.0005 \times 1000} = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$$

So there is approximately a **60.65% probability** the ventilator will operate without failure over 1000 hours, with a **mean time between failures of 2000 hours**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Signal-to-Noise Ratio and Detection Threshold

**Step 1: Define Signal Power and Noise Power**

$$P_{signal} = \frac{V_{signal}^2}{R}$$

$$P_{noise} = \frac{V_{noise}^2}{R}$$

**Step 2: Form the ratio**

$$\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{V_{signal}^2 / R}{V_{noise}^2 / R} = \left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)^2$$

**Step 3: Convert to decibels**

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$$

**Step 4: Numerical Example**

A **neural signal amplifier** detects a neural spike of **$V_{signal} = 50 \,\mu V$** in the presence of thermal noise of **$V_{noise} = 5 \,\mu V$**.

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{50 \,\mu V}{5 \,\mu V}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(10) = 20 \,\text{dB}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Formula

| Engineering Scenario | Primary Characteristic | Secondary Characteristic | Regulatory Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| ECG amplifier design | CMRR, Input Impedance | Bandwidth (0.05–150 Hz) | IEC 60601-2-25 |
| Hearing aid design | Gain, Frequency Response | SNR, Distortion (THD < 3%) | IEC 60118-0 |
| Defibrillator design | Energy delivery (Joules) | Discharge waveform shape | IEC 60601-2-4 |
| Infusion pump design | Flow accuracy ±5% | Occlusion alarm response | IEC 60601-2-24 |
| Pulse oximeter design | SpO2 accuracy ±2% | Perfusion index range | ISO 80601-2-61 |
| Fall detector (wearable) | Sensitivity, Specificity | Battery life, false alarm rate | IEC 60601-1-11 |
| Wheelchair (powered) | Load capacity, Range | Speed, Gradient climbing | ISO 7176 |
| Prosthetic limb | Range of motion | Grip force, battery duration | ISO 22523 |
