**(ii) Expected CPM for row-column:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPM_{RC} &= \frac{60}{3.33} \approx 18 \text{ CPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Approximately 18 characters per minute.

**(iii) Comparison with linear scanning:**

For linear scanning on 36 items at the same scan rate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}[T_{sel,\,linear}] &= \frac{(36-1) \cdot T_{scan}}{2} = \frac{35 \times 0.667}{2} \approx 11.67 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPM_{linear} &= \frac{60}{11.67} \approx 5.14 \text{ CPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:** Row-column scanning is approximately $\dfrac{18}{5.14} \approx 3.5\times$ faster than linear scanning on the same 36-item vocabulary. This is the engineering justification for preferring $2\sqrt{N}$-style scanning whenever the user can reliably manage two-stage selection.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Control Interfaces for Indirect Selection (Switches)**

- **Definition core:** A switch is a *transducer* that converts a voluntary action into an electrical signal used to *confirm* a scanned option — it does **not** directly pick an item.
- **Five modality classes (MPEEB):** **M**echanical, **P**neumatic, **E**MG, **E**OG, **BCI** — ordered from gross-motor to fully neural.
- **Three key switch specs:** Activation force (**g / N**), activation travel (**mm**), and feedback modality.
- **Four scanning methods:** Linear (automatic), Step, Two-switch, Row-Column.
- **Critical formula — expected selection time:**

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{sel}] = \frac{(N-1) \cdot T_{scan}}{2} = \frac{N-1}{2 \cdot v_{scan}}
$$

- **CPM formula:** $CPM = \dfrac{60}{\mathbb{E}[T_{sel}]}$
- **Row-Column advantage:** Reduces scan complexity from $O(N)$ to $O(2\sqrt{N})$ — a major reason row-column is preferred in clinical practice.
- **Debounce rule:** Always include a 5–20 ms debounce interval for mechanical switches to avoid spurious multi-presses.
- **Clinical mappings to remember:**
  * Mild Cerebral Palsy → Mechanical lever or button.
  * High SCI (C3–C4) → Sip-and-puff.
  * Mid-stage ALS → EMG or EOG.
  * Late-stage ALS / Locked-in → EOG or BCI.
- **Engineering trade-off:** Faster scanning rate = higher potential throughput *but* higher error rate if the user's reaction time is exceeded. Always check $T_{scan} \ge T_{reaction}$.
- **Universal metric:** Characters Per Minute (CPM) is the single number that compares every AAC and indirect selection method.
- **Standardization fact:** Most clinical switches use a **3.5 mm mono jack** as the common switch port, enabling any switch to drive any device.
- **Examiner's pet mistake to avoid:** Writing "switch" when the question asks for "interface" — the interface is the *system* (switch + scan engine + display), the switch is just one component.
- **Real-world benchmarks to recall:** Linear scanning typically yields **2–6 CPM**; row-column scanning **10–25 CPM**; expert direct-touch users **40+ CPM**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switch Arrays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Switch Arrays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Switch Array** is a structured collection of two or more discrete electromechanical, electronic, or bioelectric transducers organized in a defined spatial or logical topology (linear, matrix, or grid configuration) that collectively function as an **alternative access input modality** for individuals with severe physical, neuromuscular, or motor impairments. Each individual element within the array is called a **switch site** or **switch point**, and is designed to convert a voluntary physical gesture (muscle contraction, breath pressure, eye movement, head tilt, or neural signal) into a discrete binary electrical event (ON/OFF logic state).

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Assistive Medical Devices* (OEEBT722, Module 2), switch arrays constitute the **primary physical interface layer** between a user's residual motor capability and a downstream assistive output device such as an **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) device**, powered wheelchair controller, environmental control unit (ECU), or computer access system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A switch array is not a single switch; it is a *system* of switches coupled with a **scanning algorithm** that sequentially presents selection options to the user, who then activates a *single dedicated access switch* to confirm the desired choice. The combination of *spatial arrangement + temporal scanning + single confirming switch* defines the array.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "TV Remote at a Restaurant" Model

Imagine you are in a noisy restaurant and cannot reach the waiter. Instead of a full menu with 50 items, the waiter (the **scanning system**) walks down the table and calls out one food category at a time — *"Drinks?"* … *"Starters?"* … *"Mains?"*. You (the user) simply **knock once on the table** (press your **single confirming switch**) the moment he calls the category you want. The waiter then zooms into that category and lists sub-items until you knock again.

- The **categories and sub-items** = positions in the **switch array**.
- The **walker's sequential calling** = the **scanning routine** of the array.
- **Your single knock** = the activation of one **dedicated access switch**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is *why* a person with only **one controllable muscle** (e.g., a single eyelid, cheek twitch, or sip-and-puff gesture) can still operate a 100+ item communication board: the **switch array offloads the spatial selection problem onto a temporal scanning process**, and the user's body only has to provide a binary "yes/no" pulse at the right moment.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standardized parameters govern switch array design and are universally referenced in rehabilitation engineering literature:

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Activation Force** | 0.10 – 1.5 | Newtons (N) |
| **Operating Travel** | 0.5 – 5.0 | millimetres (mm) |
| **Switch Latency** | 10 – 50 | milliseconds (ms) |
| **Hold Time (debounce)** | 20 – 100 | ms |
| **Scanning Step Duration** | 0.8 – 2.0 | s |
| **Acceptable Error Rate** | < 5 | percent (%) |
| **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** | > 50,000 | actuations |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember for exams:** A well-designed switch array must be **fatigue-free** for sustained daily use (often 8–12 hours), so activation force is engineered as low as feasible — typically **less than 0.5 N (≈ 50 grams of force)** for users with muscular dystrophy, ALS, or cerebral palsy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Switch Array Architecture — The Three-Layer Model

A complete switch array system can be decomposed into three hierarchical layers:

**Layer 1 — Physical Switch Layer (Transducers)**
- Converts a *physical gesture* into an *electrical state*.
- May be mechanical, pneumatic, optical, electromagnetic, or bioelectric.
- Each switch has its own **activation force, travel, and feedback signature**.

**Layer 2 — Logical/Topological Layer (Array Geometry)**
- Defines *how* the individual switches are interconnected.
- Common topologies:
  - **Linear Array** — switches arranged sequentially (1 × N).
  - **Matrix Array** — switches arranged in rows × columns (R × C).
  - **Coded Array** — switches encoded via binary weighting.

**Layer 3 — Scanning/Control Layer (Presentation Algorithm)**
- Sequentially highlights or energises each switch site.
- Accepts a confirmation pulse from the *single access switch*.
- Translates the selection into a meaningful command (character, direction, function).

## 2.2 Types of Switches Used in Arrays

| Switch Type | Input Gesture | Typical User Population |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical lever / wobble switch** | Limb displacement | Limited hand mobility, arthritis |
| **Pneumatic (sip-and-puff)** | Oral air pressure | High-level spinal cord injury (C2–C4) |
| **Mercury / tilt switch** | Head or limb tilt | Cerebral palsy, stroke |
| **Capacitive touch sensor** | Skin contact | Limited force capability |
| **Infrared (IR) proximity** | Hand approach | Tremor disorders |
| **EMG bio-switch** | Muscle electrical activity | Locked-in syndrome |
| **EOG (electro-oculogram)** | Eye movement | ALS, brainstem stroke |
| **EEG / BCI switch** | Brain activity (P300) | Total locked-in syndrome |

## 2.3 Scanning Strategies — The Temporal Engine

Scanning is the *heart* of the switch array. The KTU syllabus mandates familiarity with the following methods:

1. **Linear (Automatic) Scanning** — The system highlights items one-by-one in a fixed order. The user hits the access switch when the target item is highlighted.
2. **Step (Manual) Scanning** — Each item is highlighted only when the user presses the switch once; the highlight advances only on the next press. *Best for users with slow, deliberate control.*
3. **Row-Column Scanning** — First, rows are scanned; once the target row is selected, only the *columns* of that row are scanned. Reduces selections from $N$ to $\sqrt{N}$.
4. **Group-Item (Block) Scanning** — Items are grouped; the user first selects a group, then an item within it. Hierarchical reduction.
5. **Directed / Inverse Scanning** — Two switches: one to *move* the highlight, one to *select*. Faster but requires two independent control sites.
6. **Auditory Scanning** — The system *speaks* each item rather than highlighting it. Essential for users with visual impairment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Principle:** There is a fundamental engineering trade-off in scanning — **scanning speed ↑** improves throughput, but **scanning speed ↑** also increases user error rate. The optimal scanning rate is typically tuned *per individual user* through clinical assessment.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** from first principles and form the high-yield numerical toolkit expected in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) questions.

### Table 2.1 — Core Switch Array Formulas

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = 2^{n}$ | Number of distinct selections with $n$ binary switches (coded array) | $N$ = selections, $n$ = number of switches |
| 2 | $T_{L} = n \cdot t_{s}$ | Total linear scan time for $n$ items | $t_{s}$ = step duration (s) |
| 3 | $T_{RC} = (R + C) \cdot t_{s}$ | Row-column scan time for $R \times C$ matrix | $R$ = rows, $C$ = columns |
| 4 | $T_{RC} = 2\sqrt{N} \cdot t_{s}$ | Minimum row-column scan time (square matrix) | $N$ = total items |
| 5 | $I = \dfrac{\log_{2}(N)}{T_{sel}}$ | Information transfer rate (bits/s) | $T_{sel}$ = mean selection time (s) |
| 6 | $f_{act} = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Activation pressure/force density | $F$ = force (N), $A$ = area (m²) |
| 7 | $\eta_{acc} = 1 - \dfrac{E}{N_{attempts}}$ | Switch array accessibility accuracy | $E$ = errors, $N_{attempts}$ = total attempts |
| 8 | $\tau_{deb} = R_{pull} \cdot C_{filter}$ | Debounce time constant (RC filter) | $R$ in ohms, $C$ in farads |
| 9 | $P_{throughput} = \dfrac{\eta_{acc} \cdot \log_{2} N}{T_{sel}}$ | Effective throughput in bits/s | Composite metric |
| 10 | $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \sigma(t - t_{act})$ | Switch output (Heaviside step model) | $V_{cc}$ = supply, $\sigma$ = unit step |

### Table 2.2 — Comparison of Scanning Topologies (Same N Items)

| Topology | Average Selections to Choose | Reduction Factor |
|---|---|---|
| Linear ($1 \times N$) | $N/2$ | 1 (baseline) |
| Row-Column ($\sqrt{N} \times \sqrt{N}$) | $\sqrt{N}$ | $\sqrt{N}/2$ |
| 3-Level Hierarchical ($n^3$ items) | $3n/2$ | $N/3$ |
| Binary Coded ($n$ switches) | $1$ (if simultaneous) | $N$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Switch arrays form the **input substrate** of nearly every commercial AAC device manufactured today (e.g., Prentke Romish, Tobii Dynavox, Smartbox). They are also embedded in:

- **Powered wheelchair controllers** — 4-switch array for directional commands.
- **Environmental Control Units (ECUs)** — IR-controlled appliances mapped to switch positions.
- **Hospital bed / nurse-call interfaces** — Large-button arrays for ICU patients.
- **Surgical robotics consoles** — Foot-pedal switch arrays for surgeon hands-free control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimal Square Matrix for Row-Column Scanning

**Problem Statement:** Given $N$ items to be selected via a row-column scanned switch array with step time $t_s$, derive the matrix dimensions $R$ and $C$ that minimise the total scan time.

**Step 1 — Express total scan time:**

$$T_{RC} = (R + C) \cdot t_s$$

**Step 2 — Impose the constraint that the matrix must hold all $N$ items:**

$$R \cdot C = N$$

**Step 3 — Solve the constraint for $C$:**

$$C = \frac{N}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the scan time equation:**

$$T_{RC}(R) = \left(R + \frac{N}{R}\right) \cdot t_s$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate with respect to $R$ and set derivative to zero (Fermat's theorem for minima):**

$$\frac{dT_{RC}}{dR} = \left(1 - \frac{N}{R^{2}}\right) \cdot t_s = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the critical $R$:**

$$1 - \frac{N}{R^{2}} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R^{2} = N \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R = \sqrt{N}$$

**Step 7 — By symmetry, $C = \sqrt{N}$. The optimal matrix is *square*.**

**Step 8 — Substitute back to find the minimum total scan time:**

$$T_{RC}^{min} = (\sqrt{N} + \sqrt{N}) \cdot t_s = 2\sqrt{N} \cdot t_s$$

**Step 9 — Compare to linear scan:**

$$\text{Speed-up factor} = \frac{T_{linear}}{T_{RC}^{min}} = \frac{N \cdot t_s}{2\sqrt{N} \cdot t_s} = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical example (KTU-style):** For $N = 100$ items and $t_s = 1$ s:
> - Linear scan: $T_L = 100$ s average
> - Row-column (10 × 10): $T_{RC} = 20$ s average
> - Speed-up = **5× faster** with the same single access switch.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Information Transfer Rate for a Switch Array

**Step 1 — Information content per selection (binary entropy assumption, equiprobable symbols):**

$$H = \log_{2}(N) \quad \text{[bits per selection]}$$

**Step 2 — Mean selection time for a row-column array (uniformly distributed target):**

$$T_{sel} = \frac{R + C}{2} \cdot t_s + t_{switch}$$

where $t_{switch}$ is the user reaction time (typically **0.2 – 0.4 s**).

**Step 3 — Substitute optimal $R = C = \sqrt{N}$:**

$$T_{sel} = \sqrt{N} \cdot t_s + t_{switch}$$

**Step 4 — Information rate:**

$$I = \frac{H}{T_{sel}} = \frac{\log_{2}(N)}{\sqrt{N} \cdot t_s + t_{switch}} \quad \text{[bits/s]}$$

**Step 5 — Asymptotic behaviour for large $N$ (interesting limit):**

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} I = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{\log_{2}(N)}{\sqrt{N} \cdot t_s} = 0$$

This shows that **information rate plateaus and eventually decreases** beyond a certain $N$ — a critical design insight: simply adding more switches does *not* always improve communication throughput.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Switch Debounce Time Constant

**Problem:** A mechanical switch is being interfaced to a microcontroller. The switch contacts bounce for approximately $t_{bounce} = 5$ ms. Design an RC low-pass filter such that the debounce time constant $\tau$ is at least $3 \times t_{bounce}$.

**Step 1 — Required time constant:**

$$\tau \geq 3 \cdot t_{bounce} = 3 \times 5 \text{ ms} = 15 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Choose a standard pull-up resistor (typical for CMOS inputs):**

$$R_{pull} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the filter capacitor:**

$$C = \frac{\tau}{R_{pull}} = \frac{15 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{3}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F} = 1.5 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the cutoff frequency is well below the bounce spectrum:**

$$f_{c} = \frac{1}{2\pi R C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{4} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 10.6 \text{ Hz}$$

This is well below the mechanical bounce harmonics (typically > 500 Hz), confirming effective filtering.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Activation Force Compliance Check

**Problem:** A user with residual grip strength of 80 grams-force (≈ 0.785 N) must operate a switch. The switch has an activation area of $A = 1.5 \text{ cm}^{2} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$. Compute the activation pressure and verify it is below the user's capability.

**Step 1 — Convert grip strength to Newtons:**

$$F_{user} = 80 \text{ gf} \times \frac{9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}}{1000} = 0.785 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute pressure on the switch activation area:**

$$f_{act} = \frac{F_{user}}{A} = \frac{0.785}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 5233 \text{ Pa} \approx 5.23 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with switch specification (typical pressure threshold = 1 kPa):**

$$f_{act} = 5.23 \text{ kPa} \gg 1 \text{ kPa} \quad \checkmark$$

The user's available force is more than sufficient — the switch is *operable* with significant margin.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Top-Level System Architecture of a Switch Array Interface

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["USER RESIDUAL MOTOR CAPABILITY"] --> nodeB["PHYSICAL SWITCH TRANSDUCER LAYER"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["SIGNAL CONDITIONING AND DEBOUNCE"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["MICROCONTROLLER INPUT CAPTURE"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["SCANNING ALGORITHM ENGINE"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["OUTPUT DEVICE - AAC WHEELCHAIR OR ECU"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["USER FEEDBACK - AUDITORY VISUAL TACTILE"]
    nodeG --> nodeA
    nodeD --> nodeH["ACCESS SWITCH CONFIRMATION PATH"]
    nodeH --> nodeE
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **closed feedback loop** (A → … → G → A) is the defining feature of a usable assistive system — the user must *perceive* each selection for the array to function. Without feedback, the system is functionally blind.

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Row-Column Scanning Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START SCAN CYCLE"] --> nodeB["HIGHLIGHT ROW 1"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"USER PRESSES ACCESS SWITCH"}
    nodeC --> nodeD["NO - MOVE TO NEXT ROW"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"ALL ROWS SCANNED"}
    nodeE --> nodeF["RESTART CYCLE"]
    nodeE --> nodeB
    nodeC --> nodeG["YES - LOCK SELECTED ROW R"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["HIGHLIGHT COLUMN 1 OF ROW R"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"USER PRESSES ACCESS SWITCH"}
    nodeI --> nodeJ["NO - MOVE TO NEXT COLUMN"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK{"ALL COLUMNS SCANNED"}
    nodeK --> nodeH
    nodeI --> nodeL["YES - SELECT ITEM AT ROW R COLUMN C"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["EXECUTE COMMAND OR OUTPUT SYMBOL"]
    nodeM --> nodeA
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Matrix Switch Array Hardware Topology (4×4 Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR1["ROW LINE 1"] --- nodeS11["SW R1C1"]
    nodeR2["ROW LINE 2"] --- nodeS21["SW R2C1"]
    nodeR3["ROW LINE 3"] --- nodeS31["SW R3C1"]
    nodeR4["ROW LINE 4"] --- nodeS41["SW R4C1"]
    nodeS11 --- nodeC1["COL LINE 1"]
    nodeS21 --- nodeC1
    nodeS31 --- nodeC1
    nodeS41 --- nodeC1
    nodeC1 --- nodeMUX["MULTIPLEXER INPUT"]
    nodeMUX --> nodeMCU["MICROCONTROLLER ADC PIN"]
    nodeMCU --> nodeDRIVE["ROW DRIVER LOGIC"]
    nodeDRIVE --> nodeR1
    nodeDRIVE --> nodeR2
    nodeDRIVE --> nodeR3
    nodeDRIVE --> nodeR4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix topology uses only **4 + 4 = 8 microcontroller I/O lines** to address **16 distinct switches**. By extension, an $8 \times 8$ matrix uses 16 lines for 64 switches. This is the *fundamental economic argument* for matrix arrays in cost-constrained assistive devices.

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Switch Classification Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["SWITCH TYPES FOR ARRAYS"] --> nodeB["CONTACT BASED"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["NON CONTACT BASED"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["BIOELECTRIC BASED"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["MECHANICAL LEVER"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["PUSHBUTTON"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["MERCURY TILT"]
    nodeB --> nodeH["REED SWITCH MAGNETIC"]
    nodeC --> nodeI["PNEUMATIC SIP PUFF"]
    nodeC --> nodeJ["CAPACITIVE TOUCH"]
    nodeC --> nodeK["INFRARED PROXIMITY"]
    nodeC --> nodeL["ULTRASONIC"]
    nodeD --> nodeM["EMG MUSCLE SIGNAL"]
    nodeD --> nodeN["EOG EYE MOVEMENT"]
    nodeD --> nodeO["EEG BCI"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]
**Q: Define a switch array in the context of assistive medical devices. List any two key design parameters that must be considered when selecting a switch for a user with severe motor impairment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A switch array is a *structured group of two or more switches organised in a defined topology and used in conjunction with a scanning algorithm* to allow a user with limited motor capability to make selections from a large set of options using a single confirming access switch.

**Key design parameters (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Activation Force** — must be low enough (typically < 0.5 N) for the user's residual strength to actuate it without fatigue.
2. **Operating Travel** — the distance the switch must move to register an actuation; should match the user's range of voluntary motion.
3. **Feedback** (tactile, auditory, visual) — confirms activation to the user.
4. **Mounting and positioning** — must be ergonomically placed for repeatable use.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the difference between linear scanning and row-column scanning in a switch array system? State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Linear Scanning | Row-Column Scanning |
|---|---|---|
| Sequence | Highlights items 1, 2, 3, … N in a single line | First scans rows, then columns of the chosen row |
| Selections needed | Up to N | Up to $2\sqrt{N}$ |
| Speed | Slow for large N | Faster for N > 9 |

**Advantage of linear scanning (1 mark):** Simpler hardware/software; only one highlight indicator; ideal for small arrays (≤ 8 items) and users with very slow, deliberate control.

**Advantage of row-column scanning (1 mark):** Drastically reduces the average number of activations required — selection time grows as $\sqrt{N}$ rather than $N$, making it suitable for large AAC vocabularies.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **one** of two alternative 14-mark questions. Both sub-parts (a) and (b) carry 7 marks each.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of switch arrays in assistive technology. With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a row-column scanned switch array system and discuss the role of the *single access switch* in such a system.

**Model Solution:**

A switch array is a logically grouped set of switches that, together with a scanning algorithm, provides a *non-keyboard* input modality for users who cannot use standard input devices. **(1 mark)**

**Architecture of a row-column scanned system — Block diagram (4 marks):**

The architecture consists of:
1. **Switch matrix** — physical switches arranged in $R$ rows × $C$ columns. Each switch is at the intersection of one row and one column line.
2. **Row driver** — sequentially activates each row line (e.g., through a multiplexer or shift register).
3. **Column reader** — senses which column line goes low (or high) when a switch is pressed.
4. **Scanning controller** — runs the algorithm: drives rows one by one; when a switch is detected, the controller highlights that row, then scans only the columns of that row.
5. **Single access switch** — a *separate* dedicated switch (e.g., a sip-and-puff or head-switch) used by the user to *confirm* a selection when the target item is highlighted.
6. **Feedback module** — provides auditory click, visual LED highlight, or vibration so the user knows the system state.
7. **Output interface** — transmits the selected character/command to the host device (AAC, wheelchair, ECU).

**Role of the single access switch (2 marks):** The single access switch is the *only* physical input the user must control. It decouples the *spatial selection problem* (choosing from many items) from the *physical control problem* (moving many body parts). By acting as a binary "yes/confirm" trigger during scanning, it allows users with **one controllable muscle** to operate large arrays — this is the core principle that makes assistive communication possible for high-level quadriplegics and locked-in patients.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** A wheelchair controller uses a 4-position switch array (Forward, Reverse, Left, Right) with a single access switch, operated in row-column scanning mode. If the step time of the scanner is $t_s = 1.0$ s and the user reaction time is $t_r = 0.3$ s, calculate:
   (i) the average selection time for any direction,
   (ii) the maximum possible time to confirm a direction,
   (iii) the information transfer rate in bits/s.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $N = 4$ directions, $t_s = 1.0$ s, $t_r = 0.3$ s.

**Step 1 — Choose the matrix form (4 = 2 × 2 square matrix):** $R = 2$, $C = 2$. **(1 mark)**

**(i) Average selection time (3 marks):**

$$T_{avg} = \frac{R + C}{2} \cdot t_s + t_r$$

$$T_{avg} = \frac{2 + 2}{2} \times 1.0 + 0.3 = 2.0 + 0.3 = 2.3 \text{ s}$$

**(ii) Maximum selection time (worst case — target is the last scanned item) (2 marks):**

$$T_{max} = (R + C) \cdot t_s + t_r = 4 \times 1.0 + 0.3 = 4.3 \text{ s}$$

**(iii) Information transfer rate (1 mark):**

$$H = \log_{2}(N) = \log_{2}(4) = 2 \text{ bits per selection}$$

$$I = \frac{H}{T_{avg}} = \frac{2}{2.3} \approx 0.87 \text{ bits/s}$$

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Alternative Set]

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast the various scanning strategies used in switch array systems (linear, step, row-column, group-item, directed). Under what clinical conditions would you recommend each?

**Model Solution:**

| Scanning Strategy | Description | Clinical Indication |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear (automatic)** | Items highlighted sequentially at fixed intervals | Users with reliable single switch and good reaction time; beginners |
| **Step (manual)** | Highlight advances *only* on each switch press | Users with slow, deliberate control; cognitive fatigue; tremor disorders |
| **Row-column** | Two-stage: select row, then column | Large vocabularies (N > 16); users with moderate proficiency |
| **Group-item (block)** | Hierarchical: first select a group, then an item | Very large arrays (N > 100); users who can categorise |
| **Directed (inverse)** | Two switches: one moves highlight, one confirms | Users with two reliable control sites (e.g., two head switches) |
| **Auditory** | Items spoken aloud instead of highlighted | Visually impaired users; auditory learners |

**(4 marks for table, 3 marks for clinical reasoning)**

**Clinical reasoning (3 marks):** A therapist typically begins with **linear or step scanning** for initial training (lowest cognitive load), then progresses to **row-column** as the user gains proficiency. **Directed scanning** is reserved for users with *two* reliable control sites. **Auditory scanning** is chosen when visual scanning is contraindicated (low vision, dual sensory impairment).

---

**(b) [7 marks]** A 100-symbol AAC communication board is implemented as a 10 × 10 row-column switch array. The scanner step time is $t_s = 0.8$ s and the user takes 0.4 s to react. Calculate the average selection time, the speed-up factor compared to a linear scan, and the information transfer rate in bits/s.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $N = 100$, $R = 10$, $C = 10$, $t_s = 0.8$ s, $t_r = 0.4$ s.

**(i) Average selection time (3 marks):**

$$T_{avg,RC} = \frac{R + C}{2} \cdot t_s + t_r = \frac{10 + 10}{2} \times 0.8 + 0.4 = 8.0 + 0.4 = 8.4 \text{ s}$$

**(ii) Speed-up factor over linear scan (2 marks):**

$$T_{avg,linear} = \frac{N}{2} \cdot t_s + t_r = 50 \times 0.8 + 0.4 = 40.4 \text{ s}$$

$$\text{Speed-up} = \frac{40.4}{8.4} \approx 4.81$$

**(iii) Information transfer rate (2 marks):**

$$H = \log_{2}(100) \approx 6.644 \text{ bits/selection}$$

$$I = \frac{6.644}{8.4} \approx 0.79 \text{ bits/s} \approx 47.4 \text{ bits/min}$$

This is consistent with clinically reported AAC throughput for row-column scanning (typically 5 – 15 words/min).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add user reaction time** $t_r$ in the average selection time — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using the wrong formula for row-column** — many students compute $N$ scans instead of $(R+C)$ — losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing $N$ with $2^N$** — in binary coded arrays, $n$ switches give $2^n$ selections, not $n^2$ or $2n$.
> 4. **Ignoring the optimality of square matrices** — for row-column, $R = C = \sqrt{N}$ minimises scan time. Examiners explicitly test this derivation.
> 5. **Not stating units** in numerical answers — KTU strictly deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- A **switch array** is *not* a single switch — it is a system of switches + scanner + single access switch.
- The **single access switch** decouples spatial selection from motor control — a user with one controllable muscle can use a 1000-item array.
- **Activation force** for assistive switches is typically **< 0.5 N** to accommodate weak users.
- **Linear scan time** = $n \cdot t_s$.
- **Row-column scan time** = $(R + C) \cdot t_s$.
- **Optimal row-column matrix** is square: $R = C = \sqrt{N}$, giving $T_{min} = 2\sqrt{N} \cdot t_s$.
- **Speed-up factor** of optimal row-column over linear = $\sqrt{N}/2$.
- **Information rate** = $\log_2(N) / T_{sel}$ bits/s, and it **does not grow monotonically** with $N$ — there is a sweet spot.
- **Debounce time constant** = $R \cdot C$ of the filter; choose $\tau \geq 3 \times t_{bounce}$.
- **Scanning strategies** to know: linear, step, row-column, group-item, directed, auditory.
- **Clinical progression** typically moves: linear → step → row-column → directed.
- **Switch types** to know: mechanical, pneumatic, mercury tilt, capacitive, IR, EMG, EOG, EEG/BCI.
- **Matrix topology economics**: an $R \times C$ matrix uses $R + C$ I/O lines for $R \cdot C$ switches — this is why keyboards use matrix scanning.
- **Feedback is mandatory** — no feedback makes the switch array functionally useless to the user.
- For exam: **always state units, always show intermediate steps, always verify the square-matrix optimality condition.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Discrete Joysticks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Discrete Joysticks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discrete Joystick (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> A *discrete joystick* is a non-proportional, multi-directional assistive input device that produces a finite set of distinct binary (ON/OFF) electrical signals corresponding to specific angular displacements of its actuating shaft. Unlike proportional joysticks that emit a continuously variable analog voltage proportional to deflection, a discrete joystick emulates a bank of independent push-buttons arranged on a directional matrix (typically 4, 8, or 12 directions), where each direction is registered as a clean digital logic transition.

In the context of **Assistive Medical Devices**, discrete joysticks are classified under *Switches and Controls* and are primarily deployed in:
- **Powered wheelchairs** (low-end, attendant-controlled, or tremor-filtered models)
- **Environmental Control Units (ECU)** for home automation
- **Single-Switch Scanning Interfaces** for computer access
- **Surgical robotics master consoles** (low-cost, fail-safe modes)
- **Communication Aids (AAC devices)** such as text-to-speech terminals

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Old TV Remote with Arrow Keys**
> Imagine the four colored arrow buttons on a traditional TV remote (Up, Down, Left, Right). Now imagine those four buttons have been *rearranged onto the tip of a single stick* that you can wiggle. Push the stick *up* and it sends exactly one electrical pulse saying "UP." Push it *down* and it sends a "DOWN" pulse. There is no "halfway up" or "three-quarters left" — only crisp, countable clicks. That is the essence of a discrete joystick: it converts a directional gesture into a *digital word* rather than an *analog voltage*.

## 1.3 Physical Metrics & Standard Specifications

| Parameter | Typical Range / Standard | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Directions** | 4, 8, or 12 (hexa-decagonal layout) | Determines degrees of freedom for the user |
| **Activation Force** | **50 g to 250 g** | Critical for users with limited motor strength |
| **Throw (Deflection)** | 1.0 mm to 3.0 mm | Defines tactile feedback for the user |
| **Output Logic** | **TTL-compatible (0 V / 5 V)** or open-collector | Interface to microcontrollers |
| **Operating Voltage** | 3.3 V DC to 24 V DC | Compatibility with battery systems |
| **Contact Bounce Time** | < 5 ms (typical); < 1 ms (hall-effect) | Requires firmware/hardware debounce |
| **Operating Temperature** | −20 °C to +70 °C | Mobility-aid outdoor use |
| **Mechanical Life** | 10⁵ to 10⁶ actuations | Long-term durability rating |
| **IP Rating** | IP54 to IP67 | Resistance to dust/spills |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners**
> The words *discrete* and *proportional* are **never interchangeable** in an answer script. A proportional joystick outputs $V = k\theta$ (continuous), whereas a discrete joystick outputs $V \in \{V_{OH},\ V_{OL}\}$ (binary).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step by Step

The actuation chain of a discrete joystick follows a five-stage physical-to-electrical conversion:

1. **Mechanical Capture** — The user deflects the gimbal-mounted shaft from its neutral rest position. The shaft is held by a spring-return mechanism that biases it back to centre when released.
2. **Directional Selection** — As the shaft tilts past a calibrated angular threshold (typically ±15° from the cardinal axis), an internal cam or guide mechanism routes motion to **one specific microswitch** out of the bank of N switches arranged radially.
3. **Switch Actuation** — The chosen switch (mechanical, reed, or solid-state) closes a contact pair, producing a low-impedance path from $V_{CC}$ to the output pin.
4. **Signal Conditioning** — An RC debounce network or a Schmitt trigger gate (e.g., 74HC14) cleans the contact-bounce artefacts, delivering a clean logic edge.
5. **Encoding (Optional)** — In 8-direction models, a diode-matrix or priority encoder (e.g., 74LS148) converts the parallel switch closures into a 3-bit binary code for microcontroller consumption.

## 2.2 Internal Switch Architectures

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield — Three Switch Topologies**
> 1. **Mechanical Microswitch Architecture** — Uses SPDT (Single Pole Double Throw) snap-action switches. Cheapest, but subject to contact bounce and mechanical wear.
> 2. **Reed-Switch Architecture** — A magnet on the actuating shaft moves past hermetically sealed reed contacts. Bounce-free, but susceptible to external magnetic fields.
> 3. **Hall-Effect / Optical Architecture** — Solid-state; the shaft carries a magnet or an optical flag. The output is inherently debounced and provides millions of cycles of life.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Output logic level (HIGH) | $V_{OH} \approx V_{CC} - V_{OL(switch)}$ | Voltage at output when no switch closed | **V (Volts)** |
| 2 | Output logic level (LOW) | $V_{OL} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$ | Closed-switch saturation voltage | **V (Volts)** |
| 3 | Debounce RC time constant | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | RC filter delay for contact debounce | **seconds (s)** |
| 4 | Minimum debounce time | $t_{d(min)} = 3\tau = 3RC$ | Three time-constants settle to ~95% | **s** |
| 5 | Spring return force | $F = -k \cdot x$ | Linear Hooke's law restoring force | **Newtons (N)** |
| 6 | Activation force threshold | $F_{act} = k \cdot x_{act}$ | Force needed to overcome detent | **N or grams-force (gf)** |
| 7 | Angular resolution | $\theta_{res} = 360° / N$ | N = number of discrete positions | **degrees** |
| 8 | Power dissipation (per switch) | $P_d = V_{CC} \cdot I_{OL}$ | Worst-case closed-switch power | **W (Watts)** |
| 9 | Bounce frequency approximation | $f_{b} \approx 1 / (2 \cdot t_{bounce})$ | Bounce spectral peak | **Hz** |
| 10 | Encoder word width | $n = \lceil \log_{2} N \rceil$ | Bits needed to encode N directions | **bits** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**
> These equations are used to design **debounce filters** (RC choice), select **actuation force** for arthritic or paediatric users, compute **battery drain** in portable ECUs, and size **encoder ICs** for 8 or 12-direction joysticks.

## 2.4 Truth Table for a 4-Direction Discrete Joystick

| Switch Position | SW1 (Up) | SW2 (Down) | SW3 (Left) | SW4 (Right) | Encoded 2-bit Code (Q1 Q0) |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Neutral (Idle)** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 00 |
| **Up** | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 01 |
| **Down** | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 10 |
| **Left** | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 11 |
| **Right** | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 01 *(see note)* |

> [!WARNING]
> In a 4-direction joystick, encoding is sometimes done with a 2-line priority logic where *Up/Down* share one bit and *Left/Right* share another; the examiner expects you to clearly state whether diagonal motions are physically inhibited (most clinical devices block diagonals to prevent erroneous commands).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Debounce RC Time Constant

**Problem Statement:** A mechanical microswitch used in a discrete joystick has a worst-case contact-bounce duration of $t_b = 8\ \text{ms}$. Design an RC low-pass filter that eliminates the bounce without exceeding an acceptable command latency of $t_{lat} = 25\ \text{ms}$.

**Step 1 — State the required debounce time.**
The filter must treat the bounce as a glitch and reject it. Three time-constants of settle time removes ~95% of the transient, which is the engineering convention for digital filtering.

$$
t_{d} = 3 \tau
$$

**Step 2 — Equate debounce time to worst-case bounce.**

$$
3 \tau \geq t_b \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau \geq \frac{t_b}{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the RC product.**

$$
\tau \geq \frac{8\ \text{ms}}{3} = 2.67\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Choose a standard capacitor first.**
Pick $C = 0.1\ \mu\text{F}$ (a common, low-leakage film capacitor in assistive device PCBs).

**Step 5 — Solve for the resistor.**

$$
R = \frac{\tau}{C} = \frac{2.67 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 26.7\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 6 — Select the nearest E12 standard value.**

$$
R = 27\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 7 — Verify the maximum command latency.**

$$
t_{lat(actual)} = 3RC = 3 \cdot 27\ \text{k}\Omega \cdot 0.1\ \mu\text{F} = 8.1\ \text{ms}
$$

Since $8.1\ \text{ms} \ll 25\ \text{ms}$, the design is **acceptable** for real-time wheelchair control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Interpretation** — A user with cerebral palsy operating a powered wheelchair perceives a response delay of 8 ms as *instantaneous*; delays above ~50 ms begin to feel sluggish and may trigger corrective (and unsafe) secondary inputs.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Encoder Word-Width for an 8-Direction Joystick

**Problem Statement:** An 8-direction discrete joystick (N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW) must be interfaced to a microcontroller. Determine the minimum encoder bit-width.

**Step 1 — Apply the binary encoding formula.**

$$
n = \lceil \log_{2} N \rceil
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute N = 8.**

$$
n = \lceil \log_{2} 8 \rceil = \lceil 3 \rceil = 3\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Validate the 3-bit space.**

$$
2^{3} = 8 \quad \text{(exact match — no wasted codes)}
$$

**Step 4 — Generalisation for a 12-direction joystick (clinical assessment knobs).**

$$
n = \lceil \log_{2} 12 \rceil = \lceil 3.585 \rceil = 4\ \text{bits}
$$

This leaves $2^{4} - 12 = 4$ unused codes, which are typically assigned to *neutral* and *firmware-reserved* states.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spring Return Force vs. Activation Force

**Problem Statement:** A discrete joystick has a linear return spring of stiffness $k = 0.8\ \text{N/mm}$. The activation point (where the switch trips) is at deflection $x_{act} = 1.5\ \text{mm}$. The full mechanical throw is $x_{max} = 2.5\ \text{mm}$. Calculate (a) the activation force, (b) the maximum restoring force, and (c) the *hold-force margin* — the surplus force keeping the switch closed at full throw.

**Step 1 — Activation force calculation.**

$$
F_{act} = k \cdot x_{act} = 0.8\ \text{N/mm} \cdot 1.5\ \text{mm} = 1.2\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to grams-force for spec sheets.**

$$
F_{act(gf)} = \frac{F_{act}}{g} = \frac{1.2}{9.81} \times 10^{3} = 122.3\ \text{gf}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum restoring force at full throw.**

$$
F_{max} = k \cdot x_{max} = 0.8 \cdot 2.5 = 2.0\ \text{N} = 203.9\ \text{gf}
$$

**Step 4 — Hold-force margin.**

$$
F_{hold} = F_{max} - F_{act} = 2.0 - 1.2 = 0.8\ \text{N} = 81.6\ \text{gf}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Significance of Hold-Force Margin**
> The 81.6 gf surplus ensures that even a user with **tremor** (e.g., Parkinson's disease, essential tremor rated 4 on the Fahn-Tolosa-Marin scale) cannot accidentally release the switch mid-command. A hold margin below 50 gf is a documented *device-failure risk* in FDA mobility-aid recall data.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Mechanical-Microswitch Joystick | Reed-Switch Joystick | Hall-Effect / Optical Joystick |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Bounce** | 5–15 ms (requires RC debounce) | 1–3 ms (minor) | None (solid-state) |
| **Mechanical Life** | $10^{5}$ to $5 \times 10^{5}$ cycles | $10^{6}$ to $10^{7}$ cycles | $> 10^{7}$ cycles |
| **Cost (relative)** | **1×** (lowest) | 2× | 3–5× |
| **Immunity to Magnetic Field** | Excellent | **Poor** (susceptible) | Excellent |
| **Sealing / Washability** | Difficult | Moderate (glass envelope) | Easy (no openings) |
| **Power Consumption** | Zero (passive contact) | Zero (passive contact) | 5–15 mA per axis |
| **Best Use Case** | Low-budget attendant wheelchairs | Clean-room hospital ECUs | Premium powered wheelchairs |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram traces a complete signal path from the user's hand to the controlled assistive device (e.g., powered wheelchair motor driver).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[USER HAND] --> B[MECHANICAL GIMBAL SHAFT]
    B --> C[DIRECTIONAL SWITCH BANK N=4 OR 8]
    C --> D[RC DEBOUNCE NETWORK tau EQUALS R TIMES C]
    D --> E[SCHMITT TRIGGER 74HC14]
    E --> F[PRIORITY ENCODER 74LS148]
    F --> G[MCU GPIO PORT]
    G --> H[DRIVER STAGE L298N H BRIDGE]
    H --> I[DC MOTOR OR SOLENOID ACTUATOR]
    I --> J[POWERED WHEELCHAIR OR ECU APPLIANCE]

    subgraph FEEDBACK
        J --> K[TACTILE DETENT FORCE 80 TO 200 gf]
        K --> A
    end
```

## 4.2 Internal Radial Switch Layout (8-Direction)

The diagram below illustrates the geometric arrangement of the eight discrete microswitches around the central pivoting shaft, with the directional codewords used by the encoder.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NE["NORTH EAST — 000"]
    N["NORTH — 001"]
    NW["NORTH WEST — 010"]
    E["EAST — 011"]
    C["CENTRE NEUTRAL — 111 IDLE"]
    W["WEST — 100"]
    SE["SOUTH EAST — 101"]
    S["SOUTH — 110"]
    SW["SOUTH WEST — CODE 7 RESERVED"]

    NE --- N
    N --- NW
    NW --- E
    E --- C
    C --- W
    W --- SE
    SE --- S
    S --- SW
```

## 4.3 Signal-Conditioning Sub-Circuit Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[SWITCH CONTACT RAW] --> Y[PULL UP RESISTOR 10 KILOHM TO 5 V]
    Y --> Z[RC LOW PASS R 27 K C 0.1 MICROFARAD]
    Z --> AA[INVERTING SCHMITT TRIGGER GATE]
    AA --> AB[CLEANED TTL OUTPUT TO MCU]

    style X fill:#222,color:#fff
    style AB fill:#093,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read the diagram as a pipeline.** Every box represents a stage of physical or electrical transformation. The *cleaned TTL output* at the right-hand end is the only signal that should ever be sampled by the wheelchair controller firmware.

## 4.4 Application Mapping Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[DISCRETE JOYSTICK MODULE] --> R[POWERED WHEELCHAIR 4 DIRECTION]
    Q --> S[ENVIRONMENTAL CONTROL UNIT 8 DIRECTION]
    Q --> T[SINGLE SWITCH SCANNING AAC 1 DIRECTION]
    Q --> U[SURGICAL INSTRUMENT SELECTOR 12 DIRECTION]
    Q --> V[ASSESSMENT REHAB GAIT PLATFORM 8 DIRECTION]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between a discrete joystick and a proportional joystick. State two clinical scenarios where a discrete joystick is preferred over a proportional one.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Discrete Joystick | Proportional Joystick |
|---|---|---|
| **Output Signal** | Binary (ON/OFF) in fixed directions | Continuously variable analog voltage |
| **Signal Type** | Digital (TTL levels) | Analog (e.g., 0–5 V) |
| **Speed Control** | Not possible (only on/off) | Variable (proportional to deflection) |

**Two clinical scenarios (1 mark each):**
1. **Severe spasticity / tremor patients** — a proportional joystick can produce *unintended* acceleration because any micro-deflection is amplified; a discrete joystick requires a deliberate past-threshold push, suppressing tremor artefacts.
2. **Paediatric or cognitively-impaired users** — fixed discrete commands are easier to learn and reduce the cognitive load of proportional modulation.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three switch technologies used inside a discrete joystick and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Mechanical microswitch** — *Advantage:* lowest cost; no power consumption when idle. *(1 mark)*
2. **Reed switch** — *Advantage:* hermetically sealed; suitable for wash-down hospital environments. *(1 mark)*
3. **Hall-effect sensor** — *Advantage:* solid-state, no contact bounce, life exceeds 10⁷ cycles. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a 4-direction discrete joystick used in a powered wheelchair. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design an RC debounce filter for the joystick switches given a worst-case contact bounce of **10 ms** and a target command latency not exceeding **30 ms**. Assume a standard capacitor value of 0.1 μF. Justify the choice of resistor from the E12 series. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

1. **Constructional details (3 marks):** A 4-direction discrete joystick consists of a vertical shaft mounted on a gimbal pivot (1 mark). The shaft is held in a neutral position by a centred compression spring (1 mark). Four SPDT microswitches are mounted at the cardinal points (N, S, E, W) inside the housing, each triggered by a cam or actuator arm attached to the shaft (1 mark).

2. **Working (2 marks):** When the user tilts the shaft in a given direction, the cam actuates the corresponding microswitch, closing the contact and pulling the output line LOW (or HIGH, depending on pull-up configuration). The other three switches remain open. The microcontroller reads the active line, encodes the direction, and drives the wheelchair motor accordingly. Upon release, the spring returns the shaft to neutral and all four switches reopen.

3. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the *Signal-Conditioning Sub-Circuit Flow* in Section 4.3. The user input passes through the mechanical shaft → switch contact → RC debounce → Schmitt trigger → MCU GPIO → motor driver → DC motor.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Establish the debounce time requirement.**

$$
t_{d} = 3\tau \geq t_{bounce}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the minimum RC product.**

$$
\tau_{min} = \frac{t_{bounce}}{3} = \frac{10\ \text{ms}}{3} = 3.33\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the required resistance using $C = 0.1\ \mu\text{F}$.**

$$
R_{min} = \frac{\tau_{min}}{C} = \frac{3.33 \times 10^{-3}}{0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 33.3\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Select the nearest E12 standard value.**

$$
R = 33\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the resulting command latency.**

$$
t_{lat} = 3RC = 3 \cdot 33\ \text{k}\Omega \cdot 0.1\ \mu\text{F} = 9.9\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with the 30 ms limit.**

$$
9.9\ \text{ms} \leq 30\ \text{ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 7 — Provide the design justification.**
The 33 kΩ value is the closest E12 standard above the calculated minimum, leaving no design margin on the bounce side. A conservative designer would select the next E12 step up, **39 kΩ**, giving $t_{lat} = 11.7\ \text{ms}$ — still well within the latency budget. *Justification (1 mark):* The E12 series is the international standard resistor decade with 12 values per decade (10, 12, 15, 18, 22, 27, 33, 39, 47, 56, 68, 82), guaranteeing commercial availability and ±5% tolerance.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the three principal switch technologies (mechanical, reed, hall-effect) used inside discrete joysticks. Compare them on the basis of contact bounce, mechanical life, and cost. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A discrete joystick for an environmental control unit (ECU) must drive **8 distinct appliance commands** (fan, light, TV, bed, door, phone, AC, alarm). Design the encoder logic: determine the encoder bit-width, draw a complete truth table, and derive the Boolean expressions for the 3 output bits using a priority-encoder convention. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

1. **Mechanical microswitches (2 marks):** These are spring-loaded snap-action contacts. On deflection, a small internal spring rapidly transfers the contact from one position to the other, producing a clean make-or-break. *Bounce:* 5–15 ms. *Life:* $10^{5}$ to $5 \times 10^{5}$ cycles. *Cost:* lowest (1×).

2. **Reed switches (2 marks):** Two ferromagnetic reeds sealed in a glass envelope close when an external magnetic field (mounted on the joystick shaft) approaches. *Bounce:* 1–3 ms. *Life:* $10^{6}$ to $10^{7}$ cycles. *Cost:* 2× mechanical. They are immune to dust and wash-down.

3. **Hall-effect sensors (2 marks):** A solid-state IC detects the magnetic field of a small magnet on the shaft. Output is an open-collector or push-pull digital signal with no mechanical contact whatsoever. *Bounce:* zero. *Life:* $> 10^{7}$ cycles. *Cost:* 3–5× mechanical.

4. **Comparison summary (1 mark):** Already tabulated in Section 3.4 (Analytical Comparison Matrix) of these notes.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Determine the encoder bit-width.**

$$
n = \lceil \log_{2} 8 \rceil = 3\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Label the inputs and outputs.**
Let inputs be $I_0$ to $I_7$ (one per appliance), with $I_0$ having *highest* priority. Outputs are $Y_2$ (MSB), $Y_1$, $Y_0$ (LSB). Enable line $EO = 0$ when any input is active.

**Step 3 — Construct the truth table.**

| Active Input | $Y_2$ | $Y_1$ | $Y_0$ | Appliance |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| $I_0$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | Fan |
| $I_1$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | Light |
| $I_2$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | TV |
| $I_3$ | 0 | 1 | 1 | Bed |
| $I_4$ | 1 | 0 | 0 | Door |
| $I_5$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | Phone |
| $I_6$ | 1 | 1 | 0 | AC |
| $I_7$ | 1 | 1 | 1 | Alarm |
| None | 1 | 1 | 1 | Idle (default) |

**Step 4 — Derive Boolean expressions (Sum-of-Products from the truth table).**

$$
Y_0 = I_1 + I_3 + I_5 + I_7
$$

$$
Y_1 = I_2 + I_3 + I_6 + I_7
$$

$$
Y_2 = I_4 + I_5 + I_6 + I_7
$$

**Step 5 — Note on implementation.**
These three sum terms can be realised with three 4-input OR gates (e.g., 74LS32 quad-OR package, two of which suffice). For compact integration, the standard MSI 74LS148 8-to-3 priority encoder is used directly. *(1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> The most common mark-losing mistake in this question type is **forgetting to define the priority convention**. If two switches close simultaneously (e.g., North and East), the encoder must declare which one wins. Examiners *will* deduct 1–2 marks if you write down the Boolean expressions without stating "assuming $I_0$ has highest priority." Always start by writing the priority rule on the answer script.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Definition:** A discrete joystick is a *non-proportional*, multi-directional switching device that produces distinct binary outputs for each cardinal direction.
- **Key Distinction:** Discrete = digital/ON-OFF; Proportional = analog/continuous. Never confuse in script.
- **Standard Directions:** 4, 8, or 12. The 4-direction variety is the most clinically common.
- **Activation Force:** Typically **50–250 gf** — must be matched to the user's motor capability.
- **Hold-Force Margin:** Should be at least **50–80 gf** above activation to defeat tremor-induced release.
- **Debounce Filter:** $t_d = 3RC$ is the universal rule; pick the next E12 resistor value up for safety margin.
- **Encoder Width:** $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ — N=4 → 2 bits, N=8 → 3 bits, N=12 → 4 bits.
- **Three Switch Types:** Mechanical (cheap, bouncy), Reed (sealed, low-bounce), Hall-effect (solid-state, no bounce).
- **Spring Force:** $F = kx$ — used to compute activation and hold forces.
- **Clinical Use Cases:** Powered wheelchairs, ECUs, AAC devices, surgical instrument selectors, paediatric rehabilitation.
- **Examiner Traps:** Confusing discrete vs proportional; omitting the priority rule in encoder design; not converting Newtons to grams-force in clinical specs.
- **Block Diagram Recall:** User → Gimbal → Switch bank → RC debounce → Schmitt trigger → Encoder → MCU → Driver → Actuator.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chord Keyboards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Chord Keyboards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Chord Keyboard** is a compact alternative input device in which characters, commands, or symbols are generated by pressing **multiple keys simultaneously** (a "chord"), rather than by pressing individual keys one at a time as in a conventional QWERTY keyboard. The simultaneous key-press pattern is interpreted by an embedded controller or firmware and translated into a specific character, word, macro, or assistive command.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A chord keyboard is an alternative access device that uses binary-encoded combinations of a small number of keys to generate a large set of output symbols. It belongs to the family of **Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC)** input devices and is widely used in assistive medical technology for individuals with limited motor function, single-hand dexterity, or restricted mobility.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Piano Analogy"

Imagine you are sitting at a **piano**. On a regular keyboard (Typewriter model), you would press **one key at a time** to produce one note. But on a **chord keyboard**, you press **multiple keys at the same time**, just like a pianist plays a *chord* (a group of notes struck together) to produce a richer, combined musical effect.

In a piano chord keyboard analogy:
- **Each finger** represents one input key (a "bit").
- **Pressing a combination** of fingers (e.g., thumb + index + middle) generates a unique character.
- The combination is **encoded in binary** — each key is either *pressed (1)* or *released (0)*.

This is why such devices are sometimes called **"binary keyboards"** — every character is represented by a unique binary pattern of pressed/unpressed keys.

## 1.3 Real-World Example

The **Microwriter** (developed in the 1980s) is a classic example. It had just **6 keys** arranged so that a user could hold it in one hand and type by pressing chords. A user could write an entire English letter (A–Z), digits (0–9), and punctuation using just these 6 keys — over **63 unique combinations** are mathematically possible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Fact for KTU Viva:**
> With $N$ keys, the total number of meaningful chord combinations is $2^N - 1$ (we exclude the "no key pressed" state, which has no output). For $N = 5$ keys → **31** combinations; for $N = 6$ keys → **63** combinations; for $N = 7$ keys → **127** combinations (sufficient to map the entire **7-bit ASCII** character set).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Binary Encoding Theory

A chord keyboard is essentially a **binary encoder**. Each key acts as a single binary digit (bit). When the user presses a combination, the firmware reads the state of every key and produces a unique binary code.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Key Matrix Scanning:** A microcontroller continuously scans the state of all $N$ chord keys.
2. **State Vector Formation:** At any instant, a binary vector $\vec{K} = [k_1, k_2, k_3, \ldots, k_N]$ is formed, where each $k_i \in \{0, 1\}$.
3. **Chord Detection:** When two or more keys are pressed within a defined time window (debounce threshold), a valid chord is registered.
4. **Lookup Table Mapping:** The binary vector is matched against a pre-programmed **lookup table (LUT)** to retrieve the corresponding character.
5. **Character Output:** The character is sent to the host computer via USB, Bluetooth, or serial interface.
6. **Auto-Release:** Once all keys are released, the device is ready for the next chord input.

## 2.2 Mathematical Foundation — Combinations, Encoding, and Throughput

### 2.2.1 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------|------|
| 1 | Total chord combinations | $C_{\text{total}} = 2^N - 1$ | Total meaningful chords from $N$ binary keys | dimensionless |
| 2 | Non-empty combinations | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \binom{N}{i} = 2^N - 1$ | Sum of combinations choosing 1 to N keys | dimensionless |
| 3 | ASCII mapping capacity | $2^7 - 1 = 127$ | Number of unique symbols (7-bit ASCII minus null) | symbols |
| 4 | Encoding index (decimal) | $D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} k_i \cdot 2^{(i-1)}$ | Decimal value of a chord pattern | dimensionless |
| 5 | Typing speed (theoretical max) | $S_{\text{max}} = \frac{60 \cdot f_{\text{chord}}}{W}$ | Chords per minute to words per minute | **WPM** |
| 6 | Learning time | $T_{\text{learn}} \approx 20 \text{ to } 40$ hours | Empirical range for basic proficiency | hours |
| 7 | Power consumption | $P = V \cdot I_{\text{active}}$ | Battery-operated models (typical 3.3V–5V) | **mW** |
| 8 | Key actuation force | $F_{\text{key}} = 0.4 \text{ to } 0.8$ N | Recommended for users with limited grip strength | **Newtons** |
| 9 | Scan/refresh rate | $f_{\text{scan}} \geq 100$ Hz | To avoid missed chords | **Hz** |
| 10 | Error rate (skilled user) | $\varepsilon \approx 1\% \text{ to } 3\%$ | After full training | percentage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The encoding index $D$ formula treats the chord pattern as a standard binary number. This means a 5-key chord keyboard can be directly indexed into a 32-entry lookup table — a very memory-efficient firmware design.

## 2.3 Why Chord Keyboards Matter in Assistive Technology

For a person with **cerebral palsy, muscular dystrophy, spinal cord injury, or single-hand amputation**, a full QWERTY keyboard is impractical. A chord keyboard:

- **Reduces the range of motion** required — the hand does not need to travel across 100+ keys.
- **Allows single-handed operation**, which is critical for users with hemiplegia (paralysis on one side).
- **Minimizes physical effort** — only a small subset of keys (often 4–7) needs actuation.
- **Supports customization** — chords can be remapped to frequently used words, phrases, or assistive commands (e.g., "Call Nurse", "Adjust Bed").

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Medical Rehabilitation** | Input device for patients recovering from stroke |
| **Augmentative Communication** | AAC for non-verbal patients (ALS, locked-in syndrome) |
| **Wheelchair Control** | Multi-function controller integrated with powered wheelchairs |
| **Prosthetic Hands** | Pattern recognition triggers for prosthetic grip selection |
| **Hospital Bedside Terminals** | Compact, sterilizable input for clinical staff |
| **Operating Rooms** | Hands-free or single-hand control for imaging/surgical systems |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Number of Unique Chords

**Problem:** Prove that $N$ binary keys can generate $2^N - 1$ unique meaningful chord combinations.

**Step 1 — Start with the binomial expansion.**

For $N$ independent binary keys, each key has 2 possible states (pressed = 1, released = 0). The total number of state combinations (including the "no key pressed" state) is:

$$
T_{\text{all}} = 2 \times 2 \times 2 \cdots (N \text{ times}) = 2^N
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the binomial identity.**

The number of ways to choose exactly $i$ keys from $N$ to form an $i$-key chord is given by the binomial coefficient $\binom{N}{i}$. Summing over all valid values of $i$ (from 1 to $N$, since a "zero-key chord" produces no character):

$$
C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \binom{N}{i}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the binomial theorem identity.**

Recall the binomial theorem states:

$$
(1 + x)^N = \sum_{i=0}^{N} \binom{N}{i} x^i
$$

Setting $x = 1$:

$$
(1 + 1)^N = \sum_{i=0}^{N} \binom{N}{i} = 2^N
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \binom{N}{i} = 2^N - \binom{N}{0} = 2^N - 1
$$

**Step 4 — Conclude the result.**

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{total}} = 2^N - 1}
$$

> This is the **fundamental capacity equation** for any chord keyboard.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Encoding Index for a 5-Key Chord Keyboard

**Problem:** Compute the decimal encoding index for the chord pattern [Thumb + Middle] in a 5-key device where keys are labeled $K_1$ (thumb), $K_2$ (index), $K_3$ (middle), $K_4$ (ring), $K_5$ (little).

**Step 1 — Identify the binary vector.**

Pressed keys: $K_1 = 1$ (thumb), $K_3 = 1$ (middle); Released: $K_2 = 0$, $K_4 = 0$, $K_5 = 0$.

$$
\vec{K} = [1, 0, 1, 0, 0]
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the encoding index formula.**

$$
D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} k_i \cdot 2^{(i-1)}
$$

$$
D = (1)(2^0) + (0)(2^1) + (1)(2^2) + (0)(2^3) + (0)(2^4)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate each term.**

$$
D = 1 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 8 + 0 \cdot 16
$$

$$
D = 1 + 0 + 4 + 0 + 0
$$

**Step 4 — Final decimal value.**

$$
\boxed{D = 5}
$$

The firmware then looks up the entry at index **5** in the LUT to retrieve the character assigned to "Thumb + Middle" — say, the letter **"E"**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Throughput Analysis

**Problem:** A 6-key chord keyboard can be operated at $f_{\text{chord}} = 300$ chords per minute. If the average English word requires 5 keystrokes (including a space chord), find the throughput in **WPM** and compare it to a standard 40 WPM typist.

**Step 1 — Compute keystrokes per minute.**

$$
\text{Keystrokes/min} = f_{\text{chord}} = 300 \text{ chords/min}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute words per minute (5 keystrokes per word).**

$$
S_{\text{WPM}} = \frac{\text{Keystrokes/min}}{5}
$$

$$
S_{\text{WPM}} = \frac{300}{5} = 60 \text{ WPM}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with a 40 WPM standard typist.**

$$
\text{Speedup factor} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5
$$

**Step 4 — Conclude.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Chord keyboard} = 60 \text{ WPM}, \quad \text{1.5}\times \text{ faster than average typist}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, sustained speeds of **40–60 WPM** are realistic for a trained single-handed chord-keyboard user, and **80+ WPM** has been demonstrated in laboratory settings. This is the engineering basis for using chord keyboards in time-critical medical and accessibility applications.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis of Chord Keyboards

| Parameter | Microwriter (6-key) | Twiddler (12-key) | Frogpad | Standard QWERTY (104-key) |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------|---------|---------------------------|
| No. of keys | 6 | 12 | 20 | 104 |
| Theoretical combinations | $2^6 - 1 = 63$ | $2^{12} - 1 = 4095$ | $2^{20} - 1 \approx 10^6$ | N/A (1 key = 1 char) |
| Hands required | 1 | 1 | 2 (compact) | 2 |
| Typical speed (trained) | 30–50 WPM | 40–60 WPM | 30–50 WPM | 40–80 WPM |
| Learning curve | Steep | Steep | Moderate | Moderate |
| Best for | Single-hand users | Mobility-limited | Compact travel | General use |
| ASCII support | Yes (subset) | Yes (full) | Yes (full) | Yes (full) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Chord Keyboard

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER["INPUT LAYER - CHORD KEYS"]
        key1["K1 THUMB"]
        key2["K2 INDEX"]
        key3["K3 MIDDLE"]
        key4["K4 RING"]
        key5["K5 LITTLE"]
        key6["K6 FUNCTION"]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL_LAYER["CONTROL LAYER - MICROCONTROLLER"]
        scan["KEY MATRIX SCANNER"]
        debounce["DEBOUNCE and CHORD DETECTOR"]
        encoder["BINARY ENCODER"]
        lut["LOOKUP TABLE LUT in FLASH"]
        charout["CHARACTER BUFFER"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER["OUTPUT LAYER - HOST INTERFACE"]
        usb["USB HID EMULATOR"]
        bt["BLUETOOTH MODULE"]
        serial["SERIAL UART"]
    end

    subgraph FEEDBACK_LAYER["USER FEEDBACK"]
        led["LED INDICATOR"]
        buzz["BUZZER TACTILE FEEDBACK"]
        lcd["SMALL LCD OPTIONAL"]
    end

    key1 --> scan
    key2 --> scan
    key3 --> scan
    key4 --> scan
    key5 --> scan
    key6 --> scan

    scan --> debounce
    debounce --> encoder
    encoder --> lut
    lut --> charout

    charout --> usb
    charout --> bt
    charout --> serial

    charout --> led
    charout --> buzz
    charout --> lcd
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flow moves from the physical chord keypresses (Input Layer) → microcontroller processing (Control Layer) → output to the host computer (Output Layer) → user feedback (Feedback Layer). This is a complete signal chain for any modern assistive chord keyboard.

## 4.2 Decision Logic for Chord Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["START - Key State Snapshot"]
    read["Read All N Key States"]
    checkempty{"All Keys Released?"}
    idle["Return to Idle"]
    debounce2["Wait debounce period t_d = 10 ms"]
    checkstable{"Pattern Stable?"}
    debounceretry["Continue Sampling"]
    count{"Number of Pressed Keys"}
    one["Index 0 to 31 single key"]
    multi["Index 32 to 63 multi key chord"]
    validate{"Within Valid Range?"}
    output["Output Character from LUT"]
    discard["Discard Invalid Chord"]
    loopback["Loop to Start"]

    start --> read --> checkempty
    checkempty -- Yes --> idle --> loopback
    checkempty -- No --> debounce2 --> checkstable
    checkstable -- No --> debounceretry --> checkstable
    checkstable -- Yes --> count
    count -- 1 key --> one
    count -- Multiple keys --> multi
    one --> validate
    multi --> validate
    validate -- Yes --> output --> loopback
    validate -- No --> discard --> loopback
```

## 4.3 Physical Layout of a Typical 5-Key Single-Hand Chord Keyboard

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PALM_GRIP["PALM GRIP HANDLE - LEFT HAND"]
        pinky["PINKY FINGER REST"]
        ring["RING FINGER REST"]
    end

    subgraph KEYPAD["5-KEY CHORD KEYPAD"]
        k_thumb["K1 - THUMB - UNDERNEATH"]
        k_index["K2 - INDEX - TOP"]
        k_middle["K3 - MIDDLE - TOP"]
        k_ring["K4 - RING - TOP"]
        k_pinky["K5 - PINKY - TOP"]
    end

    subgraph ELECTRONICS["EMBEDDED ELECTRONICS"]
        mcu["MICROCONTROLLER UNIT"]
        batt["RECHARGEABLE LI-ION 3.7V"]
        haptic["HAPTIC VIBRATION MOTOR"]
    end

    PALM_GRIP --> KEYPAD
    KEYPAD --> ELECTRONICS
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Design Note:** In actual assistive device design, the *thumb key* is usually placed on the underside of the device (for natural thumb opposition), while the four finger keys are placed on the top surface — mirroring the natural grip. This reduces fatigue and supports prolonged use by patients with limited grip strength.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a chord keyboard. Mention any two of its advantages in assistive medical devices. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

A chord keyboard is an alternative input device in which the user presses **multiple keys simultaneously** (a "chord") to produce a character, command, or symbol. Each key acts as a binary bit, and the combination is interpreted using a lookup table.

**Two advantages in assistive medical devices:**
1. Enables **single-handed text entry**, critical for patients with hemiplegia, single-hand amputation, or spinal cord injury.
2. **Reduces the physical range of motion** and key-travel required, making it suitable for users with muscular dystrophy, arthritis, or tremor disorders.

> [!NOTE]
> *Mark distribution: 2 marks for the definition, 0.5 marks each for two well-explained advantages, 0 marks for vague points.*

### Question 2: With the help of a formula, determine the number of unique chord combinations possible with a 7-key chord keyboard. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Model Answer:**

The number of unique chord combinations is given by:

$$
C = 2^N - 1
$$

For $N = 7$ keys:

$$
C = 2^7 - 1 = 128 - 1 = 127
$$

Therefore, a 7-key chord keyboard can theoretically generate **127 unique symbols**, which is sufficient to map the entire 7-bit ASCII character set.

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A: (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a chord keyboard** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **binary encoding scheme** and the **lookup table (LUT)** in converting a chord into a character. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 5-key chord keyboard is used by a patient to type messages. Each key is assigned a binary weight as $K_1 = 2^0, K_2 = 2^1, K_3 = 2^2, K_4 = 2^3, K_5 = 2^4$. Determine the decimal encoding index for the chord "**Thumb + Ring**" and explain how the firmware uses this index to retrieve the corresponding ASCII character. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle:**

A chord keyboard works on the principle of **binary encoding of simultaneous key presses**. The functional block diagram (as shown in SECTION 4) consists of:

1. **Key Matrix:** The 5 (or more) chord keys are arranged in a scanned matrix. Each key is monitored continuously.
2. **Scanning and Debounce:** The microcontroller polls each key at a refresh rate of $\geq 100$ Hz and applies a debounce delay (typically **10 ms**) to filter out mechanical switch bounce.
3. **Chord Detection:** When two or more keys are detected as simultaneously stable, a valid chord event is generated.
4. **Binary Encoding:** The pressed/released state of each key forms a binary vector, which is converted to a decimal index using the formula $D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} k_i \cdot 2^{(i-1)}$.
5. **Lookup Table (LUT):** The LUT, stored in non-volatile flash memory, contains a mapping from each decimal index to a specific character. Index 0 typically maps to the "null" or "function" character, while indexes 1 through $2^N - 1$ map to printable characters, commands, or macros.
6. **Output Stage:** The retrieved character is buffered and transmitted to the host computer via USB HID, Bluetooth, or a serial UART.
7. **User Feedback:** LEDs, buzzers, or haptic motors confirm the registered chord, especially important for users with visual or auditory impairments.

> The **binary encoding scheme** ensures that every possible combination of key presses maps to a unique number, while the **LUT** decouples the *physical key pattern* from the *output character* — allowing remapping, language localization, and macro definition without hardware changes.

**(b) Decimal Encoding Index for "Thumb + Ring":**

**Step 1 — Identify which keys are pressed.**
- Thumb = $K_1$ → Pressed → $k_1 = 1$
- Index = $K_2$ → Released → $k_2 = 0$
- Middle = $K_3$ → Released → $k_3 = 0$
- Ring = $K_4$ → Pressed → $k_4 = 1$
- Little = $K_5$ → Released → $k_5 = 0$

**Step 2 — Form the binary vector.**

$$
\vec{K} = [1, 0, 0, 1, 0]
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the encoding formula.**

$$
D = k_1 \cdot 2^0 + k_2 \cdot 2^1 + k_3 \cdot 2^2 + k_4 \cdot 2^3 + k_5 \cdot 2^4
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and evaluate.**

$$
D = 1 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 2 + 0 \cdot 4 + 1 \cdot 8 + 0 \cdot 16
$$

$$
D = 1 + 0 + 0 + 8 + 0
$$

$$
\boxed{D = 9}
$$

**Step 5 — Firmware LUT lookup.**

The microcontroller reads `LUT[9]` from flash memory. Suppose `LUT[9]` is pre-programmed to the ASCII code of **"I"** (ASCII 73). The character is then sent to the host as a standard keystroke.

> [!NOTE]
> The LUT is **user-programmable** in most clinical-grade chord keyboards, so a therapist can reassign index 9 to a frequently used word like "YES", "NO", or "PAIN" — making the device a powerful **augmentative communication tool**.

---

### Question B: (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **chord keyboards** with **standard QWERTY keyboards** for use by differently-abled patients. Discuss at least four parameters. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With the help of the capacity formula, prove that a 6-key chord keyboard can represent 63 unique characters. Show that this is sufficient to cover all uppercase English alphabets (26), digits (10), and 27 special characters/symbols. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (4 parameters):**

| Parameter | Chord Keyboard | Standard QWERTY |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Number of keys** | Typically 4–7 keys | 104+ keys |
| **Hands required** | Can be operated with **one hand** | Requires **two hands** |
| **Range of motion** | Minimal — keys are clustered | Large — fingers must travel across the full width |
| **Learning curve** | Steep (memorize chord patterns) | Moderate (spatial memory of layout) |
| **Speed (trained user)** | 40–60 WPM (single hand) | 40–80 WPM (two hands) |
| **Best suited for** | Single-hand, mobility-limited users | General population |
| **Customizability of layout** | Highly customizable via firmware LUT | Fixed physical layout |
| **Physical fatigue** | Low (small motion) | Higher (long travel) |

> **Conclusion:** For a patient with a single functional hand, a chord keyboard is **objectively superior**; for an able-bodied user performing general office work, a QWERTY keyboard remains the standard.

**(b) Proof of 63-character capacity:**

**Step 1 — State the formula.**

For $N$ binary keys:

$$
C = 2^N - 1
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $N = 6$.**

$$
C = 2^6 - 1
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$
C = 64 - 1 = 63
$$

**Step 4 — Show that 63 is sufficient.**

The total required symbols are:
- Uppercase alphabets: **26**
- Digits: **10**
- Special characters and symbols: **27**

$$
\text{Total required} = 26 + 10 + 27 = 63
$$

**Step 5 — Compare.**

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{available}} = 63 \quad \geq \quad C_{\text{required}} = 63}
$$

Therefore, a 6-key chord keyboard has **exactly enough** unique chords to represent all uppercase alphabets, digits, and 27 special characters — making it a perfectly balanced design choice for assistive medical devices. *(In practice, a 7-key device with 127 combinations is often preferred for full ASCII support including lowercase letters.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "-1" in the capacity formula** — many students write $2^N$ instead of $2^N - 1$, losing 1 mark. The subtraction accounts for the "no key pressed" state, which is not a valid chord.
> 2. **Mixing up chord keyboard with keypad/T9 phone input** — T9 is a *predictive text* system, not a chord keyboard. KTU examiners specifically penalize this confusion.
> 3. **Skipping the debounce step** in the block diagram — debounce and chord detection are mandatory blocks; omitting them loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not explaining the role of the LUT** — the lookup table is the *heart* of the chord-to-character mapping; simply stating "it maps keys to characters" is incomplete.
> 5. **Wrong formula for the encoding index** — the LSB ($K_1$) must always carry $2^0 = 1$ weight; the MSB carries $2^{(N-1)}$ weight. Inverting this is a common 1-mark error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Chord Keyboards**

- **Definition:** Input device where multiple keys are pressed simultaneously to generate a character.
- **Core Formula:** $C = 2^N - 1$ (number of unique chords from $N$ keys).
- **Encoding Formula:** $D = \sum_{i=1}^{N} k_i \cdot 2^{(i-1)}$ (decimal index of a chord).
- **Typical key count:** 4, 5, 6, or 7 keys — smaller than 100+ in QWERTY.
- **Example devices:** Microwriter (6-key), Twiddler (12-key), Frogpad (20-key).
- **Block diagram essential blocks:** Key matrix → Scanner → Debounce → Encoder → LUT → Output buffer → Host interface.
- **Debounce time:** typically **10 ms** to avoid switch bounce.
- **Scan rate:** $\geq 100$ Hz.
- **Typing speed (trained):** **30–60 WPM** for clinical-grade single-handed use.
- **Key actuation force:** 0.4–0.8 N (gentle on weak grip).
- **Advantages:** Single-hand use, compact, customizable, low fatigue.
- **Limitations:** Steep learning curve, chord memorization required, slower for absolute beginners.
- **KTU must-mention keywords:** Binary encoding, lookup table (LUT), AAC, single-hand operation, capacity formula, debounce, USB HID output.
- **Application domains:** AAC for locked-in syndrome, prosthetic control, wheelchair control, hospital bedside terminals, surgical systems.
- **ASCII coverage:** 7 keys → 127 unique combinations (full 7-bit ASCII).
- **Examiner trick question:** "Why is it $2^N - 1$ and not $2^N$?" — Answer: To exclude the "no key pressed" idle state.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple versus Integrated Control Interfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
