# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define a "frame" in the context of assistive medical devices. List any four different types of walking-aid frames.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** A frame in an assistive medical device is the **primary load-bearing structural skeleton** that supports the user, transmits body weight and reaction forces to the ground, and provides mounting points for functional components like wheels, seats, and brakes. It determines the device's stability, ergonomics, and mechanical efficiency.

**Four types of walking-aid frames (1 mark — ¼ mark each):**
1. **Pick-up walker (Zimmer frame)** — four-legged rigid frame, no wheels.
2. **Two-wheeled walker** — front wheels + rear glide tips.
3. **Four-wheeled rollator** — four wheels with hand brakes, seat, and pouch.
4. **Hemi-walker (side-stepper)** — single-handle asymmetric frame for hemiplegic users.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between a rigid frame wheelchair and a folding (cross-brace) frame wheelchair. Mention two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> | Parameter | Rigid Frame | Folding (Cross-Brace) Frame |
> |---|---|---|
> |---|---|---|
> | **Mechanism** | Welded, no hinge | X-shaped scissor linkage |
> | **Energy efficiency** | 100% (no hinge loss) | 85–95% (friction loss at pivot) |
> | **Weight** | Lighter (7–10 kg) | Heavier (12–15 kg) |
> | **Transport** | Not foldable | Folds for car trunk |
> | **Maintenance** | Very low | Periodic hinge lubrication |
> | **Stiffness** | Maximum | Lower due to pivot |
> 
> **Rigid frame advantages:** (i) Higher propulsion efficiency; (ii) Lower weight; (iii) Better vibration transfer. *(Pick any 2)*
> 
> **Folding frame advantages:** (i) Compact for transport; (ii) Easier storage; (iii) Adjustable to user dimensions. *(Pick any 2)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Describe in detail the **classification of wheelchair frames** based on structural geometry and drive type. Explain the **load transfer mechanism** in a rigid manual wheelchair.

> **(b)** [7 Marks] A wheelchair frame member is a hollow aluminum tube of outer diameter 30 mm and wall thickness 2 mm. The supported length is 0.50 m, fixed at both ends. Calculate the **Euler critical buckling load**. Given: $E = 69$ GPa, $\sigma_y = 240$ MPa. Comment on the safety.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Classification of wheelchair frames (5 marks):**

1. **Manual Wheelchair Frames:**
   - **Rigid frame** — welded box chassis, ultralight, energy-efficient.
   - **Folding (cross-brace) frame** — X-linkage, transport-friendly, hinge loss.
   - **Tilt-in-space frame** — entire seat rotates about rear pivot for pressure relief.
   - **Reclining frame** — backrest pivots backward independently.
   - **Standing frame wheelchair** — allows user to assume upright posture.

2. **Power Wheelchair Frames (by drive type):**
   - **Rear-wheel drive (RWD)** — stable at speed, good outdoor.
   - **Mid-wheel drive (MWD)** — tightest turning radius, indoor.
   - **Front-wheel drive (FWD)** — best curb climbing.

**Load transfer in rigid manual wheelchair (2 marks):**

$$F_{user} \rightarrow \text{Seat sling} \rightarrow \text{Frame rails} \rightarrow \text{Axle plates} \rightarrow \text{Wheel hubs} \rightarrow \text{Tire contact} \rightarrow \text{Floor}$$

The **rigid frame** ensures direct, lossless force transfer because there are no intermediate hinges. Energy from the **push-rim** is transmitted via the **axle plate** (welded to frame) directly to the **rear wheel hub**, with ~100% conversion to forward motion.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Given:**
- Outer diameter: $D = 30$ mm = 0.030 m
- Wall thickness: $t = 2$ mm → Inner diameter: $d_i = 30 - 2(2) = 26$ mm = 0.026 m
- Length: $L = 0.50$ m
- **Fixed at both ends → $K = 0.5$**
- $E = 69 \times 10^9$ Pa

**Step 1 — Compute $I$:**
$$I = \frac{\pi}{64} (D^4 - d_i^4)$$
$$D^4 = (0.030)^4 = 8.1 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$
$$d_i^4 = (0.026)^4 = 4.5698 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^4$$
$$I = \frac{\pi}{64} (8.1 \times 10^{-7} - 4.5698 \times 10^{-7})$$
$$= \frac{\pi}{64} \times 3.5302 \times 10^{-7}$$
$$= 1.7328 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2 — Effective length:**
$$L_e = K \cdot L = 0.5 \times 0.50 = 0.25 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Euler critical load:**
$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \, E \, I}{L_e^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 69 \times 10^9 \times 1.7328 \times 10^{-8}}{(0.25)^2}$$

Numerator:
$$\pi^2 \times 69 \times 10^9 \times 1.7328 \times 10^{-8} = 9.8696 \times 1195.6 = 11800$$

Denominator:
$$(0.25)^2 = 0.0625$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{11800}{0.0625} = 188{,}800 \text{ N} \approx 188.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-sectional area:**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d_i^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.030^2 - 0.026^2)$$
$$= \frac{\pi}{4} (9.0 \times 10^{-4} - 6.76 \times 10^{-4}) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 2.24 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$= 1.759 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Yield load:**
$$P_{yield} = \sigma_y \cdot A = 240 \times 10^6 \times 1.759 \times 10^{-4} = 42{,}217 \text{ N} \approx 42.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Comment on safety:**
Since $P_{cr} = 188.8$ kN $> P_{yield} = 42.2$ kN, the column will **yield before it buckles**. This is a **stocky column** (low slenderness ratio), and Euler's formula gives an upper bound. The actual failure load is closer to $P_{yield}$. For a 90 kg user applying even 50% body weight (~440 N) to one frame member, the FoS is $42.2/0.44 \approx 96$ — extremely safe.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **biomechanical rationale** for using different standing frames (prone, supine, mobile). List at least **three clinical indications** for standing frame prescription.

> **(b)** [7 Marks] A rollator frame has **four wheels** of diameter 200 mm. The user (75 kg) is supported by the **handgrips** at a height of 0.95 m from the ground. The handgrip handles are 0.40 m apart. Calculate the **static stability margin** and the **maximum tip-over angle** for the rollator when the user leans fully to one side, assuming the user's CG shifts to within 0.05 m of the handle's outer edge.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Biomechanical rationale for standing frames (4 marks):**

1. **Prone Stander (anterior support):**
   - User leans forward against anterior pads.
   - Promotes **hip extension**, stretches tight **gastrocnemius-soleus** complex.
   - Encourages **head and trunk control** in upright posture.
   - Best for users with **good head control** but weak hip extensors.

2. **Supine Stander (posterior support):**
   - User supported from behind, can gradually tilt to vertical.
   - Suitable for users with **poor head/neck control** (e.g., severe CP, brain injury).
   - Provides **passive stretch** of hip flexors and hamstrings.

3. **Mobile Stander (wheeled base):**
   - Combines standing posture with **self-propelled mobility**.
   - Promotes **bilateral upper-limb use**, **cardiovascular exercise**, and **psychological well-being** (user is at peer eye level).
   - Used in **rehabilitation programs** for spinal cord injury and spina bifida.

**Three clinical indications (3 marks — 1 each):**
1. **Prevention of osteoporosis** — weight-bearing loads maintain bone mineral density in SCI patients.
2. **Prevention of contractures** — prolonged stretch maintains hip and knee range.
3. **Improved circulation, renal function, and bowel/bladder dynamics** in chronic wheelchair users.
4. *(Optional fourth)* **Psychosocial benefit** — peer-level interaction, improved self-esteem.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Given:**
- Rollator wheel diameter: 200 mm → wheel radius: $r = 0.10$ m
- Handgrip height: $h = 0.95$ m
- Handgrip spacing: $0.40$ m
- User mass: $m = 75$ kg
- CG shift to within 0.05 m of outer handle edge when leaning

**Step 1 — Identify tip-over pivot:**
When user leans to one side, the rollator tips about the **outer wheel's outer contact edge** (or, more practically, about the wheel's contact point on the lean-side).

**Step 2 — Geometry of tip-over:**
- Handgrip separation: $0.40$ m → user's body is roughly centered between handles
- Half-handgrip width: $0.20$ m
- CG shift to within 0.05 m of outer handle → CG is at $0.20 + 0.05 = 0.25$ m **beyond centerline** of rollator

Wait — re-interpretation. The pivot is the **outer wheel contact** on the lean side. The CG is offset horizontally by $0.25$ m from the rollator's centerline.

**Step 3 — Recompute:**
- Horizontal distance from CG to outer pivot wheel: $d = 0.25$ m
- Height of CG: $H_{cg} = 0.95$ m (assume CG at handle level for upper-body lean)

**Step 4 — Static Stability Margin:**
$$SSM = \frac{d}{H_{cg}} = \frac{0.25}{0.95} = 0.263$$

> [!TIP]
> **Threshold for rollators:** $SSM \geq 0.15$ is acceptable for a four-wheeled rollator (less strict than wheelchair, since the **user is not seated inside** and can self-correct).

**Step 5 — Maximum tip-over angle:**
$$\theta_{tip} = \arctan\left(\frac{d}{H_{cg}}\right) = \arctan(0.263) = 14.7°$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:**
The rollator can be tilted up to **14.7°** laterally before tipping. Since most walking surfaces are level (±3°), this provides adequate margin. However, on **steeply cambered sidewalks** (>10°), lateral tip-over becomes a real risk — motivating the need for **hand brakes** and **wide wheelbase** designs.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in "Frame Types" Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "rigid" and "folding" wheelchair frames:** Students often write "rigid = heavy" and "folding = light." In reality, **rigid frames are LIGHTER** (no hinge hardware, no cross-brace). The KTU examiner will deduct 1 mark.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting units in Euler's formula:** A bare numerical answer of "14625" without "N" is a guaranteed ½ mark deduction. Always write units.
> 
> 3. **Wrong K-factor for boundary conditions:** Most students assume $K = 1$ (pinned-pinned) by default. For a walker leg fixed at the cross-frame, $K = 0.7$ (fixed-pinned). Mismatch loses 1 mark.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up drive configurations:** RWD = rear wheels drive (stable, outdoor). MWD = mid wheels drive (tightest turn). FWD = front wheels drive (best climbing). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 
> 5. **Writing "frame" without qualifying "load-bearing structure":** In Part A definitions, vague answers lose 1 mark. Use the KTU phrase: *"primary load-bearing structural skeleton."*
> 
> 6. **Not mentioning ISO/RESNA standards:** Adding a sentence like *"per ISO 7176, the SSM must be ≥ 0.20"* elevates your answer from good to examiner-favorite. Worth ½–1 bonus mark in borderline cases.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Frame Types (Module 2)**

- [x] **Definition:** Frame = **primary load-bearing structural skeleton** of an assistive device.
- [x] **Five major categories:** Walking aids, Wheelchairs, Standing frames, Orthotic/Prosthetic, Transfer frames.
- [x] **Walker types:** Pick-up (4 legs, max stability) → 2-wheeled (front wheels) → 4-wheeled rollator (brakes + seat) → Hemi-walker (stroke).
- [x] **Wheelchair frame types:** **Rigid** (welded, energy-efficient) vs **Folding** (X-brace, transport-friendly) vs **Tilt-in-space** (pressure relief) vs **Reclining** (backrest pivot) vs **Power** (RWD/MWD/FWD).
- [x] **Euler's buckling formula:** $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / (KL)^2$ — must know K-factors (1.0 pinned-pinned, 0.5 fixed-fixed, 0.7 fixed-pinned, 2.0 fixed-free).
- [x] **Static Stability Margin:** $SSM = d/H_{cg}$, minimum **0.20** for wheelchairs, **0.15** for rollators.
- [x] **Material hierarchy (by specific strength $\eta$):** Carbon fiber > Titanium > 7000-series Al > Steel > 6061-T6 Al.
- [x] **Folding hinge loss:** **5–15%** of push energy lost at cross-brace pivot.
- [x] **Standing frame types:** **Prone** (anterior, good head control) vs **Supine** (posterior, poor head control) vs **Mobile** (wheeled, self-propelled).
- [x] **Power wheelchair drives:** **RWD** (outdoor, stable) / **MWD** (indoor, tightest turn) / **FWD** (curb climbing).
- [x] **Standards:** ISO 9999, ISO 7176, RESNA WC-1, WHO GATE initiative.
- [x] **Key numbers to memorize:** SSM ≥ 0.20, FoS ≥ 3.0, cycle fatigue ≥ 1,00,000, walker handle at wrist crease height, user SSM tip angle ~20° typical.
- [x] **Biomechanical decision logic:** Always match frame to **user's specific impairment + environment + activity level**, not just diagnosis.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy (KTU 2024):**
> - Part A (3-mark) questions: lead with the **formal KTU definition** in one sentence, then list 4 items with **one-line descriptors** each.
> - Part B (14-mark) questions: always draw the **load-path diagram** (5–7 marks guaranteed), then write the **derivation with units** (5–7 marks), then conclude with **clinical/commentary** (2 marks).
> - Use the **exact phrases** "per RESNA," "per ISO 7176," "specific strength" — these signal to the examiner that you know the standards.

---

*End of Module 2 — Frame Types | OEEBT722 — Assistive Medical Devices | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Propelling Structure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Propelling Structure # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **PROPELLING STRUCTURE (KTU 2024 Definition)** > A *propelling structure* in the context of assistive medical devices is the **mechanical sub-system of a mobility aid (typically a manual or powered wheelchair, or a prosthetic limb)** that converts user input — muscular effort, electrical energy, or hybrid bio-mechanical actuation — into the **linear/angular displacement of the device over the ground**. It comprises all force-transmission elements: the drive wheels, hand rims, push levers, gear hubs, drive motors, sprocket-chain assemblies, and their kinematic linkages. In the KTU 2024 syllabus (OEEBT722, Module 2), the propelling structure is treated as the *functional interface* between the **user (operator)** and the **terrain**. It is therefore evaluated against three engineering figures-of-merit: 1. **Propulsion Mechanical Advantage (MA)** — ratio of output force to input effort. 2. **Propulsion Efficiency ($\eta_p$)** — fraction of metabolic/input energy converted to useful kinetic energy. 3. **Ergonomic Compatibility (EC)** — degree to which the geometry matches the user's range-of-motion (ROM), strength, and disability profile. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy: The "Engine Room" of a Wheelchair** > Think of a wheelchair as a small, human-powered *car*. The **propelling structure is the engine + gearbox + drivetrain of that car** — but here, the *fuel* is the user's arm, shoulder, or hand muscles. Just as a small hatchback and a heavy truck need very different engines, a paraplegic athlete and a geriatric hemiplegic patient need *very different* propelling structures. A racing chair uses ultra-light push-rims (low MA, high speed); a geriatric one-arm-drive chair uses a **linked double-rim with a lag lever** (high MA, low speed, but operable with one functional hand). ## 1.3 Engineering Metrics & Physical Constants (Bold for Emphasis) | Symbol | Metric | Typical Value | Unit | |--------|--------|---------------|------| | $\eta_p$ | Propulsion efficiency (manual) | **15 – 25 %** | dimensionless | | $M_A$ | Mechanical advantage (lever drive) | **1.5 – 2.5** | dimensionless | | $R_r$ | Rolling resistance coefficient | **0.005 – 0.015** (PU tire on tile) | dimensionless | | $F_{rr}$ | Rolling resistance force (90 kg user) | **4.4 – 13.2** | N | | $T_p$ | Push-rim torque per stroke | **15 – 40** | N·m | | $f_s$ | Stroke cadence | **0.6 – 1.2** | Hz | | $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81** | m/s² | > [!NOTE] > The coefficient of rolling resistance $R_r$ is **empirically** determined (ISO 7176-11 standard). Always quote it as a *dimensionless multiplier* on the normal load, **never** as a force value. ## 1.4 Syllabus Scope of "Propelling Structure" (KTU OEEBT722, Module 2) The KTU 2024 module groups the following sub-topics under propelling structures: 1. Hand-rim propelled wheels (the *de facto* standard for manual wheelchairs). 2. Lever-driven propulsion (geared hub, push-lever, anti-rollback). 3. One-arm drive (OAD) for hemiplegic users. 4. Power-assist wheels (e-motion®, Twion, Yamax). 5. Hub motors in powered wheelchairs. 6. Crank-and-sprocket drives (rare; mostly for racing or paediatric). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — Six Step Chain The propelling structure, regardless of its physical form, follows the same **energy-flow chain**: 1. **Energy input stage** — User applies force $F_{in}$ (hands on rim, palm on lever, joystick voltage). 2. **Force transmission stage** — Force is routed through a **kinematic linkage** (rim → spokes → hub, or lever → link rod → drive sprocket). 3. **Gear reduction stage** — A gear train (planetary, spur, or worm) trades speed for torque by a ratio $N$ (e.g., $N = 1.5:1$). 4. **Drive-wheel traction stage** — The drive wheel contacts the ground; tangential force $F_{out} = T_{wheel} / r_{wheel}$ must overcome $F_{rr} + F_{grade} + F_a$ (resistive, gravitational, inertial). 5. **Rolling/translation stage** — The chair accelerates according to $F_{net} = m_{total} \cdot a$. 6. **Feedback stage** — Tactile, vestibular, and visual feedback close the loop for the user's next stroke. > [!IMPORTANT] > The "Why" behind the gear-reduction stage: a human arm produces peak force at low cadence, but a wheelchair needs to maintain a usable *cruise speed*. The gear ratio $N$ resolves this **impedance mismatch** (analogous to bicycle gears). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Master Table) | # | Formula | LaTeX | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity | |---|---------|-------|-------------------|---------------------| | 1 | Rolling resistance force | $F_{rr} = R_r \cdot W = R_r \cdot m_{total} \cdot g$ | $R_r$ = coeff. (–), $W$ = N, $m$ = kg | PU tire, smooth surface | | 2 | Grade resistance | $F_g = m_{total} \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | $\theta$ = slope (°) | $\theta \leq 10°$ for manual | | 3 | Inertial resistance | $F_a = m_{total} \cdot a$ | $a$ = m/s² | Negligible for steady cruise | | 4 | Total resistive force | $F_R = F_{rr} + F_g + F_a$ | N | Vector sum on flat ground = $F_{rr}$ | | 5 | Required drive-wheel torque | $T_{wheel} = F_R \cdot r_{wheel}$ | $r$ = m, T = N·m | Static analysis | | 6 | Push-rim torque (direct) | $T_{rim} = T_{wheel}$ | N·m | $1:1$ push-rim drive | | 7 | Push-rim torque (geared) | $T_{rim} = T_{wheel} / N$ | N·m | $N > 1$ for geared hub | | 8 | Mechanical advantage | $M_A = F_{rim} / F_{out} = r_{rim} / (N \cdot r_{wheel})$ | – | $M_A > 1$ ⇒ force amplifier | | 9 | Push-rim speed | $v_{rim} = N \cdot v_{chair}$ | m/s | Angular velocity scaled | | 10 | Power demand (mech.) | $P_{mech} = F_R \cdot v_{chair}$ | W | Cruise power | | 11 | Power demand (electric) | $P_{elec} = P_{mech} / \eta_{motor} \eta_{gear}$ | W | $\eta_{motor} \approx 0.85$, $\eta_{gear} \approx 0.90$ | | 12 | Metabolic cost | $C_{met} = P_{mech} / \eta_p$ | W | $\eta_p \approx 0.15$–$0.25$ for manual | | 13 | Stroke work | $W_{stroke} = \int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} T_{rim} \, d\theta$ | J | Per propulsion cycle | | 14 | Push-rim contact angle | $\phi_c \approx 90°$ – $120°$ | ° | ISO 7176-5 range | | 15 | Slip threshold | $\mu \geq F_R / W$ | – | $\mu \approx 0.7$ PU-rubber on dry tile | > [!NOTE] > **All five key drive-train efficiencies for powered wheelchairs stack multiplicatively**: $\eta_{total} = \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{chain} \cdot \eta_{bearing} \cdot \eta_{tire}$. A "90 % efficient" drivetrain × 5 stages is actually only $\approx 59\%$ efficient overall. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The propelling structure is the **single biggest determinant of usability** in any wheelchair prescription. Clinically: - A **high $M_A$** (geared, lever drive) is prescribed for elderly / arthritic users with limited shoulder strength — they can climb ramps that would otherwise stall them. - A **low $M_A$** (direct push-rim) is prescribed for active paraplegics needing high *push frequency* and quick manoeuvring. - **Power-assist wheels** add an in-hub motor to a manual chair, giving the metabolic cost of an electric chair while preserving the *form factor* of a manual one — this is now the **gold standard** in KTU-aligned clinical practice for ambulant long-term users. ## 2.4 Push-Rim Geometry — The Hidden Multiplier The push-rim diameter $d_{rim}$ is **larger** than the tire diameter $d_{wheel}$ (offset $\approx 20$–$30$ mm radially outward). This *mechanical advantage by geometry* gives: $$M_{A,geo} = \frac{d_{rim}}{d_{wheel}} \approx 1.10 \text{ to } 1.18$$ Combined with a geared hub of ratio $N$: $$M_{A,total} = N \cdot \frac{d_{rim}}{d_{wheel}}$$ # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Rolling Resistance Force for a Manual Wheelchair ### 3.1.1 Problem Statement Derive the rolling resistance force $F_{rr}$ experienced by a manual wheelchair whose **combined user + chair mass** is $m_{total}$, rolling on a tiled floor with rolling-resistance coefficient $R_r$. ### 3.1.2 Given - $m_{total} = m_{user} + m_{chair}$ (kg) - Gravitational acceleration $g = 9.81$ m/s² - Coefficient $R_r$ (dimensionless, ISO 7176-11) - Flat, level surface ### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Identify the normal load.** On a horizontal surface, the total normal force on the wheels equals the weight of the system. $$W = m_{total} \cdot g$$ **Step 2 — Apply the empirical rolling-resistance model.** The standard Coulomb-friction form of rolling resistance is: $$F_{rr} = R_r \cdot W$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the weight expression.** $$F_{rr} = R_r \cdot m_{total} \cdot g$$ **Step 4 — Numerical sanity check.** Take a typical adult user: $m_{total} = 90 + 12 = 102$ kg, $R_r = 0.008$ (PU tire on smooth tile). $$F_{rr} = 0.008 \cdot 102 \cdot 9.81 \;\text{N}$$ Breaking it down: $$\begin{aligned} 0.008 \cdot 102 &= 0.816 \\ 0.816 \cdot 9.81 &= 8.005 \;\text{N} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Conclusion.** $F_{rr} \approx 8$ N. This is a remarkably small force — *yet* the manual-wheelchair user expends over **80 W of metabolic power** to overcome it at 1.4 m/s, because the human arm is a terribly inefficient engine ($\eta_p \approx 0.18$). ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Drive-Wheel Torque & Push-Rim Torque with Geared Hub ### 3.2.1 Problem Statement A geared-hub manual wheelchair has wheel radius $r_{wheel} = 0.30$ m, push-rim radius $r_{rim} = 0.33$ m, and an internal planetary gear ratio $N = 1.8 : 1$ (input:output, i.e., **one turn of the rim = 0.556 turns of the wheel**). If the chair must climb a $5°$ slope at constant speed, find the push-rim torque required. ### 3.2.1 Given - $m_{total} = 102$ kg (from §3.1) - $R_r = 0.008$, $r_{wheel} = 0.30$ m, $r_{rim} = 0.33$ m - $N = 1.8$, slope $\theta = 5°$ - Steady speed $\Rightarrow F_a = 0$ ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Compute rolling resistance force (from §3.1).** $$F_{rr} = 0.008 \cdot 102 \cdot 9.81 = 8.00 \;\text{N}$$ **Step 2 — Compute grade resistance force.** $$F_g = m_{total} \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$ Compute $\sin(5°)$: $$\sin(5°) \approx 0.0872$$ Then: $$\begin{aligned} F_g &= 102 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.0872 \\ &= 102 \cdot 9.81 = 1000.62 \\ 1000.62 \cdot 0.0872 &\approx 87.25 \;\text{N} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Total resistive force.** $$F_R = F_{rr} + F_g = 8.00 + 87.25 = 95.25 \;\text{N}$$ **Step 4 — Required drive-wheel torque.** $$T_{wheel} = F_R \cdot r_{wheel} = 95.25 \cdot 0.30 = 28.58 \;\text{N·m}$$ **Step 5 — Relate wheel torque to rim torque via the gear ratio.** With a geared hub, the wheel turns *slower* than the rim. To maintain the same output torque, the *rim torque* must be **lower** by a factor of $N$: $$T_{rim} = \frac{T_{wheel}}{N} = \frac{28.58}{1.8} = 15.88 \;\text{N·m}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to rim force for ergonomic check.** $$F_{rim} = \frac{T_{rim}}{r_{rim}} = \frac{15.88}{0.33} = 48.12 \;\text{N}$$ **Step 7 — Interpretation.** A force of $\approx 48$ N at the push rim is *well within* the comfortable capacity of an average adult shoulder (≈ 200 N) and even within the capacity of many elderly users (≈ 50–80 N). **Therefore, this chair is clinically appropriate** for climbing 5° slopes. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Total Mechanical Advantage of a Lever-Drive Wheelchair ### 3.3.1 Problem Statement A hemiplegic user employs a *push-lever* wheelchair. The lever arm is $L_{lever} = 0.40$ m, the connecting link transmits force to the drive-wheel sprocket at radius $r_{sp} = 0.08$ m, and the drive wheel is $r_{wheel} = 0.30$ m. Derive the total mechanical advantage $M_A$. ### 3.3.2 Derivation **Step 1 — Lever mechanical advantage.** $$M_{A,lever} = \frac{L_{lever}}{r_{sp}} = \frac{0.40}{0.08} = 5.0$$ **Step 2 — Sprocket-to-wheel mechanical advantage (a sprocket on a wheel gives no further ratio; it merely transmits torque).** $$M_{A,sprocket} = 1$$ **Step 3 — Total mechanical advantage.** $$M_{A,total} = M_{A,lever} \cdot M_{A,sprocket} = 5.0 \cdot 1 = 5.0$$ **Step 4 — Effective rim-equivalent force.** The user pushes with $F_{in} = 30$ N at the lever tip; the effective tangential force at the wheel contact patch is: $$F_{out} = M_{A,total} \cdot F_{in} = 5.0 \cdot 30 = 150 \;\text{N}$$ **Step 5 — Speed trade-off check.** By conservation of power: $v_{lever} = M_{A,total} \cdot v_{wheel}$. If $v_{wheel} = 1.0$ m/s, then $v_{lever} = 5.0$ m/s — meaning the lever tip must traverse **5 metres per second**, an obvious ergonomic burden. This is the classic *force-velocity trade-off* in lever drives, and **explains why lever drives are prescribed only for low-speed indoor use** (e.g., hemiplegic patients in their home). ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power & Energy Demand of a Manual Chair ### 3.4.1 Problem Statement The wheelchair of §3.1 cruises at $v = 1.4$ m/s on flat ground. Find the mechanical, electrical (in a power-assist configuration), and metabolic power demands. ### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Mechanical power.** $$P_{mech} = F_R \cdot v = F_{rr} \cdot v = 8.00 \cdot 1.4 = 11.2 \;\text{W}$$ **Step 2 — Electric input power (assume power-assist wheel with combined drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{dt} = 0.75$).** $$P_{elec} = \frac{P_{mech}}{\eta_{dt}} = \frac{11.2}{0.75} = 14.93 \;\text{W}$$ **Step 3 — Battery energy for a 1-hour drive.** $$E_{batt} = P_{elec} \cdot t = 14.93 \cdot 3600 = 53,748 \;\text{J} \approx 14.93 \;\text{Wh}$$ **Step 4 — Metabolic power of a manual user (propulsion efficiency $\eta_p = 0.18$).** $$P_{met} = \frac{P_{mech}}{\eta_p} = \frac{11.2}{0.18} = 62.2 \;\text{W}$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation.** A manual wheelchair user expends $\approx 62$ W just to cruise — this is the metabolic equivalent of a *brisk walk*. A power-assist wheel reduces this to the *resting* metabolic rate, which is why long-term manual users experience chronic shoulder pain and rotator-cuff injuries. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Propelling Structure The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional flow** of energy and signals through a generic propelling structure. This is the *master architecture* you should memorise for KTU 2024 exams. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[USER INPUT
Muscular Force F_in
or Joystick Voltage V_in] --> B[FORCE TRANSMISSION
Push-rim / Lever / Joystick Linkage] B --> C[GEAR REDUCTION STAGE
Planetary or Spur Gear Train
Ratio N colon 1] C --> D[DRIVE WHEEL TRACTION
Wheel-Ground Contact Patch
Slip check using mu] D --> E[ROLLING TRANSLATION
Chair Frame Linear Motion v_chair] E --> F[RESISTIVE LOAD
F_rr plus F_g plus F_a] F -. feedback via vibration and vestibular sense .-> A B --> G[ERGONOMIC SENSOR LAYER
Strain Gauges on Rim
Contact Angle phi_c] G -. user adjusts stroke pattern .-> A ``` ## 4.2 Classification of Propelling Structures ```mermaid flowchart TD P[PROPELLING STRUCTURE] --> P1[MANUAL DRIVE] P --> P2[POWER ASSIST] P --> P3[FULLY POWERED] P1 --> P1A[Direct Push Rim
MA equals 1.1 to 1.18] P1 --> P1B[Geared Push Rim
N equals 1.5 to 2.5] P1 --> P1C[Lever Drive
MA equals 3 to 6] P1 --> P1D[One Arm Drive OAD
Linked Double Rim] P2 --> P2A[Hub Motor Power Assist
e-motion Twion] P2 --> P2B[Push Rim Activated
Torque Sensor Based] P3 --> P3A[Rear Wheel Drive RWD] P3 --> P3B[Mid Wheel Drive MWD] P3 --> P3C[Front Wheel Drive FWD] ``` ## 4.3 Force / Torque Vector Diagram at the Push Rim ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Rim_Push_Cycle direction LR A0[Stroke Start
phi equals 0 deg] --> A1[Power Phase
phi equals 30 to 110 deg
T_rim positive] A1 --> A2[Recovery Phase
phi equals 110 to 360 deg
T_rim approx 0] A2 --> A0 end subgraph Force_Vectors direction TB B1[F_in at rim tangent
perpendicular to rim radius] B2[T_rim equals F_in times r_rim
vector along axle] B3[F_out at contact patch
tangential to wheel] B1 --> B2 B2 --> B3 end ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Matrix of Drive Types ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Direct Push Rim] -- Low MA, High Speed --> R[Best for Active User] B[Geared Push Rim] -- Med MA, Med Speed --> S[Best for Elderly] C[Lever Drive] -- High MA, Low Speed --> T[Best for Hemiplegic Indoor] D[Power Assist] -- Variable, Low Effort --> U[Best for Long Term Manual User] E[Fully Powered] -- Highest Effort Offload --> V[Best for High Lesion Tetraplegic] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Q1]** > *Define "propelling structure" in the context of assistive medical devices. List any **three** classifications of manual wheelchair propelling structures.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A propelling structure is the mechanical sub-system of a mobility aid that converts user input into the linear displacement of the device over the ground. (1 mark) It comprises force-transmission elements such as drive wheels, hand rims, push levers, gear hubs, drive motors, and kinematic linkages. (1 mark) Three classifications: (1) Direct push-rim drive, (2) Geared push-rim drive, (3) Lever drive, and (4) One-arm drive. (1 mark for any three) --- ### Question 2 > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q2]** > *What is **mechanical advantage** in a geared-hub wheelchair? Why is a high mechanical advantage prescribed for geriatric users?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Mechanical advantage $M_A$ is the ratio of output force at the wheel contact patch to the input force applied at the push rim. (1 mark) Mathematically: $M_A = N \cdot (d_{rim}/d_{wheel})$, where $N$ is the gear ratio. (1 mark) A high $M_A$ reduces the force required at the push rim at the cost of lower maximum speed (force-velocity trade-off). Geriatric users have limited shoulder strength but typically do not require high speeds, so a high $M_A$ chair lets them climb ramps and traverse carpets that would otherwise stall a direct-drive chair. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks) ### Question A (Choice 1) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper ESE Module 2 Q7, 14 Marks]** > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a generic propelling structure in a manual wheelchair. Label all six energy-flow stages. **[7 Marks]** > **(b)** A manual wheelchair has a geared hub with $N = 2.0$, push-rim diameter $d_{rim} = 660$ mm, drive-wheel diameter $d_{wheel} = 600$ mm, and total mass $m = 95$ kg. Calculate (i) the total mechanical advantage, (ii) the rolling-resistance force on a tiled floor with $R_r = 0.01$, and (iii) the required push-rim force to maintain a steady $4$ km/h cruise on flat ground. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks** 1. **Energy input stage (1 mark):** User applies muscular force $F_{in}$ to push rim or lever. 2. **Force transmission stage (1 mark):** Hand-rim → spokes → wheel hub (or lever → link rod → sprocket). 3. **Gear reduction stage (1 mark):** Planetary or spur gears with ratio $N$, trading speed for torque. 4. **Drive-wheel traction stage (1 mark):** Wheel–ground contact patch; tangential force $F_{out} = T_{wheel}/r_{wheel}$ must exceed $F_R$. 5. **Rolling translation stage (1 mark):** Chair frame translates with $v_{chair} = \omega_{wheel} \cdot r_{wheel}$. 6. **Feedback stage (1 mark):** Tactile/vestibular feedback closes the loop to the user's next stroke. 7. **Schematic clarity (1 mark):** Awarded for a clean block diagram with arrows showing energy flow. **Part (b) — 7 marks** **(i) Total mechanical advantage (2 marks)** $$M_{A,total} = N \cdot \frac{d_{rim}}{d_{wheel}} = 2.0 \cdot \frac{660}{600} = 2.0 \cdot 1.10 = 2.20$$ **(ii) Rolling-resistance force (2 marks)** $$F_{rr} = R_r \cdot m \cdot g = 0.01 \cdot 95 \cdot 9.81$$ $$\begin{aligned} 0.01 \cdot 95 &= 0.95 \\ 0.95 \cdot 9.81 &= 9.32 \;\text{N} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Required push-rim force at $v = 4$ km/h (3 marks)** On flat ground at steady speed, $F_R = F_{rr} = 9.32$ N (note: $F_a = 0$ and $F_g = 0$). Tangential force at the wheel contact patch: $$F_{out} = F_{rr} = 9.32 \;\text{N}$$ By definition of mechanical advantage: $$F_{rim} = \frac{F_{out}}{M_{A,total}} = \frac{9.32}{2.20} = 4.24 \;\text{N}$$ **Comment:** Note that the speed of 4 km/h = 1.11 m/s is not required to find the *force* at steady state — speed would only enter if we wanted *power*. This is a common student trap. --- ### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper ESE Module 2 Q8, 14 Marks]** > **(a)** Compare **direct push-rim**, **lever-drive**, and **power-assist** propelling structures in terms of (i) mechanical advantage, (ii) maximum speed, (iii) user metabolic cost, and (iv) clinical indication. **[7 Marks]** > **(b)** A hemiplegic patient uses a lever-drive wheelchair with a lever arm $L_{lever} = 0.42$ m and a sprocket radius $r_{sp} = 0.07$ m driving a wheel of radius $r_{wheel} = 0.32$ m. If the patient can push with $F_{in} = 35$ N, calculate (i) the mechanical advantage, (ii) the resulting tangential force at the wheel contact patch, and (iii) the velocity ratio if the chair moves at $0.6$ m/s (i.e., find lever-tip speed). **[7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks** Present the answer in the form of a comparison table (1 mark for table format). Then fill in 6 cells, with each correctly populated cell worth 1 mark: | Parameter | Direct Push-Rim | Lever Drive | Power-Assist | |-----------|-----------------|-------------|--------------| | Mechanical advantage $M_A$ | 1.10 to 1.18 | 3 to 6 | Variable, typically 1.0 to 4.0 | | Maximum speed $v_{max}$ | High (8 to 10 km/h athletic) | Low (3 to 5 km/h) | Medium (6 km/h) | | User metabolic cost | High (chronic injury risk) | Medium-High (low speed) | Low (motor assists) | | Clinical indication | Active paraplegic | Hemiplegic, indoor | Long-term manual user, shoulder preservation | **Part (b) — 7 marks** **(i) Mechanical advantage (2 marks)** The lever provides $M_{A,lever} = L_{lever}/r_{sp}$, and the sprocket-on-wheel adds $M_A = 1$: $$\begin{aligned} M_{A,total} &= \frac{L_{lever}}{r_{sp}} \cdot 1 \\ &= \frac{0.42}{0.07} = 6.0 \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Tangential force at the contact patch (2 marks)** $$F_{out} = M_{A,total} \cdot F_{in} = 6.0 \cdot 35 = 210 \;\text{N}$$ **(iii) Lever-tip speed (3 marks)** By conservation of power (in an ideal mechanism): $F_{in} \cdot v_{lever} = F_{out} \cdot v_{chair}$. Therefore: $$v_{lever} = M_{A,total} \cdot v_{chair} = 6.0 \cdot 0.6 = 3.6 \;\text{m/s}$$ **Comment:** A lever tip speed of 3.6 m/s is at the **upper edge** of comfortable arm movement for an elderly user, which confirms the clinical decision to use a lever drive *only* for low-speed indoor ambulation. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting units.** Always write N, N·m, m/s, or W after every numerical answer. A correct number without a unit is treated as a *conceptual error* by KTU examiners. > 2. **Confusing the direction of $N$.** If "one turn of the rim gives 0.5 turns of the wheel", then $N = 2.0$ and $T_{rim} = T_{wheel} / N$. Many students invert this. > 3. **Using $W = mg$ without the rolling-resistance coefficient.** $F_{rr} \ne m \cdot g$. The correct form is $F_{rr} = R_r \cdot m \cdot g$. > 4. **Omitting the $F_g$ term on a slope.** Even a $3°$ slope contributes $\approx 5\%$ of body weight, which dominates over $F_{rr}$ for many indoor ramps. > 5. **Writing $v_{rim} = v_{wheel}$.** The rim is *bigger* than the wheel, so $v_{rim} > v_{wheel}$. Many students forget this and lose 1 mark per sub-question. > 6. **Mixing up "geared hub" and "geared motor".** A geared hub in a *manual* chair is purely mechanical (planetary gears). A geared motor in a *powered* chair is an additional gear stage *after* the motor. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 OEEBT722 Module 2** - [x] A **propelling structure** is the *engine + drivetrain* of a mobility aid; it converts user input into ground-rolling motion. - [x] **Three clinical classes**: direct push-rim, geared push-rim, lever drive — and two hybrid classes: power-assist and fully powered. - [x] The **rolling-resistance force** is $F_{rr} = R_r \cdot m_{total} \cdot g$ — *never* $m \cdot g$. - [x] The **grade force** is $F_g = m_{total} \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$. - [x] **Total mechanical advantage** = $N \cdot (d_{rim}/d_{wheel})$ for a geared hub, and $= L_{lever}/r_{sp}$ for a lever drive. - [x] **Force-velocity trade-off**: higher $M_A$ ⇒ lower rim force, but lower maximum speed (and higher lever-tip speed). - [x] **Propulsion efficiency** of a human arm on a manual chair is **15 – 25 %** — this is the *root cause* of chronic shoulder injuries in long-term manual users. - [x] **Power-assist wheels** reduce metabolic cost to the resting level while preserving the manual chair's form factor — gold standard for ambulant long-term users. - [x] **Push-rim contact angle** $\phi_c$ is **90° – 120°** per ISO 7176-5 — outside this range the user cannot generate useful torque. - [x] **Efficiencies stack multiplicatively** in a powered drivetrain: $\eta_{total} = \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{chain} \cdot \eta_{bearing} \cdot \eta_{tire}$. - [x] For a hemiplegic user, the **one-arm drive (OAD)** with linked double rim is the standard prescription — *not* a lever drive (which is for low-speed indoor only). - [x] **Slip threshold**: $\mu \ge F_R / W$; with $\mu \approx 0.7$ (PU rubber on dry tile) and $F_R \approx 0.1 \, W$, slip is *never* the failure mode — the user runs out of strength first. - [x] **Always** state units (N, N·m, m/s, W) in the KTU 2024 answer script — losing a unit = losing the mark.

topics.title: Specialized Bases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Specialized Bases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Specialized Bases** in the context of Assistive Medical Devices (KTU OEEBT722, Module 2) refer to the engineered foundational platforms upon which mobility, seating, positioning, and rehabilitative assistive technologies are constructed. These bases provide the **structural framework, propulsion interface, stability core, and load-bearing chassis** required to support the user, the seating system, and all associated electronic/control subsystems.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, specialized bases primarily encompass:

1. **Power Wheelchair Bases (PWB)** – motorized chassis with drive-wheel configurations
2. **Manual Wheelchair Bases (MWB)** – rigid, folding, and ultralight frames
3. **Pediatric/Stroller Bases** – designed for children and special pediatric needs
4. **Standing Wheelchair Bases** – for assisted standing posture
5. **Scooter Bases (Mobility Scooters)** – three or four-wheeled platforms
6. **Tilt-in-Space and Recline Bases** – for pressure relief and postural management
7. **Bariatric Bases** – reinforced platforms for higher weight capacities

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A specialized base is NOT just a frame. It is an integrated electromechanical system that defines the **kinematic behavior, dynamic stability, drive characteristics, and terrain adaptability** of the entire assistive mobility device.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Think of a specialized base as the **"chassis of a car"** — but designed for a person instead of cargo.

Imagine a recliner chair that needs wheels, motors, batteries, and a steering joystick. The **specialized base** is the metal/carbon-fiber skeleton underneath the seat that holds everything together. It determines:

- How the chair **turns** (like the difference between a shopping cart and a forklift)
- How **stable** it is on a slope
- How **far** it can travel on one charge
- How **heavy** a person it can safely carry

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** If the seating system is the "cabin" of a vehicle, the specialized base is the "chassis + drivetrain + wheels + suspension" — all engineered as one unit to carry a human occupant safely across varied environments (home, hospital, street, ramp).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Engineering Constants

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Range | Standard Reference |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|-------------------|
| Load Capacity | $M_{max}$ | **100 kg – 250 kg** (bariatric up to 400 kg) | ISO 7176-5 |
| Base Width | $W_b$ | **0.55 m – 0.75 m** | ISO 7176-5 |
| Base Length | $L_b$ | **0.80 m – 1.20 m** | ISO 7176-5 |
| Ground Clearance | $h_g$ | **50 mm – 150 mm** | Manufacturer spec |
| Turning Radius | $R_t$ | **0.45 m – 1.30 m** | ISO 7176-5 |
| Static Stability Angle | $\theta_s$ | **≥ 9° (forward, rear, lateral)** | ISO 7176-1 |
| Maximum Safe Slope | $\alpha$ | **6° – 10°** (dynamic) | ISO 7176-2 |
| Speed (PWB) | $v_{max}$ | **6 km/h – 10 km/h** | ISO 7176-6 |
| Range (PWB) | $D$ | **15 km – 40 km** | Battery dependent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants Used Throughout:**
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$
> - Standard occupant mass: $M_{user} = 75 \, kg$ (ISO 7176 default)
> - Coefficient of friction (rubber tire on dry concrete): $\mu \approx 0.8$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Specialized Bases — Structural Logic

### A. Power Wheelchair Bases (PWB) — Drive Configurations

The **drive-wheel position** defines the kinematic behavior. KTU examiners frequently test this concept.

- **Rear-Wheel Drive (RWD):** Drive wheels at the back, casters at the front.
  - *Behavior:* Stable at high speeds, large turning radius, tends to "fishtail" on sharp turns.
  - *Use case:* Outdoor use, high-speed applications.
- **Mid-Wheel Drive (MWD):** Drive wheels in the center, casters front and rear (six-wheel configuration).
  - *Behavior:* **Smallest turning radius**, intuitive joystick control, highly maneuverable indoors.
  - *Use case:* Indoor/home use, tight spaces (most popular modern configuration).
- **Front-Wheel Drive (FWD):** Drive wheels at the front, casters at the rear.
  - *Behavior:* Excellent curb climbing, good obstacle negotiation, can feel "pulled" on slopes.
  - *Use case:* Outdoor rough terrain, ramp climbing.

### B. Manual Wheelchair Bases (MWB)

- **Rigid Frame Base:** Fixed frame, no folding mechanism. Lightest, most energy-efficient (less frame flex).
- **Folding Frame Base (Cross-brace):** X-frame hinge allows folding. Heavier, more flex, but transportable.
- **Tilt-in-Space Base:** Seat-to-back angle is fixed, but the entire seat frame tilts backward to relieve pressure.
- **Reclining Base:** Backrest angle changes relative to seat (for users with limited hip flexion).
- **Standing Base:** Powered or manual mechanism lifts user to standing posture.

### C. Specialized Pediatric Bases

Designed for children with growth adjustability, lower seat heights, and crash-tested for vehicle transport (WC19/ISO 7176-19).

### D. Scooter Bases

Three-wheeled (tight turning) or four-wheeled (stable, outdoor) chassis with a tiller steering column.

## 2.2 Engineering Design Requirements (Why and How)

**Why a specialized base exists:**
- To translate user input (joystick, attendant, hand-rim) into controlled motion
- To maintain static and dynamic stability on slopes and uneven terrain
- To distribute load across wheels to prevent tipping
- To provide structural mounting for batteries, motors, controllers, and seating

**How it achieves this:**
- **Drive train** (motors + gears + wheels) converts electrical energy to mechanical motion
- **Caster wheels** provide passive stability and omnidirectional rolling
- **Anti-tip wheels** prevent rearward overturning on acceleration
- **Suspension system** (springs/dampers) absorbs shock on uneven terrain

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Equations for Board Examinations**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Total Load on Base | $M_{total} = M_{user} + M_{seat} + M_{components}$ | User + seating + electronics | kg |
| 2 | Drive Force Required (slope) | $F_d = M_{total} \cdot g \cdot (\sin\alpha + \mu \cos\alpha)$ | Slope angle, friction coeff | N |
| 3 | Motor Torque | $\tau = F_d \cdot r_w$ | Wheel radius $r_w$ | N·m |
| 4 | Motor Power | $P_m = F_d \cdot v$ | Velocity $v$ | W |
| 5 | Battery Energy | $E_{bat} = V \cdot C$ | Voltage V, Capacity C | Wh |
| 6 | Range | $D = \frac{E_{bat} \cdot \eta}{F_d} \cdot \frac{1}{1000}$ (if $E$ in kJ) | Efficiency $\eta$ | km |
| 7 | Turning Radius (MWD) | $R_t = \frac{L_b}{2}$ (approx.) | Base length | m |
| 8 | Static Stability (Tipping) | $\theta_{tip} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{d_{cg}}{h_{cg}}\right)$ | CG distance, height | degrees |
| 9 | Dynamic Braking Distance | $d_b = \frac{v^2}{2\mu g}$ | Velocity, friction | m |
| 10 | Hill Hold Torque | $\tau_{hold} = M_{total} \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot r_w$ | Slope angle | N·m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by biomedical engineers in the design of motorized wheelchairs, rehabilitation robots, and hospital transport devices. They are also essential for **CE Marking and ISO 7176 compliance testing**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Rehabilitation Engineering:** Customization of bases for cerebral palsy, spinal cord injury, and muscular dystrophy patients
- **Hospital Mobility:** Transport chair bases with attendant-driven push handles
- **Sports Assistive Devices:** Specialized racing wheelchair bases (3-wheeled, cambered) for Paralympic athletes
- **Disaster Response:** All-terrain powered bases with tracked or multi-wheel configurations
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Drive Force on an Inclined Surface

When a powered wheelchair base moves up a slope of angle $\alpha$ with total loaded mass $M_{total}$, two resistance forces oppose motion:

1. **Gravitational component** along the slope: $F_g = M_{total} \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha$
2. **Rolling friction** (opposing motion): $F_f = \mu \cdot M_{total} \cdot g \cdot \cos\alpha$

The drive wheels must overcome both:

$$F_d = F_g + F_f$$

Substituting:

$$F_d = M_{total} \cdot g \cdot \sin\alpha + \mu \cdot M_{total} \cdot g \cdot \cos\alpha$$

Factoring out $M_{total} \cdot g$:

$$F_d = M_{total} \cdot g \cdot (\sin\alpha + \mu \cos\alpha)$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-typical):**
Given: $M_{total} = 120$ kg, $\alpha = 8°$, $\mu = 0.02$ (rubber on concrete, low-speed rolling), $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

Step 1: Compute $\sin 8°$ and $\cos 8°$:
$\sin 8° \approx 0.1392$, $\cos 8° \approx 0.9903$

Step 2: Substitute into equation:
$F_d = 120 \times 9.81 \times (0.1392 + 0.02 \times 0.9903)$

Step 3: Evaluate inner bracket:
$0.1392 + 0.0198 = 0.1590$

Step 4: Multiply:
$F_d = 120 \times 9.81 \times 0.1590 = 1177.2 \times 0.1590 \approx 187.2 \, N$

> **Result:** The base requires approximately **187.2 N of drive force** to climb an 8° slope with a 120 kg total load.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Motor Torque and Power Sizing

The drive force is delivered at the wheel-ground contact point. The torque required at the drive wheel axle is:

$$\tau = F_d \cdot r_w$$

where $r_w$ is the drive wheel radius.

**Numerical Example (KTU-typical):**
Given: $F_d = 187.2 \, N$ (from above), $r_w = 0.15$ m (typical 12-inch drive wheel)

$$\tau = 187.2 \times 0.15 = 28.08 \, N \cdot m$$

**Power required** at maximum safe speed $v = 6$ km/h $= 1.667$ m/s:

$$P_m = F_d \cdot v$$

$$P_m = 187.2 \times 1.667 \approx 312.1 \, W$$

> **Result:** Each motor must deliver at least **28 N·m of torque and 312 W of mechanical power**. With a typical gear-motor efficiency of 75%, the **electrical power input** is:
> $P_{elec} = P_m / \eta = 312.1 / 0.75 \approx 416 \, W$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Battery Range Estimation

For two 12V, 50Ah batteries (typical PWB configuration):

$$E_{bat} = V \cdot C = 2 \times 12 \times 50 = 1200 \, Wh = 1.2 \, kWh$$

In kilojoules: $E = 1.2 \times 3600 = 4320 \, kJ$

Assuming flat ground, $\alpha = 0°$, $F_d = \mu \cdot M_{total} \cdot g = 0.02 \times 120 \times 9.81 = 23.5 \, N$ (rolling resistance only).

Total range:

$$D = \frac{E_{bat} \cdot \eta}{F_d \cdot 1000} \quad (\text{in km, with E in kJ})$$

$$D = \frac{4320 \times 0.75}{23.5 \times 1000} = \frac{3240}{23500} \approx 0.138 \, \text{km}$$

This is unrealistically low — recheck with practical consumption. In practice, **specific energy consumption** of ~25 Wh/km is typical for PWB:

$$D = \frac{E_{bat}}{\text{specific consumption}} = \frac{1200 \, Wh}{25 \, Wh/km} = 48 \, km$$

> **Result:** With two 12V/50Ah batteries, the practical range is approximately **48 km** under ideal conditions.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Static Stability (Tipping Angle)

For a power wheelchair base, the **rearward tipping moment** during acceleration or on a forward uphill slope is:

$$\theta_{tip} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{d_{cg}}{h_{cg}}\right)$$

where:
- $d_{cg}$ = horizontal distance from rear anti-tip wheel axis to combined center of gravity
- $h_{cg}$ = height of combined CG above ground

**Numerical Example (KTU-typical):**
Given: $d_{cg} = 0.40$ m, $h_{cg} = 0.50$ m

$$\theta_{tip} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.40}{0.50}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.8) \approx 38.7°$$

> **Result:** The base will theoretically tip rearward only at slopes steeper than **38.7°**, far above the operational limit of 10°, providing a **large safety margin**. This is why **anti-tip wheels are critical**: they lower the effective tipping point and add redundancy.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Braking Distance

The dynamic stopping distance on flat ground, neglecting aerodynamic drag, is given by work-energy equivalence:

$$\frac{1}{2} M_{total} v^2 = F_{brake} \cdot d_b = \mu M_{total} g \cdot d_b$$

Solving for $d_b$:

$$d_b = \frac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-typical):**
Given: $v = 6$ km/h $= 1.667$ m/s, $\mu = 0.8$ (locked wheel on dry concrete), $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

$$d_b = \frac{(1.667)^2}{2 \times 0.8 \times 9.81} = \frac{2.779}{15.696} \approx 0.177 \, m$$

> **Result:** At 6 km/h on dry concrete, the theoretical locked-wheel braking distance is only **17.7 cm** — but on wet surfaces ($\mu \approx 0.3$), this grows to **47 cm**, which is why **electronic regenerative braking with motor controllers** is preferred over mechanical locks.

## 3.6 Summary of Analytical Matrix

| # | Derivation | Key Output | KTU Cognitive Level | Likely Marks |
|---|-----------|-----------|---------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Drive force on slope | Force in N | Apply | 3–7 |
| 2 | Motor torque/power sizing | Torque, Power | Apply / Analyze | 7 |
| 3 | Battery range | Distance in km | Analyze | 7 |
| 4 | Static stability | Angle in degrees | Apply | 3–7 |
| 5 | Braking distance | Distance in m | Apply | 3–7 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
