<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**Define the term "Specialized Base" in the context of assistive medical devices. List any two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
A specialized base is the engineered structural platform of an assistive mobility device that integrates the frame, drive system, wheels, and stability elements to support the user, seating system, and control electronics.

**Examples (any two):**
1. Mid-Wheel Drive Power Wheelchair Base
2. Tilt-in-Space Manual Wheelchair Base
3. Pediatric Stroller Base
4. Bariatric Power Wheelchair Base

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between Mid-Wheel Drive (MWD) and Rear-Wheel Drive (RWD) power wheelchair bases in terms of turning radius and indoor maneuverability.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | MWD Base | RWD Base |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| Drive wheel position | Center of base | Rear of base |
| Turning radius | **Smallest** (≈ 0.5 m) | **Larger** (≥ 0.9 m) |
| Indoor maneuverability | Excellent | Moderate |
| High-speed stability | Moderate | Excellent |
| Use case | Indoor, tight spaces | Outdoor, open areas |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice — 14 Marks Each

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **major subsystems of a Power Wheelchair Base** and their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A power wheelchair base has a total loaded mass of **130 kg** and must climb a **6° slope**. The drive wheel radius is **0.16 m**, and the coefficient of rolling friction is **0.025**. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
   (i) The required drive force
   (ii) The motor torque at the drive wheel
   (iii) The mechanical power required at a speed of **5 km/h**

**Model Solution (a):**

Refer to **Section 4.2** for the block diagram. Major subsystems and functions:

1. **Battery Pack** – Stores electrical energy (typically 24V DC from two 12V batteries)
2. **Motor Controller** – Receives joystick input, modulates current to drive motors using PWM
3. **Drive Motors (Left/Right)** – Convert electrical energy to mechanical rotation; differential speed between left/right wheels enables steering
4. **Drive Wheels** – Provide traction and propulsion
5. **Caster Wheels (Front/Rear)** – Provide passive support and omnidirectional motion
6. **Anti-Tip Wheels** – Prevent rearward tipping on acceleration/uphill
7. **Chassis Frame** – Rigid structure holding all subsystems and providing seating interface
8. **Joystick/Input Device** – User interface for velocity and direction control

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $M_{total} = 130$ kg, $\alpha = 6°$, $r_w = 0.16$ m, $\mu = 0.025$, $v = 5$ km/h, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$

**(i) Drive Force:**

$$F_d = M_{total} \cdot g \cdot (\sin\alpha + \mu \cos\alpha)$$

$\sin 6° \approx 0.1045$, $\cos 6° \approx 0.9945$

$$F_d = 130 \times 9.81 \times (0.1045 + 0.025 \times 0.9945)$$

$$F_d = 1275.3 \times (0.1045 + 0.0249) = 1275.3 \times 0.1294 \approx 165.0 \, N$$

**(ii) Motor Torque:**

$$\tau = F_d \cdot r_w = 165.0 \times 0.16 = 26.4 \, N \cdot m$$

**(iii) Mechanical Power** at $v = 5$ km/h $= 5/3.6 = 1.389$ m/s:

$$P_m = F_d \cdot v = 165.0 \times 1.389 \approx 229.2 \, W$$

> **Final Answers:** (i) $F_d \approx 165$ N, (ii) $\tau \approx 26.4$ N·m, (iii) $P_m \approx 229$ W

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Alternative]

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Rigid Frame, Folding Cross-Brace, and Tilt-in-Space** manual wheelchair bases. Mention at least three parameters for comparison. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pediatric specialized base has the combined center of gravity located at $d_{cg} = 0.35$ m horizontally from the rear anti-tip wheel axis and $h_{cg} = 0.45$ m above ground. Calculate the **theoretical rearward tipping angle** and comment on the safety margin if the maximum safe operating slope is **8°**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

| Parameter | Rigid Frame | Folding Cross-Brace | Tilt-in-Space |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|----------------|
| Weight | **Lightest** (ultralight 5–8 kg) | Heavier (10–15 kg) | Heaviest (15–25 kg) |
| Portability | Low (non-folding) | **High** (folds for transport) | Low |
| Energy Efficiency | **Highest** (rigid, less flex) | Lower (frame flex wastes energy) | Moderate |
| Adjustability | Moderate | Moderate | **Highest** (tilt 0°–45°) |
| Clinical Use | Active users, sports | Travel, occasional use | Postural management, CP, pressure relief |
| Durability | **High** (few joints) | Moderate (hinge wear) | Moderate |

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $d_{cg} = 0.35$ m, $h_{cg} = 0.45$ m

**Tipping angle:**

$$\theta_{tip} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{d_{cg}}{h_{cg}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.35}{0.45}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.7778) \approx 37.9°$$

**Safety margin:**

$$\Delta\theta = \theta_{tip} - \alpha_{max} = 37.9° - 8° = 29.9°$$

> **Comment:** The base has a **safety margin of approximately 30°** above the maximum operating slope. This is a substantial margin (≈ 4.7× the operating angle), confirming the design is safe for pediatric use. However, the designer must still account for **dynamic effects** (sudden acceleration, uneven terrain), which can reduce this margin. **Anti-tip wheels** should engage before the effective tipping point is reached, typically at **20°–25°**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** $v = 5$ km/h must be converted to m/s. Marks lost for leaving km/h in the power equation.
> 2. **Wrong formula selection:** Using $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ instead of $P = F \cdot v$ for translation. Both are correct, but if $\omega$ is not given, use $P = F \cdot v$.
> 3. **Confusing $\alpha$ and $\theta$:** Slope angle is measured from horizontal, not vertical. Use $\sin\alpha$ correctly.
> 4. **Ignoring mass of components:** Forgetting to add the mass of batteries (~10 kg), motors (~5 kg), and seat (~8 kg) to the user mass.
> 5. **Stability derivation shortcut:** Writing $\theta_{tip} = d/h$ without the $\tan^{-1}$ — KTU examiners specifically look for the inverse tangent.
> 6. **No block diagram in part (a):** Many students describe subsystems in prose without a diagram — KTU 2024 Scheme requires a neat labeled block diagram for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Specialized Bases (Module 2)**

- ☐ **Definition:** A specialized base is the **integrated structural, propulsion, and stability platform** of an assistive mobility device.
- ☐ **Power Wheelchair Drive Configurations:** **RWD, MWD, FWD** — know turning radius, stability, and best-use case for each.
- ☐ **MWD** has the **smallest turning radius** and is **best for indoor use**.
- ☐ **RWD** has the **highest high-speed stability** and is **best for outdoor use**.
- ☐ **FWD** has the **best curb climbing** capability.
- ☐ **Manual bases:** Rigid, Folding, Tilt-in-Space, Reclining, Standing — know clinical use.
- ☐ **Key drive force equation:** $F_d = M_{total} g (\sin\alpha + \mu \cos\alpha)$
- ☐ **Motor torque:** $\tau = F_d \cdot r_w$
- ☐ **Motor power:** $P_m = F_d \cdot v$ (use v in m/s, not km/h)
- ☐ **Battery range:** $D = E_{bat} / \text{specific consumption} \approx 25$ Wh/km typical
- ☐ **Tipping angle:** $\theta_{tip} = \tan^{-1}(d_{cg} / h_{cg})$
- ☐ **Braking distance:** $d_b = v^2 / (2\mu g)$
- ☐ **Standards to memorize:** **ISO 7176-1, 7176-2, 7176-5, 7176-6, 7176-19** (wheelchair standards)
- ☐ **Anti-tip wheels** are mandatory safety features on powered bases.
- ☐ **Bariatric bases** are engineered for **load capacity > 150 kg** up to 400 kg.
- ☐ **Pediatric bases** must comply with **WC19 / ISO 7176-19** for vehicle transport safety.
- ☐ **Always include a block diagram** when KTU questions ask for system description — partial marks are guaranteed for a labeled diagram even if explanation is incomplete.
- ☐ **Unit conversion is the #1 mistake** — KTU examiners specifically deduct for mixed unit usage in numerical problems.

> **End of Module 2 — Specialized Bases Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart Wheelchairs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Smart Wheelchairs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smart Wheelchair (SW):** A *motorized mobility platform* integrated with **sensors, embedded controllers, and intelligent algorithms** that augments or replaces conventional manual/power wheelchair operation. It is classified as a *Class I/II Active Medical Device* (per ISO 13485 and IEC 60601-1) intended for users with severe motor, cognitive, or sensory impairments, providing **semi-autonomous or fully autonomous navigation, obstacle avoidance, and multi-modal human–machine interfacing.**

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **OEEBT722 (Assistive Medical Devices)**, a smart wheelchair is a cyber-physical system that bridges the gap between a *conventional electric wheelchair* and a *mobile robot*, layered with a patient-centric assistive interface.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Car That Drives Itself, but Cares About You"**
>
> Imagine you are sitting in a **self-driving car**, but the steering wheel has been replaced by your **voice, your eye movements, or even a small head-tilt sensor**. The car uses **cameras and ultrasonic sensors** (like a bat's echo) to "see" walls, furniture, and people. If it senses an obstacle, it *automatically stops or reroutes*, even if you accidentally tell it to move forward. This is essentially what a smart wheelchair does — only it is designed to carry a single human being safely through indoor corridors, hospital wards, and home environments.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Operating Voltage:** **24 V DC** (typical for wheelchair traction motors).
- **Maximum Safe Speed:** **≤ 6 km/h** (≈ 1.67 m/s) per ISO 7176-1 indoor mobility standards.
- **Sensor Range:** Ultrasonic **0.2 m – 4.0 m**; IR **0.05 m – 1.5 m**; LiDAR **0.1 m – 30 m**.
- **Payload Capacity:** **110 kg – 150 kg** (standard adult).
- **Battery:** **2 × 12 V, 35–75 Ah** sealed lead-acid or LiFePO₄.
- **MCU Clock:** Typically **72 MHz – 180 MHz** (ARM Cortex-M3/M4).

### 1.4 Why Smart Wheelchairs? — Clinical Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Spinal Cord Injury (SCI):** C4–C5 tetraplegia eliminates joystick control → need for *sip-and-puff*, *head-array*, or *BCI*.
> - **ALS / Motor Neuron Disease:** Progressive loss of fine motor control → *eye-gaze* or *voice* required.
> - **Cerebral Palsy & Multiple Sclerosis:** Uncontrolled tremor → *shared autonomy* with collision-override.
> - **Elderly Users:** Cognitive decline → *guided navigation* between waypoints.

The smart wheelchair therefore serves as an **enabling technology** that restores **Activities of Daily Living (ADL)** and **Instrumental ADL (IADL)** independence.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Block Decomposition

A smart wheelchair is a layered **Sense → Think → Act** system. Below is the textbook engineering breakdown:

**Layer 1 — Perceptual Layer (Sensors)**
- *Exteroceptive:* Ultrasonic (HC-SR04), Infrared (Sharp GP2Y0A21), LiDAR (RPLIDAR A1), RGB-D cameras (Intel RealSense), IMU (MPU6050).
- *Proprioceptive:* Wheel encoders, motor current sensors, gyroscope, accelerometer.
- *Physiological (optional):* Galvanic Skin Response (GSR), SpO₂, ECG for fatigue monitoring.

**Layer 2 — Cognitive Layer (Controller)**
- Microcontroller: **STM32F4**, **ESP32**, or **Raspberry Pi 4** for higher-level SLAM.
- Algorithms: *Obstacle Avoidance (VFH, Dynamic Window Approach)*, *SLAM (Hector, Gmapping)*, *Speech Recognition (CMU Sphinx, Google STT)*.

**Layer 3 — Actuation Layer (Drivers)**
- DC geared motors with **H-bridge motor drivers (VNH2SP30, BTS7960)**.
- Wheelchair kinematics: typically **Differential Drive (2-WD)** or **Omnidirectional (4-Mecanum)**.

**Layer 4 — Human–Machine Interface (HMI)**
- Modes: **Joystick → Voice → Eye-gaze → Head-control → BCI → Shared-Autonomy Touchscreen**.

### 2.2 Why Each Layer Matters — The "Why & How"

- **Why sensors?** To convert physical phenomena (distance, light, sound) into electrical signals for the controller.
- **Why a microcontroller?** To execute real-time decision algorithms (e.g., stop within 200 ms of obstacle detection).
- **Why motor drivers?** Microcontroller GPIO pins cannot supply the **5 A – 20 A** needed by traction motors; H-bridges act as current amplifiers with direction control.
- **Why HMI?** To accommodate the *residual abilities* of each user — the core philosophy of assistive technology.

### 2.3 Kinematic Modeling — Differential Drive

For a differential-drive wheelchair with wheel separation $2L$ and wheel radius $r$:

$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$$

$$\omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$$

where $v$ is linear velocity, $\omega$ is angular velocity, and $\omega_R, \omega_L$ are the right and left wheel angular speeds (rad/s).

The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** lies along the axle extension, at distance $R$ from the wheel midpoint:

$$R = \frac{L(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{(\omega_R - \omega_L)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special case — Zero-Radius Turn:** When $\omega_R = -\omega_L$, then $R = 0$, which is the basis of *in-place rotation* — a critical maneuver in tight hospital corridors.

### 2.4 Sensor Coverage Geometry

For an array of $n$ ultrasonic sensors placed at angles $\theta_i$ on a circular bumper of radius $d$ centered at the wheelchair's geometric center, the **detection footprint** is:

$$A_{coverage} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \int_{\theta_i - \Delta\theta}^{\theta_i + \Delta\theta} r_{max}^2 \, d\theta$$

where $\Delta\theta$ is the half-beam angle (≈ 15° for HC-SR04) and $r_{max}$ is the maximum reliable detection range (typically 4 m).

### 2.5 Obstacle Avoidance — The "Bubble" Safety Zone

A **Dynamic Safety Bubble** of radius $R_s$ is enforced around the wheelchair:

$$R_s = R_{chair} + v \cdot t_{reaction} + d_{braking}$$

where:
- $R_{chair}$ = half-width of wheelchair (≈ 0.35 m),
- $t_{reaction}$ = controller latency (≈ 0.1 s),
- $d_{braking}$ = stopping distance under maximum deceleration.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Velocity | $v = \tfrac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$ | m/s | 0 – 1.5 m/s |
| Angular Velocity | $\omega = \tfrac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$ | rad/s | –2 to +2 rad/s |
| ICR Radius | $R = \tfrac{L(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$ | m | 0 – ∞ |
| Safety Bubble | $R_s = R_{chair} + v t_{reaction} + d_{braking}$ | m | 0.5 – 1.2 m |
| Stopping Distance | $d_{braking} = \tfrac{v^2}{2 a_{max}}$ | m | 0.05 – 0.3 m |
| Beam Footprint | $A = r_{max}^2 \cdot 2\Delta\theta$ | m² | 0.45 m² (per sensor) |
| Motor Power | $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ | W | 100 – 300 W per motor |
| Battery Energy | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Wh | 35 – 100 Wh |
| LiDAR Resolution | $\theta_{res} = \tfrac{360°}{N_{samples}}$ | deg | 0.25° – 1° |
| SLAM Map Update | $\mu_{new} = g(\mu_{old}, u_t, z_t)$ | – | Probabilistic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** These equations are used in **ROS (Robot Operating System)** nodes like *dwa_local_planner* and *move_base* to navigate hospital corridors autonomously.

### 2.7 Engineering Trade-offs (High-Yield for KTU Viva)

- **More sensors → higher reliability but higher cost, weight, and battery drain.**
- **Higher processing → better SLAM but increased latency and heat.**
- **More autonomy → less user control → potential safety liability under FDA/IEC 62304.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stopping Distance Under Maximum Braking

**Statement:** Show that the minimum safe stopping distance from a velocity $v$ under maximum deceleration $a_{max}$ is given by $d_{braking} = \tfrac{v^2}{2 a_{max}}$.

**Step 1 — Start with Newton's second equation of motion.**
The wheelchair decelerates uniformly, so we use the kinematic relation:

$$v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2 a_{max} d_{braking}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the boundary condition.**
The wheelchair must come to rest, therefore $v_f = 0$:

$$0 = v^2 - 2 a_{max} d_{braking}$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for the stopping distance.**

$$2 a_{max} d_{braking} = v^2$$

$$d_{braking} = \frac{v^2}{2 a_{max}}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check with units.**
$$\left[ d_{braking} \right] = \frac{(\text{m/s})^2}{\text{m/s}^2} = \text{m} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example for KTU exam.**
Let $v = 1.5 \text{ m/s}$ and $a_{max} = 2.5 \text{ m/s}^2$ (typical for an electric wheelchair):

$$d_{braking} = \frac{(1.5)^2}{2 \times 2.5} = \frac{2.25}{5.0} = 0.45 \text{ m}$$

> **Conclusion:** From full speed, the wheelchair will come to a complete halt within **45 cm** under emergency braking. This distance must be **strictly less than** the minimum sensor detection range to guarantee collision avoidance.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dynamic Safety Bubble Radius

**Statement:** Derive the minimum dynamic safety bubble radius $R_s$ that ensures no collision under worst-case conditions.

**Step 1 — Decompose the risk envelope.**
The wheelchair must remain clear of any obstacle for the combined physical footprint, reaction-time travel, and braking margin. Therefore:

$$R_s = R_{chair} + d_{travel} + d_{braking}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the travel distance during reaction latency.**
During the controller reaction time $t_{reaction}$, the wheelchair covers:

$$d_{travel} = v \cdot t_{reaction}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the kinematic braking distance.**

$$d_{braking} = \frac{v^2}{2 a_{max}}$$

**Step 4 — Combine all three terms into the final expression.**

$$R_s = R_{chair} + v \cdot t_{reaction} + \frac{v^2}{2 a_{max}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.**
Using $R_{chair} = 0.35$ m, $v = 1.5$ m/s, $t_{reaction} = 0.1$ s, $a_{max} = 2.5$ m/s²:

$$R_s = 0.35 + (1.5)(0.1) + \frac{(1.5)^2}{2 \times 2.5}$$

$$R_s = 0.35 + 0.15 + 0.45 = 0.95 \text{ m}$$

> **Interpretation:** The wheelchair's controller must abort any forward motion command that would bring an obstacle closer than **95 cm** to the wheelchair's geometric center. This is the practical implementation of *shared autonomy* — the bubble cannot be violated even if the user commands full forward speed.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — ICR (Instantaneous Center of Rotation) for Differential Drive

**Step 1 — Set up the velocity vectors at the contact points.**
For the right wheel, the ground contact point moves with velocity $v_R = r \omega_R$ in the forward direction. For the left wheel, $v_L = r \omega_L$.

**Step 2 — Express the linear and angular velocities of the chassis.**

$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{2}$$

$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{2L} = \frac{r(\omega_R - \omega_L)}{2L}$$

**Step 3 — The ICR radius equals the ratio of linear to angular velocity.**

$$R = \frac{v}{\omega} = \frac{\tfrac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{\tfrac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by canceling $r$ and multiplying numerator/denominator by $2L$.**

$$R = \frac{L(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$$

**Step 5 — Validation of special cases.**
- *Straight line* ($\omega_R = \omega_L$): $R = \infty$ (no rotation, pure translation) ✓
- *In-place turn* ($\omega_R = -\omega_L$): $R = 0$ (zero-radius turn) ✓
- *Pure rotation about left wheel* ($\omega_L = 0$): $R = L$ ✓

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Control Modes

| Parameter | Joystick | Voice | Eye-Gaze | Head-Tilt | BCI (EEG) | Touchscreen |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Latency | 50 ms | 300 ms | 200 ms | 80 ms | 1000 ms | 70 ms |
| Accuracy | High | Medium | High | Medium | Low | High |
| Fatigue on user | Low | Medium | High | Low | High | Low |
| Cost | ₹₹ | ₹₹₹ | ₹₹₹₹ | ₹₹ | ₹₹₹₹₹ | ₹₹ |
| Cognitive Load | Low | Medium | High | Low | Very High | Low |
| Suitable for SCI C4-C5 | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Suitable for ALS Late Stage | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Suitable for Elderly | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The *best* control mode is the one that matches the user's **residual ability** (per the *HAAT model — Human Activity Assistive Technology*). No single mode is universally superior.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userA[User Input] --> intfA[Human Machine Interface Layer]
    voiceA[Voice Mic] --> intfA
    eyeA[Eye Gaze Tracker] --> intfA
    headA[Head Array Sensor] --> intfA
    bciA[EEG Headset] --> intfA
    intfA --> ctrlA[Central Controller STM32]
    ultraA[Ultrasonic HC-SR04 x6] --> ctrlA
    irA[IR Sharp GP2Y0A21 x4] --> ctrlA
    lidarA[LiDAR RPLIDAR A1] --> ctrlA
    imuA[IMU MPU6050] --> ctrlA
    encA[Wheel Encoders x2] --> ctrlA
    ctrlA --> algoA[Obstacle Avoidance VFH]
    ctrlA --> slamA[SLAM Hector Mapping]
    ctrlA --> driverA[Motor Driver BTS7960 H-Bridge]
    driverA --> motorA[Right DC Motor 24V 250W]
    driverA --> motorB[Left DC Motor 24V 250W]
    motorA --> wheelA[Right Drive Wheel]
    motorB --> wheelB[Left Drive Wheel]
    ctrlA --> battA[Battery 24V LiFePO4]
    ctrlA --> alarmA[Buzzer and LED Indicator]
    ctrlA --> telemA[WiFi Telemetry ESP32]
```

### 4.2 Sense–Think–Act Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Sensor Read] --> s2[Filter Kalman]
    s2 --> s3[Map Update]
    s3 --> s4[Path Plan]
    s4 --> s5[Collision Check Safety Bubble]
    s5 -->|Safe| s6[Motor Command PWM]
    s5 -->|Unsafe| s7[Emergency Stop]
    s7 --> s8[Buzzer Activate]
    s6 --> s9[Encoder Feedback]
    s9 --> s1
```

### 4.3 State Machine — Smart Wheelchair Operating Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> idle
    idle --> manual : joystick engaged
    idle --> semiAuto : waypoint selected
    idle --> auto : autonomous start
    manual --> semiAuto : assistance requested
    semiAuto --> manual : user override
    semiAuto --> auto : full autonomy
    auto --> manual : emergency stop pressed
    manual --> idle : timeout
    auto --> idle : battery low
    semiAuto --> idle : standby
```

### 4.4 Sensor Placement Map (Top-Down View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    f1[Front Bumper Ultrasonic at 0 degrees] --> wc[Wheelchair Body Center]
    f2[Front Right Ultrasonic at 30 degrees] --> wc
    f3[Right Side IR at 90 degrees] --> wc
    f4[Rear Right Ultrasonic at 150 degrees] --> wc
    f5[Rear Center Ultrasonic at 180 degrees] --> wc
    f6[Rear Left Ultrasonic at 210 degrees] --> wc
    f7[Left Side IR at 270 degrees] --> wc
    f8[Front Left Ultrasonic at 330 degrees] --> wc
    wc --> topSensor[Top Mounted LiDAR 360 degrees at 1.2m height]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Ultrasonic sensors cover the *horizontal plane* with overlapping cones (±15°), while the top-mounted LiDAR provides a full 360° horizontal sweep. The forward hemisphere has *higher sensor density* because forward motion is the dominant collision risk in wheelchair navigation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Define a *smart wheelchair*. List any **four** sensors commonly integrated into it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **smart wheelchair** is a conventional electric wheelchair augmented with **microcontrollers, sensors, and intelligent software** to provide semi-autonomous or fully autonomous navigation for users with motor, cognitive, or sensory impairments *(1 mark)*.

**Four common sensors:**

1. **Ultrasonic sensor (HC-SR04)** — for short-range (0.2–4 m) obstacle detection *(0.5 mark)*.
2. **Infrared proximity sensor (Sharp GP2Y0A21)** — for lateral obstacle awareness *(0.5 mark)*.
3. **LiDAR (RPLIDAR A1)** — for 360° 2D mapping and SLAM *(0.5 mark)*.
4. **IMU (MPU6050)** — 6-axis accelerometer/gyroscope for orientation and tilt safety *(0.5 mark)*.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
What is the **Dynamic Safety Bubble** in smart wheelchair navigation? Write its mathematical expression.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Dynamic Safety Bubble** is a virtual spherical/cylindrical region around the wheelchair within which no obstacle is permitted to intrude *(1 mark)*. Its radius scales with velocity to account for reaction-time travel and braking distance *(1 mark)*.

$$R_s = R_{chair} + v \cdot t_{reaction} + \frac{v^2}{2 a_{max}} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **functional block diagram** of a smart wheelchair with the *Sense–Think–Act* architecture. List the components of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **kinematic equations** for a differential-drive smart wheelchair and compute the linear velocity, angular velocity, and ICR radius for a wheelchair with $L = 0.3$ m, $r = 0.15$ m, $\omega_R = 10$ rad/s, $\omega_L = 6$ rad/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional Block Diagram (7 marks):**

The smart wheelchair is organized into **three primary layers** plus an HMI overlay:

- **SENSE Layer (2 marks):** Contains exteroceptive sensors — *ultrasonic array, IR sensors, LiDAR, RGB-D camera* — and proprioceptive sensors — *wheel encoders, IMU*. Physiological sensors (ECG, SpO₂) can monitor user fatigue.

- **THINK Layer (2 marks):** The embedded controller (STM32/ESP32/RPi) executes algorithms for *obstacle avoidance (VFH, DWA)*, *SLAM (Hector, Gmapping)*, *voice recognition (CMU Sphinx)*, and *path planning*. It also enforces the **Dynamic Safety Bubble** and arbitrates between manual and autonomous commands.

- **ACT Layer (2 marks):** Motor drivers (H-bridge BTS7960) amplify controller PWM signals to drive 24 V DC geared motors coupled to drive wheels. Battery management, emergency stop relay, and telemetry modules belong here.

- **HMI Layer (1 mark):** Multi-modal input — joystick, voice, eye-gaze, head-array, BCI — selected based on user residual ability (HAAT model).

**(b) Kinematic Derivation (7 marks):**

For a differential-drive wheelchair, the contact-point velocities of the two wheels are $v_R = r \omega_R$ and $v_L = r \omega_L$. The chassis linear and angular velocities are:

$$v = \frac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L) \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

$$\omega = \frac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L) \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

The **Instantaneous Center of Rotation (ICR)** radius is:

$$R = \frac{L(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{\omega_R - \omega_L} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Numerical computation (4 marks):**
- $v = \tfrac{0.15}{2}(10 + 6) = \tfrac{0.15 \times 16}{2} = 1.2$ m/s.
- $\omega = \tfrac{0.15}{2 \times 0.3}(10 - 6) = \tfrac{0.15 \times 4}{0.6} = 1.0$ rad/s.
- $R = \tfrac{0.3 \times 16}{10 - 6} = \tfrac{4.8}{4} = 1.2$ m.

> **Result:** The wheelchair moves forward at **1.2 m/s**, rotates at **1 rad/s** counter-clockwise, and rotates about a point **1.2 m** to the *left* of the wheelbase midpoint.

---

#### **Question B — [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **shared-autonomy control scheme** in smart wheelchairs. Compare it with full manual and full autonomous modes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smart wheelchair is moving at **1.8 m/s** when its ultrasonic array detects an obstacle at **0.9 m**. The reaction latency is **0.1 s** and the maximum deceleration is **2.0 m/s²**. Determine whether the controller must issue an **emergency stop** command. The chair half-width is 0.35 m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Shared-Autonomy (7 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** *Shared autonomy* is a hybrid control mode where the user issues high-level motion intent (e.g., "go forward," "turn left") and the embedded controller continuously arbitrates the low-level motor commands to **prevent collisions** by enforcing the safety bubble.

- **Block diagram (2 marks):**
  *User Intent → Intent Classifier → Path Planner → Safety Bubble Check → Final Command to Motors*.
  If the bubble is violated, the controller overrides the user's command with a *safe* alternative (e.g., stop or reroute).

- **Comparison (3 marks):**
  - *Full Manual:* User controls motors directly. Fast, flexible, but **unsafe** for cognitively impaired or tremor-affected users.
  - *Full Autonomous:* Controller plans everything. Safe but **de-skills** the user and fails in unmapped environments.
  - *Shared Autonomy:* User leads, controller safeguards. **Optimal balance** of dignity, independence, and safety — recommended by the HAAT model.

**(b) Emergency Stop Decision (7 marks):**

**Given:** $v = 1.8$ m/s, $R_{chair} = 0.35$ m, $t_{reaction} = 0.1$ s, $a_{max} = 2.0$ m/s², obstacle distance $d_{obs} = 0.9$ m.

**Step 1 — Compute the stopping distance.**

$$d_{braking} = \frac{v^2}{2 a_{max}} = \frac{(1.8)^2}{2 \times 2.0} = \frac{3.24}{4.0} = 0.81 \text{ m} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 2 — Compute the total distance covered before the chair comes to rest.**

$$d_{total} = d_{braking} + v \cdot t_{reaction} = 0.81 + (1.8)(0.1) = 0.81 + 0.18 = 0.99 \text{ m} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Step 3 — Compute the minimum required obstacle distance (Safety Bubble).**

$$R_s = R_{chair} + d_{total} = 0.35 + 0.99 = 1.34 \text{ m} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 4 — Decision logic.**

Since the obstacle is at **0.9 m** but the safety bubble requires **1.34 m**:

$$d_{obs} = 0.9 \text{ m} < R_s = 1.34 \text{ m} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

> **Conclusion:** **YES, the controller MUST issue an emergency stop command.** The obstacle violates the safety bubble by 0.44 m, risking collision. *(1 mark for conclusion)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add the chair half-width $R_{chair}$** to the stopping distance — students often compute only the braking distance and miss the geometric buffer (loses 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Mixing up angular velocity units:** Wheel speed must be in **rad/s**, not RPM. If the problem gives RPM, convert via $\omega = \tfrac{2\pi N}{60}$.
> 3. **Skipping the ICR validation step:** Always verify the special cases ($\omega_R = \omega_L$ → straight; $\omega_R = -\omega_L$ → in-place turn) to score full marks.
> 4. **Not drawing the block diagram in shared-autonomy questions** — a missing diagram loses 2 marks even if the text is correct.
> 5. **Sign convention errors:** Counter-clockwise rotation is conventionally **positive** in the right-hand rule; ensure consistent sign usage in kinematic derivations.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **smart wheelchair** = *electric wheelchair + sensors + embedded intelligence + multi-modal HMI*.
- [x] Core architecture: **Sense → Think → Act**, augmented by an HMI layer.
- [x] Common sensors: **Ultrasonic, IR, LiDAR, IMU, Encoders** — high-yield list for viva.
- [x] Actuators: **24 V DC geared motors** driven by **H-bridges (BTS7960 / VNH2SP30)**.
- [x] Standard speed limit: **≤ 6 km/h (1.67 m/s)** per ISO 7176-1.
- [x] Differential-drive kinematics: $v = \tfrac{r}{2}(\omega_R + \omega_L)$, $\omega = \tfrac{r}{2L}(\omega_R - \omega_L)$.
- [x] ICR radius: $R = \tfrac{L(\omega_R + \omega_L)}{\omega_R - \omega_L}$.
- [x] Dynamic Safety Bubble: $R_s = R_{chair} + v t_{reaction} + \tfrac{v^2}{2 a_{max}}$.
- [x] Stopping distance: $d_{braking} = \tfrac{v^2}{2 a_{max}}$.
- [x] Control modes: **Joystick, Voice, Eye-gaze, Head-tilt, BCI, Touchscreen** — match to user residual ability.
- [x] **Shared autonomy** is the clinically preferred mode (user intent + controller safeguard).
- [x] Power budget: **100–300 W per motor**, battery **24 V / 35–75 Ah**.
- [x] Standards: **ISO 7176**, **IEC 60601-1**, **IEC 62304 (SW lifecycle)**, **ISO 13485 (QMS)**.
- [x] Clinical populations served: **SCI (C4–C5), ALS, MS, CP, Elderly**.
- [x] Trade-off principle: *More autonomy* ↔ *Less user control* — must be balanced for dignity + safety.
- [x] SLAM algorithms frequently tested: **Hector, Gmapping, Cartographer**.
- [x] Obstacle avoidance algorithms: **VFH (Vector Field Histogram), DWA (Dynamic Window Approach)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line KTU Mantra for Smart Wheelchairs:**
> *"Smart Wheelchair = Sense (Sensors) + Think (Controller) + Act (Motors) + HMI (User Interface) — governed by the Dynamic Safety Bubble to enable shared autonomy."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Considered for Selecting a Wheelchair
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors Considered for Selecting a Wheelchair

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wheelchair Selection** is a multidisciplinary clinical and engineering decision-making process in which a rehabilitation specialist, biomedical engineer, or assistive technology practitioner evaluates the intersection of **user characteristics**, **functional requirements**, **environmental constraints**, and **technical specifications of the wheelchair** to prescribe the most appropriate mobility device that maximizes user independence, safety, comfort, and long-term musculoskeletal health.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (OEEBT722):**
> Wheelchair selection is **NOT** a one-size-fits-all purchase decision — it is a *clinical prescription* based on the WHO's *Guidelines on the Provision of Manual Wheelchairs in Less-Resourced Settings* and the *International Society of Prosthetics and Orthotics (ISPO)* framework, where the device must conform to the person, the purpose, and the place.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are buying a **pair of running shoes**.

Would a marathon runner pick the same shoes as a person who walks to the grocery store? Would a person with a flat foot choose the same shoe as someone with a high arch? Would you buy snow boots for a beach vacation?

**Exactly the same logic applies to wheelchairs.**

A wheelchair is the user's **legs for 8–14 hours a day**. Selecting one without considering the user's body, their daily life, and where they live is like forcing a fish to climb a tree — it will fail, cause harm, and get abandoned.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Golden Questions every wheelchair selection must answer:**
> 1. **WHO** is the user? (Anthropometry, disability, cognition)
> 2. **WHAT** will they do in it? (Indoor, outdoor, sport, work, school)
> 3. **WHERE** will they use it? (Home, rough terrain, narrow corridors, transport)

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics Used Globally

The following constants and standardized ranges govern wheelchair prescription worldwide:

| Reference Metric | Standard Value / Symbol |
|---|---|
| Adult average hip width (P50) | **~ 36 cm** |
| Standard wheelchair seat width (adult) | **40 cm, 45 cm, 50 cm** |
| Maximum recommended push frequency | **~ 60 cycles/min** |
| ISO 7176 series | **International wheelchair standards** |
| Minimum doorway clearance (residential) | **~ 76 cm (30 in)** |
| ADA accessible doorway (public) | **~ 91.5 cm (36 in)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Pillar Model of Wheelchair Selection

KTU 2024 curriculum expects students to recognize that wheelchair selection rests on **four interacting pillars**. Misalignment in even one pillar leads to device rejection, secondary injuries (pressure sores, shoulder impingement, scoliosis progression), or social abandonment of the device.

**Pillar 1 — User Factors (The Person)**
- Age (pediatric, adult, geriatric)
- Body weight and height (anthropometry)
- Type and level of disability (spinal cord injury level, cerebral palsy pattern, post-polio, amputation)
- Postural control (trunk balance, head control)
- Range of motion (hip flexion contractures, hamstring tightness)
- Cognitive and visual ability
- Skin integrity (pressure sore history)
- Motivation and family support

**Pillar 2 — Functional / Activity Factors (The Purpose)**
- Primary use (indoor / outdoor / both)
- Distance traveled per day (short < 1 km; medium 1–5 km; long > 5 km)
- Frequency of transfers (bed, toilet, car)
- Need to self-propel vs. be pushed (attendant-propelled)
- Sports, work, or school requirements
- Transport in vehicle (foldable? crash-tested?)

**Pillar 3 — Environmental Factors (The Place)**
- Doorway widths, corridor widths, turning radius
- Floor surface (smooth tile vs. rough kuchha road)
- Slopes and kerbs
- Climate (humidity, rain, dust)
- Storage space at home
- Availability of repair workshops

**Pillar 4 — Wheelchair Technical Factors (The Device)**
- Type: **Manual self-propelled**, **Manual attendant-propelled**, **Powered (electric)**, **Sports wheelchair**
- Frame: Rigid vs. folding; material (steel, aluminium, titanium, carbon fibre)
- Seat dimensions (width, depth, height)
- Backrest (height, angle, tension-adjustable)
- Cushion (foam, gel, air, hybrid — pressure redistribution)
- Armrests (desk-length, full-length, flip-back, removable)
- Footrests (swing-away, elevating, fixed)
- Wheels (rear drive wheel size 24", 26"; castor size 5", 6", 8")
- Pushrims (standard, contoured, coated)
- Brakes (push-to-lock, pull-to-lock, drum brake, hub brake)
- Anti-tip devices, seat belts, tipping levers
- Weight capacity

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following anthropometric and biomechanical relationships form the **numerical backbone** of wheelchair prescription and are frequently tested in KTU exams.

> [!NOTE]
> All linear measurements are in **centimetres (cm)** unless stated. Add **2.5 cm (1 inch)** of cushion thickness when prescribing seat height.

$$
\boxed{SW = HW + 2 \text{ to } 4 \text{ cm}}
$$

where $SW$ = Seat Width, $HW$ = Hip Width (widest point). This clearance prevents skin friction against the sideguards while supporting the greater trochanters.

$$
\boxed{SD = BL - 5 \text{ to } 7.5 \text{ cm}}
$$

where $SD$ = Seat Depth, $BL$ = Buttock-to-Back-of-Knee length (Buttock Popliteal Length). Two-finger gap behind the knee avoids popliteal pressure and circulation restriction.

$$
\boxed{SHt = LLL + CH + \text{Footrest clearance}}
$$

where $SHt$ = Seat Height (floor to top of seat pan), $LLL$ = Lower Leg Length (Popliteal to Heel), $CH$ = Cushion Height. Footrest clearance typically 5 cm.

$$
\boxed{BHt = \text{Seat pan} \rightarrow \text{Inferior angle of scapula} \;-\; 4 \text{ cm}}
$$

where $BHt$ = Backrest Height. For users needing trunk support (e.g., high-level SCI), backrest extends to shoulder or occipital region.

$$
\boxed{AHt = \text{Seat pan to elbow} \;+\; 2.5 \text{ cm}}
$$

where $AHt$ = Armrest Height. Supports forearm with shoulder relaxed.

$$
\boxed{F = \dfrac{W \cdot g \cdot r}{\eta \cdot R}}
$$

Simplified pushrim force equation, where $F$ = propulsive force on pushrim, $W$ = total user + wheelchair weight, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$, $r$ = rolling resistance coefficient, $\eta$ = mechanical efficiency (~ 0.10–0.25 for manual), $R$ = pushrim radius.

$$
\boxed{RR = \dfrac{F_N}{W_{\text{total}}}}
$$

Rolling Resistance ($RR$) where $F_N$ = normal force, $W_{\text{total}}$ = total weight on wheel. Pneumatic tyres on smooth floor: $r \approx 0.005$; on rough terrain: $r \approx 0.05$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always write the **unit** of measurement. KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units in formula-based answers.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This selection framework is used by:
- **Rehabilitation Engineers** in hospitals (NIEPMD, NIRTAR, AIIPMR)
- **Biomedical Designers** at manufacturing firms (Ottobock, Invacare, Karma Medical)
- **ISPO-certified prosthetists/orthotists** in mobility clinics
- **Ergonomics consultants** in workplace accessibility audits
- **Disaster-response teams** (WHO emergency wheelchair kits)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivation, Measurement Protocols & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Anthropometric Measurement Protocol for Wheelchair Prescription

The following 7-step clinical protocol is followed by ISPO-certified professionals and is a **high-yield KTU expected answer** for 7-mark sub-parts.

**Step 1 — Hip Width Measurement**

The user sits on a firm flat surface with thighs fully supported. Measure the **widest distance across the hips (greater trochanters)** using a caliper or rigid tape.
Record value as $HW$.

**Step 2 — Buttock-to-Popliteal Length (Seat Depth)**

User sits upright with knees at 90°. Measure from the **most posterior point of the buttock** to the **posterior crease of the knee (popliteal fossa)**.
Record value as $BL$.

**Step 3 — Lower Leg Length (Seat Height)**

Measure from the **popliteal fossa (under the knee)** to the **bottom of the heel**, with foot at 90°.
Record value as $LLL$.

**Step 4 — Backrest Height Determination**

For active self-propellers with good trunk control: measure from the **seat pan** to the **inferior angle of the scapula**, then subtract 4 cm for push freedom.
For users needing trunk support: extend to the **acromion (shoulder)** or **occiput** for high-level SCI.

**Step 5 — Armrest Height**

With shoulder relaxed and elbow flexed 90°, measure from the **seat pan** to the **underside of the forearm (olecranon)**. Add 2.5 cm.

**Step 6 — Cushion Selection**

Based on skin integrity risk:
- Low risk → foam cushion (3–5 cm)
- Medium risk → contoured foam or gel
- High risk (SCI, insensate) → air-cell or hybrid (Roho, Vicair)

**Step 7 — Wheel Configuration**

For indoor use → small rear wheels (24") with anti-tip devices.
For outdoor / uneven terrain → large rear wheels (26") with pneumatic tyres and suspension forks.
For attendant-propelled → transit wheels (12") with drum brakes.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Anthropometric Prescription

**Case Scenario:** A 28-year-old male, post-traumatic T6 spinal cord injury, weight 72 kg, height 178 cm, complete paraplegia, uses wheelchair full-time, lives in a 2-floor home with narrow doors (78 cm), travels by auto-rickshaw daily, works as a software engineer.

Given measurements:
$$ HW = 38 \text{ cm}, \quad BL = 48 \text{ cm}, \quad LLL = 46 \text{ cm}, \quad \text{Scapula-inferior-angle} = 22 \text{ cm from seat pan} $$

**Step 1 — Seat Width Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SW &= HW + \text{clearance} \\
SW &= 38 + 2 \\
SW &= 40 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

We select the **40 cm (16 inch)** chair. Clearance of 2 cm (rather than 4 cm) is chosen because user self-propels actively and a tighter chair improves push efficiency.

**Step 2 — Seat Depth Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SD &= BL - \text{popliteal clearance} \\
SD &= 48 - 5 \\
SD &= 43 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

A 43 cm seat depth gives a 5 cm gap behind the knee, preventing popliteal vein compression.

**Step 3 — Seat Height Calculation**

Cushion selected: 5 cm air-floatation cushion (for pressure sore prevention in insensate T6 patient).
Footrest clearance = 5 cm above ground.

$$
\begin{aligned}
SHt &= LLL + CH + \text{footrest clearance} \\
SHt &= 46 + 5 + 5 \\
SHt &= 56 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Backrest Height Calculation**

Active user with intact upper trunk and shoulder control.

$$
\begin{aligned}
BHt &= \text{Seat-to-scapula} - 4 \\
BHt &= 22 - 4 \\
BHt &= 18 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Low backrest for maximum shoulder mobility and pushing freedom.

**Step 5 — Pushrim Force Estimate**

On tiled home floor: $r \approx 0.008$, on rough road: $r \approx 0.04$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{smooth}} &= \dfrac{72 \times 9.81 \times 0.008}{0.15 \times 0.30} \\
F_{\text{smooth}} &= \dfrac{5.65}{0.045} \\
F_{\text{smooth}} &\approx 125.6 \text{ N} \\
F_{\text{rough}} &= \dfrac{72 \times 9.81 \times 0.04}{0.15 \times 0.30} \\
F_{\text{rough}} &\approx 627.8 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

This justifies providing a **lightweight rigid-frame aluminium wheelchair** (instead of heavy steel) to keep pushrim force within the user's shoulder capability.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Matching User Profile to Wheelchair Type

| User Profile | Seat Width Logic | Drive Wheel | Cushion | Frame | Special Add-ons |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Active adult paraplegic | Tight (HW + 2) | 24"–26" mag | Air / hybrid | Rigid aluminium | Pushrim covers, anti-tip |
| Geriatric / frail | Wide (HW + 4) | 12" transit | Foam + gel | Folding steel | Drum brake, pelvic belt |
| Pediatric CP with poor trunk | Wide with abductor | 16"–20" | Contoured foam | Tilt-in-space | Chest harness, headrest |
| Hemiplegic (one-arm drive) | Standard | One-arm-drive 24" | Foam | Folding | Single-side pushrim |
| Bilateral amputation | Extra depth, low seat | 24" rear | High-density foam | Reinforced | Anti-tip mandatory |
| Outdoor rough terrain | Standard | 26" pneumatic with suspension | Foam | Folding | Mountain bike tyres, mudguards |

### 3.4 Clinical Reasoning Chain — Why Each Factor Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Logic Chain:** Whenever you answer "why" a factor is important, write it as **CAUSE → EFFECT → CLINICAL CONSEQUENCE**.

Example chain:
**Wrong seat depth (CAUSE)** → popliteal pressure (EFFECT) → venous thrombosis / skin breakdown (CLINICAL CONSEQUENCE).

**Wrong seat width (CAUSE)** → arm constantly rubbing sideguard (EFFECT) → pressure sore on lateral humerus (CLINICAL CONSEQUENCE).

**Wrong seat height (CAUSE)** → shoulder abduction during push (EFFECT) → rotator cuff impingement (CLINICAL CONSEQUENCE).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.1 Wheelchair Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid

    W4[Wrong Cushion] --> R4[Ischial Pressure]
```
