```mermaid
    C --> C1[Powered Exoskeletons]
    C1 --> C1a[Armeo Power Stroke Rehab]
    C1 --> C1b[MyoPro Myoelectric Exo]
    C1 --> C1c[Industrial Lift Assist]

    D --> D1[Feeding Aids]
    D --> D2[Page Turners and Book Holders]
    D --> D3[Wheelchair Mounted Manipulator Arms]
    D --> D4[Environmental Control Units ECU]

    D3 --> D3a[JACO Arm 6 DOF]
    D3 --> D3b[MANUS ARM iARM]
    D3 --> D3c[Raptor Arm Wheelchair Mounted]

    D4 --> D4a[Voice Activated ECU]
    D4 --> D4b[Sip and Puff ECU]
    D4 --> D4c[Brain Computer Interface ECU]
```

> **Reading guide:** The chart above maps the **ISO 9999** taxonomy used in KTU 2024 Module 3. Focus your revision on the *purple* path: **Prosthetic → Myoelectric** and the *green* path: **Wheelchair Mounted → JACO Arm**, as these recur in past year papers.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Myoelectric Prosthetic Arm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Residual Muscle EMG Signal 50 uV to 5 mV] --> A2[Surface Electrode Pair Differential Amplification]
    A2 --> A3[Bandpass Filter 20 to 500 Hz]
    A3 --> A4[Rectifier and Smoothing Envelope Detection]
    A4 --> A5[Microcontroller STM32 DSP Unit]
    A5 --> A6[Pattern Classifier SVM or LDA]
    A6 --> A7[Command Decoder Hand Open, Close, Pinch, Tripod]
    A7 --> A8[Servo Motor Driver H Bridge PWM]
    A8 --> A9[DC Servo Motor Coupled to Harmonic Drive]
    A9 --> A10[Mechanical Fingers and Thumb with Tendon Cables]
    A10 --> A11[Grasped Object Feedback Force Sensor]
    A11 --> A6
    A5 --> A12[Li Ion Battery 7.4 V 2200 mAh]
    A12 --> A5
```

> **Operating principle in one line:** *Phantom muscle intent → surface EMG → DSP classification → motor drive → tendon-actuated fingers → object grasp.* The dashed feedback loop represents the **closed-loop force control** that prevents crushing delicate objects like an eggshell or a paper cup.

---

## 4.3 Kinematic Block Diagram of a Wheelchair-Mounted 6-DOF Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[Base Mounted on Wheelchair Frame] --> K2[Joint 1 Shoulder Yaw Rotation Z Axis]
    K2 --> K3[Joint 2 Shoulder Pitch Up Down Y Axis]
    K3 --> K4[Joint 3 Elbow Flexion X Axis]
    K4 --> K5[Joint 4 Forearm Roll Wrist Yaw Z Axis]
    K5 --> K6[Joint 5 Wrist Pitch Y Axis]
    K6 --> K7[Joint 6 End Effector Roll X Axis]
    K7 --> K8[Two Finger Gripper or Three Finger Gripper]
    K8 --> K9[Workspace Spherical Cap Radius 0.9 m]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 6 DOF?** A rigid body in 3-D space requires **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations) to be fully positioned and oriented. The 6th DOF (end-effector roll) is essential for **pouring, screwing a lid, or aligning a key with a lock**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Define special-purpose electromechanical aids for manipulation. List any four examples with their primary function.**

> **Model Answer:**
> Special-purpose electromechanical aids for manipulation are powered devices that use **electrical actuators, mechanical linkages, and electronic control** to assist or substitute upper-limb function in persons with physical impairments.
>
> **Four examples:**
> 1. **Wheelchair-mounted robotic arm (e.g., JACO Arm)** – Object pick-and-place for quadriplegics
> 2. **Myoelectric prosthetic hand (e.g., i-Limb)** – Grasp and manipulation for transradial amputees
> 3. **Powered feeding device (e.g., Obi feeder)** – Self-feeding for individuals with limited arm mobility
> 4. **Page turner (e.g., Elliot Page Turner)** – Reading independence for bedridden or arthritis patients
>
> *(2 marks for definition, 1 mark for correctly listing four examples)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**What is the typical range of surface EMG signal amplitude and frequency? Why is this relevant to the design of a myoelectric prosthetic hand?**

> **Model Answer:**
> - **Amplitude:** 50 µV to 5 mV
> - **Frequency:** 20 Hz to 500 Hz (dominant energy: 50–150 Hz)
>
> **Design relevance:** The signals are very weak (sub-millivolt), so the prosthetic requires a **high-gain, low-noise differential amplifier** (e.g., INA128 with CMRR > 80 dB) and a **bandpass filter (20–500 Hz)** to extract useful muscle intent. The **signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)** determines the classification accuracy of grasp modes (open, close, pinch, tripod).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Solve *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **working principle of a wheelchair-mounted 6-DOF robotic manipulator arm**. Explain the role of the user interface, controller, motor driver, and feedback sensors in achieving reliable object manipulation.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Block Diagram (refer Section 4.3):**
> A wheelchair-mounted 6-DOF manipulator consists of:
> 1. **Human-Machine Interface (HMI):** A joystick, sip-and-puff, or head-array switch mounted near the user's head/hand. Converts user intent into electrical signals.
> 2. **Controller:** A microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4) running inverse kinematics algorithms. The user specifies a *task-space coordinate* $(x, y, z)$; the controller solves for the *joint angles* $(\theta_1, \theta_2, ..., \theta_6)$ using the inverse Jacobian.
> 3. **Motor Drivers:** Six PWM-based H-bridge drivers (e.g., VNH2SP30) one per joint, providing bi-directional current to each DC servo motor.
> 4. **Sensors:**
>    - *Encoders* (optical or magnetic) on each joint for position feedback
>    - *Current sensors* (Hall-effect) for torque estimation
>    - *Force sensor* (FSR or strain gauge) on the gripper for grasp feedback
> 5. **Mechanical Structure:** Six revolute joints arranged in a shoulder–elbow–wrist kinematic chain, terminating in a two- or three-finger gripper.
>
> **Working sequence:**
> - User commands "Pick the cup at coordinates (0.5, 0.2, 0.3) m"
> - Controller computes joint angles via **inverse kinematics**
> - Six motor drivers energize servo motors in coordinated PWM pulses
> - Encoders report actual joint positions; PID loops minimize error
> - Gripper closes until force sensor reads 2 N (gentle grasp)
> - Arm lifts and places object at target location
>
> **Safety features:** Software limit switches, current limiters, emergency stop button.
>
> *(Award 4 marks for block diagram, 2 marks for working, 1 mark for safety features)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **different types of control interfaces** (switch-based, proportional, and bioelectric) used in manipulation aids. Compare their suitability for users with **cervical spinal cord injury (C4–C7 levels)**.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> | **Interface Type** | **Mechanism** | **Suitable SCI Level** | **Pros** | **Cons** |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | **Single Switch (Scanning)** | Auto-scan + selection | C1–C3 (high injury) | Works with any residual motion | Very slow (~ 5 commands/min) |
> | **Multi-Switch Array** | Multiple discrete buttons | C4 (limited shoulder) | Fast, simple | Requires at least one controllable site |
> | **Joystick (Proportional)** | 2-axis analog stick | C5–C6 (wrist/biceps) | Intuitive, fast | Needs wrist or finger control |
> | **Sip-and-Puff** | Pressure on straw | C2–C4 (no arm use) | Hands-free, reliable | Limited to 4 commands (sip, puff, hard sip, hard puff) |
> | **Head Tracking (Ultrasonic/IR)** | Head motion sensors | C3–C4 | Proportional control | Neck fatigue |
> | **Voice Recognition** | Speech commands | C1–C4 | Hands-free, flexible | Affected by dysarthria, noisy environments |
> | **EMG / Myoelectric** | Residual muscle signals | C5–C7 (some arm) | Natural, intuitive | Electrode sweat, signal drift |
> | **EEG / BCI** | Brain signals | C1–C2 (locked-in) | No motor requirement | Very low bandwidth, high cost |
>
> **Conclusion:** For **C4–C7 quadriplegics**, the best choice is a **proportional joystick** (C5–C6) or **sip-and-puff** (C4). Myoelectric is ideal if even weak forearm muscles are intact.
>
> *(Award 3 marks for classification table, 2 marks for SCI level matching, 2 marks for conclusion)*

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **working principle of a myoelectric prosthetic arm** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the functions of the **surface electrodes, signal conditioning circuit, pattern classifier, and motor driver**.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Working Principle:**
> A myoelectric prosthetic arm uses **electromyographic (EMG) signals** from the user's residual limb muscles to control the prosthesis. The phantom or remaining muscles still receive neural commands from the brain, producing measurable electrical activity on the skin surface.
>
> **Block Diagram Stages (refer Section 4.2):**
>
> 1. **Surface Electrodes (Ag/AgCl, bipolar):** Detect differential voltage between two points over a muscle belly. Amplitude: **50 µV – 5 mV**.
>
> 2. **Signal Conditioning:**
>    - *Instrumentation amplifier* (gain 1000–5000, CMRR > 80 dB)
>    - *Bandpass filter* (20–500 Hz) to remove motion artifact and 50/60 Hz line noise
>    - *Rectifier + low-pass filter* to extract signal envelope
>
> 3. **Pattern Classifier (DSP / Microcontroller):**
>    - Features extracted: **MAV (Mean Absolute Value), RMS, Zero Crossings, Waveform Length**
>    - Classifier: **LDA (Linear Discriminant Analysis)** or **SVM (Support Vector Machine)**
>    - Output: Classified grasp intent (open, close, pinch, lateral, tripod)
>
> 4. **Motor Driver and Actuator:**
>    - *H-bridge MOSFET driver* provides bi-directional current to DC servo motors
>    - Motors coupled to the fingers via **tendons (cables)** or **harmonic drives**
>    - PWM duty cycle (0–100%) controls motor speed; current sense resistor monitors torque
>
> 5. **Feedback:**
>    - *Force-sensitive resistors (FSR)* on fingertips prevent crushing
>    - *Encoder* on motor shaft tracks finger position
>    - Closed-loop PID maintains desired grip force
>
> **Power Supply:** Rechargeable **7.4 V Li-ion battery** (2200 mAh) for 12–24 hours of intermittent use.
>
> *(Award 3 marks for block diagram, 2 marks for signal conditioning, 1 mark for classification, 1 mark for actuation)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** A manipulator arm joint uses a **DC servo motor** with the following specifications:
- Motor rated torque: $\tau_m = 0.4$ N·m
- Gear reduction ratio: $N = 50 : 1$
- Gear train efficiency: $\eta = 75\%$
- Link length from joint to end-effector: $L = 0.35$ m
- Arm rotation speed: $N_{rpm} = 24$ rpm
- Motor torque constant: $K_t = 0.04$ N·m/A

**Calculate:** (i) the torque at the joint, (ii) the maximum end-effector force, (iii) the mechanical power at the joint, and (iv) the armature current required.

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(i) Joint Torque:**
> $$\tau_{joint} = \tau_m \cdot N \cdot \eta = 0.4 \times 50 \times 0.75 = 15.0 \text{ N·m}$$
>
> **(ii) End-Effector Force:**
> $$F_{ee} = \frac{\tau_{joint}}{L} = \frac{15.0}{0.35} \approx 42.86 \text{ N}$$
>
> **(iii) Joint Angular Velocity and Power:**
> $$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 24}{60} = \frac{48\pi}{60} = 0.8\pi \approx 2.513 \text{ rad/s}$$
> $$P_{joint} = \tau_{joint} \cdot \omega = 15.0 \times 0.8\pi \approx 37.70 \text{ W}$$
>
> **(iv) Armature Current:**
> $$I_a = \frac{\tau_m}{K_t} = \frac{0.4}{0.04} = 10.0 \text{ A}$$
>
> **Final Answer Matrix:**
>
> | Quantity | Value |
> |---|---|
> | Joint torque | **15.0 N·m** |
> | End-effector force | **≈ 42.86 N** |
> | Joint power | **≈ 37.70 W** |
> | Armature current | **10.0 A** |
>
> *(Award 2 marks each for correct part (i)–(iv); 1 mark for unit consistency throughout)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Module 3)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting gear-train efficiency (η):** Students often write $\tau_{joint} = \tau_m \times N$ and ignore η. **This loses 1–2 marks every time.** Always multiply by η (typically 0.6–0.85).
> 2. **rpm vs. rad/s confusion:** The KTU answer key **always converts to rad/s** in power calculations. Writing $P = \tau \times N_{rpm}$ directly is incorrect and gets zero for that step.
> 3. **Missing the static force equation $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{J}^T \boldsymbol{\tau}$:** In multi-DOF questions, students often assume 1-DOF and lose 3 marks. Always check if the problem involves a Jacobian.
> 4. **No units in the final answer:** KTU strictly deducts 0.5 marks if N·m, rad/s, or A are missing.
> 5. **EMG amplitude confusion:** Writing "EMG is in volts" is too vague. Specify **"50 µV – 5 mV, 20–500 Hz"** for full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Special-purpose electromechanical manipulation aids = powered devices that **substitute or augment upper-limb function** using motors, gears, and electronic control.
- **Classification:** Three broad families — *Substitution* (prosthetics), *Augmentation* (exoskeletons), *Task-specific ADL aids* (feeding, page turning, ECU, manipulator arms).
- **Key Equations to Memorize:**
  - $\tau_{out} = \tau_m \cdot N \cdot \eta$
  - $F_{ee} = \tau_{joint} / L$
  - $\omega = 2\pi N_{rpm} / 60$
  - $P = \tau \cdot \omega$
  - $I_a = \tau_m / K_t$
  - $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{J}^T \boldsymbol{\tau}$
  - $V_{ws} = \frac{4}{3}\pi R_{max}^3$
- **Critical Numbers:** EMG = 50 µV – 5 mV / 20–500 Hz; Payload = 0.5–5 kg (ADL); Workspace reach = 0.6–1.2 m; 6 DOF for full spatial manipulation.
- **Commercial Benchmarks to Remember:**
  - **JACO Arm** (Kinova): 6 DOF, 1.4 kg payload, 24 V DC
  - **i-Limb Ultra** (Össur): 5 individually motorized fingers, myoelectric
  - **MyoPro** (Myomo): EMG-driven elbow/hand exoskeleton for stroke
  - **Obi Feeder**: robotic arm with switch or sip-puff control for self-feeding
- **Safety & Standards:** ISO 9999 (classification), IEC 60601 (medical electrical safety), FDA Class I/II (US regulation).
- **Design Trade-off Triangle:** *Payload ↔ Speed ↔ Reach* — improving any one typically degrades another.
- **Control Hierarchy:** User intent → HMI → DSP/Controller → Motor Driver → Mechanical Linkage → End Effector → Object → (Feedback loop).
- **Examiner's golden rule:** Always include **units, numerical substitution, and a real-world sanity check** in your final numerical answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electronic Aids to Daily Living
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electronic Aids to Daily Living

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electronic Aids to Daily Living (EADLs)**, also referred to in the KTU 2024 syllabus as *Environmental Control Units (ECUs)*, are specialized assistive medical devices that enable individuals with physical, cognitive, or sensory disabilities to independently operate a wide spectrum of household, workplace, and personal electronic appliances. Formally defined by the **Rehabilitation Engineering and Assistive Technology Society of North America (RESNA)**, an EADL is *"an assistive device that integrates a person's ability to control a number of electrical and electronic devices through a single interface, thereby providing increased functional independence in daily living."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** EADLs are explicitly distinguished from simple remote controls because they incorporate **personalized input modalities** (switches, sip-and-puff, voice, eye-gaze) that bypass the limitations of the user's natural motor/sensory channels.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a person who is **paralyzed from the neck down (quadriplegic)** but mentally fully alert. The television is across the room, the fan is on the ceiling, the doorbell needs answering, and the bedroom lights need to be turned off. Without assistance, none of these tasks are possible.

An **EADL is essentially a "Universal Remote for Life"** — but vastly more powerful. Instead of one remote that only works the TV, an EADL is a *single central brain* (a microcontroller-based hub) that connects, via either **infrared (IR)**, **radio-frequency (RF)**, **Wi-Fi**, or **hardwired relays**, to:

- **Lights, fans, and air conditioners**
- **Television, music systems, and telephones**
- **Doors, windows, and curtains**
- **Emergency call systems and hospital beds**
- **Computers and smart appliances**

The user issues a single command (a sip on a straw, a blink, a spoken phrase, or a button press), and the EADL intelligently translates that command into the correct electrical action on the correct appliance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** A *remote control* sends a fixed code to one device. An **EADL** uses a *configurable control matrix* — a lookup table that maps user inputs to specific appliance functions, allowing **N-to-M mapping** (N inputs controlling M appliances in M different modes).

### 1.3 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **EADL** | Electronic Aid to Daily Living |
| **ECU** | Environmental Control Unit (synonymous with EADL) |
| **ADL** | Activities of Daily Living (eating, bathing, dressing, toileting, transferring, continence) |
| **IADL** | Instrumental ADL (cooking, shopping, telephone use, managing finances) |
| **SIP** | Scanning Input Panel — a single-switch scanning interface |
| **AAC** | Augmentative and Alternative Communication |
| **IR** | Infrared (line-of-sight wireless protocol) |
| **X10** | Legacy powerline communication protocol for home automation |
| **AT** | Assistive Technology |

### 1.4 Standard Physical / Clinical Metrics

The clinical efficacy of an EADL is quantified by three standardized metrics:

- **FIM (Functional Independence Measure):** Score range **18 – 126**; higher is better.
- **CHART (Craig Handicap Assessment and Reporting Technique):** Used to measure handicap reduction.
- **PIADS (Psychosocial Impact of Assistive Devices Scale):** Range **−3 to +3** for subscales of *competence*, *adaptability*, and *self-esteem*.

> [!NOTE]
> The standard **acceptance rate** of EADLs in clinical populations is reported in literature as **~60–75%** (well below satisfaction, indicating the importance of personalized interface design).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Decomposition of an EADL System

Every EADL, regardless of manufacturer, conforms to a **four-layer functional architecture**:

**Layer 1 — Input Subsystem (User Interface Layer)**
Captures the user's intent. Because the user typically has a motor impairment, the input must be compatible with their **best reliable motor movement**. Common inputs include:

- **Mechanical Switches** (single, dual, or multi-switch arrays)
- **Sip-and-Puff Pneumatic Switches** (uses negative/positive pressure)
- **Voice Recognition Modules** (e.g., dedicated DSPs like the HM2007)
- **Eye-Gaze Trackers** (IR camera + corneal reflection)
- **EMG / EEG Bioelectric Sensors** (for high-level quadriplegia)
- **Head/Chin Joysticks** (mechanical proportional controllers)

**Layer 2 — Control Unit (Processing Layer)**
A microcontroller (commonly **Arduino/ATmega328**, **PIC**, or **ARM Cortex-M**) running the **state machine logic**. It:
- Interprets the input signal.
- References a stored **Appliance-Command Matrix**.
- Generates the appropriate output protocol (IR code, relay pulse, X10 packet, Wi-Fi MQTT message).

**Layer 3 — Output Subsystem (Actuation Layer)**
Translates the digital command into physical actuation. Sub-categorized into:

- **Wireless Output:** IR LED transmitter (typically **38 kHz carrier**, NEC protocol), RF transmitter (315 MHz / 433 MHz), Wi-Fi/BLE module (ESP8266, HM-10).
- **Wired Output:** Electromagnetic **relays** (5V/12V coil, 250V/10A contact rating), **triacs** (for AC dimming), solid-state relays (SSR).
- **Powerline Output:** X10 controllers, Insteon, UPB (Universal Powerline Bus).

**Layer 4 — Appliance / Load Layer**
The target devices — lights, fans, doors, telephone, etc.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Switch Scanning Rate** | $R_s = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan} \cdot N_{items}}$ | Scan rate in choices/sec; $T_{scan}$ is dwell time, $N_{items}$ is menu size |
| **Information Transfer Rate (ITR)** | $ITR = \dfrac{\log_2 N}{T}$ bits/s | Bits per second of user throughput |
| **Fitts' Law (Target Acquisition)** | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Movement time to hit target; $D$ = distance, $W$ = width |
| **Relay Coil Current (Ohm's Law)** | $I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{coil}}{R_{coil}}$ | Holding current for the relay's electromagnetic coil |
| **IR Carrier Frequency** | $f_{c} = 38\,\text{kHz}$ (standard) | Standard modulated IR carrier for remote control |
| **NEC Protocol Frame** | $T = 9\,\text{ms} + 4.5\,\text{ms} + 32\,\text{bits} \cdot 1.12\,\text{ms}$ | Total transmission time for a single NEC command |
| **X10 Bit Duration** | $t_{bit} = 1\,\text{ms}$ (1 in 120 Hz zero-cross) | Bit timing on AC powerline carrier |
| **Sip-and-Puff Pressure Threshold** | $P_{sip} = -5\,\text{cmH}_2\text{O},\ P_{puff} = +5\,\text{cmH}_2\text{O}$ | Standard clinical actuation thresholds |
| **Voice Command Word Count** | $N_{vocab} = 20 \text{ to } 200$ typical | Range for embedded speech recognition ICs |
| **Battery Autonomy (EADL)** | $t_{bat} = \dfrac{C_{mAh}}{I_{mA}}$ hours | Where $C$ is capacity, $I$ is average draw |
| **Wien's Law (IR Wavelength)** | $\lambda_{peak} = \dfrac{b}{T}$ where $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m·K}$ | Peak IR emission wavelength of a blackbody |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the formula table matters for KTU 2024:** Numerical problems on EADLs often involve **Fitts' Law** (computing selection time for a scanning interface) or **scanning rate analysis** (a single-switch user selecting an appliance from a menu of N items). Memorize the structure of both.

### 2.3 Classification of EADLs

KTU examiners frequently test the **classification tree**. The three accepted classifications are:

**(a) By Control Modality**
1. **Direct-selection EADL** — User picks the target directly (e.g., touchscreen, voice).
2. **Scanning EADL** — System highlights options sequentially; user activates a single switch when target is highlighted.
3. **Coded EADL** — User enters a numeric/alpha code corresponding to the desired function.

**(b) By Transmission Medium**
1. **Wired (Hardwired)** — Most reliable; requires fixed installation. Used in hospitals.
2. **Wireless (RF / IR)** — Portable; line-of-sight limitation for IR.
3. **Powerline Carrier (X10)** — Uses existing AC wiring; no new cables.

**(c) By Complexity / Functionality**
1. **Simple EADLs** — Control a few appliances (1–4); e.g., a single-switch lamp controller.
2. **Integrated EADLs** — Control 8–32 devices with full feedback LCD/LED.
3. **Smart Home / IoT EADLs** — Voice + cloud + AI (e.g., Google Home, Alexa for accessibility).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

EADL design is foundational to multiple modern engineering domains:

- **Smart Healthcare (IoMT):** Hospital-bed ECU integration.
- **Accessible Smart Homes:** Universal design for aging-in-place.
- **Disability Rights Engineering:** Mandated by the **UN CRPD Article 20** (Personal Mobility) and **Section 508 (US Rehabilitation Act)**.
- **Industrial Ergonomics:** Hands-free factory control using sip-and-puff or voice.
- **Space Medicine:** NASA has used sip-and-puff and head-controlled ECUs for astronaut training.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Single-Switch Scanning Throughput

The most numerically tested KTU concept is the **scanning menu throughput** of a single-switch user. Let us derive it step by step.

**Scenario:** A quadriplegic user accesses an EADL through a **single sip-and-puff switch**. The EADL presents a **scanning menu of $N = 16$ appliances** (lights, fan, TV, phone, door, bed, etc.). The system automatically highlights each menu item in sequence. The user must *sip* when the desired appliance is highlighted. The system then enters a **sub-scan mode** to choose a specific command (e.g., ON/OFF/DIM) for that appliance. Assume:

- The scanning highlight advances every $T_{scan} = 1.5$ seconds.
- There are $N = 16$ top-level appliances and $M = 4$ sub-commands (ON, OFF, BRIGHT, DIM) for the lighting device.
- The user's average reaction time is $R = 0.4$ s.

**Step 1 — Define the expected number of highlights to reach a target.**
For uniform random scanning, the expected number of highlights before the user identifies their target (when no prior information is used) is:

$$
E[H] = \frac{N + 1}{2}
$$

For $N = 16$: $E[H] = \frac{16 + 1}{2} = 8.5$ highlights.

**Step 2 — Compute the time to acquire the top-level selection.**

$$
T_{top} = E[H] \cdot T_{scan}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{top} = 8.5 \times 1.5\,\text{s} = 12.75\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Add the sub-scan time.**
For the lighting device, the sub-menu has $M = 4$ options. The same logic gives:

$$
E[H_{sub}] = \frac{M + 1}{2} = \frac{4 + 1}{2} = 2.5
$$

$$
T_{sub} = 2.5 \times 1.5\,\text{s} = 3.75\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the user's reaction time.**
The user must *react* to the highlight, so we add $R$ to the total:

$$
T_{total} = T_{top} + T_{sub} + R
$$

$$
T_{total} = 12.75\,\text{s} + 3.75\,\text{s} + 0.4\,\text{s} = 16.9\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Information Transfer Rate (ITR).**
The user has effectively communicated a unique command from a vocabulary of size $N \times M$:

$$
V = \log_2(N \times M) = \log_2(16 \times 4) = \log_2(64) = 6\,\text{bits}
$$

$$
ITR = \frac{V}{T_{total}} = \frac{6\,\text{bits}}{16.9\,\text{s}} \approx 0.355\,\text{bits/s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Compare this to a **direct-selection voice user** for the same vocabulary, who might achieve $ITR_{voice} \approx 12$ bits/s. The single-switch scanning method is **~34× slower**, which is why **direct-selection modalities (voice, eye-gaze, touchscreen)** are preferred whenever the user can operate them.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Input Modalities

This matrix is a high-yield KTU comparison. Memorize the relative strengths:

| Input Modality | Min. Motor Ability Required | Typical ITR (bits/s) | Fatigue Level | Cost (USD) | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Switch** | Single reliable movement (finger, toe, head) | 0.3 – 1.0 | Low | $5 – $50 | Severe quadriplegia |
| **Sip-and-Puff** | Oral/facial control | 0.5 – 1.5 | Low–Medium | $150 – $500 | High-level SCI (C2–C4) |
| **Head/Chin Joystick** | Head/neck control | 2 – 6 | Medium | $100 – $800 | Mid-level SCI (C5–C6) |
| **Voice Recognition** | Intelligible speech | 8 – 15 | Low | $50 – $300 | Dysarthria-free users |
| **Eye-Gaze Tracker** | Reliable ocular control | 10 – 30 | Medium–High | $2,000 – $10,000 | ALS, late-stage SCI |
| **EEG / BCI** | None (motor-independent) | 0.2 – 1.0 (current) | Low | $5,000 – $50,000 | Locked-in syndrome, research |
| **EMG Switch** | Voluntary muscle twitch | 1 – 3 | Low | $50 – $200 | Incomplete SCI, amputees |

### 3.3 Derivation: Fitts' Law Application to EADL Menu Design

**Problem:** An EADL has a touchscreen menu where each appliance icon is a square of width $W = 1.5$ cm, centered at a distance $D = 4$ cm from the user's typical finger-rest position. Fitts' constants for stylus-based touchscreen selection are $a = 50$ ms and $b = 150$ ms/bit. Calculate the movement time.

**Step 1 — Compute the Index of Difficulty (ID).**

$$
ID = \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

$$
ID = \log_2\left(\frac{4}{1.5} + 1\right) = \log_2(2.667 + 1) = \log_2(3.667)
$$

$$
ID = \frac{\ln(3.667)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{1.2994}{0.6931} \approx 1.875\,\text{bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Fitts' Law.**

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID
$$

$$
MT = 50 + 150 \times 1.875 = 50 + 281.25 = 331.25\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify with Throughput.**
Throughput $TP$ is defined as:

$$
TP = \frac{ID}{MT} = \frac{1.875}{0.33125} \approx 5.66\,\text{bits/s}
$$

This is well within the typical human motor throughput range of **3 – 10 bits/s**, confirming the menu is ergonomically designed.

### 3.4 Analytical Derivation: IR NEC Protocol Timing

**Problem:** A KTU question may ask: *"What is the total transmission duration of a standard NEC IR command?"*

**Step 1 — Identify the NEC frame structure.**

A NEC transmission consists of:
- **1 × Lead-in pulse** (9 ms HIGH + 4.5 ms LOW) = **13.5 ms**
- **1 × Address byte** (8 bits × 1.12 ms/bit) = **8.96 ms**
- **1 × Address inverse byte** (8 bits × 1.12 ms/bit) = **8.96 ms**
- **1 × Command byte** (8 bits × 1.12 ms/bit) = **8.96 ms**
- **1 × Command inverse byte** (8 bits × 1.12 ms/bit) = **8.96 ms**
- **1 × Lead-out pulse** (560 µs HIGH + 40 ms LOW idle) = **~40.56 ms**

**Step 2 — Sum the durations.**

$$
T_{NEC} = 13.5 + 4 \times 8.96 + 40.56
$$

$$
T_{NEC} = 13.5 + 35.84 + 40.56 = 89.9\,\text{ms}
$$

So a standard NEC command takes **~90 ms** end-to-end, which is fast enough that the user perceives the appliance as instantaneously responding.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid System Block Diagram: Complete EADL Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User with Disability] --> B[Input Subsystem]
    B --> B1[Mechanical Switch]
    B --> B2[Sip-and-Puff]
    B --> B3[Voice Recognition]
    B --> B4[Eye-Gaze Tracker]
    B --> B5[Head Joystick]
    B --> B6[EMG Sensor]
    B1 --> C{Microcontroller Core}
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    B6 --> C
    C --> D[Appliance Command Matrix Lookup]
    D --> E[Output Subsystem]
    E --> E1[IR Transmitter 38kHz]
    E --> E2[RF Transmitter 433MHz]
    E --> E3[Relay Driver 5V coil]
    E --> E4[Triac for AC Dimming]
    E --> E5[WiFi Module ESP8266]
    E1 --> F[Target Appliances]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    F --> F1[Lights and Fan]
    F --> F2[TV and Audio]
    F --> F3[Telephone and Door]
    F --> F4[Hospital Bed]
    F --> F5[Emergency Alarm]
    C --> G[Feedback LCD Buzzer]
    G --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram: Scanning Menu Logic

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> ScanningTop: System initiates
    ScanningTop --> WaitSwitch: Highlight advances every Tscan
    WaitSwitch --> SwitchDetected: User activates sip puff or switch
    SwitchDetected --> ScanningSub: Locked on top level item
    ScanningSub --> CommandExecute: Sub command selected
    ScanningSub --> ScanningTop: User aborts with puff
    CommandExecute --> IRTransmit: Generate NEC code
    CommandExecute --> RelayActivate: Close relay contact
    CommandExecute --> WiFiPublish: Send MQTT message
    IRTransmit --> Idle
    RelayActivate --> Idle
    WiFiPublish --> Idle
    WaitSwitch --> TimeoutAbort: No input within 30s
    TimeoutAbort --> Idle
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: Voice-Controlled EADL Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Speech] --> B[Microphone]
    B --> C[Pre-amplifier and Bandpass 300Hz to 3.4kHz]
    C --> D[ADC Sampling at 8kHz]
    D --> E[Speech Recognition IC HM2007]
    E --> F[Keyword Match]
    F --> G{Valid Command?}
    G --> H[No Match: Beep Error] --> A
    G --> I[Match: Lookup Appliance ID]
    I --> J[Generate IR or RF Code]
    J --> K[Transmit to Target Appliance]
    K --> L[Visual LED Confirmation]
    L --> M[User receives Feedback]
    M --> A
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: User–EADL Interaction

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant I as Input Subsystem
    participant M as Microcontroller
    participant O as Output Subsystem
    participant A as Appliance
    U->>I: Sip negative pressure
    I->>M: Digital HIGH on pin 7
    M->>M: Increment scan counter
    M->>O: Pulse highlight LED row 3
    U->>I: Puff positive pressure
    I->>M: Select row 3 = Lights
    M->>M: Enter sub-scan for Lights
    M->>O: Highlight ON option
    U->>I: Sip to confirm
    M->>O: Generate NEC code 0x40BF and send
    O->>A: IR 38kHz burst
    A-->>U: Lamp turns on
    M->>U: Buzzer beep and LCD updated
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define Electronic Aids to Daily Living (EADL). List any four examples of appliances that can be controlled by an EADL.**

**Model Answer:**
An EADL is an assistive device that allows a person with physical disability to operate multiple household and personal electronic devices through a single, accessible interface (switch, voice, or sip-and-puff), thereby promoting independence in Activities of Daily Living (ADL).

Four examples of controllable appliances:

1. **Lights and fans** (lighting and climate control).
2. **Television and audio systems** (entertainment).
3. **Telephone** (communication).
4. **Doors, windows, and curtains** (access and environment).

*Additional valid examples:* Hospital bed, emergency call system, computer, microwave oven.

**Mark Distribution:** Definition = 1.5 marks, Four examples = 0.375 × 4 = 1.5 marks.

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**Differentiate between direct-selection and scanning-based EADL input methods. State one clinical advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Direct-Selection EADL | Scanning-Based EADL |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanism** | User directly chooses the target (e.g., touchscreen tap, voice command) | System auto-highlights options sequentially; user activates a single switch at the desired option |
| **Motor Demand** | Requires the ability to discriminate and target multiple buttons | Requires **only one reliable motor movement** |
| **Speed** | Fast — governed by Fitts' Law | Slow — governed by scan rate and menu size |
| **Clinical Advantage** | Higher throughput; ideal for users with adequate dexterity | Accessible to users with severe quadriplegia who can operate only one switch |

**Mark Distribution:** Tabular differentiation = 2 marks, one clinical advantage each = 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme, ESE Module Question]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of an EADL system**, identifying the four major subsystems and their roles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-switch EADL is configured to scan a menu of **$N = 32$ appliances** with **$M = 6$ sub-commands per appliance**. The scanning highlight advances every **$T_{scan} = 1.2$ s**, and the user's mean reaction time is **$R = 0.35$ s**. Compute: **(i)** the expected time to acquire the top-level selection, **(ii)** the expected time to acquire the sub-command, **(iii)** the total acquisition time, and **(iv)** the Information Transfer Rate (ITR) in bits/s. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Block Diagram and Explanation [7 marks]**

A complete EADL has **four subsystems**:

1. **Input Subsystem (2 marks):** Captures the user's intent via a modality compatible with their residual motor/sensory function. Examples: single switch, sip-and-puff, voice, head joystick, eye-tracker. Converts the physical action into a clean electrical signal (typically logic-level TTL 0/5V).

2. **Control Unit / Microcontroller (2 marks):** The "brain" of the EADL. Implemented on an 8-bit or 32-bit microcontroller. It runs a state machine that (a) manages the scan timing, (b) detects valid input events, (c) looks up the target command in the **Appliance-Command Matrix** (stored in EEPROM), and (d) generates the appropriate output protocol (NEC IR code, relay pulse, X10 packet, MQTT message).

3. **Output Subsystem (2 marks):** Physically actuates the appliance. Sub-divided into:
   - **Wireless output:** IR LED with 38 kHz modulation, RF transmitter at 315/433 MHz, Wi-Fi/BLE module.
   - **Wired output:** Electromechanical relays (5V/12V coil), solid-state relays, triacs for AC dimming.
   - **Powerline output:** X10 modules that superimpose 120 kHz carrier on 50/60 Hz mains.

4. **Appliance / Load Layer (1 mark):** The target devices — lamps, fans, TV, telephone, door lock, hospital bed, emergency alarm. The EADL is "appliance-agnostic" — it learns or is programmed with each appliance's control codes.

> [!NOTE]
> A feedback path (LCD, buzzer, LED) is essential to inform the user that their command was registered, addressing the **closed-loop usability** principle of assistive technology.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**Given:** $N = 32$, $M = 6$, $T_{scan} = 1.2$ s, $R = 0.35$ s.

**(i) Top-level acquisition time (2 marks):**

The expected number of highlights before selection in uniform scanning is:

$$
E[H_{top}] = \frac{N + 1}{2} = \frac{32 + 1}{2} = 16.5
$$

$$
T_{top} = E[H_{top}] \times T_{scan} = 16.5 \times 1.2\,\text{s} = 19.8\,\text{s}
$$

**(ii) Sub-command acquisition time (2 marks):**

$$
E[H_{sub}] = \frac{M + 1}{2} = \frac{6 + 1}{2} = 3.5
$$

$$
T_{sub} = E[H_{sub}] \times T_{scan} = 3.5 \times 1.2\,\text{s} = 4.2\,\text{s}
$$

**(iii) Total acquisition time (1.5 marks):**

$$
T_{total} = T_{top} + T_{sub} + R = 19.8 + 4.2 + 0.35 = 24.35\,\text{s}
$$

**(iv) Information Transfer Rate (1.5 marks):**

The vocabulary size is $N \times M = 32 \times 6 = 192$ distinct commands. Information content:

$$
V = \log_2(192) = \log_2(128 \times 1.5) = 7 + \log_2(1.5) = 7 + 0.585 = 7.585\,\text{bits}
$$

$$
ITR = \frac{V}{T_{total}} = \frac{7.585\,\text{bits}}{24.35\,\text{s}} \approx 0.311\,\text{bits/s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in this type of question:**
> 1. **Forgetting the reaction time R** in the total. Many students write $T_{total} = T_{top} + T_{sub}$ and lose 0.5 marks.
> 2. **Confusing vocabulary size:** A frequent error is to use $N$ (appliances) instead of $N \times M$ (appliance × sub-command combinations) for the $\log_2$ argument.
> 3. **Off-by-one error:** Using $N/2$ instead of $(N+1)/2$ for the expected highlight count. The correct formula assumes the user may be lucky and select on the *first* highlight.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the **classification of EADLs** based on (i) control modality and (ii) transmission medium. Give one clinical example for each class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to **Fitts' Law**, design the layout of a 4-icon EADL touchscreen control panel. Given target width $W = 2$ cm, target distance from rest position $D = 5$ cm, and Fitts' constants $a = 60$ ms, $b = 180$ ms/bit: compute the **Index of Difficulty (ID)**, **Movement Time (MT)**, and **Throughput (TP)**. Comment on whether the layout is ergonomically acceptable. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Classification of EADLs [7 marks]**

**(i) By Control Modality (3.5 marks):**

1. **Direct-Selection EADL:** User selects the target appliance directly without scanning.
   - *Example:* A voice-activated hub where the user says "Fan on" and the fan is switched on immediately.
   - *Clinical use:* For users with intelligible speech and no dysarthria.

2. **Scanning EADL:** System auto-highlights options sequentially; user triggers a single switch to lock selection.
   - *Example:* A row of LEDs cycles through "Lights → Fan → TV → Phone → Door → Bed"; user sips when the desired option is highlighted.
   - *Clinical use:* For C2–C4 spinal-cord-injury users with only sip-and-puff or head-switch control.

3. **Coded EADL:** User enters a code (numeric or Morse) corresponding to the desired function.
   - *Example:* A 2-digit code "11" for light ON, "12" for light OFF.
   - *Clinical use:* For users with reliable two-switch Morse input.

**(ii) By Transmission Medium (3.5 marks):**

1. **Wired EADL:** All appliances are hardwired to a central controller.
   - *Example:* Hospital bedside ECU with relay rack.
   - *Advantage:* Highly reliable, no RF interference. *Disadvantage:* Not portable.

2. **Wireless EADL (IR/RF):** Commands transmitted as infrared or radio-frequency signals.
   - *Example:* Quha Zono head-mouse sending RF to a receiver paired with a TV.
   - *Advantage:* Portable, easy to retrofit. *Disadvantage:* IR requires line-of-sight; RF may need pairing.

3. **Powerline Carrier EADL (X10):** Commands modulated onto the AC mains wiring.
   - *Example:* X10 lamp module plugged into a wall socket, controlled by an X10 transmitter at another socket.
   - *Advantage:* No new wiring needed. *Disadvantage:* Legacy, slow (~1 ms per bit), and susceptible to line noise.

---

**(b) Fitts' Law Design Calculation [7 marks]**

**Given:** $W = 2$ cm, $D = 5$ cm, $a = 60$ ms, $b = 180$ ms/bit.

**Step 1 — Index of Difficulty (2 marks):**

$$
ID = \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right) = \log_2\left(\frac{5}{2} + 1\right) = \log_2(2.5 + 1) = \log_2(3.5)
$$

$$
ID = \frac{\ln(3.5)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{1.2528}{0.6931} \approx 1.807\,\text{bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Movement Time (2 marks):**

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID = 60 + 180 \times 1.807
$$

$$
MT = 60 + 325.26 = 385.26\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Throughput (1.5 marks):**

$$
TP = \frac{ID}{MT} = \frac{1.807}{0.38526} \approx 4.69\,\text{bits/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Ergonomic Comment (1.5 marks):**

The human motor throughput range for arm/finger movements is typically **3 – 10 bits/s**. The computed $TP = 4.69$ bits/s falls **comfortably within this range**, indicating that the layout is **ergonomically acceptable** and would not impose excessive motor demand on the user. The movement time of ~385 ms per icon is also consistent with typical touch-selection latencies in commercial tablets (300 – 500 ms), further validating the design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Units of a and b:** They are in *milliseconds* (ms), not seconds. Convert at the end if the ITR is needed in bits/s.
> 2. **D/W + 1, not D/W:** The "+1" inside the logarithm is a defining feature of Fitts' Law and frequently missed by students.
> 3. **Comment on TP is mandatory:** KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes *Outcome-Based Education*; merely computing numbers without a clinical/engineering comment loses the application marks (1.5 marks at stake).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Print This Before Exam)**

- **Definition (MUST memorize):** EADL = single-interface controller for multiple home/workplace appliances, RESNA definition.
- **Four Subsystems:** Input → Microcontroller (with Appliance-Command Matrix) → Output → Appliance. Always draw all four in the diagram.
- **Three classifications:** By control modality (direct / scanning / coded), by transmission medium (wired / wireless / powerline), by complexity (simple / integrated / smart).
- **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$ — index of difficulty in **bits**, throughput in **bits/second**.
- **Scanning Rate:** Expected time = $\frac{N+1}{2} \cdot T_{scan} + R$ (do not forget the reaction time R).
- **ITR Formula:** $\log_2(V) / T_{total}$ bits/s, where V is the total vocabulary size (appliances × sub-commands).
- **IR NEC Protocol:** 9 ms + 4.5 ms lead-in, 32 data bits @ 1.12 ms/bit, 38 kHz carrier, total ~90 ms.
- **Standard carrier frequency for remote controls:** **38 kHz** (always state this explicitly).
- **Sip-and-Puff thresholds:** $\pm 5$ cmH$_2$O clinical default.
- **Clinical metrics:** FIM (18–126), PIADS (−3 to +3), CHART.
- **Acceptance rate reality:** ~60–75% — not 100%; emphasize the need for personalized training.
- **EADL vs Remote Control:** EADL = personalized input + configurable command matrix; Remote = one-to-one fixed pairing.
- **Standards & Mandates:** UN CRPD Article 20, Section 508 (US), ISO 9999 (Assistive Products Classification).
- **Numerical Traps:** (i) $N/2$ vs $(N+1)/2$, (ii) missing R, (iii) D/W without +1, (iv) ms vs s units.
- **IoT Migration:** Modern EADLs use MQTT over Wi-Fi (ESP8266/ESP32) and integrate with Alexa/Google Home for voice-first accessibility.
- **Always close the loop:** Feedback (LCD, buzzer, LED) is non-negotiable for cognitive/visual accessibility.
- **Clinical priority order of input choice:** Voice > Eye-gaze > Head joystick > Sip-and-puff > Single switch (when patient capability allows).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotic Aids - Principles of Assistive Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robotic Aids - Principles of Assistive Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assistive Robot:** An assistive robot is a mechatronic device that integrates sensing, computation, and actuation to physically or cognitively augment the functional capabilities of individuals with disabilities or impairments, thereby restoring, improving, or substituting human motor, sensory, or social performance.

According to the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO 8373:2021)**, a medical or rehabilitation robot is a "robot intended to be used as a medical device, including rehabilitation robot, surgical robot, and assistive robot" – these are explicitly designed to support clinical or daily-living tasks for persons with disabilities.

For KTU examination purposes, the defining triad of an assistive robot comprises:
1. **Physical Embodiment** (a mechanical structure that interacts with the user/environment).
2. **Sensing & Intelligence** (sensors + algorithms enabling perception–cognition).
3. **Purposeful Human Augmentation** (the device must serve a human with impairment, not a generic task).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine your body as a sophisticated factory with a control room (the brain), assembly lines (muscles), and delivery trucks (limbs). When a worker gets injured and cannot lift boxes, a **forklift** is brought in. The forklift is **not** the worker, but it restores the *output* of the factory. An assistive robot plays the same role: it is a **mechanical co-worker** that the human "supervises" via shared control. A wheelchair-mounted robotic arm is to a paralyzed user what the forklift is to the injured worker.

A more relatable analogy: think of a **prosthetic leg** as a "tool" — but a powered exoskeleton is a *robot* because it has its own sensors detecting gait intent and its own actuators producing the correct walking motion, **autonomously adjusting in real time**.

## 1.3 Core Operational Metrics (KTU-Mandated Vocabulary)

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent joint motions available. A human arm has **7 DOF**; a wheelchair robot typically has 4–6 DOF.
- **Payload (kg):** Mass the manipulator can carry while maintaining rated performance.
- **Reach (m):** Maximum radial extent of the workspace.
- **Repeatability (mm):** Ability to return to a programmed position; medical robots demand ≤ **0.1 mm**.
- **Workspace (m³ or m²):** Volume/area accessible to the end-effector.
- **Control Bandwidth (Hz):** Frequency range of closed-loop response; rehabilitation robots typically operate at **100 Hz – 1 kHz**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Whenever a question describes an assistive robot, you must mention **(i) the target impairment, (ii) the actuation principle, and (iii) the control paradigm**. Examiners award marks for these three identifiers.

## 1.4 Sub-Categories of Assistive Robots (Syllabus Map)

| # | Category | Target User | Example | Key Function |
|---|----------|-------------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | **Mobility Robots** | Lower-limb impaired | Smart wheelchairs, autonomous walkers | Navigation, obstacle avoidance |
| 2 | **Manipulation Robots** | Upper-limb impaired | Wheelchair-mounted arms (e.g., Manus ARM) | ADL tasks (feeding, drinking) |
| 3 | **Rehabilitation Robots** | Post-stroke / spinal injury | Lokomat, Armeo Power | Gait / arm therapy |
| 4 | **Wearable Exoskeletons** | Paraplegia, industrial workers | ReWalk, Ekso GT | Powered ambulation |
| 5 | **Prosthetic Robots** | Amputees | Bebionic hand, LUKE arm | Substitute missing limb |
| 6 | **Social / Companion Robots** | Autism, dementia, elderly | PARO, NAO | Emotional / cognitive support |
| 7 | **Surgical Robots** | Surgeons | Da Vinci, Mako | Precision minimally-invasive surgery |
| 8 | **Sensory Substitution** | Blind / deaf | Tongue-display, haptic vest | Translate one modality to another |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Functional Layers of an Assistive Robot

An assistive robot is best understood as a **layered mechatronic system**. From the physical signal-flow perspective, three distinct layers operate in parallel:

### Layer A — Perception Layer (Sensors)
- **Proprioceptive sensors** measure the *internal state* of the robot: joint encoders, motor current, inertial measurement units (IMU), force/torque sensors.
- **Exteroceptive sensors** measure the *external world*: cameras (RGB, depth, IR), LiDAR, ultrasonic rangers, tactile/pressure arrays, EMG electrodes (for bioelectric intent detection), EEG (for brain-computer interface).
- The sensor sampling rates and noise characteristics define the **smallest controllable motion** and the **largest stable feedback gain**.

### Layer B — Cognition/Decision Layer (Controller)
- Translates high-level intent (button press, voice command, EMG burst, joystick deflection, BCI signal) into low-level actuator commands.
- Implemented as a real-time digital controller (DSP, microcontroller, or PC with RTOS).
- Mathematical core: forward/inverse kinematics, dynamics, trajectory generation, shared-control arbitration.

### Layer C — Actuation Layer (Effectors)
- Converts electrical/hydraulic/pneumatic energy into mechanical motion.
- Common actuators: DC servomotors with harmonic-drive gearheads, brushless DC (BLDC) motors, series elastic actuators (SEA), pneumatic muscles (McKibben), shape-memory alloys (SMA).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three layers?** Separation of concerns allows modular design, safety certification (actuators can be hard-killed independently of perception), and incremental upgrading (sensors can be swapped without redesigning the controller).

## 2.2 Degrees of Freedom and the Grübler–Kutzbach Criterion

The **DOF** dictates whether the robot can replicate the natural human motion envelope. The number of independent motions in a planar mechanism is given by Grübler–Kutzbach:

$$M \;=\; 3\,(n-1) \;-\; 2\,j_1 \;-\; j_2$$

where $n$ is the number of links, $j_1$ is the number of lower pairs (1 DOF joints), and $j_2$ is the number of higher pairs (2 DOF joints). For a spatial mechanism, the general mobility equation is:

$$M \;=\; 6\,(n-1) \;-\; 5\,j_1 \;-\; 4\,j_2 \;-\; 3\,j_3 \;-\; 2\,j_4 \;-\; j_5$$

**Step-by-step reading:**
1. The term $6(n-1)$ counts the unconstrained relative motions between $n$ rigid links in 3D space.
2. Each 1-DOF joint removes 5 DOF (a revolute or prismatic joint constrains 5 motions and allows 1).
3. Each 2-DOF joint (universal) removes 4 DOF.
4. Each 3-DOF joint (spherical) removes 3 DOF.

**Real-world utility:** For KTU problems, a 6-DOF manipulator is the **minimum** required to position the end-effector at any (x, y, z) with arbitrary orientation in 3D space (3 translations + 3 rotations). A 7-DOF arm (like the Manus ARM) adds **redundancy**, which is exploited to avoid obstacles and singularities in cluttered home environments.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** Memorize the bold-typed quantities. They are the marks-fetching variables in numerical problems.

| # | Concept | Equation | Variable Definitions | Typical Units |
|---|---------|----------|----------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Grübler planar mobility** | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$=links, $j_1$=1-DOF joints, $j_2$=2-DOF joints | dimensionless |
| 2 | **Grübler spatial mobility** | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | As above for spatial case | dimensionless |
| 3 | **DH homogeneous transform** | $A_i = \mathrm{Rot}_{z,\theta_i} \cdot \mathrm{Trans}_{z,d_i} \cdot \mathrm{Trans}_{x,a_i} \cdot \mathrm{Rot}_{x,\alpha_i}$ | $\theta_i$=joint angle, $d_i$=link offset, $a_i$=link length, $\alpha_i$=link twist | rad, m, m, rad |
| 4 | **End-effector pose** | $T_{0}^{n} = A_1 A_2 \cdots A_n$ | Product of DH transforms | homogeneous 4×4 |
| 5 | **Forward kinematics** | $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$ | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ task space, $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ joint space | m, rad |
| 6 | **Differential kinematics** | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = J(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $J(\mathbf{q})$ is the **Jacobian** | $m \times n$ |
| 7 | **Inverse kinematics (least-squares)** | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = J^{\dagger} \dot{\mathbf{x}} + (I - J^{\dagger} J) \mathbf{z}$ | $J^{\dagger}$ is Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse | – |
| 8 | **PD control law** | $\tau = K_p (\mathbf{q}_d - \mathbf{q}) + K_d (\dot{\mathbf{q}}_d - \dot{\mathbf{q}})$ | $\tau$=torque, $K_p$, $K_d$=gains | N·m, N·m·s/rad |
| 9 | **Lagrangian dynamics** | $M(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + C(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + G(\mathbf{q}) = \tau$ | $M$=inertia, $C$=Coriolis, $G$=gravity | – |
| 10 | **Critically damped response** | $\zeta = 1,\; \omega_n = \sqrt{K_p / J_{eq}}$ | $J_{eq}$=equivalent inertia | rad/s |
| 11 | **Snell's law for haptics** | $\sin\theta_i / \sin\theta_t = v_i / v_t$ | $v$=wave speed in medium | m/s |
| 12 | **EMG-RMS** | $u = \sqrt{ \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{k=1}^{N} x_k^2 }$ | $x_k$ = sampled EMG | mV |

**Real-world engineering utility:** The Grübler equation is used by KTU examiners to test whether your proposed mechanism has the correct number of joints. The Jacobian relation governs **singularity analysis** — when $\det J = 0$, the robot loses controllability in one or more task-space directions, which is critical in rehabilitation robots that must never trap a patient.

## 2.4 The Five Principles of Assistive Robot Design (KTU 2024 Module-3 Highlight)

1. **User-Centeredness** – design is *for* and *with* the disabled user, not *on* them.
2. **Safety First** – hardware and software must fail-safe (limit switches, software bounds, compliance).
3. **Affordability & Accessibility** – the device must be reproducible at low cost; open-source platforms (e.g., Arduino, ROS) are encouraged.
4. **Transparency of Control** – the user must always understand "who is in charge": user, robot, or shared control.
5. **Modularity & Interoperability** – sensors, actuators, and software modules must be swappable and standardized (this is the **openEHR / ROS** philosophy).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Forward Kinematics of a 2-DOF Planar Manipulator (KTU Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation?** KTU examiners frequently test the simplest multi-DOF mechanism to check if a student can **systematically** apply the DH convention. A 2-DOF planar arm is the canonical example.

### Given
- Link 1 of length $a_1$ (m), rotated by joint angle $\theta_1$ from the base x-axis.
- Link 2 of length $a_2$ (m), rotated by $\theta_2$ relative to link 1.
- All joints are **revolute**, motion is confined to the **xy-plane** (so $d_i = 0$ and $\alpha_i = 0$).

### Step 1 — Position of the End-Effector (Geometric Approach)

We project link 1 and link 2 onto the x and y axes separately.

**Step 1.1:** The endpoint of link 1 (which is the joint between link 1 and link 2) is located at:
$$P_1 \;=\; \bigl( a_1 \cos\theta_1,\; a_1 \sin\theta_1 \bigr)$$

**Step 1.2:** Link 2 makes an angle of $(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ with the x-axis. Therefore, the end-effector position $P_2 = (x, y)$ is:
$$x \;=\; a_1 \cos\theta_1 \;+\; a_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

**Step 1.3:** The y-coordinate is the sum of the y-projections of both links:
$$y \;=\; a_1 \sin\theta_1 \;+\; a_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

### Step 2 — Compactly Write the Forward Kinematics Vector

We collect both equations into a single vector-valued function:
$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{x} \;=\; f(\mathbf{q}) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \cos\theta_1 + a_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\[4pt] a_1 \sin\theta_1 + a_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

where $\mathbf{x} = (x, y)^T$ is the **task-space coordinate** and $\mathbf{q} = (\theta_1, \theta_2)^T$ is the **joint-space coordinate**.

### Step 3 — Derive the Jacobian by Differentiation

Differentiate $x$ and $y$ with respect to time to obtain the **velocity relationship**.

**Step 3.1:** Differentiate $x$ with respect to $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_1} \;=\; -a_1 \sin\theta_1 - a_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_2} \;=\; -a_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.2:** Differentiate $y$ with respect to $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_1} \;=\; a_1 \cos\theta_1 + a_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\[4pt]
\frac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_2} \;=\; a_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.3:** Assemble the **Jacobian matrix** $J(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}$:
$$\begin{aligned}
J(\mathbf{q}) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_1} & \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_1} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_2} \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 \sin\theta_1 - a_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -a_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\[6pt] a_1 \cos\theta_1 + a_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & a_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.4:** Write the velocity relationship in compact form:
$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} \;=\; J(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

### Step 4 — Singularity Condition (Examiner's Favourite Follow-Up)

Compute the determinant of the 2×2 Jacobian:
$$\begin{aligned}
\det J(\mathbf{q}) \;=\; \bigl(-a_1 \sin\theta_1 - a_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr)\bigl(a_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr) \\[4pt] \;\;\; - \;\bigl(-a_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr)\bigl(a_1 \cos\theta_1 + a_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\bigr)
\end{aligned}$$

Expanding and applying the identity $\sin A \cos B - \cos A \sin B = \sin(A-B)$:
$$\det J(\mathbf{q}) \;=\; a_1 a_2 \,\sin\theta_2$$

**Singularity occurs when $\sin\theta_2 = 0$, i.e., $\theta_2 = 0$ or $\pi$.** Physically, this corresponds to the **fully extended** or **fully folded** configuration where the manipulator loses the ability to move in the radial direction.

## 3.2 Derivation: Grübler–Kutzbach Mobility for a 4-Bar Wheelchair Suspension

### Given
- $n = 4$ rigid links (ground, two side-links, coupler).
- $j_1 = 4$ revolute joints (each 1 DOF).
- $j_2 = 0$ (no higher pairs).

### Step 1 — Apply the Planar Grübler Formula

$$M \;=\; 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \;=\; 3(4-1) - 2(4) - 0 \;=\; 9 - 8 \;=\; 1$$

**Interpretation:** A four-bar linkage has exactly **1 DOF** — driving any one joint uniquely determines the configuration of all other joints. This is the *minimum* required for a passive suspension; active suspension adds an actuator to drive the same DOF.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Control Architectures

| # | Architecture | Strength | Weakness | KTU Example |
|---|--------------|----------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Hierarchical (sense → plan → act)** | Strong task reasoning | Slow; brittle to environment change | Surgical robot pre-planning |
| 2 | **Reactive (sense → act)** | Real-time, robust to environment | No long-term memory or planning | Mobile wheelchair obstacle avoidance |
| 3 | **Hybrid (3-T architecture)** | Combines both | Design complexity | Smart wheelchair with shared control |
| 4 | **Behavior-based** | Distributed, fault-tolerant | Hard to verify formally | ASIMO social robot |
| 5 | **Learning-based (imitation/RL)** | Adapts to user preference | Data-hungry, opaque | Prosthetic arm trained on user EMG |
| 6 | **Shared control (user + autonomy)** | Safe, intuitive | Arbitration is hard | Wheelchair-mounted robot arm |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A 2-DOF planar arm has link lengths $a_1 = 0.5$ m, $a_2 = 0.4$ m. For joint angles $\theta_1 = 30°$ and $\theta_2 = 45°$, compute (a) the end-effector position, and (b) the determinant of the Jacobian.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Convert degrees to radians: $\theta_1 = \pi/6$, $\theta_2 = \pi/4$. Sum: $\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 75° = 5\pi/12$.

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= a_1 \cos\theta_1 + a_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
&= 0.5 \cos(30°) + 0.4 \cos(75°) \\
&= 0.5(0.8660) + 0.4(0.2588) \\
&= 0.4330 + 0.1035 \\
&= 0.5365 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
y &= a_1 \sin\theta_1 + a_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
&= 0.5 \sin(30°) + 0.4 \sin(75°) \\
&= 0.5(0.5000) + 0.4(0.9659) \\
&= 0.2500 + 0.3864 \\
&= 0.6364 \;\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b):**
$$\det J \;=\; a_1 a_2 \sin\theta_2 \;=\; (0.5)(0.4)\sin(45°) \;=\; 0.20 \times 0.7071 \;=\; 0.1414 \;\text{m}^2$$

The non-zero determinant confirms the configuration is **not singular** — the arm can move freely in both task-space directions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Control of an Assistive Robot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Intent\nEMG, Voice, BCI, Joystick] --> B[Perception Layer\nSensors + Signal Processing]
    B --> C[Cognition Layer\nController + Trajectory Planner]
    C --> D[Actuation Layer\nMotors + Drivers + Gearbox]
    D --> E[Mechanical Structure\nLinks and Joints]
    E --> F[Human and Environment]
    F --> G[Haptic Feedback\nForce Torque Sensor]
    G --> C
    B --> H[Safety Supervisor\nWatchdog and E-Stop]
    H --> D
    style A fill:#e1f5fe
    style B fill:#b3e5fc
    style C fill:#4fc3f7
    style D fill:#0288d1
    style E fill:#01579b
    style F fill:#ffd54f
    style G fill:#ffb74d
    style H fill:#e57373
```

**Reading the diagram:** The user expresses intent via physiological or manual signals. The perception layer digitizes and filters these signals. The cognition layer decides *what* motion the robot should execute. The actuation layer physically drives the joints. The mechanical structure interacts with the human and the environment. Force and tactile information loops back to the controller for closed-loop stability. The **safety supervisor** is an independent hardware channel that can hard-stop the actuators regardless of controller state — a non-negotiable feature for medical-grade assistive devices.

## 4.2 Classification Tree — Assistive Robots

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Assistive Robots] --> B[Physical Assistance]
    A --> C[Cognitive and Social Assistance]
    A --> D[Surgical Assistance]
    B --> B1[Mobility Aids]
    B --> B2[Manipulation Aids]
    B --> B3[Rehabilitation Therapy]
    B --> B4[Exoskeletons]
    B --> B5[Prosthetic Limbs]
    C --> C1[Companion Robots]
    C --> C2[Therapy Robots for Autism]
    C --> C3[Reminder and Prompting Robots]
    D --> D1[Master Slave Surgical Systems]
    D --> D2[Image Guided Robots]
    D --> D3[Rehabilitation Surgery Robots]
    style A fill:#fff9c4
    style B fill:#c8e6c9
    style C fill:#ffccbc
    style D fill:#d1c4e9
```

**Reading the diagram:** The top-level split separates *physical* from *cognitive/social* from *surgical* assistance. Each branch then enumerates clinically deployed sub-categories. The colour coding visually distinguishes the domains for faster recall during exams.

## 4.3 Kinematic Chain — 2-DOF Planar Arm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    base[Base Link\nFixed to Wheelchair] --> j1[Revolute Joint 1\ntheta1]
    j1 --> l1[Link 1\nLength a1]
    l1 --> j2[Revolute Joint 2\ntheta2]
    j2 --> l2[Link 2\nLength a2]
    l2 --> ee[End Effector\nGripper]
    style base fill:#b0bec5
    style j1 fill:#ff8a65
    style j2 fill:#ff8a65
    style ee fill:#81c784
```

**Reading the diagram:** The base link anchors the manipulator to the wheelchair frame. Joint 1 (revolute, $\theta_1$) provides shoulder yaw. Joint 2 (revolute, $\theta_2$) provides elbow pitch. The end-effector (gripper) is the terminal link that physically interacts with the object.

## 4.4 Shared-Control Arbitration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U[User Command] --> ARB[Arbitrator]
    A[Autonomous Assistance] --> ARB
    ARB --> R1[Is User Command Valid?]
    R1 -->|Yes| OUT[Send User Command to Robot]
    R1 -->|No| R2[Is Risk High?]
    R2 -->|Yes| OUT2[Send Stop Command]
    R2 -->|No| OUT3[Blend User and Autonomous Commands]
    OUT --> ACT[Actuator]
    OUT2 --> ACT
    OUT3 --> ACT
    style ARB fill:#fff176
    style ACT fill:#4fc3f7
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **arbitrator** is the software module that decides whether the user's command, the autonomous suggestion, or a weighted blend of both is sent to the actuators. The arbitration logic must run at a rate higher than the actuators' control loop and must always default to the **safest** option in case of a sensor failure.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define an assistive robot and list **any four** categories with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

An **assistive robot** is a mechatronic device that uses sensors, controllers, and actuators to physically or cognitively augment the functional capacity of an individual with impairment, helping them perform activities of daily living (ADL) that they could not otherwise perform independently.

Four categories:
1. **Mobility robot** – e.g., smart wheelchair with LiDAR-based navigation.
2. **Manipulation robot** – e.g., Manus ARM mounted on a powered wheelchair.
3. **Rehabilitation robot** – e.g., Lokomat for gait therapy.
4. **Social / companion robot** – e.g., PARO seal for dementia therapy.

(Other valid examples: exoskeleton, prosthetic arm, surgical robot.)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between a **prosthetic robot** and a **rehabilitation robot**. Mention the user and the operating environment for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Prosthetic Robot | Rehabilitation Robot |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| User | Person with limb amputation | Patient with neurological/muscular impairment (intact limb) |
| Operating environment | Community, home, workplace | Clinical / hospital setting |
| Purpose | **Substitute** a missing limb permanently | **Restore / retrain** function of an existing limb during therapy |
| Example | LUKE arm (DEKA), Bebionic hand | Armeo Power, Lokomat |
| Control | EMG from remaining muscles | Therapist-supervised, performance-based |

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) — Inverse Kinematics & Grübler Mobility

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 3 Internal Choice, Set A]**

(a) A planar assistive manipulator mounted on a wheelchair has **3 rigid links** and **3 revolute joints**. Using the Grübler–Kutzbach criterion, determine the number of degrees of freedom. Justify whether the manipulator is sufficient to position the end-effector anywhere in a 2D plane with arbitrary orientation. **\[7 marks\]**

(b) Derive the forward kinematics of a **2-DOF planar manipulator** with link lengths $a_1$ and $a_2$ and joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$. Hence compute the **Jacobian matrix** and identify the **singular configuration**. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Grübler Mobility:**

Step 1: Identify the parameters: $n = 3$ (3 rigid links — base + link 1 + link 2), $j_1 = 3$ (three revolute joints, each providing 1 DOF), $j_2 = 0$ (no higher pairs).

Step 2: Apply the planar Grübler formula:
$$M \;=\; 3(n-1) - 2 j_1 - j_2 \;=\; 3(3-1) - 2(3) - 0 \;=\; 6 - 6 \;=\; 0$$

**Result:** $M = 0$ — the mechanism is a **structure**, not a mechanism! It cannot move.

Step 3: To make it a moving manipulator, we must add **one more link** (the moving end-effector) and a third joint between link 2 and the end-effector, giving $n = 4$, $j_1 = 4$:
$$M \;=\; 3(4-1) - 2(4) \;=\; 9 - 8 \;=\; 1$$

Wait — this is a **1-DOF** mechanism (a four-bar). To obtain a 2-DOF manipulator that can position the end-effector anywhere in the plane, we use $n = 3$ (ground, link 1, link 2) and $j_1 = 2$ (joints at base and elbow), giving:
$$M \;=\; 3(3-1) - 2(2) \;=\; 6 - 4 \;=\; 2$$

Step 4: A 2-DOF planar arm provides **2 translational DOF** (x, y) but **no rotational DOF** (no $\phi$). Therefore, the manipulator is **insufficient** if arbitrary orientation of a grasped object is required. A 3-DOF planar arm (R-R-R) is needed to also control the end-effector's planar orientation.

**Conclusion:** A 2-DOF planar arm has 2 DOF, sufficient for **positioning** the gripper tip anywhere within its reachable workspace, but **not** sufficient for arbitrary planar **orientation**.

**Part (b) — Forward Kinematics & Jacobian:**

Step 1: Place the base frame at the origin. The first joint is revolute with axis along z, and the first link has length $a_1$ along the rotated x-axis. The DH parameters are:

| $i$ | $a_i$ | $\alpha_i$ | $d_i$ | $\theta_i$ |
|-----|-------|------------|-------|------------|
| 1 | $a_1$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_1$ |
| 2 | $a_2$ | 0 | 0 | $\theta_2$ |

Step 2: The homogeneous transform for joint $i$ (revolute, axis z) using the standard DH convention:
$$A_i \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i & 0 & a_i\cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i & 0 & a_i\sin\theta_i \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 3: Compute $A_1$ and $A_2$ separately:
$$A_1 \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} c_1 & -s_1 & 0 & a_1 c_1 \\ s_1 & c_1 & 0 & a_1 s_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad A_2 \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} c_2 & -s_2 & 0 & a_2 c_2 \\ s_2 & c_2 & 0 & a_2 s_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $c_i = \cos\theta_i$ and $s_i = \sin\theta_i$.

Step 4: Multiply $T = A_1 A_2$ to obtain the end-effector pose. The translation column (x, y) yields:
$$\begin{aligned}
x &= a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} \\
y &= a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12}
\end{aligned}$$
where $c_{12} = \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$, $s_{12} = \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$.

Step 5: The Jacobian is obtained by differentiating $\mathbf{x} = (x, y)^T$ with respect to $\mathbf{q} = (\theta_1, \theta_2)^T$:
$$J(\mathbf{q}) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -a_1 s_1 - a_2 s_{12} & -a_2 s_{12} \\ a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12} & a_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

Step 6: Compute the determinant:
$$\det J \;=\; (-a_1 s_1 - a_2 s_{12})(a_2 c_{12}) - (-a_2 s_{12})(a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12})$$

Step 7: Expand and simplify:
$$\det J \;=\; -a_1 a_2 s_1 c_{12} - a_2^2 s_{12} c_{12} + a_1 a_2 s_{12} c_1 + a_2^2 s_{12} c_{12}$$

The $a_2^2$ terms cancel. Using $s_{12} c_1 - s_1 c_{12} = s_{12 - 1} = s_2$:
$$\det J \;=\; a_1 a_2 \,s_2$$

Step 8: The Jacobian is **singular** when $s_2 = 0$, i.e., when $\theta_2 = 0$ (arm fully stretched out) or $\theta_2 = \pi$ (arm folded back on itself). At these configurations the manipulator loses one task-space direction (it cannot move toward/away from the base) and the inverse-velocity relationship $J^{-1}$ does not exist.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Control Architectures & Shared Control

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 3 Internal Choice, Set B]**

(a) Explain the **three-layer functional architecture** of an assistive robot. Identify the safety-critical layer and justify why it must be **hardware-independent** of the main controller. **\[7 marks\]**

(b) With a neat block diagram, describe the **shared-control arbitration scheme** used in a smart wheelchair. Discuss **two** challenges in implementing shared control. **\[7 marks\]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Three-Layer Architecture:**

Step 1: An assistive robot is composed of three logically and often physically distinct layers:

1. **Perception Layer** — Converts physical phenomena (joint angles, forces, images, bioelectric signals) into digital data using sensors such as encoders, IMUs, cameras, force/torque sensors, EMG electrodes.
2. **Cognition/Decision Layer** — A digital computer that processes sensor data, runs kinematics/dynamics algorithms, generates trajectories, and decides actuator commands in real time.
3. **Actuation Layer** — Drivers and motors (DC, BLDC, stepper, pneumatic, hydraulic) that convert electrical/pneumatic energy into mechanical motion.

Step 2: An **independent fourth channel** — the **Safety Supervisor** — runs in parallel. It directly reads the **emergency stop button**, **limit switches**, and **watchdog timer**, and can cut power to the actuators using a hardware relay that bypasses the main controller entirely.

Step 3: Justification for hardware independence:
- **Software bugs in the cognition layer** (e.g., division by zero, infinite loop, memory leak) must not be able to disable the safety stop.
- **Controller hang or power loss** of the main CPU must not lock the user inside a powered exoskeleton.
- Regulatory standards (ISO 13482 for personal-care robots, IEC 60601 for medical electrical equipment) mandate **hardware-based** safety stops with a response time ≤ **50 ms**.
- A hardware relay (or a separate safety PLC) guarantees fail-safe behaviour because any software or firmware fault leaves the safety circuit intact.

**Conclusion:** The safety layer must be **electrically, computationally, and functionally** independent of the main controller.

**Part (b) — Shared-Control Arbitration:**

Step 1: Define shared control: the **real-time blending** of user intent with autonomous robot assistance to achieve a task that the user could not accomplish alone. In a smart wheelchair, the user provides high-level direction (turn left, go forward) while the robot handles low-level concerns (obstacle avoidance, wall following, ramp detection).

Step 2: The **arbitration block diagram** consists of:

1. **User input module** — joystick, sip-and-puff, head-array, or BCI.
2. **Autonomous perception module** — LiDAR / camera-based obstacle detection and SLAM.
3. **Arbitrator** — software module implementing a policy such as:
$$u_{\text{final}} = (1-\lambda)\,u_{\text{user}} + \lambda\,u_{\text{auto}}, \quad 0 \le \lambda \le 1$$
where $\lambda$ is dynamically adjusted based on context (e.g., $\lambda = 1$ near obstacles, $\lambda = 0$ in open space).
4. **Low-level motion controller** — converts $u_{\text{final}}$ into motor PWM signals.
5. **Safety supervisor** — overrides the arbitrator in emergencies.

Step 3: **Two implementation challenges:**

1. **Latency and predictability:** The arbitrator must run at a guaranteed real-time rate (typically ≥ 100 Hz) to avoid jerky motion. General-purpose operating systems (Windows, Linux without RT patch) introduce jitter that can disorient the user or cause collisions. A real-time OS (RTOS) or a dedicated microcontroller is often needed.

2. **User-acceptance and trust calibration:** If the autonomous layer overrides the user too often, the user feels out of control; if it overrides too rarely, collisions occur. Calibrating $\lambda$ to the user's cognitive and physical profile (and continuously re-calibrating as the user fatigues) is an open research problem addressed by adaptive shared-control algorithms using POMDPs or reinforcement learning.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety layer** — A 14-mark answer that describes perception/cognition/actuation but omits an **independent** safety supervisor is capped at ~10 marks.
> 2. **Confusing prosthetic with rehabilitation** — These are *not* synonyms. Prosthetic = substitutes a *missing* limb. Rehabilitation = *retrains* an *existing* limb.
> 3. **No units in the formula sheet** — Even in derivations, write the unit (m, rad, m²) next to the determinant of the Jacobian. Examiners reward dimensional awareness.
> 4. **Skipping the singularity condition** — Whenever you derive a Jacobian, *always* compute $\det J$ and state where it vanishes. This is worth 2–3 marks and is the most-overlooked step.
> 5. **Using the wrong Grübler formula** — Use the **planar** (3 n – 1 ...) form for planar mechanisms and the **spatial** (6 n – 5 j₁ ...) form for 3D mechanisms. Mixing them up costs full marks on the part.
> 6. **Vague control terminology** — Specify *which* controller (PID, impedance, admittance, adaptive) and *why* it suits the application. "PD controller" alone is incomplete without the gain tuning rationale.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition triad of an assistive robot:** embodiment + sensing/intelligence + human augmentation.
- **Three functional layers:** perception → cognition → actuation, with a fourth independent **safety layer**.
- **Categories:** mobility, manipulation, rehabilitation, exoskeleton, prosthetic, social/companion, surgical, sensory substitution.
- **Grübler planar mobility:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$. **Spatial:** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$.
- **DH transform product:** $T_0^n = A_1 A_2 \cdots A_n$.
- **Jacobian relation:** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = J(\mathbf{q}) \dot{\mathbf{q}}$; singularity when $\det J = 0$.
- **For the 2-DOF planar arm:** $x = a_1 c_1 + a_2 c_{12}$, $y = a_1 s_1 + a_2 s_{12}$, $\det J = a_1 a_2 \sin\theta_2$, singular at $\theta_2 = 0, \pi$.
- **PD control law:** $\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$ where $e = q_d - q$.
- **Five design principles:** user-centeredness, safety, affordability, transparency, modularity.
- **Standard compliance:** ISO 8373, ISO 13482, IEC 60601.
- **Shared control:** $u = (1-\lambda) u_{\text{user}} + \lambda u_{\text{auto}}$.
- **Workspace DOF rule of thumb:** 6 DOF for full 3D position + orientation; 7 DOF adds redundancy (singularity avoidance).
- **Real-time constraint:** controller loop ≥ **100 Hz**; safety stop response ≤ **50 ms**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robots as Personal Assistants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robots as Personal Assistants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Personal Assistant Robot (PAR)** is defined as a *semi-autonomous or autonomous mechatronic system* equipped with sensors, actuators, artificial intelligence (AI), and human-machine interface (HMI) modules, designed to perform **service, companionship, surveillance, fetching, and physical assistance tasks** for an individual user within domestic, clinical, or institutional environments. Under the **International Federation of Robotics (IFR)** classification, these fall under the category of **"Service Robots for Personal/Domestic Use."**
