| 7 | $P_{\text{req}} = 1/\text{BCVA}_{\text{decimal}}$ | Kestenbaum rule | Dioptres | Prescription of reading magnifier |
| 8 | $M_{\text{tele}} = -f_o / f_e$ | Telescope magnification | Dimensionless | Design of bioptic telescopic spectacles |
| 9 | $D_{\text{exit}} = D_{\text{obj}} / M_{\text{tele}}$ | Exit-pupil diameter | mm | Optical matching to patient pupil |
| 10 | $\theta_{\text{prism}} = \Delta / (100 \times d_v)$ | Field-expansion prism formula | degrees / ∆ | Fresnel-prism low-vision fitting |
| 11 | $M_{\text{CCTV}} = M_{\text{opt}} \times M_{\text{dig}} \times (d_{vd}/0.25)$ | Electronic-magnifier total | Dimensionless | Specification of video magnifiers |
| 12 | $P_{\text{eq}} = P_1 + P_2 - (d/1000) P_1 P_2$ | Equivalent power of two thin lenses separated by $d$ mm | Dioptres | Design of compound telescopic optics |
| 13 | $\alpha_{\text{resolution}} = 1.22 \lambda / D$ | Rayleigh diffraction limit of a magnifier | radians | Maximum resolvable angular detail |
| 14 | $SNR_{\text{contrast}} = (L_{\max} - L_{\min})/(L_{\max} + L_{\min})$ | Michelson contrast | Dimensionless | Electronic contrast-enhancement design |
| 15 | $t_{\text{read}} = (A \times M^2) / (R \times F)$ | Approx. reading time (A = text area, R = recognition rate, F = flicker/refresh) | seconds | Ergonomic evaluation of CCTV |

> **Engineering Insight (KTU 2024):** A common clinical-engineering mistake is to assume that a higher $P$ (dioptric power) always yields a "better" magnifier. The **Kestenbaum rule** combined with the **field-of-view equation** shows that prescribing a 10× magnifier (P = 40 D) to a patient with BCVA 6/24 (P required ≈ 4 D) is wasteful and counterproductive — the FOV becomes tiny (≤ 5 mm) and the WD becomes impractical (2.5 cm). The art of low-vision prescription is to **match the lowest magnification that still permits the patient's task**, with the largest possible field.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Spectacle Magnifier Equivalent Magnification ($M = N/f$)

**Engineering Goal:** Derive the equivalent angular magnification of a simple plus lens worn as a spectacle magnifier, taking a normally-sighted observer's reference viewing distance as 25 cm.

**Step 1 — Define the reference (unaided) viewing condition.**

A person with normal vision can comfortably focus on an object placed at the **near point of distinct vision**, $N = 25$ cm. The angle subtended at the eye (the *visual angle*) by an object of height $h$ placed at this distance is:

$$\theta_{\text{unaided}} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{N}\right) \approx \dfrac{h}{N}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the magnifier.**

A plus lens of focal length $f$ is placed before the eye. The object is now placed at the **focal plane** of the lens (i.e., at distance $f$ from the lens). Light rays from the object emerge parallel after refraction, simulating an object at infinity — which the relaxed (unaccommodated) normal eye can focus effortlessly on the retina.

**Step 3 — Compute the aided visual angle.**

The object of height $h$ at distance $f$ subtends:

$$\theta_{\text{aided}} \approx \dfrac{h}{f}$$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio.**

The equivalent angular magnification is defined as the ratio of the *aided* to the *unaided* visual angle:

$$M = \dfrac{\theta_{\text{aided}}}{\theta_{\text{unaided}}} = \dfrac{h/f}{h/N} = \dfrac{N}{f}$$

**Step 5 — Express in dioptres.**

Because $P$ (in dioptres) $= 1/f$ (in metres), and $N = 0.25$ m:

$$M = N \times P = 0.25 \times P = \dfrac{P}{4}$$

> **Conclusion:** A +8 D lens is therefore a $0.25 \times 8 = 2\times$ spectacle magnifier.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kestenbaum Rule from First Principles

**Engineering Goal:** Show that the dioptric power of a spectacle magnifier required to read newspaper print equals the *reciprocal* of the patient's decimal BCVA.

**Step 1 — Define the visual task.**

Standard newspaper print corresponds to a **1 M-unit** character — a letter 1.45 mm tall, which subtends **5 minutes of arc (5′)** at the eye when viewed at 25 cm. A normally-sighted observer resolves this character with the 1× retinal image.

**Step 2 — Define the patient's acuity.**

A patient with BCVA = 6/60 sees the same character at 6 m, where it subtends only 1/10 the angle — i.e., the character is effectively 10× smaller on their retina. To make it appear the same size as it does to a normal observer at 25 cm, the patient needs **10× angular magnification**.

**Step 3 — Apply the magnifier formula.**

Using $M = P/4$:

$$P = 4M = 4 \times 10 = 40\ \text{D}$$

**Step 4 — Generalize.**

For any BCVA expressed as a decimal fraction (6/60 → 0.1, 3/60 → 0.05, 1/60 → 0.0167):

$$M_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{BCVA}_{\text{decimal}}}$$

$$P_{\text{required}} = 4 \times M_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{4}{\text{BCVA}_{\text{decimal}}}$$

> **Note:** The Kestenbaum rule as classically stated (1/BCVA) refers to the *equivalent print size* $M$ rather than dioptres. The 1/BCVA value gives the *times magnification* needed; to convert to dioptres, multiply by 4.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Telescope Magnification and Exit Pupil Matching

**Step 1 — Telescope optics.**

A Galilean telescope has an objective lens of focal length $f_o$ and a diverging eyepiece of focal length $f_e$ (with $f_e < 0$). An incoming parallel beam is brought to an intermediate focus by the objective, then the diverging eyepiece renders it parallel to the eye.

**Step 2 — Angular magnification.**

The angular magnification of any afocal telescope is the ratio of the focal lengths (with sign):

$$M_{\text{tele}} = -\dfrac{f_o}{f_e}$$

For a Galilean telescope ($f_e < 0$), the result is positive, giving an *erect* image. For a Keplerian telescope ($f_e > 0$), the result is negative, giving an *inverted* image (hence the need for image-erecting prisms).

**Step 3 — Exit pupil geometry.**

Rays passing through the edge of the objective at height $h_o$ exit the eyepiece at height $h_e$. By similar triangles in the afocal system:

$$h_e = \dfrac{f_e}{f_o} \times h_o = \dfrac{h_o}{M_{\text{tele}}}$$

The exit-pupil diameter is therefore:

$$D_{\text{exit}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{objective}}}{M_{\text{tele}}}$$

**Step 4 — Matching to the patient.**

The patient's pupil in bright daylight is typically 2–3 mm; in dim conditions, 5–7 mm. If $D_{\text{exit}} > 7$ mm, light is wasted (vignetting by the iris); if $D_{\text{exit}} < 2$ mm, the image is dim and the diffraction limit (Eq. 13) becomes clinically significant.

> **Design rule:** For daytime bioptic telescopic spectacles, $D_{\text{exit}}$ should be **2 to 4 mm** to match the photopic pupil.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Field of View of a Stand Magnifier with Eye at the Lens

**Step 1 — Geometry.**

A stand magnifier of lens diameter $D$ rests on the page, with the eye at the lens's anterior vertex. The lens produces a virtual image at the *least distance of distinct vision* (25 cm) for comfortable viewing.

**Step 2 — Ray trace.**

A ray from the edge of the lens aperture at height $D/2$ emerges after refraction toward the eye. The angle this ray makes with the optical axis, when the object is at the focal plane, equals $D/(2f)$.

**Step 3 — Apparent field of view.**

Projecting the lens aperture into the image plane at 25 cm, the *apparent* field diameter is:

$$d_{\text{apparent FOV}} = D \times \dfrac{N}{f} = D \times M$$

**Step 4 — The *real* field of view on the page.**

The lens sees a real object plane on the page, and the diameter of that patch is simply $D$ (the lens diameter), because the lens-to-object distance is just the stand-height. Therefore the real FOV on the page equals the lens diameter itself, and the magnification $M$ enlarges this real patch to an *apparent* patch of diameter $D \times M$ in the patient's perceived field.

> **Engineering insight:** To increase the FOV at a fixed magnification, the **only** parameter available to the designer is $D$. That is why low-vision magnifiers with $M > 10\times$ are physically *large* (50–100 mm diameter) — they cannot be made smaller without sacrificing FOV.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Device Selection by Pathology (KTU 2024 Clinical Engineering)

| Ocular Pathology | Principal Visual Deficit | Recommended SPVA Class | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Age-related macular degeneration (AMD)** | Central scotoma, reduced acuity | Spectacle microscope + CCTV for reading; prism relocation to use eccentric viewing | Magnify to overcome scotoma; relocate image to healthy parafoveal retina |
| **Diabetic retinopathy / maculopathy** | Variable central blur, contrast loss | High-contrast filters + CCTV with contrast inversion | Contrast enhancement compensates for retinal oedema |
| **Retinitis pigmentosa (RP)** | Constricted peripheral field, night blindness | Fresnel prism field-expanders + amber/red tinted filters | Field expansion to overcome tunnel vision; filters reduce rod saturation |
| **Glaucoma (advanced)** | Peripheral field loss, glare | Yellow tints, anti-glare coatings, prism relocation | Reduce disability glare; expand remaining field |
| **Cataract (pre-operative)** | Diffuse blur, glare, low-contrast | High-illumination magnifier + absorptive lenses | Brighter image penetrates cloudy lens |
| **Albinism / congenital nystagmus** | Photophobia, reduced acuity, glare | Photochromic / polarised tints, high-power spectacle loupes | Reduce light scatter; magnify because acuity is poor |
| **Optic atrophy** | Severe acuity loss (6/60 or worse) | CCTV with high zoom (30–75×) and OCR text-to-speech | Magnification + sensory substitution (auditory) |
| **Homonymous hemianopia** | Half-field loss (e.g., post-stroke) | Peli prisms (20–40 ∆) on temporal side of spectacles | Image relocation to compensate for lost hemifield |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Take-away:** Device selection is **not** based on the disease label alone, but on three functional dimensions: **(1) required visual angle** (drives M), **(2) field of view** (drives aperture), and **(3) lighting/glare environment** (drives filter and illumination choice). The clinical exam question often asks: *"Given a patient with BCVA 6/36 and a 15° central scotoma, design a low-vision reading solution"* — the model answer must show the Kestenbaum calculation, the FOV check, and the illumination strategy in one integrated response.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern Reminder:**
> - **Part A:** Short answer, **3 marks each**, no choice, ~9–10 questions per paper.
> - **Part B:** Long answer, **14 marks each**, internal choice between two full questions (Or-pattern). Each Part B question carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.
> - Module-3 distribution typically contributes **2 Part A + 1 Part B** questions in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024 Style]**

Define *Special Purpose Visual Aids* and list any **four** broad categories with one example device in each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Special Purpose Visual Aids (SPVAs) are non-conventional optical, electro-optical, or electronic devices designed to rehabilitate patients whose visual impairment cannot be adequately corrected by standard spectacles or contact lenses, typically those with best-corrected visual acuity (BCVA) in the range of **6/18 to 3/60** (low vision per WHO classification). (1 mark)

The four broad categories with examples are:

| # | Category | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Optical near-vision aids | Spectacle-mounted microscope (loupe) |
| 2 | Optical distance-vision aids | Galilean bioptic telescope |
| 3 | Electronic / electro-optical aids | CCTV video magnifier with OCR |
| 4 | Sensory substitution / tactile aids | Refreshable Braille display |

(2 marks — ½ mark per correct category-example pair)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2023 Style]**

State the **Kestenbaum rule** and compute the required spectacle-magnifier power for a patient with BCVA **6/36**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Kestenbaum rule** states that the *equivalent angular magnification* ($M$) required to enable a low-vision patient to read newspaper print (1 M-equivalent) is approximately the *reciprocal* of the patient's decimal visual acuity. (1 mark)

Mathematically:

$$M_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{BCVA}_{\text{decimal}}}$$

(1 mark)

For a patient with BCVA = 6/36:

$$\text{BCVA}_{\text{decimal}} = \dfrac{6}{36} = 0.1667$$

$$M_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{1}{0.1667} = 6\times$$

The required spectacle-magnifier dioptric power:

$$P = 4M = 4 \times 6 = 24\ \text{D}$$

(1 mark)

**Final Answer:** A **+24 D spectacle microscope** (≈ **6× magnification**) is required, with a working distance of $1/24\ \text{m} \approx 4.2$ cm.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Module 3, Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024 Style]**

> *(a)* With the aid of a labelled block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a closed-circuit television (CCTV) video magnifier system** used in low-vision rehabilitation. Discuss the role of contrast inversion, edge enhancement, and digital zoom. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* A 68-year-old patient has BCVA 6/60 in the better eye due to age-related macular degeneration. The patient wishes to read a bank statement. **(i)** Calculate the **spectacle-magnifier power** required using the Kestenbaum rule. **(ii)** Compute the **working distance** of this magnifier. **(iii)** State **two disadvantages** of prescribing a single high-power spectacle microscope (e.g., +40 D) instead of a CCTV system for this patient. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A CCTV video magnifier replaces the optical path between the patient's eye and the page with a **camera–display chain**, enabling magnification, contrast manipulation, and freeze-frame that are not physically possible with a single lens. (1 mark)

**Block diagram (7 functional blocks):**

1. **Object stage** — X-Y reading table for smooth scanning.
2. **Integrated LED illumination** — cool-white, high-CRI ring light to lift retinal contrast.
3. **Camera** — high-resolution CCD/CMOS sensor, 1080p or higher, with motorised zoom objective.
4. **Analogue front-end** — auto-gain, white-balance, gamma correction.
5. **ADC → DSP** — digitisation, then image processing (zoom, contrast inversion, edge enhancement, false-colour mapping).
6. **Display** — 19″ to 27″ LCD/OLED monitor placed at the patient's preferred reading distance.
7. **User console** — zoom rocker, brightness, contrast, polarity toggle, freeze frame, line markers.

(3 marks — ½ mark per block, well-labelled diagram earns the rest)

**Role of key features:**

- **Contrast inversion** flips white-on-black to black-on-white. Many patients with macular disease retain higher contrast sensitivity to *white letters on black* (reduced glare from scattered light). (1 mark)
- **Edge enhancement** boosts high-spatial-frequency components, sharpening letter borders — directly compensating for the receptive-field dropout in the diseased macula. (1 mark)
- **Digital zoom** permits continuous magnification from ~2× to 75× without changing the working distance, eliminating the need to bring the eye uncomfortably close. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Spectacle-magnifier power by Kestenbaum rule:** (3 marks)

$$\text{BCVA}_{\text{decimal}} = \dfrac{6}{60} = 0.1$$

$$M_{\text{required}} = \dfrac{1}{0.1} = 10\times$$

$$P_{\text{required}} = 4M = 4 \times 10 = \mathbf{40\ D}$$

**(ii) Working distance:** (2 marks)

$$WD = \dfrac{1}{P} = \dfrac{1}{40}\ \text{m} = 0.025\ \text{m} = \mathbf{2.5\ cm}$$

**(iii) Two disadvantages of a +40 D spectacle microscope (vs. CCTV):** (2 marks)

1. **Field of view collapse.** For a 50 mm lens at 10×, FOV = 50/10 = 5 mm — only a few characters visible at a time; reading becomes painfully slow.
2. **Illumination problem.** At 2.5 cm, ambient illumination is physically obstructed by the patient's nose, brow, and the lens housing itself; the page appears dim and uneven, defeating the very purpose of magnification.
3. *(Optional 3rd point for completeness)* **Convergence/accommodation strain.** The eyes must converge to 2.5 cm, which is impossible for an elderly patient with reduced binocular reserve; the patient can effectively use only one eye, halving the available retinal real estate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students compute $P = 40$ D correctly but **omit the working distance calculation** (a full 2 marks) or **forget the units** ($WD$ in cm, not m). Also, in Part (a) the **contrast-inversion physiology** is the high-value content — do not merely mention it; *explain why* AMD patients prefer white-on-black (less intra-retinal scatter). Dropping the "why" costs 1 full mark on a 7-mark sub-question.

---

### Question B — Module 3, Choice 2 (Internal-Choice Alternative)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2023 Style]**

> *(a)* Explain with neat diagrams the construction, optical principle, and clinical applications of a **Galilean telescopic spectacle** used as a low-vision distance aid. Compare its merits and demerits with a **Keplerian telescope** for the same purpose. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* A 22-year-old law student with retinitis pigmentosa has tunnel vision of 8° diameter in each eye and wishes to ambulate independently in a busy college campus. **(i)** Calculate the **prism dioptre power** required to expand the effective field by 15°, assuming a vertex distance of 12 mm. **(ii)** Recommend **two additional non-prism low-vision interventions** that would complement the prism device. **(iii)** Briefly justify why amber-tinted lenses are clinically preferred over neutral-grey tints in this patient. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Galilean telescope construction (2 marks):**

A Galilean telescope consists of a **converging objective** of focal length $f_o$ (e.g., +100 mm) and a **diverging eyepiece** of focal length $f_e$ (e.g., −25 mm for a 4× system), separated by the algebraic sum $|f_o| - |f_e|$ (e.g., 75 mm). The overall tube is short, lightweight, and may be spectacle-mounted (bioptic) or hand-held (monocular). A field stop is often placed at the common focal plane to limit aberration.

**Optical principle (2 marks):**

Parallel rays from a distant object are brought to a real focus by the objective, *before* reaching the eyepiece. The diverging eyepiece intercepts these converging rays and renders them *parallel* before they enter the patient's eye. Because the system is *afocal*, the eye remains relaxed (no accommodation). The angular magnification is:

$$M = -\dfrac{f_o}{f_e} = -\dfrac{+100}{-25} = +4\times$$

The positive sign confirms an **erect** image — essential for clinical use.

**Clinical applications (1 mark):**

- Watching television (1.5× to 2.5×)
- Classroom blackboard viewing (2.5× to 4×)
- Theatre, sports, face recognition (1.5× to 3×)
- Bioptic driving in some jurisdictions (4× to 6×)

**Galilean vs. Keplerian comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Galilean | Keplerian |
|---|---|---|
| Image | Erect (no erecting prism) | Inverted (needs prisms) |
| Tube length | Short (≈ $|f_o| - |f_e|$) | Long (≈ $f_o + f_e$) |
| Magnification range | 1.5× to 4× (clinical limit) | 4× to 12× |
| Field of view | Narrow at high $M$ | Wider at equivalent $M$ |
| Aberration control | Limited (spherical) | Excellent (relay lenses) |
| Weight, cosmetics | Light, cosmetically acceptable | Bulkier |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

**Conclusion:** Galilean systems are preferred for *low-magnification, cosmetically acceptable, erect-image* distance tasks. Keplerian systems are reserved for *high-magnification, vocational-distance* spotting where inverted image can be corrected by prism erecting modules.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Prism power for 15° field expansion at 12 mm vertex distance:** (3 marks)

The relation between prism power, vertex distance, and field shift is:

$$\text{Field shift} = 0.57 \times \Delta \times d_v \quad (\Delta \text{ in prism dioptres, } d_v \text{ in cm})$$

or equivalently, the required prism dioptre:

$$\Delta = \dfrac{\theta_{\text{shift (degrees)}}}{0.57 \times d_v \text{(cm)}}$$

Substituting $\theta = 15°$ and $d_v = 1.2$ cm:

$$\Delta = \dfrac{15}{0.57 \times 1.2} = \dfrac{15}{0.684} \approx 21.9\ \Delta$$

**Round to ~22 ∆**, typically realised as a base-out **Fresnel prism** (to keep weight low) on the temporal aspect of the spectacle lens.

**(ii) Two complementary non-prism low-vision interventions:** (2 marks)

1. **Amber / 500 nm cut-off tinted lenses** to reduce rod-system saturation and improve contrast in bright daylight.
2. **Orientation-and-mobility (O&M) training with a long cane or guide-dog** combined with **reverse-contrast CCTV** for any residual central reading needs; **tactile landmark labels** on classroom doors; **audible traffic-signal assistive devices** at campus crossings.

**(iii) Why amber tints over neutral-grey in RP:** (2 marks)

In retinitis pigmentosa, the *rod* photoreceptors are preferentially lost, and the remaining *cones* are highly susceptible to photophobic glare from short-wavelength (blue) light. An **amber tint (cut-off ~500 nm)** blocks the blue–green wavelengths that:

- Scatter most heavily within the media and at the level of the damaged outer retina,
- Cause disabling glare, and
- May contribute to further phototoxic damage of the surviving cones.

A neutral-grey tint merely reduces overall luminance *uniformly* across wavelengths — it does not selectively remove the offending blue band, and it reduces the patient's already-compromised mesopic vision. The amber filter therefore improves *functional contrast* rather than merely dimming the world, which is the central clinical rationale.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In the prism-calculation sub-part, the most common error is **forgetting the conversion factor 0.57** between prism dioptres and degrees (or equivalently, mixing up centimetres and millimetres for vertex distance). A student who writes "15 ÷ 1.2 = 12.5 ∆" will lose **2 of 3 marks**. Always state the formula first, substitute carefully, and box the numerical answer with units (∆ or prism dioptres). Also, in Part (a), examiners specifically look for the **sign convention** in $M_{\text{tele}}$ — a missing minus sign on a Keplerian magnification indicates the student has not understood the image-inversion principle and forfeits a mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Special Purpose Visual Aids**

- **Definition:** SPVAs are devices for patients with **BCVA 6/18 to 3/60** (WHO low vision) where standard refractive correction is insufficient.
- **Three families:** (i) Optical/non-optical, (ii) Electronic/electro-optical, (iii) Sensory substitution/tactile. Know **two examples** of each.
- **Five must-know formulas:**
  1. $M = N/f = P/4$ — spectacle magnifier.
  2. $M = P/4 + 1$ — handheld/stand magnifier (eye at lens).
  3. $d_{\text{FOV}} = D/M$ — field of view.
  4. $P = 1/\text{BCVA}_{\text{decimal}} \times 4$ — Kestenbaum rule (in dioptres).
  5. $M_{\text{tele}} = -f_o/f_e$ — telescope magnification (sign carries image-orientation info).
- **Galilean vs. Keplerian:** Galilean = erect, short, ≤ 4×. Keplerian = inverted, long, 4–12×, needs erecting prism.
- **Exit-pupil rule:** $D_{\text{exit}} = D_{\text{obj}} / M_{\text{tele}}$; match to patient's photopic pupil (2–4 mm).
- **Field expansion:** Fresnel or Peli prisms (15–40 ∆) base-out for RP / hemianopia; formula: $\Delta = \theta_{\text{shift}} / (0.57 \times d_v \text{ cm})$.
- **CCTV architecture:** Camera → AFE → ADC → DSP (zoom, invert, enhance) → DAC → Display. Contrast inversion is *clinically*, not just cosmetically, valuable.
- **Pathology-to-device matching:** AMD → spectacle loupe + CCTV; RP → prism + amber tints; cataract → illumination + absorptive lens; hemianopia → Peli prisms; profound loss → OCR text-to-speech.
- **Common examiner traps:**
  - Converting $P$ to $f$ and vice versa.
  - Forgetting the 0.57 factor in prism–degree conversion.
  - Failing to justify the *type* of magnifier (hand vs. stand) by the patient's age and tremor.
  - Quoting magnification as a "lens property" instead of as an *equivalent angular* quantity.
  - Ignoring the **field-of-view** trade-off when prescribing high-power magnifiers.
- **Clinical-engineering insight:** The "best" SPVA is the one with the **lowest magnification** that still allows the patient's *specific task* to be completed, with the **largest field of view**, and the **most comfortable working distance**. Magnification is a *means*, not a *goal*.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Special Purpose Visual Aids.**
> *(Compiled per KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 alignment, OBE-mapped Course Outcomes.)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hearing Aids - Types - Body Worn
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Hearing Aids - Types - Body Worn ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) A **Body-Worn Hearing Aid (BWHA)** is a type of air-conduction hearing aid in which the **microphone, amplifier, battery, and signal-processing circuitry** are housed in a single rigid case (typically measuring approximately 7 cm × 5 cm × 2 cm) that is worn on the body — usually clipped to a shirt pocket, clipped to a belt, or hung from a neck cord. The amplified electrical signal is delivered to the ear through a flexible external cable (the "cords") that terminates in a small **button receiver / external earphone**, which is placed inside or against the concha of the ear, often held in place by a custom earmould. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Body-worn hearing aids are categorized as the *earliest commercially mass-produced* style of wearable electronic hearing aid, having been in clinical use since the 1930s (carbon models) and the 1950s (transistor models). They remain pedagogically important in biomedical engineering curricula because they isolate every functional block of a hearing aid into a single, easily demonstrable unit. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are sitting in the **back row of a huge noisy lecture hall** trying to hear a soft-spoken friend seated in the front. You ask your friend to use a **walkie-talkie clipped to their chest pocket**: their voice is picked up by a microphone on the chest unit, the electronics inside amplify it, and a thin wire carries the louder sound to a tiny speaker placed near your ear. That is exactly how a body-worn hearing aid works — the entire "amplifier box" is worn on the chest like a small transistor radio, and a thin cord carries the louder, cleaner sound to the ear. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Performance Metric (KTU 2024):** The peak acoustic gain of a typical body-worn hearing aid is **between 50 dB and 80 dB**, with a **maximum saturated SSPL (Sound Pressure Level) of 110 dB to 135 dB SPL**, making it suitable for users with **severe to profound** sensorineural or conductive hearing loss. The standard reference test gain measurement follows **ANSI S3.22 / IEC 60118-0** standards. ### 1.3 Position in the Hearing Aid Family Tree Hearing aids, by their **wearing position**, are classified into five major families: 1. **Body-Worn (BWHA)** — case on torso, cord to ear (oldest) 2. **Behind-The-Ear (BTE)** — case behind the auricle 3. **In-The-Ear (ITE)** — case fills the concha 4. **In-The-Canal (ITC)** — case in the ear canal 5. **Completely-In-Canal (CIC)** — case deep in the canal (smallest) This family classification is the most commonly examined in KTU Module 4 short-answer questions. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture A body-worn hearing aid consists of the following functional blocks arranged in series: **Microphone → Pre-amplifier → Tone Control / Filter → Power Amplifier → Volume Control → Cord → External Receiver (Earphone) → Earmould → Ear Canal** - **Microphone**: A miniature electret or dynamic microphone converts acoustic pressure waves (sound) into a small alternating electrical voltage. Typical sensitivity: **−46 dB to −54 dB re 1 V/Pa**. - **Pre-amplifier**: A low-noise transistor or op-amp stage (e.g., TL072, AD8628) provides the initial voltage gain $A_{v1}$, typically 10× to 30× (20 dB to 30 dB). - **Tone Control / Filter**: Passive RC network or active filter (low-pass, high-pass, or band-pass) shapes the frequency response. Typical low-frequency cut-off $f_L \approx 200$ Hz, high-frequency cut-off $f_H \approx 5000$ Hz. - **Power Amplifier**: Class-A or Class-AB push-pull amplifier delivers the bulk of the gain. Total gain $A_{v,\text{total}}$ can be 60 dB to 75 dB. - **Volume Control**: A logarithmic potentiometer (audio taper) varies the overall gain — usually marked in 1 dB to 5 dB detents. - **Cord**: 3-conductor shielded cable, length **approximately 80 cm to 100 cm**, terminated in a sub-miniature connector (e.g., 2.5 mm or 3.5 mm jack at the receiver). - **External Receiver**: A button-type magnetic or balanced-armature transducer with sensitivity around **100 dB SPL/mW** at 1 kHz. ### 2.2 Power Supply & Battery Body-worn aids use **larger primary cells** because the case volume permits it. - Common cell types: **AA (R6), C (R14), or 675 (mercury-replaced by zinc-air)**. - Operating voltage: **1.3 V to 1.5 V** for single cell, or **2.6 V to 3.0 V** for two cells. - Battery life: **200 to 700 hours** (significantly longer than ITE/CIC aids which last 50–150 hours). - Current drain: **3 mA to 12 mA** depending on gain setting and class of amplifier. > [!NOTE] > **Why bigger battery matters for KTU reasoning:** Battery capacity in mAh scales roughly with volume. A C-cell (≈26 cm³) holds about **7800 mAh**, ~50× the capacity of a tiny zinc-air 312 cell (160 mAh, ≈0.5 cm³). This is why body-worn aids can drive a high-power output stage continuously. ### 2.3 The Acoustic Coupling Chain Sound entering the ear canal from a body-worn system travels as follows: $$p_{\text{ear}}(f) = p_{\text{env}}(f) \cdot G_{\text{mic}}(f) \cdot A_{v,\text{total}}(f) \cdot \eta_{\text{cord}}(f) \cdot S_{\text{rec}}(f) \cdot T_{\text{mould}}(f) \cdot 10^{\frac{\text{REIG}_{\text{ear}}}{20}}$$ where: - $p_{\text{env}}(f)$ = environmental acoustic pressure at the microphone - $G_{\text{mic}}(f)$ = microphone sensitivity (V/Pa) - $A_{v,\text{total}}(f)$ = total voltage gain of the amplifier - $\eta_{\text{cord}}(f)$ = cord transmission efficiency (≈ 0.85 to 0.95 for $f < 8$ kHz) - $S_{\text{rec}}(f)$ = receiver sensitivity (Pa/V) - $T_{\text{mould}}(f)$ | transfer function of the earmould (often $\approx 1$ to 1.2 for an open mould at 1 kHz) - $\text{REIG}_{\text{ear}}$ | Real-Ear Insertion Gain in dB, typically 25 dB to 50 dB for a BWHA ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Table 2.1 — KTU High-Yield Formula Reference for Body-Worn Hearing Aids** | # | Quantity / Symbol | Formula / Standard Value | Units | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Acoustic Gain $G$ | $G = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{\text{out}}}{p_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | dB | Pressure amplification delivered to the eardrum | | 2 | Total Voltage Gain $A_v$ | $A_v = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} \cdot A_{v3}$ | V/V (linear) | Cascaded amplifier stages | | 3 | SSPL90 (max output) | $p_{\text{max}} = 110$ to $135$ | dB SPL | Saturation sound pressure level at 90 dB input | | 4 | REIG (Real-Ear Insertion Gain) | $\text{REIG} = \text{REAG} - \text{REUG}$ | dB | Net benefit after removing the unoccluded ear canal resonance | | 5 | Battery life $T$ | $T = \dfrac{C_{\text{mAh}}}{I_{\text{mA}}}$ | hours | mAh ÷ mA | | 6 | Microphone Sensitivity | $S_{m} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{out}}}{1\,\text{Pa}}\right)$ | dB re 1 V/Pa | Voltage delivered per Pascal of acoustic pressure | | 7 | Cut-off frequency (1st-order RC) | $f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}$ | Hz | Bandwidth limit of tone control | | 8 | Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) | $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_{n}^{2}}}{V_{1}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Non-linearity of amplifier stage | | 9 | Equivalent Input Noise (EIN) | $\text{EIN} \le 25$ to $30$ | dB SPL | Internal self-noise floor of the aid | | 10 | Standard reference input | $p_{\text{ref}} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$ | Pa | Threshold of hearing reference pressure | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Severe/Profound Pediatric Cases:** Because BTE and custom aids often cannot produce enough gain for a child with **> 90 dB HL loss**, body-worn aids were historically prescribed for infants and toddlers prior to the wide availability of high-gain digital BTEs. - **Resource-Limited Settings:** In rural clinics of low-and-middle-income countries, body-worn aids are still used because they are **robust, repairable with off-the-shelf parts, and run on AA batteries available everywhere**. - **Long-Duration Use:** Patients who wear their aid > 16 h/day benefit from the very long battery life of the C-cell version. - **Demonstrative Tool in Biomedical Labs:** Because the body case is large enough, students can open it, identify the microphone, amplifier IC, potentiometer, battery contacts, and receiver, and **trace the signal path with an oscilloscope** — something not feasible with miniature BTE/ITC/CIC aids. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Overall Acoustic Gain (Three-Stage Amplifier) > **Problem (KTU ESE pattern):** A body-worn hearing aid uses three cascaded amplifier stages. Stage 1 (pre-amp) has voltage gain $A_{v1} = 10$, Stage 2 (tone-control stage) has gain $A_{v2} = 8$, and Stage 3 (output power stage) has gain $A_{v3} = 25$. The microphone sensitivity is $S_m = -50$ dB re 1 V/Pa and the receiver sensitivity is $S_r = 100$ dB SPL / V (i.e., 2 Pa/V). A 60 dB SPL speech signal impinges on the microphone. Compute (a) the total voltage gain in dB, (b) the voltage at the receiver input, and (c) the acoustic output pressure in dB SPL at the eardrum. #### Part (a) — Total Voltage Gain in dB **Step 1: Compute the total linear voltage gain.** $$A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \times A_{v2} \times A_{v3}$$ $$A_{v,\text{total}} = 10 \times 8 \times 25 = 2000$$ **Step 2: Convert linear gain to dB.** $$A_{v,\text{total}}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(2000)$$ $$A_{v,\text{total}}(\text{dB}) = 20 \times \left(\log_{10}(2) + \log_{10}(1000)\right)$$ $$A_{v,\text{total}}(\text{dB}) = 20 \times (0.3010 + 3) = 20 \times 3.3010 = 66.02 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 3: Equivalent individual-stage dB values for verification.** $$ \begin{aligned} A_{v1}(\text{dB}) &= 20 \log_{10}(10) &= 20.00 \text{ dB}\\ A_{v2}(\text{dB}) &= 20 \log_{10}(8) &= 18.06 \text{ dB}\\ A_{v3}(\text{dB}) &= 20 \log_{10}(25) &= 27.96 \text{ dB} \end{aligned} $$ $$A_{v,\text{total}}(\text{dB}) = 20.00 + 18.06 + 27.96 = 66.02 \text{ dB} \quad \checkmark$$ #### Part (b) — Voltage at the Receiver Input **Step 4: Convert the 60 dB SPL input into a pressure in Pascals.** $$p_{\text{in}} = p_{\text{ref}} \times 10^{\,S_{\text{SPL}}/20} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{60/20}$$ $$p_{\text{in}} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 10^{3} = 20 \times 10^{-3} = 0.020 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 5: Convert microphone sensitivity from dB to V/Pa (linear).** $$S_m(\text{V/Pa}) = 10^{S_m(\text{dB})/20} = 10^{-50/20} = 10^{-2.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V/Pa}$$ **Step 6: Compute microphone output voltage.** $$V_{\text{mic}} = p_{\text{in}} \times S_m = 0.020 \times 3.162 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$V_{\text{mic}} = 6.324 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V} = 63.24 \,\mu\text{V}$$ **Step 7: Apply the total voltage gain to find the voltage at the receiver input.** $$V_{\text{rec,in}} = V_{\text{mic}} \times A_{v,\text{total}} = 6.324 \times 10^{-5} \times 2000$$ $$V_{\text{rec,in}} = 0.1265 \text{ V} \approx 126.5 \text{ mV}$$ #### Part (c) — Acoustic Output Pressure in dB SPL **Step 8: Apply receiver sensitivity.** $$p_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{rec,in}} \times S_r = 0.1265 \times 2 = 0.2530 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 9: Convert output pressure back to dB SPL.** $$S_{\text{out}}(\text{dB SPL}) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{out}}}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right)$$ $$S_{\text{out}}(\text{dB SPL}) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.2530}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(12{,}651)$$ $$S_{\text{out}}(\text{dB SPL}) = 20 \times \log_{10}(1.2651 \times 10^{4})$$ $$S_{\text{out}}(\text{dB SPL}) = 20 \times (4 + 0.1021) = 20 \times 4.1021 = 82.04 \text{ dB SPL}$$ **Step 10: Compute the acoustic gain (output SPL − input SPL).** $$G_{\text{acoustic}} = 82.04 - 60.00 = 22.04 \text{ dB}$$ > This 22 dB acoustic gain is consistent with a body-worn hearing aid for a **moderate** loss; severe cases typically achieve **40–55 dB** of acoustic gain at the eardrum. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Body-Worn vs. Other Styles > [!NOTE] > **Table 3.1 — Analytical Decision Matrix (High-Yield KTU Comparison)** | Parameter | Body-Worn | BTE | ITE | ITC | CIC | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Case location | Chest / belt | Behind ear | Concha | Canal entrance | Deep canal | | Peak gain (dB) | 50–80 | 35–75 | 25–55 | 20–45 | 15–35 | | SSPL90 (dB SPL) | 110–135 | 110–135 | 105–125 | 100–120 | 100–115 | | Battery | AA / C / 675 | 13 / 675 | 13 / 312 | 312 | 10 / 312 | | Battery life (h) | 200–700 | 150–400 | 80–250 | 70–200 | 50–150 | | Cosmetic appeal | **Poor** | Moderate | Good | Very good | Excellent | | Clothing noise | **High** | None | None | None | None | | Severe loss use | **Excellent** | Good | Fair | Limited | Not suitable | | Repairability | **Easy** | Easy | Difficult | Difficult | Very difficult | | Typical user | Pediatric / rural / profound loss | All ages | Adults | Adults | Adults (mild) | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Body-Worn Hearing Aid ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Acoustic Source
Speech Signal] --> B[Microphone
Electret Type] B --> C[Pre Amplifier
Stage 1 Gain 20 dB] C --> D[Tone Control
RC Filter Network] D --> E[Power Amplifier
Class AB Push Pull] E --> F[Volume Control
Audio Taper Pot] F --> G[Output Cord
3 Conductor 90 cm] G --> H[External Receiver
Button Earphone] H --> I[Custom Earmould
Acrylic or Silicone] I --> J[Ear Canal
Eardrum Stimulation] K[AA or C Cell Battery
1.5 V DC] -.powers.-> C K -.powers.-> E L[On Off Switch] -.controls.-> K ``` **Reading the diagram:** Acoustic pressure from a speech source is captured by the electret microphone on the body case. The signal passes through a pre-amplifier (Stage 1, ~20 dB), then through a passive RC tone-control filter that shapes the frequency response, then a high-power Class-AB output stage (~25–40 dB), then a logarithmic volume control (audio-taper potentiometer). The amplified electrical signal travels down a 3-conductor shielded cord (~90 cm long) to an external button-type magnetic receiver, which converts the electrical signal back into sound pressure. The sound is delivered to the ear canal through a custom earmould, and finally reaches the eardrum. The whole circuit is powered by a single AA or C cell (1.5 V) with an on-off switch. ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Signal Path with Acoustic Parameters ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[Pin 60 dB SPL] -->|p equals 0.020 Pa| P1[Mic Output
63 microvolt] P1 -->|Av1 equals 10| P2[After Preamp
0.633 mV] P2 -->|Av2 equals 8| P3[After Tone Ctrl
5.06 mV] P3 -->|Av3 equals 25| P4[After Power Amp
126.5 mV] P4 -->|Cord eta equals 0.90| P5[At Receiver
113.8 mV] P5 -->|Sr equals 2 Pa per V| P6[Ear Canal SPL
82 dB SPL] P6 -->|REIG correction| P7[Effective Gain
22 dB at Eardrum] ``` **Reading the diagram:** This is the analytical signal-flow corresponding exactly to the derivation in Section 3.1. It traces the pressure, voltage, and SPL level at every node in the system, making it useful for board-exam sketching answers. ### 4.3 Physical Layout Schematic (ASCII) ``` +--------------------------------------+ | BODY-WORN CASE | | +-------+ +-------+ +-------+ | Acoustic | | MIC |---| PRE |---| TONE | | input ---> | +-------+ +-------+ +-------+ | | | | | +-------+ | | | VOLUME| | | +-------+ | | | | | +-------+ | | | PWR | | | +-------+ | | | | | [AA / C CELL 1.5 V] [ON-OFF] | +-----------------|-----|-------------+ | | v v +-----------------+ | 3-COND CORD | (~90 cm) +-----------------+ | v +-----------------+ | BUTTON RX | | (External | | Earphone) | +-----------------+ | v [Earmould] --> [Ear Canal] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1. (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` > Define a **body-worn hearing aid**. State any **two** advantages and **two** disadvantages of body-worn aids compared to behind-the-ear (BTE) aids. **Model Answer (Examiner's Key):** A body-worn hearing aid is an electroacoustic device in which the **microphone, amplifier, battery, and tone-control circuitry are housed in a single case worn on the body** (typically clipped to a chest pocket or belt), and the amplified sound is delivered to the ear through a **flexible external cord terminating in a button receiver** placed in the ear canal with a custom earmould. **Advantages over BTE:** 1. **High acoustic gain** (50–80 dB) and very high SSPL90 (110–135 dB SPL) — suitable for **severe to profound** hearing losses. 2. **Long battery life** (200–700 hours) because the case accommodates **AA or C cells**, vs. BTE which uses miniature 13/675 cells. 3. **Easy to repair and service** because the electronics are accessible in the larger case. **Disadvantages compared to BTE:** 1. **Poor cosmetic acceptance** — the body case is large and visible, with a visible cord. 2. **Clothing noise** — friction of clothes against the body-case microphone generates unwanted low-frequency noise. 3. **Cord tangles and breakage** — the external cable is a moving mechanical part that fails with use. 4. **Restricts body movement** — the cord length limits comfortable head movement. **(Examiner's note:** Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for two advantages, 1 mark for two disadvantages.)** --- #### **Question 2. (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` > List the **main functional blocks** of a body-worn hearing aid in the order in which the electrical signal flows, starting from the microphone. Briefly state the function of any **two** blocks. **Model Answer:** The electrical signal flows through the following blocks in order: **Microphone → Pre-amplifier → Tone control → Power amplifier → Volume control → Cord → External receiver (button earphone) → Earmould → Ear canal.** **Functions of two blocks (any two):** 1. **Pre-amplifier:** Provides the initial low-noise voltage gain (typically 20 dB) to bring the weak microphone signal (tens of microvolts) up to a level suitable for further amplification. Uses low-noise transistors or op-amps (e.g., TL072, AD8628) to keep the Equivalent Input Noise (EIN) below 30 dB SPL. 2. **Tone Control:** A passive RC network (or active filter) that shapes the frequency response, allowing low-frequency emphasis for conductive losses or high-frequency emphasis for steeply sloping sensorineural losses. Typical cut-offs: $f_L = 200$ Hz and $f_H = 5$ kHz. 3. **External Receiver:** A magnetic or balanced-armature transducer that converts the amplified electrical signal back into sound pressure waves. Sensitivity is typically 100 dB SPL/mW at 1 kHz. 4. **Volume Control:** A logarithmic (audio-taper) potentiometer that allows the user to vary the overall gain continuously, usually with detents every 1–5 dB. --- ### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions > **Internal Choice (Module 4):** Answer **ONE** of the following 14-mark questions. --- #### **Option A — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 + Model 2024 Scheme]` > **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a body-worn hearing aid**. List the **typical values** of gain, SSPL90, battery type, and battery life. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Compare a body-worn hearing aid with a **BTE (Behind-The-Ear) hearing aid** under the following heads: (i) location of components, (ii) acoustic gain, (iii) cosmetic appeal, (iv) suitability for severe/profound loss, (v) battery life, (vi) maintenance. **(7 Marks)** ##### **Model Solution for (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks)** **Block diagram (1.5 marks):** *(see Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid block diagram — reproduce in hand-drawn form for the exam)* The functional blocks of a body-worn hearing aid are: **Microphone → Pre-amplifier → Tone control → Power amplifier → Volume control → Output cord → External receiver → Earmould → Ear canal → Eardrum.** **Construction (3 marks):** The body-worn aid consists of a **rigid plastic case** (typically ABS) worn on the body by means of a **shirt clip, belt clip, or neck cord**. The case contains: - A miniature **electret microphone** on the upper face. - A **printed circuit board (PCB)** carrying the pre-amplifier (often an integrated low-noise op-amp), the tone-control RC network, the output power amplifier (Class-AB push-pull), and the volume potentiometer. - A **battery compartment** accepting one or two **AA / C / 675 cells** (1.5 V or 3 V total). - A **2.5 mm or 3.5 mm sub-miniature socket** on the side panel to which the output cord is connected. - An **on/off switch** and often a **tone (H-L) switch**. The output is a **3-conductor shielded cable** of length ≈ 80–100 cm, terminated in a **button-type magnetic receiver** weighing 2–3 g. The receiver is held against the concha or inside the canal by a **custom acrylic or silicone earmould**. **Working (1.5 marks):** Sound waves enter the microphone and are converted to a small alternating voltage. This voltage is amplified by the pre-amplifier, shaped by the tone-control filter, further amplified by the power stage, and attenuated by the volume control. The resulting amplified electrical signal travels down the cord to the external receiver, which converts it back to amplified sound pressure. The sound passes through the earmould into the ear canal and stimulates the eardrum. The user thus hears the originally soft sound at a much louder and clearer level. **Typical specifications (1 mark):** | Parameter | Typical Value | |---|---| | Peak acoustic gain | 50–80 dB | | SSPL90 | 110–135 dB SPL | | Frequency range | 200 Hz – 5 kHz | | Battery | 1 or 2 × AA / C / 675 cells (1.5–3.0 V) | | Battery life | 200–700 hours | | THD | < 5 % at full gain | | EIN | < 30 dB SPL | ##### **Model Solution for (b) — BWHA vs BTE Comparison (7 Marks)** *(1 mark per comparison point; up to 6 well-explained points plus 1 mark for the tabular or summary format)* | S.No. | Head | Body-Worn Hearing Aid | Behind-The-Ear (BTE) Aid | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Location of components** | Microphone, amplifier, battery all in a case worn on the **chest / belt**; cord runs to ear | All components inside a case worn **behind the auricle**; only a thin tube goes to the ear | | 2 | **Acoustic gain** | Very high: **50–80 dB** | High: **35–75 dB** (less than BWHA) | | 3 | **Cosmetic appeal** | **Poor** — body case and cord are clearly visible | **Good** — small, partially hidden behind the ear | | 4 | **Suitability for severe/profound loss** | **Excellent** — preferred for **profound (> 90 dB HL)** losses and pediatric cases | Good for severe; newer high-power digital BTEs can also handle profound losses | | 5 | **Battery life** | **200–700 h** (AA / C cells) | **150–400 h** (13 / 675 cells) | | 6 | **Maintenance** | **Easy** — case can be opened, parts are accessible and off-the-shelf | Easy for the BTE body; the earmould and tube need careful cleaning | | 7 | **Clothing noise** | **High** — friction of clothes against the case mic produces noise | **None** — mic is on top of the pinna, away from clothing | | 8 | **Freedom of movement** | **Limited** — cord length restricts head movement | **Full** — no external wires | > **Conclusion (1 mark):** Body-worn aids are functionally equivalent to BTEs in basic amplification but offer **higher power and longer battery life** at the cost of **cosmetic appeal and mobility**. Modern BTE aids have largely replaced body-worn aids except in cases of **profound loss, pediatric use, and resource-limited clinical settings**. --- #### **Option B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023 + Model 2024 Scheme]` > **(a)** Explain in detail the **electroacoustic performance specifications** of a body-worn hearing aid as per **ANSI S3.22 / IEC 60118-0** standards. Define: (i) Acoustic Gain, (ii) SSPL90, (iii) Frequency Response, (iv) Total Harmonic Distortion (THD), and (v) Equivalent Input Noise (EIN). **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** A body-worn hearing aid is fitted on a patient. The patient's audiogram shows a **70 dB HL flat sensorineural hearing loss**. The hearing aid provides an **insertion gain of 30 dB** and a **peak SSPL90 of 130 dB SPL**. (i) Calculate the **average output SPL** delivered to the eardrum for a **65 dB SPL conversational input**. (ii) Verify if the output is within the patient's **LDL (Loudness Discomfort Level)** if LDL is 110 dB SPL. (iii) State the **gain rule** used for fitting body-worn hearing aids and explain the **1/3 to 1/2 gain rule**. **(7 Marks)** ##### **Model Solution for (a) — Performance Specifications (7 Marks)** *(1.4 marks per definition, 5 definitions × 1.4 = 7)* **(i) Acoustic Gain (1.4 marks):** Acoustic gain $G$ is the **difference in sound pressure level (in dB)** at the eardrum produced by the hearing aid **with the aid turned on** versus **with the aid turned off**, for the same acoustic input. It is measured per the **ANSI S3.22** standard at a reference input of **60 dB SPL** at 1 kHz. $$G = L_{\text{out}} - L_{\text{in}} \quad [\text{dB}]$$ For a body-worn aid, the peak acoustic gain is **50–80 dB** and the HFA (High-Frequency Average) gain is typically **40–60 dB**. **(ii) SSPL90 (1.4 marks):** **SSPL90 = Saturation Sound Pressure Level at 90 dB SPL input.** It is the **maximum output SPL** the hearing aid can deliver when driven by a 90 dB SPL pure-tone input at 1 kHz, with the volume control at full-on. It defines the **upper limit of amplified sound** and must be matched to the patient's **Loudness Discomfort Level (LDL)** to prevent further hearing damage. For a body-worn aid, SSPL90 ranges from **110 to 135 dB SPL**, with a typical "HFA-SSPL90" of **120–128 dB SPL**. **(iii) Frequency Response (1.4 marks):** The frequency response is the **plot of acoustic gain (in dB) versus frequency (in Hz, log scale)** when a constant 60 dB SPL swept sine input is applied. The standard frequency range is **200 Hz to 5 kHz** for a body-worn aid. A "smooth" or "broad" curve is desirable; a "sharp peak" indicates resonant feedback or poor tone-control design. The HFA (High-Frequency Average) is the mean gain at **1, 1.6, and 2.5 kHz**. **(iv) Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) (1.4 marks):** THD measures the **non-linearity of the amplifier** — the extent to which the hearing aid generates harmonic frequencies (2f, 3f, 4f, …) that were **not present in the input**. It is defined as: $$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} \times 100\,\%$$ The ANSI limit is **THD < 5 % at full-on gain, measured at 500, 800, and 1600 Hz**. Body-worn aids using Class-AB output stages typically achieve THD of **2 % to 5 %**. **(v) Equivalent Input Noise (EIN) (1.4 marks):** EIN is the **internal self-noise of the hearing aid** referred back to the input. It is the SPL of an external source that would produce the same output as the aid's internal noise. It is calculated as: $$\text{EIN} = \text{Output Noise} - \text{Gain} \quad [\text{dB SPL}]$$ A **low EIN (< 25 dB SPL)** is desirable so that the aid does not add perceptible hiss. Body-worn aids with good pre-amplifier design achieve EIN of **20–30 dB SPL**. ##### **Model Solution for (b) — Numerical + Fitting Rule (7 Marks)** **Given:** - Input SPL = $L_{\text{in}} = 65$ dB SPL - Insertion gain (IG) = $G = 30$ dB - Peak SSPL90 = 130 dB SPL - Patient LDL = 110 dB SPL - Patient's audiogram = 70 dB HL flat loss **Step (i) — Average output SPL at the eardrum (2 marks):** The output SPL delivered to the eardrum is: $$L_{\text{out}} = L_{\text{in}} + G = 65 \text{ dB SPL} + 30 \text{ dB} = 95 \text{ dB SPL}$$ **Step (ii) — Verification against LDL (2 marks):** The output 95 dB SPL is **less than the LDL of 110 dB SPL** by 15 dB, meaning the patient has a **15 dB headroom** before discomfort. ✔ **The fitting is safe.** Also verify against SSPL90: 95 dB SPL < 130 dB SPL (peak SSPL90), so the amplifier is operating in its **linear region** (not saturated). ✔ > **Examiner's note:** A 15 dB headroom is considered the **minimum safe margin** in clinical practice. A comfortable margin is 20–25 dB. **Step (iii) — The 1/3 to 1/2 Gain Rule (3 marks):** The **"1/3 to 1/2 gain rule"** (also called the **Libby 1/3 rule** or **prescriptive gain rule**) is a clinical fitting rule used to choose the **insertion gain of a hearing aid** from the patient's audiogram. It states: > **Insertion gain ≈ 1/3 to 1/2 of the hearing loss (in dB HL) at each frequency.** **For this patient (70 dB HL flat loss):** - **1/3 rule:** IG = $\frac{1}{3} \times 70 \approx 23$ dB - **1/2 rule:** IG = $\frac{1}{2} \times 70 = 35$ dB The prescribed IG should lie between 23 dB and 35 dB, i.e., around **25–35 dB** in the 1–4 kHz range. The actual fitted IG of **30 dB** lies comfortably in this range, confirming a **valid fitting** for a body-worn hearing aid on a patient with a 70 dB HL flat sensorineural loss. **Why this rule works:** It provides a comfortable amplified signal that is **loud enough to be intelligible** but not so loud that it crosses the patient's LDL. It compensates for only a fraction of the loss, relying on the brain's residual auditory processing to fill in the gaps (a concept related to sensorineural recruitment). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting to convert dB SPL to Pa in numerical questions** — many students directly add/subtract dB SPL and dB gain; always remember that **dB values are logarithmic and CAN be added/subtracted directly, but converting to linear Pa is required when multiplying/dividing** gains. > 2. **Mixing up SSPL90 and peak acoustic gain** — SSPL90 is the **maximum output at saturation** (input 90 dB SPL), while acoustic gain is the **amplification factor** (output SPL − input SPL). They are different quantities. > 3. **Confusing microphone sensitivity in dB re 1 V/Pa vs dB SPL** — microphone output is a **voltage** (referenced to 1 V/Pa), while receiver output is a **pressure** (referenced to 20 μPa). Do not mix reference pressures. > 4. **Forgetting cord losses** — in a body-worn aid, the cord has a transmission efficiency of **0.85–0.95** and the 90 cm length can attenuate high frequencies (> 4 kHz) by 3–6 dB. Many students compute the voltage at the output of the amplifier and forget to multiply by $\eta_{\text{cord}}$. > 5. **Not verifying the LDL margin** — in numerical problems, students often forget to check whether the calculated output is safe relative to the patient's LDL. This is a critical clinical check worth 1–2 marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Body-Worn Hearing Aids** - ⭐ **Definition:** Case on body, cord to ear — oldest wearable hearing aid style. - ⭐ **Components in body case:** Microphone, pre-amplifier, tone control, power amplifier, volume control, battery, on/off switch. - ⭐ **External part:** 3-conductor cord (~90 cm) + button receiver + custom earmould. - ⭐ **Typical gain:** 50–80 dB acoustic gain; HFA gain 40–60 dB. - ⭐ **SSPL90:** 110–135 dB SPL — the highest of all hearing aid styles, suitable for profound loss. - ⭐ **Battery:** AA / C / 675 cell, 1.5–3.0 V, life **200–700 hours**. - ⭐ **Frequency range:** 200 Hz – 5 kHz. - ⭐ **THD < 5 %**, **EIN < 30 dB SPL** (ANSI S3.22 / IEC 60118-0). - ⭐ **Use case:** Severe-to-profound loss, pediatric patients, resource-limited clinics, long-duration users. - ⭐ **Disadvantages:** Poor cosmetic appeal, clothing noise, cord breakage, restricted head movement. - ⭐ **Replacement:** Largely superseded by **BTE** and **custom (ITE/ITC/CIC)** aids in developed countries; still in use for profound loss and LMIC settings. - ⭐ **Reference standards:** **ANSI S3.22 (USA) / IEC 60118-0 (Europe)** for electroacoustic specifications. - ⭐ **Gain Rule:** Insertion gain ≈ **1/3 to 1/2** of hearing loss (dB HL) per octave/band (Libby's rule). - ⭐ **Key safety check:** Output SPL at the eardrum should be **at least 10–15 dB below** the patient's LDL. - ⭐ **Examiner hot topics:** Block diagram (always expected), comparison with BTE, formula-based numerical on acoustic gain, definition of SSPL90, mention of ANSI/IEC standards. ---

topics.title: Behind the Ear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Behind the Ear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Behind-The-Ear (BTE) Hearing Aid:** A Behind-The-Ear (BTE) hearing aid is a class of externally worn, air-conduction **electroacoustic prosthetic device** that houses all primary electronic components — microphone(s), digital signal processor (DSP) amplifier, battery compartment, and receiver (loudspeaker) — inside a single curved, banana-shaped plastic shell that rests on the **pinna (auricle)** and behind the ear. Sound is routed from the receiver, through a clear plastic tube (the **ear hook** and **lumen tubing**), to a custom-molded **earmold** seated inside the concha and ear canal of the wearer.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus classification, the BTE belongs to the family of **Air-Conduction Hearing Aids (ACHA)** and represents the **most widely prescribed form factor** for moderate-to-severe, profound, and sloping high-frequency sensorineural hearing losses in both pediatric and geriatric populations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Mini-PA System on Your Ear"**
> Imagine a small personal public-address (PA) amplifier system that a schoolteacher wears clipped to her collar. The microphone (near the mouth) picks up her voice, the amplifier boosts it, and a tiny speaker (the receiver) plays it louder into the ear of the listener. The **BTE hearing aid works on exactly the same principle**, except the "listener" is the wearer's own cochlea, the "PA speaker" is a millimetre-scale balanced-armature or dynamic receiver, and the entire PA system is shrunk to fit behind a single ear.

Think of the BTE as a **three-stage acoustic relay station**:

$$
\text{Acoustic Wave} \xrightarrow{\text{Microphone}} \text{Electrical Signal} \xrightarrow{\text{Amplifier/DSP}} \text{Boosted Signal} \xrightarrow{\text{Receiver}} \text{Acoustic Wave (ear canal)}
$$

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (KTU Highlights)

The following standard parameters are routinely tested on a BTE during calibration and are universally marked in **bold** in examiner's key sheets:

- **Maximum Acoustic Gain:** typically **40 dB to 70 dB** for standard BTE; up to **80 dB** for power BTE.
- **Maximum Power Output (MPO / OSPL90):** **110 dB SPL to 140 dB SPL**.
- **Frequency Bandwidth:** approximately **200 Hz to 8000 Hz**.
- **Battery Type:** **Zinc-air** cells (sizes 13, 312, 675) or rechargeable Li-ion.
- **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD):** less than **5%** at reference test gain.
- **Equivalent Input Noise (EIN):** less than **25 dB SPL**.
- **Attack Time (compression):** **5–20 ms**; **Release Time:** **50–500 ms**.

### 1.4 Classification Tree (Quick Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> BTE devices are officially classified by the KTU 2024 syllabus under the broader heading of *"Body-Worn and Ear-Level Hearing Aids."* They are further sub-divided by the position of the receiver:

| Sub-type | Receiver Location | Indication |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard BTE** | Inside the shell | Moderate–Severe loss |
| **Mini-BTE** | Inside the shell (smaller shell) | Mild–Moderate loss |
| **Open-Fit BTE (OTE)** | Inside the shell, open dome | High-frequency loss |
| **Receiver-In-Canal (RIC / RITE)** | In the ear canal | Mild–Severe loss |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a BTE — The Six Functional Subsystems

Every BTE hearing aid, regardless of brand (Phonak, Oticon, Widex, Siemens/Signia, Resound, Starkey), is built from the same six logical subsystems. KTU examiners almost always ask the question: *"List the parts of a BTE hearing aid and explain its working."* — so memorize the order below.

1. **Microphone(s)** — One or two MEMS or Electret Condenser Microphones (ECM) mounted on the top of the shell. Converts acoustic pressure variations into a small analog voltage. Modern BTEs use **dual-microphone directional arrays** (front and rear) to enable adaptive beamforming.
2. **Pre-amplifier / Analog Front-End (AFE)** — A low-noise pre-amp that raises the microphone signal (~1–10 mV) to a level suitable for the ADC.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — Typically 16- to 24-bit resolution, sampling at **32 kHz** (covers up to 16 kHz audio band, well beyond the hearing aid range).
4. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** — The "brain." Performs **amplification, compression, noise reduction, feedback cancellation, frequency shaping, and directional processing**.
5. **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) + Output Stage** — Converts the processed digital signal back to analog and drives the receiver.
6. **Receiver (Loudspeaker)** — A miniature **balanced-armature** or **dynamic moving-coil** transducer that converts the amplified electrical signal back into sound pressure, which is then routed through the **ear hook → tubing → earmold** into the ear canal.

Additional mechanical parts include the **battery compartment / door**, **volume control wheel/button**, **program button**, and (in modern devices) a **telecoil (T-coil)** and **wireless antenna** (2.4 GHz Bluetooth/Near-Field Magnetic Induction).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the microphone placed at the TOP of the shell, not at the bottom?**
> Placing the microphone(s) at the top preserves the natural **pinna shadow effect** and the **head-related transfer function (HRTF)** — i.e., it lets the BTE collect sound the way a normal ear would. A bottom-mounted microphone would destroy the natural localization cues the brain uses to detect whether a sound is coming from front/back/above.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the amplifier non-linear (compressive)?**
> A human cochlea with sensorineural loss has a **reduced dynamic range**. A normal ear hears sounds from **0 dB HL (threshold) to ~120 dB SPL (loudness discomfort)**. A hearing-impaired ear may have a threshold of **60 dB HL** but the same 120 dB SPL upper limit. The BTE must therefore **squeeze 120 dB of input range into 60 dB of usable range** — a process called **Wide Dynamic Range Compression (WDRC)**.

### 2.3 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Acoustic Path

1. Sound waves arrive at the microphone (front port).
2. Microphone diaphragm vibrates → produces tiny AC voltage proportional to sound pressure.
3. ADC digitizes the signal at 32 kHz.
4. **DSP algorithms execute in sequence:**
   - Feedback cancellation (adaptive notch filtering).
   - Noise reduction (spectral subtraction, modulation-based detection).
   - Directional processing (delay-and-sum beamforming from dual mics).
   - **WDRC compression** — multi-channel (often 4 to 20 bands).
   - Frequency-dependent gain shaping (matches audiogram).
5. DAC converts back to analog; output stage drives the receiver.
6. Receiver produces amplified sound, which travels down the **#13 standard tubing** into the earmold.
7. Earmold delivers sound directly to the tympanic membrane via the ear canal.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** The following formulas are the **most frequently tested** quantitative relationships for BTE and air-conduction hearing aids. Memorize the units in **bold**.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Standard Symbol Form | Units | Use in BTE Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voltage Gain (linear) | $A_v = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$ | unitless | Stage-level amplification |
| 2 | Voltage Gain (dB) | $A_{v,\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10} \!\left(\dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$ | **dB** | Acoustic gain specification |
| 3 | Power Gain (dB) | $G_{p,\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10} \!\left(\dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$ | **dB** | Amplifier efficiency |
| 4 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10} \!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right),\ p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | **dB SPL** | Reference for all acoustic measurements |
| 5 | OSPL90 (Maximum Output) | $\text{OSPL90} = 20 \log_{10} \!\left(\dfrac{p_{\max}}{20\ \mu\text{Pa}}\right)$ | **dB SPL** | Saturation output at 90 dB input |
| 6 | Acoustic Gain | $G_{\text{ac}} = L_{p,\text{out}} - L_{p,\text{in}}$ | **dB** | BTE datasheet specification |
| 7 | Compression Ratio | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{Input (dB)}}{\Delta \text{Output (dB)}}$ | unitless | WDRC characteristic (1:1 = linear, ∞:1 = limiting) |
| 8 | Compression Threshold (Kneepoint) | $L_{\text{kneepoint}}$ | **dB SPL** | Input level at which compression begins |
| 9 | Battery Life | $t_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{mAh}}}{I_{\text{draw,mA}}}$ | **hours** | Estimating zinc-air cell longevity |
| 10 | Wavelength in air | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f},\ c \approx 343\ \text{m/s}$ | **m** | Determining tubing resonance, vent sizing |
| 11 | 1/3-Octave Bandwidth | $\Delta f = f_0 \cdot (2^{1/6} - 2^{-1/6})$ | **Hz** | Multi-channel DSP band design |
| 12 | Feedback Margin (gain-before-feedback) | $\text{GF} = 20 \log_{10} \!\left(\dfrac{\text{OSPL90}}{A_v}\right)$ | **dB** | Available headroom before oscillation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX/Markdown Note for Students:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in tables, **not the vertical pipe `|` character**, or your markdown table will break. Example: write $\vert V_{out} \vert$ instead of $|V_{out}|$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The BTE form factor is the **clinical workhorse of audiology** for three engineering reasons:
- **High gain headroom:** Larger shell = larger battery = larger amplifier = up to 80 dB gain, sufficient for profound loss.
- **Serviceability:** Battery door, microphone inlet, and ear hook are user-replaceable.
- **Robustness:** Electronics are sealed in the shell, away from cerumen (earwax) and moisture, unlike in-canal (ITC/CIC) devices.

The same engineering principles (MEMS mic + DSP + balanced-armature receiver + WDRC) form the basis of all modern **Personal Sound Amplification Products (PSAPs)**, **hearables**, and **True Wireless Stereo (TWS) earbuds with transparency/hearing-assist modes** (e.g., Apple AirPods Pro 2, Sony CRE-C20, Jabra Enhance).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Acoustic Gain of a Multi-Stage BTE

A BTE amplifier is typically a cascade of three stages: **pre-amp → compressor → output driver**. We want to derive the total acoustic gain delivered at the eardrum in dB SPL.

**Step 1 — Define the input acoustic signal.**
Let $p_{in}$ be the sound pressure at the microphone diaphragm. Convert to dB SPL:

$$
L_{p,\text{in}} = 20 \log_{10} \!\left(\dfrac{p_{in}}{p_0}\right), \quad p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Microphone sensitivity (free-field).**
Microphone sensitivity $S_M$ is defined as the output voltage per unit input sound pressure at 1 kHz:

$$
S_M = \dfrac{V_M}{p_{in}} \quad \text{[\,V/Pa\,]}
$$

For a typical BTE electret mic, $S_M \approx 10\text{ mV/Pa}$. So the microphone voltage is:

$$
V_M = S_M \cdot p_{in} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \cdot p_{in}
$$

**Step 3 — Voltage gain of the amplifier stages.**
Let the pre-amp, compressor, and output stage have linear voltage gains $A_1$, $A_2$, $A_3$ respectively. The total linear voltage gain is the product:

$$
A_{v,\text{total}} = A_1 \cdot A_2 \cdot A_3
$$

Converting to dB:

$$
A_{v,\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10} (A_1) + 20 \log_{10} (A_2) + 20 \log_{10} (A_3)
$$

**Step 4 — Output voltage of the amplifier.**

$$
V_{\text{out}} = A_{v,\text{total}} \cdot V_M = A_1 A_2 A_3 \cdot S_M \cdot p_{in}
$$

**Step 5 — Receiver output sound pressure.**
The receiver (loudspeaker) has a sensitivity $S_R$ [Pa/V] (inverse of microphone sensitivity). The pressure generated in a **2-cc coupler** (industry-standard HA-1 or HA-2 coupler) is:

$$
p_{\text{2cc}} = S_R \cdot V_{\text{out}} = S_R \cdot A_1 A_2 A_3 \cdot S_M \cdot p_{in}
$$

**Step 6 — Total acoustic gain in dB.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_{\text{ac,dB}} &= 20 \log_{10} \!\left(\dfrac{p_{\text{2cc}}}{p_{in}}\right) \\
&= 20 \log_{10} (S_R \cdot A_1 A_2 A_3 \cdot S_M) \\
&= 20 \log_{10}(S_R) + 20 \log_{10}(A_1) + 20 \log_{10}(A_2) + 20 \log_{10}(A_3) + 20 \log_{10}(S_M)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Real-ear delivered gain.**
The acoustic gain measured at the eardrum (Real-Ear Aided Gain, REAG) differs from the 2-cc coupler gain by the **Real-Ear-to-Coupler Difference (RECD)**:

$$
\text{REAG}_{\text{dB}} = G_{\text{2cc,dB}} + \text{RECD}_{\text{dB}}
$$

where RECD is typically **+5 to +10 dB** for adults and can be **+15 dB or more** for infants (due to smaller ear canal volume).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters clinically:** A BTE is programmed using the patient's **audiogram + RECD** to deliver a target gain matching a validated prescriptive formula such as **NAL-NL2** or **DSL v5**. Without the RECD correction, the aid will under-amplify for infants and over-amplify for adults.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Battery Life Estimation for a BTE

**Step 1 — Define battery capacity and load current.**
A size-13 zinc-air cell has a rated capacity $C = 310$ mAh. The BTE draws a steady-state current $I_{\text{draw}} = 1.5$ mA from the cell (assuming the hearing aid is in active mode 16 hours/day, and idle the remaining 8 hours with negligible draw).

**Step 2 — Linear estimate of runtime.**

$$
t_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{C}{I_{\text{draw}}} = \dfrac{310\ \text{mAh}}{1.5\ \text{mA}} = 206.7\ \text{h}
$$

**Step 3 — Adjust for actual usage pattern (duty cycle).**
If the aid is worn 16 hours per day, the effective days of use is:

$$
\text{Days} = \dfrac{206.7}{16} = 12.9\ \text{days} \approx 13\ \text{days}
$$

**Step 4 — High-power BTE correction.**
For a power BTE drawing 3.0 mA, battery life halves:

$$
t_{\text{life}} = \dfrac{310}{3.0} = 103.3\ \text{h} \approx 6.5\ \text{days at 16 h/day use}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Real-World Utility:** This is precisely how audiologists counsel patients on battery consumption. The **higher the gain, the higher the current draw, the shorter the battery life** — a fundamental trade-off in BTE design.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Compression Ratio (WDRC) — Numerical Example

**Problem:** A BTE receives a 60 dB SPL input speech signal. The patient's audiogram shows a 50 dB HL threshold at 2 kHz. The compressor is set to a kneepoint of 50 dB SPL and a compression ratio of 3:1 above the kneepoint. What is the output level?

**Step 1 — Locate the input relative to the kneepoint.**
The input is 60 dB SPL; the kneepoint is 50 dB SPL. The input is **10 dB above** the kneepoint.

**Step 2 — Apply the compression ratio.**
For every 3 dB of input above the kneepoint, the output rises by 1 dB:

$$
\text{Output above kneepoint} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{Input above kneepoint}}{\text{CR}} = \dfrac{10}{3} = 3.33\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Add the linear-region gain below the kneepoint.**
Assume linear gain of unity (1:1) below the kneepoint, so output at the kneepoint equals the kneepoint: 50 dB SPL.

**Step 4 — Compute the final output.**

$$
L_{\text{out}} = L_{\text{kneepoint}} + 3.33 = 50 + 3.33 = 53.33\ \text{dB SPL}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 60 dB SPL input was compressed to a 53.3 dB SPL output, comfortably above the patient's 50 dB HL threshold (i.e., audible) but well below the discomfort level (~120 dB SPL). **This is the central engineering purpose of WDRC in a BTE.**

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — BTE vs. Other Form Factors

| Parameter | **BTE** | **ITC (In-The-Canal)** | **CIC (Completely-In-Canal)** | **IIC (Invisible-In-Canal)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gain Range** | 30–80 dB | 25–55 dB | 20–45 dB | 15–35 dB |
| **MPO (OSPL90)** | 110–140 dB SPL | 100–120 dB SPL | 95–115 dB SPL | 90–110 dB SPL |
| **Battery Life** | 7–14 days | 5–10 days | 3–7 days | 2–5 days |
| **Ventilation** | Excellent (open) | Moderate | Limited | Poor |
| **Visibility** | Mildly visible | Low | Very low | Invisible |
| **Cerumen/Moisture** | Low exposure | Moderate | High | Very high |
| **Pediatric Suitability** | **Excellent (most prescribed)** | Poor | Unsuitable | Unsuitable |
| **Severe/Profound Loss** | **Yes (Power BTE)** | No | No | No |
| **Telecoil / Wireless** | Standard | Optional | Rare | None |
| **Cost (₹ in India, approx)** | ₹15,000 – ₹3,50,000 | ₹20,000 – ₹2,50,000 | ₹25,000 – ₹3,00,000 | ₹40,000+ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1: Physical Anatomy of a BTE Hearing Aid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Pinna Auricle] --> A2[Ear Hook Curved Plastic Tube]
    A2 --> A3[Clear Tubing 13 Standard]
    A3 --> A4[Custom Earmold]
    A4 --> A5[Ear Canal]
    A5 --> A6[Tympanic Membrane Eardrum]
    A6 --> A7[Cochlea Hearing Organ]

    B1[BTE Main Shell Behind The Ear] --> A2
    B1 --> B2[Microphone 1 Front Port]
    B1 --> B3[Microphone 2 Rear Port Directional]
    B1 --> B4[Battery Compartment Size 13 or 312]
    B1 --> B5[Volume Control Wheel]
    B1 --> B6[Program Button]
    B1 --> B7[DSP Chip and Amplifier]
    B1 --> B8[Receiver Balanced Armature]

    B8 --> A2

    style A1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style A7 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style B1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style B7 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style B8 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
```

### 4.2 Figure 2: Block-Level Functional Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INP[Acoustic Input Sound Waves] --> MIC[MEMS Microphone Transducer]
    MIC --> AFE[Analog Front End Preamp and ADC]
    AFE --> DSP1{DSP Block}

    DSP1 --> SUB1[1 Feedback Cancellation Adaptive Filter]
    SUB1 --> SUB2[2 Noise Reduction Modulation Based]
    SUB2 --> SUB3[3 Directional Beamforming Dual Mic]
    SUB3 --> SUB4[4 WDRC Compression Multi Channel]
    SUB4 --> SUB5[5 Frequency Shaping NAL NL2 or DSL v5]

    SUB5 --> DAC[DAC and Output Driver]
    DAC --> RCV[Receiver Balanced Armature Speaker]
    RCV --> COUPLER[2cc Coupler or Ear Canal]
    COUPLER --> OUTP[Acoustic Output to Tympanic Membrane]

    BATT[Zinc Air or Li Ion Battery] -. Power .-> AFE
    BATT -. Power .-> DSP1
    BATT -. Power .-> DAC

    style INP fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style MIC fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style DSP1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style RCV fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style OUTP fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style BATT fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c
```

### 4.3 Figure 3: BTE Fitting Workflow (Clinical Path)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Patient Audiogram dB HL vs Frequency] --> S2[Select BTE Form Factor Standard Mini or Power]
    S2 --> S3[Choose Earmold Type Hard Soft or Open Dome]
    S3 --> S4[Initial Fit with Manufacturer First Fit Algorithm]
    S4 --> S5[Real Ear Measurement REM Verification]
    S5 --> S6{Target Match within 5 dB?}
    S6 -- No --> S7[Adjust Gain Compression and MPO]
    S7 --> S5
    S6 -- Yes --> S8[Fine Tuning with Patient Feedback]
    S8 --> S9[Counseling on Use Battery and Maintenance]
    S9 --> S10[Follow Up Visit at 2 Weeks and 3 Months]

    style S1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style S5 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style S10 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
```

### 4.4 Figure 4: Frequency-Response Curve of a Typical BTE (Schematic Gain vs Frequency)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[100 Hz Low Cut 10 dB Gain] --> F2[500 Hz 25 dB Gain]
    F2 --> F3[1000 Hz 35 dB Gain]
    F3 --> F4[2000 Hz 50 dB Gain Peak]
    F4 --> F5[4000 Hz 55 dB Gain Peak]
    F5 --> F6[6000 Hz 40 dB Gain]
    F6 --> F7[8000 Hz 25 dB Gain Roll Off]

    style F1 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style F4 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style F5 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style F7 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Figure 4:** The schematic shows a typical BTE frequency response for a **sloping high-frequency hearing loss** — gain rises from low frequencies, peaks at **2–4 kHz** (the region critical for speech intelligibility, especially consonants like /s/, /sh/, /t/), and rolls off above 6 kHz to avoid feedback and discomfort.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]** 
*Define a Behind-The-Ear (BTE) hearing aid. List its main components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Behind-The-Ear (BTE) hearing aid is an ear-level air-conduction **electroacoustic device** that houses its microphone, amplifier, battery, and receiver inside a single shell worn behind the pinna, with sound delivered to the ear canal via an **ear hook, tubing, and earmold**. (**1 mark** for definition)

**Main components:** (**½ mark each, total 2 marks**)
1. Microphone (one or two)
2. Amplifier / Digital Signal Processor
3. Receiver (speaker)
4. Battery compartment
5. Ear hook and tubing
6. Earmold (custom or standard dome)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*What is Wide Dynamic Range Compression (WDRC) and why is it used in BTE hearing aids?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

WDRC is a non-linear amplification strategy in which the **gain is automatically reduced as input level increases**, characterized by a **compression ratio (CR)** greater than 1:1 above a defined **kneepoint**. (**1 mark**)

**Why it is used:** Patients with sensorineural hearing loss have a **reduced dynamic range** (loudness recruitment) — soft sounds are inaudible but loud sounds are uncomfortable. WDRC maps the wide range of real-world sounds (~120 dB) into the patient's narrow residual dynamic range, improving audibility of soft speech while preventing loud sounds from becoming painful. (**2 marks**)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Read Carefully!**
> In BTE questions, students most commonly **lose 2–3 marks** in the following ways:
> 1. **Skipping the diagram.** Always draw a **labelled block diagram** of the BTE. Even a hand-drawn sketch gets you **1 to 2 free marks** under "illustrations / diagrams."
