# Cochlear Implant — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]`
**Differentiate between a hearing aid and a cochlear implant. State two clinical indications for cochlear implantation.**

**Model Answer:**

A **hearing aid** is an *acoustic amplifier* that increases the sound pressure level delivered to the ear canal; it requires functionally intact hair cells in the cochlea to transduce the amplified sound into neural signals. A **cochlear implant** is a *neural prosthesis* that bypasses damaged hair cells and directly stimulates the auditory nerve via intracochlear electrodes using biphasic charge-balanced current pulses.

**Clinical indications:**
1. Bilateral severe-to-profound sensorineural hearing loss (> 70 dB HL) with speech recognition score < 50 % even with optimally fitted hearing aids.
2. Adult post-lingually deaf patients (or pre-lingually deaf children above 12 months of age) with no medical contraindication to surgery and a patent cochlear lumen (absence of cochlear ossification or aplasia).

> [!NOTE]
> For 3-mark questions, the keyword-difference + **two** clinical points pattern is the safest answer format.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**What is the tonotopic organization of the cochlea? How is this principle exploited in a multi-channel cochlear implant?**

**Model Answer:**

**Tonotopic organization** refers to the systematic spatial arrangement of frequency sensitivity along the length of the cochlea, with the **base responding maximally to high frequencies (~ 8–20 kHz)** and the **apex responding maximally to low frequencies (~ 100–500 Hz)**. This arises from the gradient of basilar membrane stiffness and is mathematically described by the **Greenwood function**.

**Exploitation in CI:** A multi-channel implant places its electrode array along the scala tympani such that the **most basal electrode corresponds to the highest-frequency filter band** and the **most apical electrode corresponds to the lowest-frequency band**. The external sound processor decomposes the input audio into logarithmically spaced bands (matching Greenwood's map) and assigns each band's envelope to its corresponding electrode, thereby recreating a crude but functional place-pitch map.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination pattern: a Module 4 14-mark question will have two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student must answer either **Question A OR Question B** (full internal choice).

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a multi-channel cochlear implant system. Clearly label the external and implanted subsystems, and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 22-channel cochlear implant must cover the input audio band 188 Hz to 7938 Hz with logarithmically spaced channels. Compute:
   (i) The geometric ratio $r$ between adjacent channel center frequencies.
   (ii) The center frequency of the 11th channel.
   (iii) The insertion depth (in mm from the stapes) required for an electrode that must stimulate a neuron tuned to 500 Hz, using the Greenwood function with $A = 165.4$, $a = 0.06$, $k = 0.88$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

The answer must include the following elements (each ~ 1 mark):

1. **Two-subsystem architecture:** External (sound processor, mic, transmitter coil) and Internal (receiver-stimulator, electrode array).
2. **Microphone → AGC → ADC → Filterbank → Envelope → Compression → RF Encoder** in the external path.
3. **Receiver Coil → Demodulator → Current Source DACs → Electrode Array** in the internal path.
4. **Transcutaneous RF link** (~ 5 MHz inductive coupling) clearly labeled.
5. **Reference (return) electrode** shown completing the current loop.
6. **Place-pitch principle:** Tonotopic mapping from filterbank band to electrode position.
7. **Safety:** Biphasic charge-balanced pulses; note charge density limit.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

Given: $f_L = 188\ \text{Hz}$, $f_H = 7938\ \text{Hz}$, $N = 22$.

**(i) Geometric ratio (2 marks):**

$$r = \left(\frac{f_H}{f_L}\right)^{\frac{1}{N-1}} = \left(\frac{7938}{188}\right)^{\frac{1}{21}} = (42.2238)^{\frac{1}{21}}$$

$$\ln r = \frac{\ln 42.2238}{21} = \frac{3.7428}{21} = 0.17823$$

$$r = e^{0.17823} \approx 1.1951$$

**(ii) Center frequency of channel 11 (2 marks):**

$$f_{11} = f_L \cdot r^{10} = 188 \cdot (1.1951)^{10}$$

$$(1.1951)^{10} = e^{1.7823} \approx 5.943$$

$$f_{11} \approx 188 \cdot 5.943 \approx 1117.3\ \text{Hz}$$

**(iii) Insertion depth for 500 Hz (3 marks):**

Using $x = \frac{1}{a}\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{f}{A} + k\right)$:

$$x = \frac{1}{0.06}\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{500}{165.4} + 0.88\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.06}\,\log_{10}\!(3.0229 + 0.88) = \frac{1}{0.06}\,\log_{10}\!(3.9029)$$

$$= \frac{1}{0.06} \cdot 0.5913 \approx 9.86\ \text{mm}$$

**Answer:** The 500 Hz electrode must be inserted ~ **9.86 mm** from the stapes.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Alternative]`

**(a)** Explain the Continuous Interleaved Sampling (CIS) and Advanced Combination Encoder (ACE) speech coding strategies used in cochlear implants. Compare them in terms of channel interaction, power consumption, and speech intelligibility. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Pt-Ir intracochlear electrode has a geometric surface area of $0.25\ \text{mm}^2$. The stimulator delivers a biphasic current pulse of $150\ \mu\text{A}$ amplitude and $25\ \mu\text{s}$ phase duration.
   (i) Compute the charge per phase and the charge density.
   (ii) Verify whether the pulse satisfies the **Shannon safety limit** of $D \cdot Q \le 0.002\ \mu\text{C}^2/\text{cm}^2$.
   (iii) If the limit is exceeded, suggest a redesign so the limit is just met (state the new $t_{\text{phase}}$ in µs, keeping $I = 150\ \mu\text{A}$). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

| Aspect | CIS | ACE |
|---|---|---|
| Channel selection | All N channels stimulated per cycle | Only M highest-energy channels (M < N) |
| Pulse rate | High (~ 800–2500 pps per channel) | Very high per selected channel; skipped channels save power |
| Channel interaction | Minimized by **interleaved** (non-simultaneous) stimulation | Same as CIS (still interleaved) |
| Power consumption | Higher (continuous stimulation) | Lower (sparse selection) |
| Spectral detail | Constant across input | Adaptive to input spectrum |
| Used in | Med-El, older Cochlear devices | **Nucleus CI24RE, CI512, CI422 (current standard)** |
| Speech outcome | Good in quiet | Better in noise; more natural |

> **Examiner's model answer cue:** Always state that **both CIS and ACE are interleaved** — the key differentiator is **sparse spectral selection in ACE** and the resulting **power saving**.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

Given: $A = 0.25\ \text{mm}^2 = 0.0025\ \text{cm}^2$, $I = 150\ \mu\text{A}$, $t_{\text{phase}} = 25\ \mu\text{s}$.

**(i) Charge and charge density (2 marks):**

$$Q_{\text{phase}} = I \cdot t_{\text{phase}} = 150 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 25 \times 10^{-6} = 3.75 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{C} = 0.00375\ \mu\text{C}$$

$$D = \frac{Q_{\text{phase}}}{A} = \frac{0.00375\ \mu\text{C}}{0.0025\ \text{cm}^2} = 1.5\ \mu\text{C}/\text{cm}^2$$

**(ii) Shannon product (2 marks):**

$$D \cdot Q = 1.5 \cdot 0.00375 = 0.005625\ \mu\text{C}^2/\text{cm}^2$$

Since $0.005625 \gt 0.002$, the pulse **violates** the Shannon limit and is **NOT SAFE**.

**(iii) Redesign — find new $t_{\text{phase}}$ (3 marks):**

We require $D \cdot Q = 0.002$ exactly, with $I$ fixed at 150 µA.

Let $Q = I \cdot t$ (in µC, with $t$ in µs and $I$ in µA: $Q\ [\mu\text{C}] = I\ [\mu\text{A}] \cdot t\ [\mu\text{s}] \times 10^{-6}$, but more simply $Q\ \text{[nC]} = I\ \text{[µA]} \cdot t\ \text{[µs]} / 1000$ — easier to keep in SI).

Using SI units for clarity: $I = 150 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A}$, $A = 0.0025\ \text{cm}^2$.

We need:

$$\left(\frac{I \cdot t}{A}\right) \cdot (I \cdot t) = 0.002 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C}^2/\text{cm}^2$$

Let $Q = I \cdot t$. Then $Q^2 = 0.002 \times 10^{-12} \cdot A = 0.002 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 0.0025 = 5 \times 10^{-18}$.

$$Q = \sqrt{5 \times 10^{-18}} = 2.236 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{C} = 2.236\ \text{nC}$$

New phase duration:

$$t = \frac{Q}{I} = \frac{2.236 \times 10^{-9}}{150 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.491 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{s} = 14.91\ \mu\text{s}$$

**Answer:** Reduce $t_{\text{phase}}$ from 25 µs to approximately **14.9 µs** (or any value < 14.9 µs for safety margin).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Top 3 Reasons Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the +1 vs −1 confusion in $r^{N-1}$:** Some students write $r^N$, giving a wrong ratio by one channel-step. Always check: with $N$ channels there are $N-1$ intervals.
> 2. **Mixing up Greenwood's $x$ direction:** $x$ is measured *from the stapes into the cochlea*, so low $x$ = high frequency. Reversing this inverts the mapping.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in Shannon limit:** Charge must be in **µC** and area in **cm²** — the product is $\mu\text{C}^2/\text{cm}^2$. Carrying SI units gives numerically correct but dimensionally "ugly" answers; examiners expect the clinical units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Cochlear Implant** = neural prosthesis that **bypasses hair cells** and directly stimulates the **auditory nerve (CN VIII)**.
- [ ] Two subsystems: **External** (mic + DSP + transmitter coil) and **Internal** (receiver + electrode array in scala tympani), linked by **transcutaneous RF telemetry**.
- [ ] **Indication:** Severe-to-profound bilateral sensorineural hearing loss (> 70 dB HL) with poor speech scores despite hearing aids.
- [ ] **Greenwood function** $f(x) = 165.4\,(10^{0.06x} - 0.88)$ defines the tonotopic map; electrodes must be placed at depths matching the desired characteristic frequencies.
- [ ] **Sound processing chain:** Mic → AGC → ADC → Filterbank (log-spaced) → Envelope extraction → Log compression (T–C map) → Biphasic pulse train → RF link.
- [ ] **CIS** = all channels stimulated continuously; **ACE** = sparse selection of highest-energy peaks; both are interleaved to prevent channel interaction.
- [ ] **Charge safety:** $Q = I \cdot t_{\text{phase}}$; **Shannon limit** $D \cdot Q \le 0.002\ \mu\text{C}^2/\text{cm}^2$ must be respected.
- [ ] **Place pitch** (which electrode fires) and **rate/temporal pitch** (how it fires) jointly encode frequency; modern CIs use place as the primary cue.
- [ ] **Pitch coding limits:** ~ 22 electrodes ⇒ ~ 22 place pitches; spectral resolution far below the ~ 3500 inner hair cells of a normal cochlea — hence CI users struggle with music and speech-in-noise.
- [ ] **Future directions:** totally implantable CIs, optical (optogenetic) stimulation, drug-eluting electrode arrays, and ABI for NF2 patients.
- [ ] **Favourite KTU keywords to memorize:** *scala tympani, spiral ganglion, charge-balanced biphasic pulse, tonotopic map, Shannon limit, ACE strategy, transcutaneous RF link, Greenwood function, place pitch, rate pitch.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auditory Brain Stem Implant Aids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Auditory Brain Stem Implant Aids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Auditory Brainstem Implant (ABI)** is a sophisticated neuroprosthetic device that restores partial auditory perception in individuals who have lost the function of their auditory (cochlear) nerves. Unlike a conventional Cochlear Implant (CI) that stimulates the cochlea, an ABI directly electrically stimulates the **cochlear nucleus (CN)** — the first central auditory relay station located on the brainstem at the junction of the pons and medulla, just lateral to the inferior cerebellar peduncle.

The electrode paddle is surgically positioned over the surface of the dorsal and ventral cochlear nucleus within the **lateral recess of the fourth ventricle**, bypassing the cochlea, auditory nerve, and inner ear entirely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** ABI is indicated for patients with **Neurofibromatosis Type 2 (NF2)** who undergo vestibular schwannoma removal, patients with **absent or aplastic cochlear nerves**, and cases of severe cochlear ossification where electrode insertion is impossible.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bypass Telephone Line

Imagine a normal telephone system: The microphone (cochlea) converts sound into electrical signals, the telephone wire (auditory nerve) carries the signal to the central exchange (brain), and finally to the listener (auditory cortex).

Now, suppose the **telephone wire is completely severed** — no matter how good the microphone is, the listener hears nothing. A standard Cochlear Implant tries to "rebuild" the wire by stimulating the microphone end. But what if the wire is missing from birth, or destroyed by a tumor?

> [!NOTE]
> **The ABI Analogy:** Think of the ABI as a system that **skips the broken wire entirely**. It picks up the sound (microphone), processes it, and delivers the electrical signal **directly into the central exchange board (cochlear nucleus)** of the brain. The brain can still interpret the signal, although not as clearly as with a normal nerve.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Electrode Channels:** Typically **21 active electrodes** arranged on a flat silicone paddle.
- **Stimulation Rate:** 250–5000 pulses per second per channel.
- **Frequency Range Covered:** ~100 Hz to ~8 kHz (synthetic).
- **Surgical Placement Depth:** Surface of cochlear nucleus, ~**2–4 mm** from the brainstem surface.
- **Sound Processor:** Worn behind the ear (BTE) similar to a CI.

## 1.4 Difference Between ABI and CI — At a Glance

| Parameter | Cochlear Implant (CI) | Auditory Brainstem Implant (ABI) |
|---|---|---|
| Stimulation Site | Cochlea (scala tympani) | Cochlear Nucleus (brainstem) |
| Indication | Sensorineural hearing loss | NF2, absent auditory nerve |
| Number of Electrodes | 12–24 | 21 (standard) |
| Tonotopic Mapping | Clear (along cochlea) | Less precise (surface stimulation) |
| Speech Understanding | High (70–80% open set) | Limited (mostly closed set / lip-reading aid) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomical Foundation — The Auditory Pathway Up to the Brainstem

Sound waves → tympanic membrane → ossicles (malleus, incus, stapes) → oval window → **cochlear perilymph** → basilar membrane vibration → hair cell stimulation → auditory (cochlear) nerve → **cochlear nucleus** → superior olivary complex → lateral lemniscus → inferior colliculus → medial geniculate body → **auditory cortex**.

The ABI intervenes at the **cochlear nucleus** — specifically, the **dorsal cochlear nucleus (DCN)** and **ventral cochlear nucleus (VCN)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The tonotopic organization (frequency mapping) is preserved in the cochlear nucleus, but with less spatial precision than in the cochlea.

## 2.2 Components of an ABI System (Functional Block View)

1. **External Sound Processor (BTE unit):** Captures sound via microphones, performs band-pass filtering, extracts envelope information, and applies a speech coding strategy.
2. **Transmitter Coil:** Sends power and processed data transcutaneously via RF link (~5 MHz).
3. **Internal Receiver-Stimulator:** Decodes the RF signal, generates biphasic current pulses.
4. **Electrode Array (Paddle):** A flat silicone carrier with **21 platinum-iridium electrode contacts** that deliver current directly to the cochlear nucleus surface.

## 2.3 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

- **Step 1 — Acoustic Capture:** Microphones pick up environmental sound.
- **Step 2 — Pre-processing:** Bandpass filtering divides the audio spectrum (~250 Hz to ~8 kHz) into **N frequency bands** corresponding to electrode positions.
- **Step 3 — Envelope Extraction:** The amplitude envelope of each band is extracted (because neurons respond to envelope, not fine temporal structure, at the brainstem level).
- **Step 4 — Pulse Generation:** Biphasic charge-balanced pulses are created for each channel.
- **Step 5 — RF Transmission:** Data + power are transmitted across the skin.
- **Step 6 — Neural Stimulation:** Current flows between an active electrode on the cochlear nucleus and a return (ground) electrode. This depolarizes the **CN neurons** directly.
- **Step 7 — Cortical Perception:** The brain interprets the spatio-temporal pattern of neural activation as sound.

## 2.4 The Stimulation Equation — Neural Activation

The current density $J$ at the electrode-tissue interface determines whether a neuron fires:

$$J = \frac{I}{A}$$

where $I$ is the injected current (in amperes) and $A$ is the active electrode surface area (in m²).

The **charge per phase** $Q$ is critical for safe stimulation:

$$Q = I \times t_{phase}$$

where $t_{phase}$ is the duration of a single phase of the biphasic pulse (typically 25–100 µs).

The **charge density** $D$ must be limited to prevent tissue damage:

$$D = \frac{Q}{A}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Safety Limit:** Charge density must be kept below **30 µC/cm² per phase** (Shannon limit) to avoid electrochemical tissue injury at the electrode.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Current Density | $J = I / A$ | A/m² | Higher J → more neurons recruited |
| Charge per Phase | $Q = I \cdot t_{ph}$ | Coulombs (C) | Determines depolarization |
| Charge Density | $D = Q / A$ | C/m² | Must be $<$ Shannon limit |
| Stimulation Frequency | $f_{stim} = 1/T_{pulse}$ | Hz | Typically 250–5000 Hz |
| Loudness Growth | $L \propto \log(I)$ | dB | Perceived loudness (Stevens' law) |
| Biphasic Pulse Condition | $Q_{+} = Q_{-}$ | C | Charge balance — prevents DC accumulation |
| Number of Channels | $N_{ch} = 21$ (ABI standard) | — | Unlike 22–24 in CI |
| Electrode Impedance | $Z = V / I$ | Ω | Monitors tissue-electrode interface |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Loudness:** Unlike the cochlea, the cochlear nucleus has fewer frequency-specific neurons, so loudness perception in ABI is often **non-tonotopic and noisy**, requiring careful gain mapping.

## 2.6 Why ABI? Clinical Engineering Rationale

- Used when **cochlear nerve is destroyed** (NF2 tumor removal).
- Used when **cochlea is malformed** (Michel deformity, severe ossification).
- Provides **auditory awareness, environmental sound recognition, and lip-reading enhancement**.
- Speech understanding in quiet: **~10–15% open set** in ABI users vs **70–80%** in CI users — a key counseling point.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Threshold Current for Neural Activation

We model the CN neuron as a membrane with capacitance $C_m$ and resistance $R_m$ in parallel, subjected to a step current $I$ through an intracellular electrode.

The membrane voltage $V_m$ follows the differential equation:

$$\tau_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + V_m = R_m \cdot I(t)$$

where $\tau_m = R_m C_m$ is the membrane time constant.

**Step 1:** Apply a constant current pulse $I(t) = I_0$ for $t \geq 0$.

$$\tau_m \frac{dV_m}{dt} + V_m = R_m I_0$$

**Step 2:** Solve the first-order linear ODE using integrating factor $e^{t/\tau_m}$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left( V_m e^{t/\tau_m} \right) = \frac{R_m I_0}{\tau_m} e^{t/\tau_m}$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from 0 to $t$:

$$V_m(t) e^{t/\tau_m} - V_m(0) = R_m I_0 \left( e^{t/\tau_m} - 1 \right)$$

**Step 4:** Assume $V_m(0) = 0$ (resting potential reference):

$$V_m(t) = R_m I_0 \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau_m} \right)$$

**Step 5:** The threshold condition is $V_m(t_{ph}) = V_{th}$, giving the rheobase current:

$$I_{th} = \frac{V_{th}}{R_m \left( 1 - e^{-t_{ph}/\tau_m} \right)}$$

**Step 6:** As $t_{ph} \to \infty$, $I_{th} \to V_{th}/R_m$ (rheobase). As $t_{ph} \to 0$, the required charge is:

$$Q_{th} = I_{th} \cdot t_{ph} \to C_m V_{th}$$

This is the **chronaxie**-based relationship (Weiss–Lapicque law):

$$I_{th} = \frac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t_{ph}/\tau_m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This means that for very short pulses, the threshold becomes *charge-limited*, not *current-limited*. ABI designers use pulses of 50–100 µs to maximize safety while maintaining low power consumption.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Stimulation Parameter Selection

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical ABI Value | Physiological Limit | Clinical Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pulse Width | $t_{ph}$ | 50–100 µs | 10–500 µs | Shorter → less neural integration |
| Current Amplitude | $I$ | 0.5–3.0 mA | $<$ 5 mA | Higher → louder but risk of non-auditory side effects |
| Inter-Phase Gap | IPG | 10 µs | 0–50 µs | Longer → lower threshold (EIS effect) |
| Stimulation Rate | $f_s$ | 250–5000 Hz/pulse | $<$ 9000 Hz | Higher → better temporal resolution |
| Charge Density | $D$ | 5–20 µC/cm² | $<$ 30 µC/cm² | Shannon limit — safety ceiling |

## 3.3 Non-Auditory Side-Effect Modelling

Because the cochlear nucleus is near other cranial nerve nuclei (CN V, VII, IX, X), overstimulation can cause:

- **Facial nerve (VII) stimulation** → facial twitching
- **Trigeminal nerve (V) stimulation** → facial tingling
- **Vagus nerve (X) stimulation** → cough/voice change

The spread function of current $S(r)$ follows roughly:

$$S(r) \propto \frac{1}{r^2}$$

This is why monopolar stimulation (one electrode active, distant return) is preferred in ABI — to confine the field and reduce cross-stimulation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an ABI System



## 4.3 Signal Processing Pipeline

```mermaid

    S1[Input Sound Wave] --> S2[Pre-emphasis Filter]
    S2 --> S3[Bandpass Filter Bank 21 Bands]

    S4 --> S5[Compression and Mapping Function]
    S5 --> S6[Pulse Train Generator Biphasic Charge-Balanced]

```

## 4.4 ABI vs CI — Comparative Architecture


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Define Auditory Brainstem Implant. List any two clinical indications where ABI is preferred over a cochlear implant.**

**Model Answer:**
An Auditory Brainstem Implant (ABI) is a neuroprosthetic device that directly stimulates the **cochlear nucleus** in the brainstem to provide auditory sensation in patients whose auditory nerve is non-functional.

**Two clinical indications:**
1. **Neurofibromatosis Type 2 (NF2)** patients undergoing removal of bilateral vestibular schwannomas, where the cochlear nerve is sacrificed.
2. **Congenital absence of the cochlear nerve** (cochlear nerve aplasia) or severe inner-ear malformations such as Michel deformity.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between the stimulation site of a cochlear implant and an auditory brainstem implant. Mention the typical number of electrodes in each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cochlear Implant | Auditory Brainstem Implant |
|---|---|---|
| Stimulation Site | Cochlea (scala tympani) | Cochlear nucleus (brainstem) |
| Number of Electrodes | 16–24 | 21 (standard) |

The ABI bypasses the entire peripheral auditory pathway and stimulates the **first central auditory nucleus**.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **components, working principle, and clinical indications** of an Auditory Brainstem Implant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **safety limits (Shannon limit) and the significance of charge-balanced biphasic pulses** in ABI stimulation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Components, Working, Indications (7 marks)**

**Components (2 marks):**
- **External:** BTE sound processor, microphone, transmitter coil.
- **Internal:** Receiver-stimulator (ASIC), 21-electrode silicone paddle array, ground/reference electrode.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
Sound → microphone → spectral band analysis → envelope extraction → pulse generation → RF transcutaneous link → receiver-stimulator → biphasic current injection via paddle electrodes placed on the **dorsal and ventral cochlear nucleus** in the lateral recess of the 4th ventricle → depolarization of CN neurons → signal travels via brainstem auditory pathways → auditory cortex perceives sound.

**Clinical Indications (2 marks):**
- NF2 with bilateral vestibular schwannomas.
- Cochlear nerve aplasia.
- Severe cochlear ossification post-meningitis.
- Failed cochlear implant with intact nerve absent.

**Part (b) — Safety and Biphasic Pulses (7 marks)**

**Shannon Limit (3 marks):**
The Shannon limit is an empirical safety boundary: $D \cdot \log(Q) \leq k$ where $D$ is charge density and $Q$ is charge per phase. The widely cited value is **$D \leq 30\, \mu C/cm^2$ per phase** (with $t_{ph}$ adjusted) to avoid electrochemical damage such as platinum dissolution and hydrogen gas evolution at the electrode-tissue interface.

**Biphasic Pulses (3 marks):**
A biphasic pulse has two phases of opposite polarity, ensuring **net charge = 0** ($Q_{+} = Q_{-}$). This prevents:
- DC residual polarization
- Electrolysis at the electrode
- Neural damage from metal ion release

**Engineering Signif

topics.title: Audio Frequency Induction Loop Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT722 - ASSISTIVE MEDICAL DEVICES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Audio Frequency Induction Loop Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **AFILS — Audio Frequency Induction Loop System**
> An **Audio Frequency Induction Loop System (AFILS)**, also known as a **Hearing Loop** or **Induction Loop System**, is a **wireless assistive listening technology** that transmits an audio signal via a **time-varying magnetic field** generated by a current-carrying loop of wire. The magnetic field is detected by a **Telecoil (T-coil)** — a small inductive pickup coil — embedded inside a hearing aid, cochlear implant, or a dedicated handheld receiver. The system conforms to the international performance standard **IEC 60118-4**.

### Operational Block Synopsis

The chain is:

$$\text{Audio Source} \rightarrow \text{AFILS Amplifier} \rightarrow \text{Induction Loop Wire} \rightarrow \text{Magnetic Field (H)} \rightarrow \text{T-coil pickup} \rightarrow \text{Output to ear}$$

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Operating Frequency Band | **100 Hz – 5 kHz** |
| Reference Magnetic Field Strength | **100 mA/m (peak)** |
| Maximum Allowable Field Strength | **400 mA/m (peak)** |
| Governing International Standard | **IEC 60118-4** |
| Magnetic Field Reference Distance | Centre of the looped area (at ear height) |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Invisible Spotlight for Sound"

> [!NOTE] **Analogy: The Movie Subtitle for the Hard-of-Hearing**
> Imagine a large room where a presenter is speaking. Speakers on the wall project the sound, but people with hearing loss struggle because of **distance**, **reverberation**, and **ambient noise** that smear the speech signal. An **induction loop** works like an **invisible spotlight made of magnetic field** projected *across the room*. Only hearing aids switched to the **"T" (Telecoil)** mode can "see" this spotlight — they pluck the clean audio signal directly out of the air, **bypassing the room acoustics entirely**.
> - The **wire loop** = the "spotlight projector"
> - The **amplifier** = the "bulb" driving the projector
> - The **T-coil in the hearing aid** = the "eye" that can detect this special light
> - The **magnetic field** = the "invisible light" carrying pure, clean audio

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why magnetic field, not radio waves?**
> The system deliberately uses a **near-field magnetic** coupling (low-impedance, quasi-static) rather than radiated electromagnetic waves. This makes the system **immune to RF interference**, **confined to the looped area** (low spillover), and **completely passive at the receiver** — the user's hearing aid needs no batteries for the T-coil pickup.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE] **Key Engineering Metrics (bold values are KTU-mandated)**
> - **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{H/m}$
> - **Reference field strength (IEC 60118-4):** **100 mA/m (peak)**
> - **Maximum permitted field strength:** **400 mA/m (peak)**
> - **Audio bandwidth:** **100 Hz to 5 kHz** (speech intelligibility range)
> - **Standard listening height:** **1.2 m** (seated ear level) or **1.7 m** (standing)
> - **Background magnetic noise floor:** must be ≤ **-22 dB relative to 100 mA/m reference**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Electromagnetic Principles

AFILS operation rests on two pillars of electromagnetism:

### 2.1.1 Ampère's Circuital Law (Generation Side)
A time-varying current in the loop wire generates a magnetic field around it.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Ampère's Law (differential form for the loop field)**
> The magnetic field strength $H$ at the centre of a circular loop of radius $R$ carrying current $I$ is:
>
> $$H = \frac{I}{2R}$$
>
> For a rectangular perimeter loop, the field at the centre may be approximated using the **focal length formula** for each straight segment. For an **infinite straight wire** at perpendicular distance $r$ from the wire:
>
> $$H = \frac{I}{2\pi r}$$
>
> AFILS designs treat the field inside the loop as **quasi-uniform** within a "listening plane" at a specified height.

### 2.1.2 Faraday's Law of Induction (Reception Side)
The time-varying magnetic flux through the T-coil induces an EMF.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Faraday's Law of Induction**
> $$\varepsilon = -N \frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$$
>
> where $N$ = number of turns in the T-coil, $\Phi_B$ = magnetic flux per turn.
>
> Since the audio signal is sinusoidal, $\Phi_B(t) = B A \cos(\omega t)$, giving a peak induced voltage of:
>
> $$\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = N \cdot B \cdot A \cdot \omega$$
>
> where $\omega = 2\pi f$, and $B = \mu_0 H$ is the magnetic flux density.

---

## 2.2 Why Use Magnetic Coupling Instead of Speakers?

| Property | Acoustic Speaker | Induction Loop (Magnetic) |
|---|---|---|
| Distance attenuation | Severe (inverse-square) | **Minimal within loop** |
| Reverberation / echo | Yes, corrupts signal | **Bypassed entirely** |
| Background noise coupling | Yes | **No (T-coil rejects)** |
| Privacy | Public (everyone hears) | **Localised to loop** |
| Receiver requirement | None | **Hearing aid with T-coil** |
| Standardisation | None | **IEC 60118-4** |

---

## 2.3 System Architecture — Block by Block

1. **Audio Source** — Microphone, TV audio output, public address (PA) feed, mixing console.
2. **AFILS Amplifier** — A specialised constant-current amplifier (NOT a voltage amp). It drives a fixed current into the loop regardless of loop impedance, maintaining a stable $H$-field.
3. **Loop Cable** — Single or multi-turn copper conductor laid along the perimeter of the room, floor, or ceiling. Typically **insulated copper wire, 0.5 mm² to 1.5 mm² cross-section**.
4. **Magnetic Listening Field** — The $H$-field established within the loop area at **listening height**.
5. **T-coil Receiver** — Inductive pickup coil (typically thousands of turns of very fine wire wound on a ferrite core) inside the hearing aid, switched via the "T" or "MT" program.
6. **Output** — Clean, amplified audio delivered directly into the hearing aid's loudspeaker / ear.

> [!NOTE] **Constant-Current Operation Justification**
> A constant-voltage amplifier would cause $H$ to vary with loop resistance (e.g., long cable runs, temperature). A **constant-current amplifier** ensures $H = I/2R$ remains stable, satisfying the IEC 60118-4 reference field.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{I}{2R}$ | Magnetic field strength at centre of circular loop | A/m |
| 2 | $H = \dfrac{I}{2\pi r}$ | Field at perpendicular distance $r$ from infinite straight wire | A/m |
| 3 | $B = \mu_0 H$ | Magnetic flux density (in free space) | Tesla (T); $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m |
| 4 | $\Phi_B = B \cdot A \cdot \cos\theta$ | Magnetic flux through pickup coil | Weber (Wb) |
| 5 | $\varepsilon = -N \dfrac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$ | Faraday's law — induced EMF | Volt |
| 6 | $\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = N B A \omega$ | Peak EMF for sinusoidal excitation | Volt; $\omega = 2\pi f$ |
| 7 | $H_{\text{ref}} = 100\ \text{mA/m (peak)}$ | IEC 60118-4 reference field | Specified value |
| 8 | $H_{\text{max}} = 400\ \text{mA/m (peak)}$ | Maximum permitted field strength | Specified ceiling |
| 9 | $P_{\text{loop}} = I^2 R_{\text{loop}}$ | Power dissipated in loop (resistive loss) | Watt |
| 10 | $R_{\text{loop}} = \rho \dfrac{L}{A_{\text{cs}}}$ | DC resistance of loop wire | $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega\cdot$m |

> [!WARNING] **Exam Pitfall — Vertical Bars in Tables**
> When writing modulus or absolute value, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX. **Never use raw `|`** inside markdown table cells, as it breaks the table syntax and KTU digital evaluation scripts may mis-render.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Public Venues:** Theatres, airports, banks, places of worship, courtrooms (often legally mandated under the **Equality Act / ADA**).
- **Transport:** Ticket counters, taxi intercoms, train station announcement desks.
- **Home Use:** TV-listening loops for personal use — a "sofa loop" around the perimeter of a living room.
- **Medical:** Hospital receptions, consultation rooms, patient bedside communication.
- **Education:** Classroom installations to assist hearing-impaired students.
- **Emergency Systems:** Integrated with fire-alarm voice announcement systems for accessibility.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Magnetic Field at Centre of a Circular Loop

> **Objective:** Derive $H = I/(2R)$ for a single-turn circular loop of radius $R$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Biot–Savart Law to a current element.**
The magnetic field contribution $dH$ at the centre from a small arc element $d\vec{\ell}$ carrying current $I$ is:

$$dH = \frac{I \, d\ell}{4\pi R^2}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate around the full circumference.**
For a complete loop, $\oint d\ell = 2\pi R$. Substituting:

$$H = \oint \frac{I \, d\ell}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{I}{4\pi R^2} \oint d\ell = \frac{I}{4\pi R^2} \cdot 2\pi R$$

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$H = \frac{2\pi R \cdot I}{4\pi R^2} = \frac{I}{2R}$$

Hence the **magnetic field at the centre of a single-turn circular loop of radius $R$** carrying current $I$ is:

$$\boxed{H = \frac{I}{2R}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Field at Distance $r$ from an Infinite Straight Wire (Ampère's Law Path)

> **Objective:** Derive $H = I/(2\pi r)$ using Ampère's Circuital Law.

**Step 1 — Statement of Ampère's Law.**

$$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{\ell} = I_{\text{enclosed}}$$

**Step 2 — Choose a circular Amperian loop** of radius $r$ concentric with the wire. By cylindrical symmetry, $\vert \vec{H} \vert$ is constant on this path and tangential to it.

$$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{\ell} = H \cdot 2\pi r$$

**Step 3 — Equate and solve.**

$$H \cdot 2\pi r = I$$

$$\boxed{H = \frac{I}{2\pi r}}$$

This is the basis for modelling **straight perimeter segments** in rectangular room loops.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Induced EMF in a T-coil (Faraday's Law)

> **Objective:** Derive the peak voltage induced in a T-coil receiver.

**Step 1 — Express the time-varying magnetic flux.**
Let the audio signal be sinusoidal with peak current $I_0$ and angular frequency $\omega$. The flux density at the T-coil is:

$$B(t) = \mu_0 H(t) = \mu_0 H_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Magnetic flux through the T-coil** of cross-sectional area $A$ and $N$ turns:

$$\Phi_B(t) = N \cdot B(t) \cdot A = N \mu_0 H_0 A \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Faraday's Law.**

$$\varepsilon(t) = -\frac{d\Phi_B}{dt} = -N \mu_0 H_0 A \omega \cos(\omega t)$$

**Step 4 — Peak value of induced EMF.**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = N \mu_0 H_0 A \omega}$$

**Interpretation:** Higher audio frequency → higher induced EMF. This is why the IEC 60118-4 standard specifies a **flat $H$-field frequency response** between **100 Hz and 5 kHz** — any droop in $H(f)$ would cause audible loss at high frequencies (consonants like /s/, /t/, /f/, crucial for speech intelligibility).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Problem Walkthrough

> [!IMPORTANT] **Worked Example (KTU-style numerical)**
> **Q:** A single-turn circular AFILS loop of radius **$R = 4\ \text{m}$** carries a sinusoidal audio current of peak amplitude **$I_0 = 2.5\ \text{A}$** at frequency **$f = 1\ \text{kHz}$**. A T-coil receiver has $N = 1000$ turns and effective pickup area $A = 0.5\ \text{cm}^2 = 0.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$.
>
> **Find:** (i) Peak magnetic field strength $H_0$ at the loop centre.
> (ii) Peak induced EMF in the T-coil.

### Solution:

**Part (i) — Magnetic field strength.**

$$H_0 = \frac{I_0}{2R} = \frac{2.5}{2 \times 4} = \frac{2.5}{8} = 0.3125\ \text{A/m}$$

Converting to mA/m:

$$H_0 = 312.5\ \text{mA/m (peak)}$$

**Comparison with IEC 60118-4:** The reference is **100 mA/m** and the maximum is **400 mA/m**. Therefore this loop's field is **3.125× the reference** — still within the maximum allowed, but a designer might reduce the drive current to bring it closer to the 100 mA/m standard for a typical speech-listening environment.

**Part (ii) — Peak induced EMF.**

Step 1 — Compute angular frequency:

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2 \pi \times 1000 = 2000\pi\ \text{rad/s} \approx 6283.19\ \text{rad/s}$$

Step 2 — Compute $B_0 = \mu_0 H_0$:

$$B_0 = (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times 0.3125 = 3.927 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{T}$$

Step 3 — Plug into Faraday's peak EMF equation:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = N \mu_0 H_0 A \omega = N B_0 A \omega$$

$$\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = 1000 \times (3.927 \times 10^{-7}) \times (0.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 6283.19$$

Step 4 — Numerator grouping for clarity:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = 1000 \times 3.927 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 6283.19$$

$$\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = 1000 \times 3.927 \times 0.5 \times 6283.19 \times 10^{-7-4}$$

$$\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = 1000 \times 12341.43 \times 10^{-11}$$

$$\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = 1.234 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 = 1.234 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{V} \times 1000$$

Recomputing more carefully:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = 1000 \times 3.927 \times 10^{-7} \times 5 \times 10^{-5} \times 6283.19$$

$$\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = 1000 \times 3.927 \times 5 \times 6283.19 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = 1000 \times 123411.66 \times 10^{-12} = 1.234 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{V} \cdot 1000 / 1000$$

$$\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} = 1.234 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{V} = 123.4\ \mu\text{V}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\text{peak}} \approx 123.4\ \mu\text{V}}$$

> [!NOTE] **Interpretation**
> This is a small but perfectly usable signal — the T-coil and hearing aid's input stage typically have sensitivities in the **µV range**, so this magnitude is sufficient to drive a hearing-impaired listener's audio chain at 1 kHz.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Loop Type vs Use Case

| Loop Topology | Geometry | Magnetic Pattern | Typical Application | Spillover |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Perimeter Loop** | Single turn around room edge | Uniform inside, sharp droop outside | Theatres, halls, classrooms | High (audible in adjacent rooms) |
| **Figure-8 Loop** | Two adjacent loops with opposite phase | Cancellation in adjacent spaces | Wall-mounted between two rooms | **Very Low** (spill suppressed) |
| **Phased Array** | Multi-segment with phase shifts | Tightly localised "spot" | Bank counters, ticket desks | **Very Low** |
| **Counter Loop** | Small mat under a desk | Localised, ~1 m radius | Reception desks, retail counters | Localised |
| **Sofa / Home Loop** | Perimeter of living room | Uniform in seating area | TV listening at home | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Architecture of an AFILS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Audio Source Microphone or TV or PA Output] --> PREAMP[Pre Amplifier and Line Driver]
    PREAMP --> AFIAMP[AFILS Constant Current Amplifier]
    AFIAMP --> LOOP[Induction Loop Perimeter Cable]
    LOOP -- generates --> HFLD[Magnetic Field H Plane at Ear Height]
    HFLD -- induces EMF in --> TCOIL[T coil inside Hearing Aid]
    TCOIL --> HAAMP[Hearing Aid Internal Amplifier]
    HAAMP --> EAR[Output to Ear via Speaker]
    LOOP -. parallel monitor .-> MON[Field Strength Monitor and Loop Listener]
    MON -- feedback --> AFIAMP

```

```mermaid
        A4 --> A1
    end

```
